subject: Dermatology
question: Which of the following is TRUE regarding comedones?
options: Comedone is a Primary Skin Lesion
Comedone is a Specific Lesion of Acne Vulgaris
Closed Comedones are also called as Black Heads
Comedones are earliest Lesions of Rosacea

explanation.short: Ans. B) Comedone is a specific Lesion of Acne Vulgaris
explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Comedones (are considered as special lesions, NOT primary skin lesions) are the specific lesions of acne vulgaris Acne is a chronic inflammatory disease of the pilosebaceous unit . Comedonal lesions are the Earliest lesions of acne, but NOT Rosacea. They represent the keratin plugs of the pilosebaceous duct. When the keratin plug is inside the follicle, it is called a closed Comedone or whitehead (NOT black head as mentioned in option C)
• When the keratin plug is exposed to the environment, it gets oxidised by the air & turns black which is known as open comedone ( black head) .
• Educational Objective:
• Comedones are specific skin lesions of Acne vulgaris, with closed comedones known as whiteheads and open comedones as blackheads , and are not related to the initial presentation of Rosacea.
• Rooks Textbook of Dermatology- 9th Edition Volume 3 Chapter 90 Page No 90.1
explanation.ref: Rooks Textbook of Dermatology- 9th Edition Volume 3 Chapter 90 Page No 90.1

subject: Dermatology
question: A 19-Year-Old Girl came to Dermatology OPD with complaints of Facial Lesions. On examination, Multiple Comedones, Papules, Pustules & Nodulocystic Lesions were present. Most likely Diagnosis for these examination findings in this patient would be?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. Acne Rosacea or Rosacea:
• It is predominantly a disease with onset in middle aged adults ( 30-50 years old ) & involves the convexities of the face . Comedones are absent in Rosacea
• Option C. Lichen Planus:
• The lesions of lichen planus can be described as 5Ps – Purple Polygonal , Pruritic , Plane Topped (Flat), Papules . NOT as described in this question.
• Adult Scabies does not have Facial lesions.
• Educational Objective:
• Acne vulgaris is diagnosed by the presence of comedones, inflammatory papules, pustules, and occasionally nodules , primarily on the face, chest, and back , and is a common dermatological condition among teenagers and young adults.
explanation.ref: Rooks Textbook of Dermatology- 9th Edition Volume 3 Chapter 90 Page No 90.21 & 90.22

subject: Dermatology
question: Which of the following is NOT a component of SAHA syndrome?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• SAHA syndrome is NOT associated with Synovitis.
• Seborrhoea Acne Hirsutism Androgenetic alopecia
• SAPHO Syndrome consists of:
• Synovitis Acne Pustulosis Hyperostosis Osteitis
• Important Syndromes Associated with Acne:
• Educational Objective:
• SAHA syndrome is a disorder characterized by Seborrhoea, Acne, Hirsutism, and Androgenetic alopecia , which are associated with increased androgen activity and do not include Synovitis, an unrelated inflammatory joint condition.
explanation.ref: Rooks Textbook of Dermatology- 9th Edition Volume 3 Chapter 90 Page No 90.8 & 90.9

subject: Dermatology
question: All of the following have an aetiological role in the causation of Acne Vulgaris EXCEPT?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Androgens are known to play a role in the development of acne by increasing sebum production, which is a key factor in acne pathogenesis
• Option B. Bacterial proliferation
• Bacterial proliferation , particularly Propionibacterium acnes , within the sebaceous follicles is a contributing factor to the development of inflammatory acne lesions
• Option C. Hypercornification of the duct
• Hypercornification of the duct , which refers to the overproduction of keratin leading to plugging of the follicle, is a primary event in acne formation
• Educational Objective:
• While androgens, bacterial proliferation, and hyper cornification of the pilosebaceous duct are established etiological factors in acne vulgaris , the role of oily food remains uncertain and is not considered a direct causative factor according to current evidence.
• Rooks Textbook of Dermatology- 9th Edition Volume 3 Chapter 90 Page No 90.18 & 90.22
explanation.ref: Rooks Textbook of Dermatology- 9th Edition Volume 3 Chapter 90 Page No 90.18 & 90.22

subject: Dermatology
question:
A teenager with oily skin presents with Nodulocystic Acne as shown in the given image below. What is the appropriate treatment?

[Image: https://cerebellum-web-static.s3.amazonaws.com/media/public/images/2024/02/24/picture5_Y2tYjaz.jpg]

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option B. Oral Steroids:
• They can be used as a short-term adjunct in severe inflammatory acne but are not the primary treatment modality.
• Option C. Topical Retinoids:
• They are used for mild to moderate acne and as maintenance therapy but are often not potent enough alone for severe nodulocystic acne.
• Option D. Topical Clindamycin
• Topical Clindamycin is an antibiotic that can help reduce the bacterial load but is also not sufficient as monotherapy for severe nodulocystic acne.
• Educational Objective:
• Oral isotretinoin is the treatment of choice for severe nodulocystic acne due to its comprehensive action on various pathogenic factors of the condition, and it is often commenced early to prevent scarring .
• Rooks Textbook of Dermatology- 9th Edition Volume 3 Chapter 90 Page No 90.7 & 90.37
explanation.ref: Rooks Textbook of Dermatology- 9th Edition Volume 3 Chapter 90 Page No 90.7 & 90.37

subject: Dermatology
question: An 18-year-old girl with pustular acne comes to your clinic for follow up saying that her acne has not reduced even after taking medications for 6 months. Which of the following drugs will you now prescribe?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. Oral Erythromycin
• It is typically used for its antibacterial properties in acne treatment but may not be effective in severe, treatment-resistant cases.
• Option B. Oral Steroids
• They can be used for their anti-inflammatory properties but are not a primary treatment for severe acne.
• Option D. Oral Tetracyclines
• Oral tetracyclines , like erythromycin, are used for their antibacterial effect but may not be sufficient in cases unresponsive to long-term treatment.
• Educational Objective:
• In cases of severe acne that does not respond to conventional therapies , isotretinoin is the most effective option and is known to significantly improve or even resolve the condition .
explanation.ref: Rooks Textbook of Dermatology- 9th Edition Volume 3 Chapter 90 Page No 90.7 & 90.37

subject: Dermatology
question: Consider the Following Statements: Trifarotene has been approved for Acne. Clascoterone Propionate Cream is effective in Acne. Which of the above statements is/are TRUE?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Clascoterone Propionate 1% cream inhibits Androgen Receptors & is Effective in Acne. Trifarotene is a Topical Fourth Generation Retinoid which was approved recently for Acne.
• Sanchez C, Keri J. Androgen Receptor Inhibitors in the Treatment of Acne Vulgaris: Efficacy and Safety Profiles of Clascoterone 1% Cream. Clin CosmetInvestig Dermatol. 2022 Jul 15; 15:1357-1366. Doi: 10.2147/CCID.S289750. PMID: 35874458; PMCID: PMC9297041.

subject: Dermatology
question: A teenage girl with moderate acne also has irregular menses. Which of the following is the drug of choice?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. Oral Acitretin
• It is a systemic retinoid used for psoriasis, not typically indicated for acne or menstrual irregularities.
• Option B. Oral Isotretinoin
• It is a potent treatment for severe recalcitrant nodular acne , not typically the first choice for moderate acne, and it requires strict contraception due to its teratogenicity.
• Option C. Oral Minocycline
• It is an antibiotic used for acne ; however, it would not address the hormonal component of the patient's irregular menses .
• Educational Objective:
• Oral contraceptive pills are a suitable treatment for a female with moderate acne and irregular menses due to their dual effect on regulating the menstrual cycle and reducing the hormonal contribution to acne.
• Fitzpatrick’s Textbook, 8th Edition Page 909
explanation.ref: Rooks Textbook of Dermatology- 9th Edition Volume 3 Chapter 90 Page No 90.43

subject: Dermatology
question: A 40-year-old woman who has had Erythematous Papulo Pustular Lesions over the Centre of her Face for the past two years comes to the Dermatology OPD. Comedones are absent. Background Erythema and Telangiectasia are present. Which diagnosis is most likely?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• It is usually accompanied by comedones , which are not present in this patient.
• Option C. Systemic Lupus Erythematosus (SLE)
• It may cause a malar rash but is typically accompanied by systemic symptoms and other cutaneous signs, which are not mentioned here.
• This presents as symmetrical hyperpigmented patches on the face , without papulopustular lesions.
• Educational Objective:
• Rosacea should be considered in adults presenting with Central Facial Erythema, Papulopustular Lesions , and Telangiectasia , particularly in the Absence of Comedones.
explanation.ref: Rooks Textbook of Dermatology- 9th Edition Volume 3 Chapter 90 Page No 91.1-6

subject: Dermatology
question: Flare of acne can be associated with all EXCEPT?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Phenytoin is known to induce acneiform eruptions as a side effect due to its influence on the metabolism of steroids within the body and its effect on the epidermal growth factor. Therefore, phenytoin can be associated with acne flares .
• Option B. Whey Protein:
• There is evidence that whey protein, which is a milk derivative , can exacerbate acne due to its ability to stimulate insulin and insulin-like growth factor (IGF-1), which in turn can increase sebum production and proliferation of skin cells , leading to acne .
• Option D. Anabolic Steroids:
• Anabolic steroids are well-documented in causing acne due to their androgenic effects , which increase sebum production and can lead to the development of acneiform eruptions , particularly on the trunk.
• Drug induced acne (Acneiform eruptions) are associated with the following:
• Topical or Systemic Steroids (most common) Anabolic steroids: Danazol Stanazolol Anticonvulsants : Carbamazepine, Phenytoin , Phenobarbitone Antituberculosis: Isoniazid, Pyrazinamide Whey protein also can be associated with Acne Flares. Clinically, Acneiform Eruptions are characteristically M onomorphic Drugs most commonly implicated in Acneiform Eruptions: World wide- Steroids India- Isoniazid
• Educational Objective:
• In considering drug-induced acne , healthcare providers should be aware that medications like phenytoin, whey protein supplements, and anabolic steroids can induce acne flares , whereas penicillin is not commonly associated with acne.
explanation.ref: Rooks Textbook of Dermatology- 9th Edition Volume 3 Chapter 90 Page No 90.10, 90.21 & 90.22

subject: Dermatology
question:
Identify the following condition?

[Image: https://cerebellum-web-static.s3.amazonaws.com/media/public/images/2024/02/24/picture13_mWEoBm1.jpg]

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• It is a chronic skin condition that can lead to rhinophyma but is characterized by flushing, redness , not the bulbous nose seen in rhinophyma.
• It is characterized by comedones, papules, pustules, and sometimes cysts, primarily affecting the face, back, and chest. It does not cause the bulbous changes of the nose seen in rhinophyma.
• This refers to a new and abnormal growth of tissue, which can be benign or malignant . While neoplasms can affect the nose, the picture shown is typical of rhinophyma, not a neoplasm.
• Educational Objective
• Rhinophyma is identified by a bulbous, red, and bumpy appearance of the nose due to sebaceous gland hyperplasia and connective tissue proliferation . It is important to differentiate it from other conditions such as rosacea , acne , and neoplasms .
• Fitzpatrick’s Dermatology in General Medicine Pg No- 1422
explanation.ref: Rooks Textbook of Dermatology- 9th Edition Volume 3 Chapter 91 Page No 91.7 & 91.14

subject: Dermatology
question: Fordyce’s Spots mainly involve which of the following Sites?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Fordyce spots / Ectopic or free-lying sebaceous glands:
• They occur most commonly on the upper lip > buccal mucosa. The heterotrophic sebaceous glands that make up Fordyce spots are connected directly to the skin or mucosal surface beneath them, in contrast to other sebaceous glands that open into follicles. They frequently appear on the areola of the breast , skin over the penile shaft , and the vermillion border of the upper lip (m/c) buccal mucosa. They frequently appear as multiple, symmetrical, discrete yellow papules. Tyson glands are Fordyce spots on the penis. They have no symptoms. Treatment: Reassurance
• Image: Fordyce spots on the vermillion border of both lips in A upper lip in B & buccal mucosa in C
• Educational Objective:
• Fordyce spots are ectopic sebaceous glands that are commonly found on the l ips and oral mucosa , presenting as small, pale, painless bumps.
explanation.ref: Bolognia Textbook of Dermatology 4 th Edition Page No 1392

subject: Dermatology
question:
Identify the CORRECT statement regarding the given condition?

[Image: https://cerebellum-web-static.s3.amazonaws.com/media/public/images/2024/02/24/picture15_jm8xdP4.jpg]

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• The image shows pearly penile papules. These are only present in males. The coronal margin of the glans penis is where they most frequently appear as smooth, flesh-coloured papules . They are benign and asymptomatic , and patient reassurance is part of the treatment.
• Note: Fordyce Spots (Tyson's glands ) are frequently mistaken for pearly penile papules.
• Fordyce Spots, which are Ectopic Sebaceous Glands, are frequently seen on the penis shaft. Fordyce spots on the penile shaft are shown in the image below.
• Educational Objective:
• Pearly penile papules are asymptomatic , benign, flesh-colored papules that typically appear around the corona of the glans penis and do not require treatment.
• Rooks Textbook of Dermatology- 9th Edition Volume 3 Chapter 90 Page No 90.24 111.5
• Fitzpatrick’s Dermatology, 9th Edition. Pg No:58
explanation.ref: Rooks Textbook of Dermatology- 9th Edition Volume 3 Chapter 90 Page No 90.24 111.5

subject: Dermatology
question:
You as an intern, are accompanying your Dermatology Senior Resident for reference of a 2-Week-Old Neonate in Paediatric Ward with the following skin finding. You diagnose it as a Disorder of Eccrine Glands, which the Senior Resident in Derma also accepts. At what level does the Obstruction occur in this condition?

[Image: https://cerebellum-web-static.s3.amazonaws.com/media/public/images/2024/02/24/picture17_ehwHjKq.jpg]

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option B: T he Malpighian Layer (also known as the Stratum Malpighii):
• It is not a site of sweat duct obstruction in miliaria crystallina.
• Option C: The Dermoepidermal Junction
• It is typically involved in miliaria profunda , a deeper form of miliaria , not seen in this case.
• Educational Objective:
• Miliaria crystallina is a disorder of the eccrine glands where the obstruction typically occurs at the level of the stratum corneum , causing the sweat to be trapped beneath the skin and leading to the appearance of tiny clear blisters or bumps on the skin .
explanation.ref: Rooks Textbook of Dermatology- 9th Edition Volume 3 Chapter 94 Page No 94.12

subject: Dermatology
question:
A 45-year-old obese female patient, complaining of repeated attacks of painful swellings & discharge over bilateral axilla since 6 years as shown in the image below. Which of the following is WRONG statement related to this condition?

[Image: https://cerebellum-web-static.s3.amazonaws.com/media/public/images/2024/02/24/picture19_MZSaKdC.jpg]
options: Hidradenitis Suppurativa is the likely Diagnosis
Smoking & Obesity are the two main factors associated with it
TNF Alpha Inhibitors like Adalimumab can be used in treatment
Fox Fordyce Disease is the other name for this condition

explanation.short: Ans. D) Fox Fordyce Disease is the other name for this condition
explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• It is a disease of occlusion of Hair Follicles & Apocrine Ducts. There are painful, deep seated inflamed lesions in the apocrine gland bearing areas of the body, most commonly the axillary inguinal & anogenital regions. More common in young women of ages 20-29 years. Obesity & smoking (Option B) are the two main factors associated with hidradenitis suppurativa.
• Antibiotics – tetracycline, clindamycin Intralesional corticosteroids Immunosuppressants Acitretin Anti TNF alpha therapy (Adalimumab i.e. Option C) Surgery
• Educational Objective:
• In patients presenting with chronic painful swellings and discharge in areas rich in apocrine glands , consider hidradenitis suppurativa as a likely diagnosis. Smokingcessation and weight management are key components of managing this condition, and TNF alpha inhibitors may be used in treatment. Fox-Fordyce disease is a separate condition and should not be confused with Hidradenitis suppurativa.
• Rooks Textbook of Dermatology- 9th Edition Volume 3 Chapter 92 Page No 92.1-10
explanation.ref: Rooks Textbook of Dermatology- 9th Edition Volume 3 Chapter 92 Page No 92.1-10

subject: Dermatology
question: Apocrine Glands are present in?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• The face primarily contains eccrine sweat glands rather than apocrine glands. Eccrine glands are involved in thermoregulation and are not typically associated with body odor.
• Option C. Palms & soles
• Like the face, the palms and soles predominantly feature eccrine sweat glands . These areas are rich in these glands due to their role in grip and thermoregulation , not in scent secretion as is the function of apocrine glands.
• The scalp does contain apocrine glands, but they are not as prevalent as those found in the axillary region. The scalp is also rich in sebaceous and eccrine glands , which contribute to the hair's health and moisture.
• Educational Objective:
• The apocrine glands are present in the axilla , areolar (around nipples) , and anogenital areas and their activity is associated with hair f ollicles in specific body regions, being primarily stimulated by hormonal changes, not by direct thermoregulatory needs.
explanation.ref: Rooks Textbook of Dermatology- 9th Edition Volume 1 Page No 2.9

subject: Dermatology
question: All of the following are examples of Ectopic Sebaceous Glands EXCEPT?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. Fordyce Spots :
• They are true ectopic sebaceous glands found in the oral and genital mucosa .
• Option B. Tyson's Glands
• They are considered a form of ectopic sebaceous glands associated with the prepuce .
• Option C. Meibomian Glands
• They are modified sebaceous glands located in the eyelids and are responsible for secreting a lipid layer of the tear film.
• Educational Objective:
• Koplik's spots are not ectopic sebaceous glands; they are an oral manifestation of measles , whereas Fordyce spots , Tyson's glands , and Meibomian glands are all forms of ectopic or modified sebaceous glands .
• Rooks Textbook of Dermatology- 9th Edition, Page No 2.9, 25.85
explanation.ref: Rooks Textbook of Dermatology- 9th Edition, Page No 2.9, 25.85

subject: Dermatology
question:
A 35-year-old female presents with Extremely Itchy Rashes in Both Armpits for the last 70 Days, as shown below. Histopathological Examination shows Dilation of the Follicular Infundibulum and Inflammatory Lymphohistiocytic Infiltration around the Infundibulum. Which Type of Glands are likely involved in this condition?

[Image: https://cerebellum-web-static.s3.amazonaws.com/media/public/images/2024/02/24/picture26_Q5bOKUM.jpg]

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. Eccrine Sweat Glands
• They are not associated with hair follicles and do not typically present with the described histopathological changes.
• Option C. Sebaceous Glands
• They are usually associated with the pilosebaceous unit and do not cause the described dilation of the follicular infundibulum with the lymphohistiocytic infiltration.
• Option D. Mammary Glands
• They are not located in the armpits and do not present with such histopathological changes
• Educational Objective:
• Fox-Fordyce disease is associated with the apocrine sweat glands and is characterized by itchy, inflamed papules in areas where these glands are concentrated, such as the armpits , with histopathological findings of dilation and inflammation around the follicular infundibulum.
• Rooks’ Textbook of Dermatology- 9th Edition, Page No 44.21
• Bolognia Textbook of Dermatology 4 th Edition Page No 755
explanation.ref: Rooks’ Textbook of Dermatology- 9th Edition, Page No 44.21

subject: Dermatology
question: A 34-year-old female suffering from Obsessive Compulsive Disorder (OCD) presents with persistent Acne Scars. What can be the probable Diagnosis?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. Acne Conglobata
• It is a severe form of acne characterized by deep, inflamed nodules and abscesses , and not typically associated with OCD.
• Option B. Acne Mechanica
• It is incorrect as this is triggered by mechanical factors such as pressure, friction, rubbing, squeezing, or stretching .
• Option C. Acne Fulminans
• It is a sudden onset, severely inflammatory acne accompanied by systemic symptoms such as fever and arthralgia , not primarily associated with OCD.
• Educational Objective:
• Acne excoriee is a form of acne that is exacerbated by the compulsive picking of the skin , often associated with psychiatric disorders such as OCD , and distinguished by Persistent Acne Scars.
• Bolognia Textbook of Dermatology 4 th Edition Page No 693
explanation.ref: Rooks Textbook of Dermatology- 9th Edition, Page No 90.23, 90.52

question:
Match the following conditions in Column A and the Column B

[Image: https://cerebellum-web-static.s3.amazonaws.com/media/public/images/2024/02/24/screenshot-2024-02-24-100951.jpg]

explanation.detail:

• The correct matching of skin conditions to their pathophysiological description aids in understanding their underlying mechanisms, which is essential for accurate diagnosis and treatment in dermatology.

subject: Dermatology
question: Which of the following is the causative agent for Acne Fulminans?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• It is a genus of gram-positive bacteria that is part of the normal flora of the human gut and does not cause acne.
• This is a genus of bacteria that can be associated with bacterial vaginosis but is not a cause of acne.
• This is a genus of bacteria that can cause actinomycosis, which typically presents as chronic progressive suppurative disease , not acne.
• Educational Objective:
• Cutibacterium acnes (formerly Propionibacterium acnes ) is the bacteria associated with the pathogenesis of acne , including severe forms such as acne fulminans.
explanation.ref: Rooks Textbook of Dermatology- 9 th Edition Page No 90.50, 154.10

subject: Dermatology
question:
A Female presented in Dermatology OPD with Acne that is not resolving on Oral Isotretinoin and Antibiotics Therapy. Which of the following is the next best step among the following options?

[Image: https://cerebellum-web-static.s3.amazonaws.com/media/public/images/2024/02/24/picture31_N2YN0PZ.jpg]

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A: Looking for Dietary Triggers
• It could be considered, but this should already be taken care of before prescribing isotretinoin and antibiotics .
• Option C: Checking for Antibiotic Resistance:
• This seems an appropriate step but can’t prioritize over ruling out HAIR-AN syndrome in this patient.
• Option D: Drug Triggers
• Drug triggers are unlikely to be the primary cause of acne patient who is not responding to isotretinoin and antibiotics.
• Moreover, lesions in Drug-induced acne are monomorphic and comedones are absent.
• Educational Objective:
• When faced with a female patient whose acne has not improved with oral isotretinoin and antibiotics , the next best step is to evaluate f or hyperandrogenism, which can point to an underlying hormonal cause such as PCOS , necessitating a different treatment approach.
explanation.ref: Williams Gynaecology 4 th Edition: Page No 392-394

subject: Dermatology
question: Identify the True statement among the following?
options: Stratum Spinosum is underdeveloped in Very Low Birth Weight infants in the initial 7 Days.
Icthyosis Vulgaris is due to the Deficiency of Filaggrin Protein which is found in Stratum Basale
The Malpighian Layer of the Epidermis includes both Stratum Basale & Stratum Granulosum as a unit.
Skin, Hairs, Nails, the Subcutaneous Tissue below the Skin & assorted Glands constitute the Integumentary System.

explanation.short: Skin, Hairs, Nails, the Subcutaneous Tissue below the Skin & assorted Glands constitute the Integumentary System.
explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• In the first seven days after birth, the stratum corneum (NOT Stratum spinosum) of very low birth weight infants is underdeveloped. In preterm infants, the stratum corneum is permeable & becomes like the adult & full-term infant after 2-3 weeks of post-natal maturation . The layers of the epidermis begin to form from the bottom up . As a result, the Stratum basale / Stratum germinativum forms first and the stratum corneum forms last.
• In icthyosis vulgaris there is deficiency of filaggrin protein. They are found in the keratohyaline granules of stratum granulosum (NOT Stratum basale) in the form of Profilaggrin (made of filaggrin monomers) Profilaggrin undergoes proteolytic processing to yield individual filaggrin monomers that bind & aggregate keratin bundles & intermediate filaments between the stratumgranulosum & corneum to form the epidermal barrier of the skin .
• The Malpighian layer is named after Marcello Malpighi . The Malpighian layer of the epidermis includes both stratum basale & stratum spinosum (NOT stratum granulosum) as a unit.
• Educational Objective:
• Understand that the integumentary system includes the skin, hair, nails, subcutaneous tissue, and associated glands , and identify the correct layers and proteins associated with specific functions and pathologies within the skin.
• Rooks textbook of dermatology- 9 th Edition: Volume 1 Chapter 2, Page no 2.2, 2.5, 2.7
explanation.ref: Rooks textbook of dermatology- 9 th Edition: Volume 1 Chapter 2, Page no 2.2, 2.5, 2.7

subject: Dermatology
question: Identify the FALSE statement among the following?
options: The Stratum Corneum, also known as the Horny Layer, consists of Cells without Nucleus & hence does not divide.
Granules of Eleidin are present in the Stratum Lucidum.
Odland Bodies are present in Stratum Basale
The Superficial Vascular Plexus of Skin is present below the Papillary Dermis.

explanation.short: Ans. C) Odland Bodies are present in Stratum Basale
explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A: Stratum Corneum: ( Horny layer )
• Composed of non-nucleated cells that don't divide . Barrier function : It stops fluids from the body from leaking into the environment and stops environmental microbes from entering the body. Changes during keratinocyte ascent:
• Loss of nucleus , Size increase , Shape flattening, Dehydration , Loss of metabolic activity.
• Option B: Stratum Lucidum (Clear Cell Layer): ( sandwiched between the granulosum and corneum )
• Found in thick skin of palms and soles . Contains eleidin granules , giving it the translucent appearance . Abundance of keratin filaments and eleidin granules . These cells could be considered transition cells because they are still nucleated.
• Below the papillary dermis is the skin's superficial vascular plexus. The vascular supply in the dermis is abundant (unlike the epidermis which is avascular ). The Dermis can be separated into papillary dermis (upper 1/10) & Reticular Dermis (lower 9/10) The Deep Vascular Plexus is present below the reticular dermis , and the superficial vascular plexus is present between the papillary and reticular dermis (Subpapillary Plexus).
• Reactive units of skin. The Superficial Reactive Unit (SRU) comprises the Epidermis (E), the Junction zone (J), and the papillary body (PB, or papillary dermis) with the superficial microvascular plexus . The Dermal Reactive Unit (DRU) consists of the Reticular Dermis (RD) and the Deep Dermal Microvascular Plexus (DVP) . The subcutaneous reactive unit (S) consists of lobules (L) and septae (Sep). A fourth unit is the appendages (A; hair and sebaceous glands are the only appendages shown). HF , hair follicle .
• Provides nutrients, oxygen , and temperature control. Superficial plexus controls blood flow for heat regulation.
• The location of the papillary and reticular layers of the dermis are depicted in the image below.
• Educational Objective:
• Understand the characteristics and contents of each layer of the epidermis, and recognize the location of various structures within the skin layers , such as the Odland bodies and the superficial vascular plexus .
• Rooks Textbook of Dermatology- 9 th Edition: Volume 1 Chapter 2, Page No 2.6, 2.7, 2.41
explanation.ref: Rooks Textbook of Dermatology- 9 th Edition: Volume 1 Chapter 2, Page No 2.6, 2.7, 2.41

subject: Dermatology
question: Which is the Correct Order of Layers of Epidermis from Deep to Superficial?
options: Stratum Corneum, Stratum Spinosum, Stratum Granulosum, Stratum Basale
Stratum Corneum, Stratum Granulosum, Stratum Spinosum, Stratum Basale
Stratum Basale, Stratum Spinosum, Stratum Granulosum, Stratum Corneum
Stratum Basale, Stratum Granulosum, Stratum Spinosum, Stratum Corneum

explanation.short: Ans. C) Stratum Basale, Stratum Spinosum, Stratum Granulosum, Stratum Corneum
explanation.detail:
• Explanation
• From deep to superficial , the layers of the epidermis are as follows:
• Stratum Basale Stratum Spinosum Stratum Granulosum Stratum Lucidum Stratum corneum
• Stratum lucidum found in thick skin (palms, soles), between corneum and granulosum . Epidermis is superficial , keratinized , stratified , squamous epithelium . Epidermis is avascular , relies on dermis for nutrition through dermoepidermal junction . Skin thickness varies: thinnest on eyelids , thickest on palms and soles.
• Educational Objective:
• Recognize the correct sequence of the epidermal layers from the deepest to the most superficial as Stratum Basale, Stratum Spinosum, Stratum Granulosum, and Stratum Corneum to understand the process of keratinocyte maturation and migration.
explanation.ref: Rooks Textbook of Dermatology- 9 th Edition: Volume 1 Chapter 2, Page No 2.5

subject: Dermatology
question: A 3-Year-Old Female Child presented with Skin Papules. You suspect it to be Langerhans's Cell Histiocytosis (LCH). Which of the following is Most Specific Marker of Langerhans Cells?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. CD1a –
• CD1a is a marker that is indeed expressed on Langerhans cells and can be used to identify them. However, it is not as specific as CD207 for Langerhans cells because CD1a can also be expressed on some other dendritic cells.
• Option B. CD3 –
• This is a T-cell marker , and while Langerhans cells are part of the immune system , they are not T-cells, so CD3 would not be the right marker for identifying them.
• Option D. CD68 –
• CD68 is a marker for macrophages and other cells of the monocyte lineage . While Langerhans cells are related to macrophages and dendritic cells , CD68 is not specific for Langerhans cells.
• Educational Objective:
• Identify CD207 (Langerin) as the most specific marker for Langerhans cells , which is crucial for the diagnosis of Langerhans cell histiocytosis .
explanation.ref: Rooks Textbook of Dermatology- 9 th Edition: Volume 1 Chapter 2, Page No 2.4

subject: Dermatology
question: What is the location of Meissner’s Corpuscles in Skin?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. Stratum Lucidum –
• Option A. Stratum Lucidum
• This is a thin , clear layer of dead skin cells in the epidermis named for its translucent appearance under a microscope. It is found only in areas of thick skin , such as the palms of the hands and soles of the feet . Meissner’s corpuscles are not located in this layer.
• Option B. Stratum Basale –
• Option B. Stratum Basale
• This is the deepest layer of the five layers of the epidermis , where cells divide and begin their journey to the surface. Meissner’s corpuscles are not located in this layer.
• Option D. Reticular Dermis –
• Option D. Reticular Dermis
• This is the deeper layer of the dermis , beneath the papillary dermis , composed of dense irregular connective tissue featuring collagen and elastic fibers . Meissner’s corpuscles are not found in this layer as they are associated with the more superficial papillary layer.
• Educational Objective
• Recognize that Meissner’s corpuscles are located in the papillary dermis of the skin, where they function as mechanoreceptors for light touch.
• Andrew’s disease of the skin: Clinical dermatology- 11 th edition page 8
• Ganong’s review of medical physiology 25 th edition pages no: 159-163
• Ganong’s review of medical physiology 26 th edition pages no: 158
explanation.ref: Andrew’s disease of the skin: Clinical dermatology- 11 th edition page 8

subject: Dermatology
question: Which of the following statements regarding Desmosomes is FALSE?
options: They connect Keratinocytes in the Epidermis of the Skin.
Acantholysis is Loss of Cohesion between Keratinocytes.
Pemphigus Vulgaris has Antibodies directed against Desmoglein.
They cannot resist Mechanical Stress as they are Weakly Adhesive.

explanation.short: They cannot resist Mechanical Stress as they are Weakly Adhesive.
explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A: Keratinocytes in the skin's epidermis are connected by desmosomes , also known as macula adherens . It is a cell structure designed specifically for adhesion between cells.
• Option B: Acantholysis can result from the separation of keratinocytes caused by the loss of any one protein .
• Option C: Pemphigus vulgaris is an autoimmune condition brought on by antibodies that target Desmoglein found in desmosomes.
• Loss of desmosomes causes keratinocyte cohesion to break down (acantholysis ), which leads to blister formation. Diseases of the skin and the heart can result from desmosome dysfunction . The primary cause of arrhythmogenic right ventricular cardiomyopathy is mutations within the desmosomes ( ARVC )
• Educational Objective:
• Desmosomes are strong adhesive junctions crucial for maintaining the structural integrity of tissues subjected to mechanical stress , and they play a critical role in connecting keratinocytes within the epidermis.
• Rooks Textbook of Dermatology- 9 th Edition: Volume 1 Chapter 2, Page No 2.2
explanation.ref: Rooks Textbook of Dermatology- 9 th Edition: Volume 1 Chapter 2, Page No 2.2

subject: Dermatology
question: Identify the INCORRECT statement regarding Dermoepidermal Junction?
options: Hemidesmosomal Plaque components include BPAg1/BP230 Plectin
The Major Component of Lamina Densa is Collagen IV
Type VII Collagen is the Major Component of Anchoring Fibrils
Pemphigus Vulgaris has Antibodies against Hemidesmosomes

explanation.short: Ans. D) Pemphigus Vulgaris has Antibodies against Hemidesmosomes
explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A . Hemidesmosomal plaque components include BPAg1/BP230 Plectin
• Hemidesmosome connects the basal cell layer to the Dermo Epidermal Junction (DEJ) or Basement Membrane Zone (BMZ) . Hemidesmosomal plaques include various proteins , among which BPAG1 (also known as BP230) and plectin are critical components that help in the attachment of the intracellular keratin cytoskeleton to the hemidesmosome.
• Image depicting structure of DEJ / BMZ
• Option B . The lamina densa, part of the basement membrane
• The lamina densa is an electron dense layer present in the dermo epidermal junction & this is rich in type IV collagen , which forms a dense network providing structural support to the dermoepidermal junction.
• Components of lamina densa:
• Collagen type IV Laminin BM-40 SPARC Perlecan
• Option C: Type VII collagen is the major component of anchoring fibrils that extend from the lamina densa into the underlying dermis to stabilize the attachment of the epidermis to the dermis in the dermo epidermal junction.
• Educational Objective:
• Pemphigus vulgaris is associated with autoantibodies against desmoglein in desmosome s , not hemidesmosomes, and understand the key components and functions of the dermoepidermal junction .
explanation.ref: Rooks Textbook of Dermatology- 9 th Edition: Volume 1 Chapter 2, Page No 2.20-2.21

subject: Dermatology
question: What is the Ratio of Type I to III Collagen in Adult Human Skin?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Type I collagen is the most abundant collagen in the adult human dermis , comprising about 80% of the total collagen content, while type III collagen makes up about 15% . This gives a ratio of approximately 8:1 for type I to type III collagen in adult human skin.
• Type I collagen is the most abundant collagen in the human dermis. Type III collagen predominates in human skin during embryonic development . During the early postnatal period , the synthesis of type I collagen accelerates . As a result, the ratio of type I to type III collagen in adult human skin becomes approximately 8:1. Type III collagen is most prominent in vascular connective tissues, the gastro-intestinal tract, and the uterus.
• Educational Objective:
• Understand that in adult human skin, the ratio of type I to type III collagen is approximately 8:1, signifying the dominance of type I collagen in the dermal structure.
• Rooks Textbook of Dermatology- 9 th Edition: Volume 1 Chapter 2, Page No 2.26-2.28
explanation.ref: Rooks Textbook of Dermatology- 9 th Edition: Volume 1 Chapter 2, Page No 2.26-2.28

subject: Dermatology
question:
What is the Type of Connective Tissue present in the Area marked by the Black Arrow in the given image?

[Image: https://cerebellum-web-static.s3.amazonaws.com/media/public/images/2024/02/20/picture10_2avm3c5.jpg]

explanation.detail:
• Explanation
• R EAD THE QUESTION CAREFULLY IN EXAMS: EXAMINER IS ASKING ABOUT BLACK ARROW , NOT YELLOW ARROW.
• EAD THE QUESTION CAREFULLY IN EXAMS: EXAMINER IS ASKING ABOUT BLACK ARROW , NOT YELLOW ARROW.
• The area marked with the black arrow is the Reticular Dermis . It is made up of Dense Irregular Connective Tissue.
• The area marked with the yellow arrow is the Papillary Dermis . It is made of loose areolar connective tissue.
• Dermis (precisely Reticular dermis) is made up of Dense Irregular Connective Tissue.
• Irregular connective tissue is characterized by collagen fibers that are randomly oriented in multiple directions.
• Irregular connective tissue is of three types: Loose, Dense & Adipose .
• Dense connective tissue can be found in areas where unsheathed organs are protected and where there is significant mechanical stress.
• This arrangement of fibers provides strength and elasticity in multiple directions, allowing the skin to resist tearing.
• Function : Provides structural integrity and support for the skin.
• It typically exists in the Reticular Dermis . Muscle and nerve sheaths made of the superficial connective tissue Adventitia of large vessels Capsules of various organs & glands (e.g.: testis sclera, periosteum, perichondrium)
• Educational Objective:
explanation.ref: Rooks textbook of dermatology- 9 th Edition: Volume 1 Chapter 2, Page no 2.34

subject: Dermatology
question: Identify the FALSE statement among the following?
options: Acantholysis is seen in Bullous Pemphigoid
Spongiosis is seen in Acute Eczema
Acanthosis is the thickening of Stratum Spinosum

explanation.short: Ans. B) Acantholysis is seen in Bullous Pemphigoid
explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A: Actinic keratosis and psoriasis frequently exhibit parakeratosis, which is suggested by nucleated stratum corneum .
• The term " parakeratosis " describes an abnormal keratinization process in which basal layer cells divide quickly , reaching the stratum corneum faster and failing to shed their nuclei . As a result, the nucleated stratum corneum is observed on histopathology .
• Parakeratosis (nucleated stratum corneum) can be seen in the below image pointed by black arrows.
• Option C: Spongiosis is the characteristic histopathological feature of acute eczema
• Intercellular edema involving the stratum spinosum is characteristic of spongiosis.
• Option D: Acanthosis is characterised by an increase in the number of stratum spinosum cells, leading to its increased thickness . Other histological changes like Hypergranulosis , Hyperkeratosis, and Papillomatosis are frequently present in patients with acanthosis. It appears in Lupus Vulgaris , Verruca Vulgaris , and Chronic Eczema .
• Below is an illustration of acanthosis. Notice the increased thickness of the Malpighian layer & elongated rete ridges .
• Educational Objective:
• Acantholysis is a feature of Pemphigus Vulgaris , not Bullous Pemphigoid, while parakeratosis is a hallmark of psoriasis , spongiosis is characteristic of acute eczema , and acanthosis indicates the thickening of the stratum spinosum.
• Rooks Textbook of Dermatology- 9 th Edition: Volume 1 Chapter 3, Page No 3.33, 3.38
explanation.ref: Rooks Textbook of Dermatology- 9 th Edition: Volume 1 Chapter 3, Page No 3.33, 3.38

subject: Dermatology
question: Dyskeratosis can be seen in which of the following conditions?

explanation.short: Ans. D) All of the above
explanation.detail:
• Explanation
• Dyskeratosis refers to the premature keratinization of individual cells or groups of cells within the epidermal layer and is characterised by nuclear pyknosis & condensation of the cytoplasm of keratinocytes.
• This can occur in:
• Option A: Malignant lesion
• Option B: Premalignant lesions
• Bowens disease (Squamous Cell Carcinoma in situ) Paget’s disease Solar keratosis
• Option C: Benign conditions
• Darier disease Hailey hailey disease
• Educational Objective:
• Dyskeratosis is a histological feature that can be seen in a variety of skin conditions including Squamous Cell Carcinoma, Bowen's disease, and Darier's disease .
• Rooks Textbook of Dermatology- 9 th Edition: Volume 1 Chapter 3, Page No 3.36-37
explanation.ref: Rooks Textbook of Dermatology- 9 th Edition: Volume 1 Chapter 3, Page No 3.36-37

subject: Dermatology
question: Identify the WRONG match among the following Micro Abscesses & the associated Diseases?
options: Spongiform Pustules of Kogoj- Lichen Planus

explanation.short: Ans. B) Spongiform Pustules of Kogoj- Lichen Planus
explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. Munro's microabscesses are collections of neutrophils in the stratum corneum and are indeed characteristic of Psoriasis.
• Option C. Pautrier's microabscesses are collections of lymphocytes within the epidermis and are a diagnostic histological feature of Mycosis Fungoides , a type of cutaneous T-cell lymphoma .
• Option D. Papillary tip microabscesses, which are collections of neutrophils at the tips of dermal papillae , are characteristic of Dermatitis Herpetiformis .
• Some types of microabscess and their examples are given below.
• Educational Objective:
• Spongiform pustules of Kogoj i s indicative of Psoriasis , not Lichen Planus.
• Rooks Textbook of Dermatology- 9 th Edition: Volume 1 Chapter 3, 50 Page No 3.38, 50.6
explanation.ref: Rooks Textbook of Dermatology- 9 th Edition: Volume 1 Chapter 3, 50 Page No 3.38, 50.6

subject: Dermatology
question: Merkel cells & Melanocytes are located in ____________ & _____________ layers in the epidermis respectively. Fill in the blanks.

explanation.short: Ans. D. Stratum Basale & Stratum Basale
explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option D . Merkel cells are found in the stratum basale layer of the epidermis, which is the deepest layer of the epidermis and provides the foundation for the skin's outer layer. Similarly, melanocytes , which are responsible for pigment production, are also located in the stratum basale
• Merkel cells ( Touch cells or Haascheiben cells or Merkel-Ranvier cells or tactile epithelial cells) are oval shaped mechanoreceptors essential for light touch sensation & are abundant in the highly sensitive areas of the skin like fingertips & synapse with somatosensory afferent nerve fibres .
• Educational Objective:
• Both Merkel cells and melanocytes are located in the stratum basale layer of the epidermis.
• Ganong’s Review of Medical Physiology 25 th Edition Pages No: 159-163
• Ganong’s Review of Medical Physiology 26 th Edition Pages no: 158
explanation.ref: Ganong’s Review of Medical Physiology 25 th Edition Pages No: 159-163

subject: Dermatology
question: Retention of Nuclei in Cells of Stratum Corneum is known as:

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Hyperkeratosis refers to the thickening of the stratum corneum , this is observed in Psoriasis, Lichen Planus, & Icthyosis . It does not necessarily imply the retention of nuclei.
• It is the term used to describe a normal stratum corneum where cells do not retain their nuclei. It is the opposite of parakeratosis.
• This refers to the abnormal formation or premature keratinization of individual cells or groups of cells below the stratum granulosum. It is a sign of cellular disorder and disruption in the normal maturation process of keratinocytes. Examples : Darier Disease, Bowen Disease, Squamous Cell Carcinoma
• Educational Objective
• Parakeratosis is characterized by the retention of nuclei in the cells of the stratum corneum, distinguishing it from hyperkeratosis (thickening), orthokeratosis (normal), and dyskeratosis (abnormal keratinization).
explanation.ref: Rooks Textbook of Dermatology- 9 th Edition: Volume 1 Chapter 3, Page No 3.38

subject: Dermatology
question: A 27-year-old male presents to the dermatology clinic with recurrent skin blisters. On examination, superficial skin erosions with clear fluid-filled blisters are observed on his trunk and limbs. Microscopic examination of a skin biopsy shows disruption between epidermal cells, but the basement membrane remains intact. The physician suspects a disorder affecting desmosomes. Desmosomes are cell junctions primarily responsible for connecting which of the following?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation
• Melanocytes are responsible for producing melanin, the pigment that gives color to the Skin, Hair, and Eyes . While they are located in the basal layer of the epidermis , they are not primarily connected by desmosomes.
• Option C. Dermis & Epidermis :
• The connection between the dermis and epidermis is facilitated by the basement membrane , and not by desmosomes. Hemidesmosomes , another type of cell junction, anchor epidermal cells to the underlying dermal layer.
• Option D. Langerhans cells : Langerhans cells are specialized immune cells found in the skin as shown in the image below.
• LC = Langerhans Cells
• Their primary function is antigen presentation . They are not primarily connected by desmosomes.
• Educational Objective
• Desmosomes are specialized cell junctions that primarily provide strong adhesive connections between keratinocytes in the epidermis, ensuring skin's structural integrity.
explanation.ref: Fitzpatrick’s Textbook of Dermatology- 9 th Edition: Volume 1 Page No 22, 23, 63.

subject: Dermatology
question: A 35-year-old female presents to the dermatology clinic with an Itchy Rash on her Arms and Legs. On examination, Erythematous Patches with oozing are noted. A Skin Biopsy is performed, which shows Intercellular Edema in the Epidermis. This histological feature is most commonly associated with which Layer of the Epidermis?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation
• Option B. Stratum Basale :
• This is the deepest layer of the epidermis, consisting of a single layer of columnar or cuboidal basal cells. It's primarily responsible for the generation of new keratinocytes.
• Option C. Stratum Corneum :
• This is the outermost layer of the epidermis, composed of dead, flattened keratinocytes known as corneocytes . These cells lack nuclei and are filled with keratin , providing a barrier function to the skin.
• Option D. Stratum Granulosum :
• This layer is located beneath the stratum corneum and consists of keratinocytes that are undergoing terminal differentiation . These cells contain keratohyaline granules which can be seen in the image below, which contribute to the formation of the skin's barrier function.
• Educational Objective
• Spongiosis , which refers to intercellular edema in the epidermis, is primarily associated with the stratum spinosum layer .
explanation.ref: Fitzpatrick’s Textbook of Dermatology- 9 th Edition: Volume 1 Page No 13, 22

subject: Dermatology
question: A 45-year-old man presents to the clinic with flaccid bullae on his trunk and oral mucosa. The blisters rupture easily upon minor trauma. A skin biopsy is taken and shows intraepidermal blisters with loss of connections between epidermal cells. What is the primary structure affected in this condition?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation
• While acantholysis manifests in the epidermis , the entire layer isn’t destroyed. Instead, acantholysis is characterized by a loss of cohesion between individual epidermal cells , specifically within the intercellular substance.
• The dermis is the layer beneath the epidermis and provides support to the epidermis . Acantholysis is primarily an epidermal process , and the dermis remains unaffected.
• Option C. Basement membrane :
• The basement membrane acts as a barrier between the epidermis and dermis , supporting the epidermal cells and anchoring them to the dermis. In acantholysis, the basement membrane remains intact.
• Educational Objective
• Acantholysis is primarily due to the loss of cohesion within the intercellular substance of the epidermis, leading to intraepidermal blistering.
explanation.ref: Fitzpatrick’s Textbook of Dermatology- 9 th Edition: Volume 1 Page No 918

subject: Dermatology
question: A 30-year-old female patient presents with concerns about patchy skin pigmentation, which has become more pronounced after her recent beach vacation. A biopsy of the affected skin is likely to show increased activity in which layer of the epidermis?

explanation.detail:
• Educational Objective
• Melanocytes , the cells responsible for pigment production, are in the Stratum Basale of the epidermis .
explanation.ref: Fitzpatrick’s Textbook of Dermatology- 9 th Edition: Volume 1 Page No 7

subject: Dermatology
question: A 55-year-old male with diabetes mellitus comes to the clinic complaining of decreased sensation in his fingertips. On examination, Rapidly adapting receptors sensitive to changes in light touch & texture discrimination are diminished. Vibration sense was intact. These findings could be attributed to the dysfunction of which tactile receptors primarily located in the skin?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation
• Options A and B. The Stratum Spinosum and Stratum Basale, respectively, are Layers of the epidermis that do not contain these sensory receptors.
• The stratum spinosum is known for its role in providing strength and flexibility to the skin, while the stratum basale is the deepest layer of the epidermis responsible for generating new skin cells .
• Option D. The Reticular dermis is the deeper layer of the dermis that provides structural support to the skin and has Pacinian corpuscles which detect deep pressure and vibration.
• Vibration sense is normal as mentioned in the question , so reticular dermis is ruled out
• Pacinian corpuscles : Depicted in the above image.
• Educational Objective:
• The rapidly adapting receptors sensitive to changes in light touch and texture discrimination , which are diminished in a patient with diabetes mellitus presenting with decreased fingertip sensation , are Meissner's corpuscles located in the papillary dermis .
explanation.ref: Fitzpatrick’s Textbook of Dermatology- 9 th Edition: Volume 1 Page No 7

subject: Dermatology
question: A 40-year-old female presents with concerns about premature skin aging. On examination, you note reduced skin elasticity and increased laxity. You explain that these changes are often related to alterations in the dermal layer of the skin where collagen fibers provide structural support. Which type of collagen, the most abundant in the human dermis, is primarily responsible for the strength and resiliency of this layer?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation
• Option B. Type II collagen
• It is mainly found in cartilage and is not a significant component of the skin's dermis. It provides cartilage with its tensile strength and resistance to pressure.
• Option C. Type III collagen
• This is associated with reticulate fibers , commonly found alongside Type I collagen in the dermis , but it is not as abundant as Type I. It is also found in blood vessels and tissues with elastic properties .
• Option D. Type IV collagen is a type of collagen found in the basal lamina (Lamina Densa) as shown in the image below.
• The layer of the extracellular matrix on which epithelial cells sit and is not present in the dermis in significant amounts. It is important for filtration and is found in the basement membranes of tissues.
• Educational Objective:
• Type I collagen is the most abundant collagen in the dermis, providing structural support and skin integrity.
explanation.ref: Rooks Textbook of Dermatology- 9 th Edition: Volume 1 Chapter 2, Page No 2.26-2.28

subject: Dermatology
question: A 52-year-old patient presents with flaccid blisters on the trunk and proximal extremities. Skin biopsy shows intraepidermal blistering with acantholytic cells. Direct immunofluorescence of the skin reveals intercellular IgG deposits within the epidermis. Below is the Assertion and Reason related to this case scenario, mark the option which correctly describes the underlying pathology of this condition? Assertion : Desmosomes are damaged in pemphigus. Reason : Pemphigus has autoimmunity against desmoglein.
options: Both Assertion and reason are true & reason is the correct explanation of assertion
Both assertion and reason are true & reason is NOT the correct explanation of assertion
Assertion is correct & reason is false
Assertion is false & reason is true

explanation.short: Ans. A) Both Assertion and reason are true & reason is the correct explanation of assertion
explanation.detail:
• Explanation
• Option B . The assertion is correct, but in this case, the reason is directly related to the assertion and is the correct explanation.
• Option C . Both the assertion and the reason are true, making this option incorrect.
• Option D . Both the assertion and the reason are true, so this option is incorrect.
• Educational Objective:
• The learner should recognize that in pemphigus, autoantibodies target Desmoglein , a constituent of desmosomes , leading to their damage and resulting in the clinical manifestation of intraepidermal blisters .
explanation.ref: Fitzpatrick’s Textbook of Dermatology- 9 th Edition: Volume 1 Page No 915

subject: Dermatology
question: A 46-year-old patient with a history of sun exposure presents with a suspicious pigmented lesion on her upper back. A biopsy is performed, and immunohistochemical staining is ordered to determine the cell type of origin. Which of the following markers is NOT typically used to identify melanocytes in the evaluation of this lesion ?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation
• Option A . HMB 45 (Human Melanoma Black -45)
• It is an antibody that reacts against an antigen present in melanocytes, used as a marker in identifying melanomas .
• Option B . S 100
• It is a protein found in cells derived from the neural crest ( melanocytes ).  It is a marker for Langerhans cells also. Neuroectodermal Markers: S 100, HMB 45, Melan A.
• Option C . Melan A
• It is another marker used in the identification of melanocytes and is also used in the diagnosis of melanoma .
• Educational Objective:
• Cytokeratin 20 is not a marker for melanocytes and recognize the use of HMB 45, S 100, and Melan A as immunohistochemical stains for the identification of melanocytes and melanoma.
explanation.ref: Fitzpatrick’s Textbook of Dermatology- 9 th Edition: Volume 1 Page No 20, 1920

subject: Dermatology
question: A patient presents with flaccid bullae and erosions on the skin. Histopathological examination reveals intraepidermal blistering and acantholysis within the epidermis. Direct immunofluorescence shows IgG antibodies against cell surface antigens in the skin. These clinical findings suggest a diagnosis of pemphigus vulgaris, which is characterized by autoantibodies against Desmogleins — components of desmosomes that are critical for cell-cell adhesion within the epidermis, particularly affecting the Malpighian layer. Which among the below options constitutes the Malpighian layer ?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation
• Option B . Corneum & Granular L ayer :
• These layers are above the Malpighian layer. The stratum corneum is the outermost layer composed of dead keratinocytes , while the stratum granulosum contains keratohyalin granules that lead to keratinization . They are not typically involved in acantholysis seen in pemphigus vulgaris.
• Option C . Granular & Spinou s Layer :
• Although the spinous layer is part of the Malpighian stratum , the granular layer is not . It is the layer just superficial to the Malpighian layer and plays a role in keratin production , not cell adhesion.
• Option D. Only Spinous Layer :
• This is partially correct , as the spinous layer is a component of the Malpighian layer ; however, it does not include the basal layer which is also a key part of the Malpighian stratum.
• Educational Objective:
• The Malpighian layer of the epidermis includes the stratum basale and stratum spinosum. These layers are crucial for keratinocyte adhesion via d esmosomes , which can be disrupted in autoimmune conditions such as pemphigus vulgaris , leading to acantholysis and blistering.
• Rooks Textbook of Dermatology- 9 th Edition: Volume 1 Chapter 2, Page No 2.5
explanation.ref: Rooks Textbook of Dermatology- 9 th Edition: Volume 1 Chapter 2, Page No 2.5

subject: Dermatology
question: Diascopy can be used for clinical examination of:

explanation.short: Ans. D) All of the above
explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A . Purpura - Diascopy is used to determine whether red or purple skin lesions are blanchable . Purpuric lesions do not blanch when pressure is applied, helping to distinguish them from lesions that are due to vasodilation .
• Option B . Lupus Vulgaris - This is a form of cutaneous tuberculosis . A positive diascopy test in lupus vulgaris shows apple jelly nodules , which are a characteristic finding.
• Option C . Nevus Anemicus - This is a congenital condition characterized by localized areas of skin that do not have normal vasomotor responses. Diascopy can demonstrate that the lesions blanch with pressure and match the surrounding skin because they do not contain more blood vessels, but rather less responsive ones.
• Blanching response with Diascopy can be seen in below image:
• (a) Brown-reddish infiltrated scaly plaques on the auricle and periauricular area ,
• (b) Diascopic examination showing the presence of light-brownish or “ apple jelly ” coloured nodules.
• Educational Objective
• Diascopy is a clinical test used to evaluate blanching of skin lesions and can be applied to a variety of conditions including purpura , lupusvulgaris , and nevusanemicus to assess the nature of skin discoloration.
• Rooks Textbook of Dermatology- 9 th Edition Volume I Chapter 4 Page No 4.19
explanation.ref: Rooks Textbook of Dermatology- 9 th Edition Volume I Chapter 4 Page No 4.19

subject: Dermatology
question: All of the following are primary skin lesions EXCEPT?

explanation.detail:
• Educational Objective:
• Crusts are secondary skin lesions resulting from the maturation and subsequent changes to primary lesions such as vesicles or pustules, or from trauma to the skin, and are not the initial manifestation of a skin pathology.
• Rooks Textbook of Dermatology- 9 th Edition Chapter 4 Page No 4.10
explanation.ref: Rooks Textbook of Dermatology- 9 th Edition Chapter 4 Page No 4.10

subject: Dermatology
question: All of the following are secondary skin lesions EXCEPT?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Lichenification , Fissure , & Ulcer are secondary skin lesions .
• Wheal is a primary skin lesion .
• Primary lesions of the skin include:
• Papule Plaque Nodule Vesicle Bulla Macule Patch Pustule Abscess Petechiae Purpura Ecchymosis Wheal
• Secondary lesions of the skin include:
• Lichenification Erosion / Excoriation Atrophy Fissure Scale Ulcer Crust Induration Scar
• Educational Objective:
• A wheal is a primary lesion characterized by transient , raised , and often itchy areas on the skin due to an allergic reaction, unlike lichenification , fissures , and ulcers , which are secondary lesions resulting from the modification of primary lesions or externaldamage to the skin
• Rooks Textbook of Dermatology- 9 th Edition Volume I Chapter 4 Page No 4.10
explanation.ref: Rooks Textbook of Dermatology- 9 th Edition Volume I Chapter 4 Page No 4.10

subject: Dermatology
question:
Match the options in Column A with the correct options in Column B:

[Image: https://cerebellum-web-static.s3.amazonaws.com/media/public/images/2024/02/20/picture19_hNF6GTW.jpg]

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option B . Branny - This term is often used to describe the fine scales that resemble bran , the layers that flake off in pityriasisversicolor , a fungal infection of the skin .
• Scales are visible exfoliation of the skin consists of flakes of stratum corneum . Parakeratosis refers to the presence of nucleated keratinocytes in the stratum corneum . It is indicative of a high turnover of keratinocytes . The scales represent compacted desquamated layers of stratum corneum, so Parakeratosis can be found in these skin lesions on Biopsy .
• Silvery - This type of scale is most associated with psoriasis . Psoriasis scales are typically silver - white and can be found as thick , plaque -like lesions on the skin.
• Mica - This type of scale is not a commonly used dermatological term but in the context of this question, it seems to be paired with pityriasis Lichenoideschronica , a rare skin disorder characterized by small , scaling papules .
• Collarette - This describes the scaling that is seen with Pityriasis Rosea , where the scale is attached at the periphery of the lesion, creating a collarette appearance .
• Various types of scales are given in below images.
• Educational Objective:

subject: Dermatology
question:
This below given lesion may be described as?

[Image: https://cerebellum-web-static.s3.amazonaws.com/media/public/images/2024/02/20/picture29_7bGgWne.jpg]

explanation.detail:

• Option C . Nodular - Nodular lesions are raised bumps that extend into the dermal layer or below. They can be associated with conditions like nodular melanoma .
• Option D . Ecchymosis - This term refers to a large bruise , which is a purplish patch resulting from extravasation of blood into the skin , often after trauma .

• Annular lesions have a ring shape, discoid lesions are coin -like , nodular lesions are palpable and extend into the dermis or subcutis , and ecchymosis represents a larger area of hemorrhage into the skin.

subject: Dermatology
question: Target Lesions are found in?

explanation.detail:

• Option A . Tinea –

• Option C . Psoriasis –
• Psoriasis lesions are typically Well - Demarcated , Erythematous Plaques with silvery scales . They do not present as target lesions.
• Option D. Scabies –

subject: Dermatology
question: Which of the following disease has a dermatomal distribution of cutaneous lesions?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option B . Herpes Genitalis - This condition is caused by the herpes simplex virus (usually type 2, and sometimes type 1). The lesions are vesicular and ulcerative but are not distributed along dermatomes ; they are localized to the genital and surrounding areas.
• In Herpes labialis & genitalis , which are produced by Herpes simplex viruses, grouped vesicles can be seen near lips & genitalia respectively.
• Option C . Incontinentiapigmenti - This is a genetic disorder affecting the skin , hair , teeth , and nails , often with swirled patterns of discoloration on the skin. It does not follow a dermatomal distribution.
• Option D . Sporotrichosis - This is a fungal infection caused by Sporothrixschenckii , typically contracted from soil or plant matter. The cutaneous form of sporotrichosis is characterized by nodular lesions along the lymphatic channels, not by dermatomal distribution.
• Educational Objective:
• Herpes zoster is the condition among the options provided that is associated with a dermatomal distribution of cutaneous lesions , following the pathway of a single spinal or cranial sensory nerve .
explanation.ref: Fitzpatrick’s Dermatology in General Medicine 8th Edition, Pages 39-41

subject: Dermatology
question: Poikiloderma is a Triad of all EXCEPT?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Poikiloderma is a special skin lesion with a triad of features {ATP}
• A trophy of skin T elangiectasia (Permanently Dilated Blood Vessels) P igment Abnormalities (Dyspigmentation, both Hypo & Hyper)
• Poikiloderma may be congenital or acquired . It is associated with Mycosis Fungoides, Dermatomyositis , Xeroderma Pigmentosum etc.
• Educational Objective:
• Poikiloderma is characterized by a triad of changes: pigment abnormalities, telangiectasia , and atrophy of the skin. The Auspitz sign is not part of this triad but is associated with psoriasis
• Rooks Textbook of Dermatology- 9 th Edition Volume I Chapter 4 Page No 4.12

subject: Dermatology
question: All of the following diseases may be diagnosed by Tzanck smear EXCEPT:

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option B . Bullous Impetigo - Tzanck smear can be used to diagnose bullous impetigo by demonstrating Acantholytic cells, aiding in differentiating it from other blistering diseases.
• Option C . Herpes Zoster - The Tzanck smear is useful in identifying Multinucleated Giant Cells and Acantholytic Cells .
• Option D . Pemphigus - This Autoimmune Blistering disorder can be tentatively diagnosed with a Tzanck smear by finding Acantholytic cells , which are detached individual Keratinocytes .
• Some important findings on Tzanck Smear are as follows:
• The Frenchman Tzanck invented the Tzanck smear , a cheap , easy-to-use bedside cytological test that aids in the diagnosis of vesicular , pustular , and bullous diseases , particularly herpes .
• Several techniques can be used to stain Tzanck smears, but Giemsa stain is the most popular.
• Haematoxylin and Eosin , Wright , Methylene Blue , Papanicolaou , and Toluidine Blue are additional stains that are employed.
• Image: Tzanck smear, showing multinucleated giant cells in center
• The Tzanck smear is a cytological test used to diagnose blistering skin diseases such as Herpes Zoster , Pemphigus , and Bullous Impetigo by demonstrating characteristic cellular changes . It is not used for the diagnosis of psoriasis.
• Rooks Textbook of Dermatology- 9 th Edition Volume I Chapter 3 Page No 3.25
explanation.ref: Rooks Textbook of Dermatology- 9 th Edition Volume I Chapter 3 Page No 3.25

subject: Dermatology
question: Woods Lamp used for diagnosis of Dermatological Disorders, what is the Wavelength used in it?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A . 200-240nm –
• This wavelength corresponds to far - UVC , which is not used in Wood's lamps due to its potential to cause corneal damage and is absorbed by the ozone layer.
• Option B . 280 - 300nm –
• This range includes UVB , which is not typically used in Wood's lamps either. UVB has a shorter wavelength and is more associated with DNAdamage than UVA.
• Option D . 500 - 700 nm –
• This range is in the visible light spectrum , specifically green to red light, and is not used in Wood's lamps.
• Different disorders will produce different color fluorescence by using woods lamp.
• Table: Various Diseases & their colour on Woods Lamp Examination
• Educational Objective:
• The Wood's lamp uses UVA light with a wavelength of 365nm ( 320 - 400 nm) for the diagnosis of various skin conditions based on the fluorescence characteristics of the skin under this specific range of ultraviolet light .
• Rooks Textbook of Dermatology- 9 th Edition Volume I Chapter 4 Page No 4.20

subject: Dermatology
question: Which of the following diseases has Extensor Involvement?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. Herpes Zoster
• It typically presents with a painful rash that follows a dermatomal pattern , affecting sensory nerves , and does not involve extensor surfaces.
• Option B. Lichen Planus
• It is a chronic inflammatory condition that mainly affects the skin and mucous membranes , causing flat-topped, itchy papules and typically involve flexor surfaces .
• Option D. Adult Phase of Atopic Dermatitis
• It involves flexor surfaces and is characterized by eczematous lesions .
• Educational Objective:
• Psoriasis is a chronic autoimmune skin disease that often affects extensor surfaces such as elbows and knees , making it distinct from other skin conditions mentioned in the options.
explanation.ref: Rooks Textbook of Dermatology- 9 th Edition Page No 4.6

subject: Dermatology
question: Which of the following is a Primary Skin Lesion?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• It is a secondary skin lesion characterized by thinning of the skin layers and loss of tissue substance.
• It is a secondary lesion formed from the dried exudate of serum, blood, or pus on the skin surface.
• It is a secondary lesion that refers to a linear crack or split in the skin, often due to dryness or excessive dry skin .
• Primary lesions of the skin include:
• Papule Plaque Nodule Vesicle Bulla Macule Patch Pustule Abscess Petechiae Purpura Ecchymosis Wheal
• Secondary lesions of the skin include:
• Lichenification Erosion / Excoriation Atrophy Fissure Scale Ulcer Crust Induration Scar
• Educational Objective:
• A primary skin lesion, such as a Bulla , represents an initial and distinct skin abnormality , whereas secondary skin lesions, like crusts or fissures, result from the evolution or manipulation of primary lesions .
explanation.ref: Rooks Textbook of Dermatology- 9 th Edition Page No 4.13

subject: Dermatology
question:
A 25-year-old female is presenting with the special lesions as shown in the below image. What are these lesions called as?

[Image: https://cerebellum-web-static.s3.amazonaws.com/media/public/images/2024/02/20/picture56_3EguY0F.jpg]

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Most common form of acne. Most characteristic lesion - Comedones . Most severe form of acne vulgaris - Grade IV / Nodulocystic Acne . Most severe form of acne - Acne conglobata .
• Comedones (are considered as special lesions ) are the specific lesions of acne vulgaris
• Acne is a chronic inflammatory disease of the pilosebaceous unit . Comedonal lesions are the Earliest lesions of acne. They represent the keratin plugs of the pilosebaceous duct.
• When the keratin plug is inside the follicle, it is called a closed comedone or white head .
• When the keratin plug is exposed to the environment, it gets oxidised by the air & turns black which is known as open comedone (blackhead)
• According to Global Alliance Algorithm for Acne, Oral isotretinoin is indicated for the treatment of severe acne.
• Global alliance algorithm for acne treatment
• Image: global alliance algorithm for improving outcomes in acne
• Educational Objective:
• C omedones are small, non-inflamed lesions found in acne, characterized by a plug of sebum and keratin within a hair follicle, presenting as blackheads or whiteheads.
• Rooks Textbook of Dermatology- 9 th Edition Volume 3 Chapter 90 Page No 90.1, 90.10, 90.13, 90.32 & 90.37
explanation.ref: Rooks Textbook of Dermatology- 9 th Edition Volume 3 Chapter 90 Page No 90.1, 90.10, 90.13, 90.32 & 90.37

subject: Dermatology
question: All the statements are true about scabies EXCEPT?
options: Burrows are present mainly in Stratum Corneum
Animal Scabies is characterized by Burrows
5% Permethrin Cream from Neck to Toe in Adults & from Head to Toe in Children is Topical Agent of Choice

explanation.short: Ans. C) Animal Scabies is characterised by Burrows.
explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Scabies is an ectoparasitic infestation caused by the Sarcoptes scabei var hominis / Itch mite / Sarcoptes scabei .
• The incubation period of scabies is 3-4 weeks (21-28 days) However, reinfection of a previously cured individual may provoke immediate symptoms . Itching worsens at night & when the patient is warm ( Option B) . Nocturnal pruritus is the most common symptom associated with scabies. Water-washed diseases, such as scabies, are infections brought on by poor personal hygiene and a lack of access to clean water . The distinctive signs include intense itching and shallow burrows . Burrows in scabies are typically found in the stratum corneum as depicted in the below image. ( Option A)
• In most people the trails of the burrowing mites appear brownish linear or s shaped tracks in the skin & occur as a result of excavation of the adult mite in the epidermis.
• S shaped track , pointed with black arrows in the below image.
• Burrows are absent in animal scabies. ( Option C ) Lesions are found in the Circle of Hebra (imaginary line formed by connecting the sites of involvement in scabies like, web spaces , feet , ventral wrists , elbows , cack , breasts , buttocks & external genitals ). A single overnight application of 5% permethrin cream from neck to toe in adults & from head to toe in children is the drug of choice for the treatment of scabies in all ages including infants (at least 2 months old ) & pregnant women. ( Option D)
• Educational Objective
• While human scabies involves burrow formation in the skin , animal scabies does not typically lead to burrow formation when transmitted to humans.
• Rooks Textbook of Dermatology- 9th Edition Chapter 34 Page No 34.39-34.47
explanation.ref: Rooks Textbook of Dermatology- 9th Edition Chapter 34 Page No 34.39-34.47

options: Erythema Multiforme Minor is the most common form
The distribution of Target Lesions is mainly over Acral Sites
NSAIDs are the most common Triggering Factor for EMF

explanation.short: NSAIDs are the most common Triggering Factor for EMF
explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Erythema Multiforme : Self-limiting cytotoxic dermatitis resulting from Delayed type of hypersensitivity . It most commonly occurs following an Infection >> Drug intake .
• Clinical Features : Classic Acral iris or 'target lesions' .
• Lesions are distributed preferentially on the Distal Extremities (Option C). Oral and genital mucosal membranes lesions are associated with Erosions. The lesions appear in successive crops for a few days and fade in 1-2 weeks , sometimes leaving Dusky Discoloration .
• The common triggers are:
• Herpes Simplex Infection (Most Common) (Option D) Mycoplasma Infection Bacterial Infections Fungal Infections Vaccinations Malignancy- Carcinoma, Lymphoma, Leukaemia Drugs- Sulphonamides, Rifampicin, Penicillin’s, Hydantoin derivatives Miscellaneous- Lupus Erythematosus (Rowell Syndrome), Sarcoidosis, Polyarteritis Nodosa
• Educational Objective
• Erythema Multiforme is often triggered by infections , particularly herpes simplex virus , rather than NSAIDs, and it commonly presents as Erythema Multiforme minor with target lesions concentrated on acral sites.
explanation.ref: Rook's Textbook of Dermatology 9th Edition Chapter 47, Page No 47.1-5

subject: Dermatology
question:
An elderly person presented with lesions as shown in the image below. What is the most likely diagnosis?

[Image: https://cerebellum-web-static.s3.amazonaws.com/media/public/images/2024/02/20/picture66_Csoc0pD.jpg]

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Chickenpox, caused by the varicella-zoster virus , typically presents with a pruritic , vesicular rash that starts on the face and trunk and spreads to the rest of the body. The lesions appear in crops and are at different stages of healing . The rash in the image does not display this typical distribution or the various stages of healing.
• Option B: Contact Dermatitis
• Contact Dermatitis is an i nflammatory skin condition caused by contact with a substance that triggers an allergic or irritant response. It presents with Erythema, Edema, and sometimes Vesicles, but these are not typically in a dermatomal distribution.
• Option D: Lichen Planus
• Lichen planus is a chronic inflammatory condition of the skin and mucous membranes . It presents with pruritic, flat-topped, violaceous papules and plaques with fine white lines ( Wickham's striae ) on the surface.
• The rash in the image does not exhibit these features.
• Educational Objective:
• Herpes zoster can be identified by its distinctive clustered vesicular eruption within a single dermatome , which is typically unilateral and does not cross the midline . This presentation is especially common in elderly or i mmunocompromise d individuals due to the reactivation of the varicella-zoster virus .
• Bolognia Textbook of Dermatology 4 th Edition Page No 4, 1412

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. Hinderer’s Lines
• Two Intersecting Lines , one drawn from the Ala to the Tragus and other from the Lateral Canthus to the Oral Commissure.
• Option B. Marionette Lines / Melomental Folds :
• They are consequences of Facial Ageing . They are curvilinear wrinkles formed because of facial movements & the ageing process . They extend downwards from the Oral Commisures .
• Option C. Relaxed Skin Tension Lines/RSTL
• They run perpendicular to the direction of contraction of the underlying muscles & parallel to the dermal collagen bundles. To produce favourable aesthetic results , incisions should generally be designed to follow RSTL as the resulting scars will be stronger & less likely to stretch .
