subject: Dermatology
question:
A child was admitted with complaints of fever, neck rigidity (meningitis features) with associated oral erosions and skin manifestations as shown in the picture. CSF analysis show normal glucose, proteins and lymphocytes increased. What is the possible diagnosis?

[Image: https://cerebellum-web-static.s3.amazonaws.com/media/public/images/2024/02/20/picture81.jpg]

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. Bacterial Meningitis
• This typically presents with high fever , neck rigidity , and altered mental status . CSF analysis often shows elevated white cells (usually neutrophils ), low glucose, and high proteins , which is not the case here.
• Option B. Hand, Foot, and Mouth Disease
• It is usually caused by Cox-Sackie viru s and primarily affects children. It presents with oral ulcer s and a rash on the hands and feet , but it is not commonly associated with meningitis.
• Option C. Tubercular Meningitis
• It would generally present with a chronic course of fever and neck rigidity . CSF analysis in tubercular meningitis often shows low glucose , elevated proteins , and lymphocytosis, which does not align with the findings presented.
• Educational Objective:
• Herpes simplex infection in a pediatric patient presenting with fever, neck rigidity, oral erosions , characteristic skin manifestations, and CSF findings consistent with viral meningitis . The recognition of oral and skin lesions typical of herpes simplex is crucial in differentiating it from other causes of meningitis.

Treatment of this condition in a Pregnant Female would be?

• It is also not advised during pregnancy as it may pose a risk to the fetus.

• This is generally not the first-line treatment for condyloma acuminata (genital warts ) in pregnancy due to the potential for bleeding and scarring . This can be treatment modality used in Giant Genital warts like Buschke Lowenstein Tumours.

subject: Dermatology
question: All of the following are correct about Tzanck smear findings EXCEPT?
options: Multinucleated Giant Cells are seen in Varicella-Zoster Infections.
Acantholytic Cells are seen in Pemphigus Vulgaris
It is the most Accurate Test for Pemphigus Vulgaris

explanation.short: It is the most Accurate Test for Pemphigus Vulgaris
explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. Multinucleated giant cells on a Tzanck Smear are seen in Varicella-Zoster and Herpes Simplex Infections.
• The Frenchman Tzanck invented the Tzanck smear, a cheap , easy-to-use bedside cytological test that aids in the diagnosis of vesicular, pustular, and bullous diseases, particularly herpes. Several techniques can be used to stain Tzanck smears, but Giemsa stain is the most popular. Haematoxylin and eosin, Wright, Methylene Blue, Papanicolaou, and Toluidine Blue are additional stains that are employed.
• Results in Tzanck Smear:
• Option B. Tzanck cells / Acantholytic cells are Keratinocytes.
• They are seen in vesiculobullous disorders, as a result of acantholysis. The keratinocytes lose their polyhedral shapes and characteristically become round with a hypertrophic nucleus, hazy or absent nucleoli, and an abundance of basophilic cytoplasm . Due to the cytoplasm's propensity to condense at the cell's periphery and create a perinuclear halo , the basophilic staining is more pronounced peripherally on the cell membrane ("mourning edged" cells).
• Some important findings on Tzanck smear are as follows:
• Image: Tzanck smear, showing multinucleated giant cells in center
• The sensitivity of the Tzanck smear exceeds 80 %, and the specificity 90 % when the investigators are experienced .
• Option D. This clinical picture is suggestive of pemphigus vulgaris. The first line investigation is Tzanck smear.
• It allows us to visualize the histology by collecting material from the floor of a fresh blister that is stained with Giemsa or any Romanowsky stain . Tzanck cells are seen on microscopy . Direct Immuno Fluorescence Microscopy (DIF) is the most accurate test .
• Educational Objective:
• The Tzanck smear can show multinucleated giant cells in varicella-zoster infections and acantholytic cells in Pemphigus vulgaris , but it is not the most accurate test for Pemphigus vulgaris; that distinction belongs to direct immunofluorescence .
• Rooks Textbook of Dermatology- 9 th Edition Page No 3.25, 50.4-50.8
explanation.ref: Rooks Textbook of Dermatology- 9 th Edition Page No 3.25, 50.4-50.8

subject: Dermatology
question: All of the following are correct statements EXCEPT?
options: Subcorneal Split occurs in Pemphigus Foliaceous
Castleman Disease is the most common cause of Paraneoplastic Pemphigus
Intra Epidermal Intercellular Deposition of IgA is seen in IgA Pemphigus

explanation.short: Ans. C) Castleman Disease is the most common cause
explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A: Penicillamine causes Drug-Induced Pemphigus
• Pemphigus caused by drugs typically develops one year after the drug was first taken. It is more common in individuals with other associated autoimmune conditions.
• Mnemonic for common drugs causing Drug-induced pemphigus – PCR
• P enicillamine, C aptopril, R ifampicin.
• The commonly implicated drugs include:
• ﻿ Penicillamine (most common) ﻿ACE inhibitors (Captopril) ﻿Antibiotics - penicillin’s, cephalosporins, rifampicin ﻿Piroxicam ﻿Phenobarbital
• Option B: Subcorneal split occurs in Pemphigus foliaceous
• In Pemphigus foliaceous the blisters are formed due to a split at the subcorneal layer .
• A less severe form of pemphigus vulgaris called pemphigus foliaceus has antibodies against desmoglein-1. Due to the lack of antibodies to Dsg-3 antigens, it exhibits flaccid bullae over skin & sparing of the Mucosal surfaces. Erythema, scales , and crust-covered erosions are the common presentation. The histopathology of Pemphigus foliaceous typically shows subcorneal blisters with acantholysis , which is the separation of keratinocytes due to the presence of autoantibodies against desmoglein 1 , a desmosomal protein.
• Histopathological image of Pemphigus foliaceous (PF) showing subcorneal cleft.
• Option D: Intra Epidermal intercellular deposition of IgA is seen in IgA pemphigus
• IgA pemphigus is characterized by lgA antibodies against desmosomal components. IntraEpidermal intercellular deposition of IgA on immunofluorescence is seen in IgA pemphigus.
• Educational Objective:
• Drugs like penicillamine can cause drug-induced pemphigus and IgA deposition is seen in IgA pemphigus, Castleman disease is not the most common cause of paraneoplastic pemphigus; neoplasms such as lymphomas are more commonly implicated.
• Rooks Textbook of Dermatology- 9 th Edition Chapter 50 Page No 50.1-50.8
explanation.ref: Rooks Textbook of Dermatology- 9 th Edition Chapter 50 Page No 50.1-50.8

subject: Dermatology
question: Nikolsky's sign is seen in all except?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Nikolsky sign is absent in subepidermal blistering disorders, so it’s not seen associated with Bullous Pemphigoid . Nikolsky’s sign : On application of tangential pressure over skin on the border of an intact blister → upper layers of epidermis separate from the lower layers →→ skin peels off → extension of the blister .
• Erosion formed → Nikolsky’s sign.
• It is seen in conditions that produce intraepidermal blisters. It is negative in subepidermal bullous conditions like bullous pemphigoid , Dermatitis Herpetiformis and epidermolysis bullosa acquisita .
• Educational Objective:
• Nikolsky's sign is positive in skin disorders with intraepidermal splitting , such as Pemphigus vulgaris, Pemphigus foliaceus, and Staphylococcal scalded skin syndrome , but it is negative in Bullous pemphigoid where the split is subepidermal.
• Rooks Textbook of Dermatology- 9 th Edition Chapter 50 Page No 50.3
explanation.ref: Rooks Textbook of Dermatology- 9 th Edition Chapter 50 Page No 50.3

subject: Dermatology
question:
A 43-year-old lady presented with flaccid blisters on her upper chest and back and mucosal lesions as shown. On examination, the bullae were filled with clear fluid. Histopathology revealed a suprabasal split. What is the diagnosis & what is the most accurate diagnostic test to diagnose this condition respectively?

[Image: https://cerebellum-web-static.s3.amazonaws.com/media/public/images/2024/02/24/picture28_GwxCWdv.jpg]

explanation.short: Ans. A) Pemphigus Vulgaris & Direct Immunofluorescence Testing
explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Flaccid bulla on the skin, oral erosions & suprabasal split on histopathology suggest a diagnosis of mucocutaneous Pemphigus Vulgaris . Direct immunofluorescence test is the most accurate test to diagnose Pemphigus Vulgaris. Clinical features : Oral erosions usually precede skin manifestations. The skin lesions are flaccid blisters with clear fluid on an erythematous base, that rupture to produce painful erosions. These are distributed over the face , scalp , upper chest , back , and neck .
• Histopathology : The epidermis splits suprabasally in Pemphigus vulgaris , which causes blisters to form. The epidermis splits suprabasally in Pemphigus vulgaris, which causes blisters to form. The basal layer remains adherent to the basement membrane and gives the resemblance to the row of the tombstones . Below image depicts Row of the tombstone’s appearance on histopathology in Pemphigus vulgaris.
• Direct immunofluorescence : Direct Immunofluorescence uses anti-|gG antibodies which detects the anti-Desmoglein IgG antibodies deposited on the Desmosomes . In Pemphigus vulgaris there is Intra Epidermal intercellular deposition of IgG and C3, which gives rise to a characteristic appearance called as Fish net / Chicken wire pattern. The below image shows a characteristic fish-net appearance on direct immunofluorescence , which is diagnostic of pemphigus vulgaris.
• Bullous pemphigoid shows subepidermal involvement with tense blisters.
• Educational Objective:
• For a patient presenting with Flaccid Blisters and Mucosal lesions and histopathology showing a Suprabasal Split, the diagnosis is Pemphigus Vulgaris , and the most accurate diagnostic test is Direct Immunofluorescence Testing .
explanation.ref: Rooks Textbook of Dermatology- 9 th Edition Chapter 50 Page No 50.4-50.8

subject: Dermatology
question: All of the following are correct about Tzanck smear findings EXCEPT?
options: Multinucleated giant cells are seen in varicella-zoster infections.
Acantholytic cells are seen in Pemphigus vulgaris
It is the most accurate test for Pemphigus vulgaris

explanation.short: Ans. D) It is the most accurate test for pemphigus vulgaris
explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A: Multinucleated giant cells on a Tzanck smear are seen in varicella-zoster and herpes simplex infections.
• The Frenchman Tzanck invented the Tzanck smear, a cheap, easy-to-use bedside cytological test that aids in the diagnosis of vesicular, pustular, and bullous diseases, particularly herpes. Several techniques can be used to stain Tzanck smears, but Giemsa stain is the most popular. Haematoxylin and eosin, Wright, methylene blue, Papanicolaou, and toluidine blue are additional stains that are employed. Results in Tzanck Smear:
• Option B: Tzanck cells / Acantholytic cells are Keratinocytes
• They are seen in vesiculobullous disorders, as a result of acantholysis. The keratinocytes lose their polyhedral shapes and characteristically become round with a hypertrophic nucleus , hazy or absent nucleoli , and an abundance of basophilic cytoplasm . Due to the cytoplasm's propensity to condense at the cell's periphery and create a perinuclear halo , the basophilic staining is more pronounced peripherally on the cell membrane ("mourning edged" cells).
• Some important findings on Tzanck smear are as follows:
• Image: Tzanck smear, showing multinucleated giant cells in center
• The sensitivity of the Tzanck smear exceeds 80%, and the specificity 90% when the investigators are experienced.
• Educational Objective:
• The Tzanck smear can show multinucleated giant cells in varicella-zoster infections and acantholytic cells in Pemphigus vulgaris , but it is not the most accurate test for Pemphigus vulgaris; that distinction belongs to direct immunofluorescence .
• Rooks Textbook of Dermatology- 9 th Edition Page No 3.25, 50.4-50.8
explanation.ref: Rooks Textbook of Dermatology- 9 th Edition Page No 3.25, 50.4-50.8

subject: Dermatology
question: A 45-year-old male presents with flaccid vesicles in oral mucosa and trunk. On examination both the bulla spread sign & Nikolsky sign are positive. What pattern will be observed in Direct Immunofluorescence of this patient ?
options: Linear IgG and C3 Deposition along the Basement Membrane Zone
IgG and C3 Deposition throughout the Epidermis
Granular IgA Deposition at the tips of Dermal Papillae
IgA Deposition in a Fishnet Pattern

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. Linear IgG and C3 deposition along the basement membrane zone –
• This pattern is indicative of Bullous Pemphigoid , an autoimmune blistering disease that affects the basement membrane zone , which is beneath the epidermis.
• Unlike Pemphigus vulgaris, Bullous Pemphigoid does not usually involve the oral mucosa as prominently and tends to present with tense rather than flaccid blisters.
• Option C. Granular IgA deposition at the tips of dermal papillae –
• This deposition pattern is seen in Dermatitis Herpetiformis , which is associated with celiac disease.
• Granular IgA deposition at the tips of dermal papillae in Dermatitis Herpetiformis. It presents with pruritic papules and vesicles , primarily on extensor surfaces, and is NOT typically associated with the Nikolsky sign.
• Option D. IgA deposition in a fishnet pattern –
• This describes the direct immunofluorescence finding in IgA Pemphigus, which is a rarer form of Pemphigus and presents with pustules rather than the flaccid vesicles seen in Pemphigus vulgaris.
• Educational Objective:
• Pemphigus vulgaris , the direct immunofluorescence pattern shows intercellular IgG and C3 deposition throughout the epidermis.
• Rooks Textbook of Dermatology- 9 th Edition Chapter 50 Page No 50.1-50.8
explanation.ref: Rooks Textbook of Dermatology- 9 th Edition Chapter 50 Page No 50.1-50.8

subject: Dermatology
question: A 38-year-old patient presents with remitting and relapsing course of mucocutaneous lesions since past 8 years. On examination there are irregular ulcers in oral cavity & crusts and erosions all over trunk & upper limbs. Skin sample for biopsy was taken and sent to pathology department. What is the most likely diagnosis in this patient which you expect to see in the report once the reporting from pathology department is ready?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. Dermatitis Herpetiformis :
• This is an intensely pruritic condition associated with gluten sensitivity and presents with grouped vesicles on extensor surfaces. Histology typically shows micro abscesses at the tips of dermal papillae as shown below
• Granular IgA deposits on Direct Immunofluorescence.
• Option C. Bullous Pemphigoid :
• It tends to occur in older patients and presents with large, tense blisters on normal or erythematous skin, rarely affecting the mucous membranes. Histology shows a subepidermal blister with eosinophils , and Direct Immunofluorescence reveals linear IgG and C3 deposits along the basement membrane zone.
• Option D. Paraneoplastic Pemphigus :
• This condition is associated with underlying neoplasms and presents with polymorphic skin lesions, including blisters, erosions, and papules. Clinically and histologically, it can resemble Pemphigus vulgaris, but it is differentiated by the presence of an underlying tumor and a more complex immunopathologic profile on Direct Immunofluorescence.
• Educational Objective:
• Diagnose Pemphigus vulgaris in a patient with a long-standing history of mucocutaneous ulcers and erosions , supported by histopathological findings of acantholysis and immunofluorescence showing intercellular IgG and C3 deposition in the epidermis.
• Rooks Textbook of Dermatology- 9 th Edition Page No 50.4
explanation.ref: Rooks Textbook of Dermatology- 9 th Edition Page No 50.4

subject: Dermatology
question: A 52-year-old woman presents to the clinic with complaints of a progressive rash that started on her face and has now spread to her chest and scalp over the last 3 months. She reports that the lesions began as small blisters that broke easily, leaving behind crusted, scaly areas and shallow erosions. She denies any mucosal involvement or recent illnesses. She is otherwise healthy and takes no regular medications. On physical examination, you note several superficial erosions with crusts on the scalp and chest. There are no intact blisters, and no lesions are found in the oral cavity. A skin biopsy is taken from the edge of an eroded lesion on the chest. Based on the clinical presentation, what will most likely be observed in the histopathology of the skin biopsy?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option B. Subcorneal non-acantholytic blister –
• This would suggest a blistering process without cell-cell detachment , which is not consistent with Pemphigus foliaceus.
• Option C. Subepidermal acantholytic blister –
• This describes a blister beneath the entire epidermis with cell-cell detachment , which is not characteristic of Pemphigus foliaceus.
• Option D. Subepidermal non-acantholytic blister –
• This would suggest a blister beneath the epidermis without acantholysis , which is seen in conditions like Bullous pemphigoid , not Pemphigus foliaceus.
• Educational Objective:
• Pemphigus foliaceus in patients presenting with superficial, crusted, scaly lesions and shallow erosions on the skin, with histopathology revealing subcorneal acantholytic blisters.
• Rooks Textbook of Dermatology- 9 th Edition Page No 50.5
• Bolognia Textbook of Dermatology 4 th Edition Page No 500
explanation.ref: Rooks Textbook of Dermatology- 9 th Edition Page No 50.5

subject: Dermatology
question: A 30-year-old male presented with recurrent episodes of non-healing oral ulcers, bullous eruptions, denuded skin all over body. Which of the following is the most likely antigen causative for this presentation?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A . BPAG 180
• Incorrect , associated with bullous pemphigoid , not pemphigus.
• Option B . BPAG 230
• Option C . Desmoglein 1
• Incorrect for this presentation, more often implicated in skin involvement without mucosal lesions .
• Educational Objective:
• Desmoglein 3 is the target antigen in pemphigus vulgaris , which presents with non-healing oral ulcers and flaccid blisters over skin .
• Rooks Textbook of Dermatology- 9 th Edition Page No 50.2
• Bolognia Textbook of Dermatology 4 th Edition Page No 498
explanation.ref: Rooks Textbook of Dermatology- 9 th Edition Page No 50.2

subject: Dermatology
question:
A 38-year-old female presents with persistent oral lesions that are characterized by ill-defined, irregular margins with peripheral extension and shedding of epithelium as shown in the image below. The lesions are painful and have been recurrent. Histopathological examination reveals suprabasal acantholysis without signs of epidermal necrosis or significant inflammatory infiltrate. The direct immunofluorescence shows intercellular deposits of IgG and C3 in the epidermis. What is the most likely diagnosis?

[Image: https://cerebellum-web-static.s3.amazonaws.com/media/public/images/2024/02/24/picture41.jpg]

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. Erythema Multiforme (EMF)
• It typically presents with target lesions, not suprabasal acantholysis on histopathology.
• Option C. Toxic Epidermal Necrolysis (TEN)
• It is characterized by widespread epidermal necrosis and detachment .
• Option D. Herpetic Gingivostomatitis
• It is usually associated with viral inclusions, not the immunopathology seen in PV.
• Educational Objective:
• Pemphigus Vulgaris clinical presentation - Painful , recurrent oral lesions and histopathological evidence of suprabasal acantholysis with intercellular IgG and C3 .
• Rooks Textbook of Dermatology- 9 th Edition Page No 50.7
• Bolognia Textbook of Dermatology 4 th Edition Page No 499
explanation.ref: Rooks Textbook of Dermatology- 9 th Edition Page No 50.7

subject: Dermatology
question: A 42-year-old woman presents with painful oral ulcers and flaccid bullae on her trunk. Physical examination shows that the bullae are easily ruptured, leaving denuded skin. A Tzanck smear from the base of a blister reveals acantholytic cells. Which condition does the patient most likely have?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. Bullous Pemphigoid –
• Incorrect , as Tzanck smear is typically negative for acantholytic cells.
• Option C. Erythema Multiforme –
• Incorrect , usually presents with target lesions.
• Option D. Dermatitis Herpetiformis –
• Educational Objective:
• Pemphigus Vulgaris by the presence of acantholytic cells on Tzanck smear.
• Bolognia Textbook of Dermatology 4 th Edition Page No 503
explanation.ref: Bolognia Textbook of Dermatology 4 th Edition Page No 503

subject: Dermatology
question: A 35-year-old male presents with flaccid vesicles over the trunk and oral mucosa with painful erosions. On examination, both the bulla spread sign and Nikolsky sign are positive. What will be the direct immunofluorescence findings of this patient?
options: Linear IgG Deposition in Dermoepidermal Junction

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. Fishnet IgM deposition in the epidermis –
• Incorrect , IgM is not typically involved in pemphigus vulgaris.
• Option C. Linear IgG deposition in dermo epidermal junction –
• Incorrect , this is indicative of bullous pemphigoid.
• Option D. Fishnet IgG deposition in the dermis –
• Incorrect , in pemphigus vulgaris, IgG deposition is intercellular within the epidermis, not the dermis.
• Educational Objective:
• Pemphigus vulgaris using DIF, shows intercellular IgG and C3 deposition in the epidermis.
• Rooks Textbook of Dermatology- 9 th Edition Page No 50.3
• Bolognia Textbook of Dermatology 4 th Edition Page No 495
explanation.ref: Rooks Textbook of Dermatology- 9 th Edition Page No 50.3

subject: Dermatology
question: A 48-year-old male presents with remitting & relapsing course of mucocutaneous lesions since last 8 years. On examination there are erosions in oral cavity and flaccid bullae over trunk. Which of the following cells are expected in the biopsy of this patient?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option B . Clue cells
• Clue cells are associated with bacterial vaginosis , not skin lesions.
• Option C . Nikolsky cells
• They are not a type of cell; the Nikolsky sign is a clinical feature where the skin sloughs of f with pressure.
• Option D . Auspitz cells
• They are not a cell type; the Auspitz sign refers to pinpoint bleeding when scales are scraped away in psoriasis .
• Educational Objective:
• A cantholytic cells in the biopsy of a patient with pemphigus vulgaris .
• Rooks Textbook of Dermatology- 9 th Edition Page No 50.7
explanation.ref: Rooks Textbook of Dermatology- 9 th Edition Page No 50.7

subject: Dermatology
question: A 31-year-old male presented with vesiculobullous lesions over the body. The Tzanck smear revealed the presence of acantholytic cells. The resident on duty ordered Direct ImmunoFluorescence which came out to be positive. What is the diagnosis?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. Bullous Pemphigoid –
• It can show linear IgG and C3 deposits at the basement membrane , but we cannot appreciate acantholytic cells in this condition as it is a subepidermal blistering disorder.
• Option B. Herpes zoster –
• This would typically show multinucleated giant cells (MNGCs) and acantholytic cells, but Direct ImmunoFluorescence would be negative in this condition.
• Tzanck smear showing secondary acantholysis in Herpes zoster. The yellow arrow points to a single acantholytic cell; the red arrow indicates a MNGC.
• Option C. Hailey Hailey Disease –
• This is a Genetic disorder in which we can see acantholytic cells, but Direct ImmunoFluorescence would be negative as there are no Autoantibodies in this condition.
• Educational Objective:
• Diagnose Pemphigus vulgaris with the presence of acantholytic cells on Tzanck smear and intercellular IgG and C3 deposition on direct immunofluorescence.
• Bolognia Textbook of Dermatology 4 th Edition Page No 495
explanation.ref: Rooks Textbook of Dermatology- 9 th Edition Page No 50.3

subject: Dermatology
question: A 35-year-old male presents with erosions in the oral cavity & flaccid blisters and ulcers over the face, trunk & lower limbs. The Tzanck smear revealed the presence of acantholytic cells. Which of the following appearances is expected in the biopsy of this patient?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. Cluster of jewels
• This is seen in Linear IgA disease as vesicles are present in annular pattern mostly over face & buttocks.
• Option B. Chicken wire
• It typically refers to the pattern of deposition of immune reactants seen in direct immunofluorescence in pemphigus.
• This refers to the pattern of IgG deposition seen in direct immunofluorescence in pemphigus.
• Educational Objective:
• "ROW OF TOMBSTONE" is a histological descriptor of pemphigus vulgaris indicating a suprabasal acantholytic blister on skin biopsy.
• Rooks Textbook of Dermatology- 9 th Edition Page No 50.7
• Bolognia Textbook of Dermatology 4 th Edition Page No 503
explanation.ref: Rooks Textbook of Dermatology- 9 th Edition Page No 50.7

subject: Dermatology
question: What is the most probable diagnosis for a 35-year-old patient who has presented with easily rupturing Flaccid Bulla on the Skin and whose Biopsy indicates a Suprabasal Split?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. Pemphigus Vegetans:
• Description: Pemphigus vegetans is a variant of pemphigus vulgaris . It features vesicles and bullae that evolve into verrucous (wart-like ) lesions, particularly in skin folds.
• Reason for exclusion from the diagnosis : The primary presentation in the question doesn't describe verrucous or wart-like lesions.
• Option C. Pemphigus foliaceus :
• Description: Pemphigus foliaceus is another type of pemphigus but is characterized by more superficial skin blisters than pemphigus vulgaris, and it doesn't typically involve the mucous membranes. Reason for exclusion from the diagnosis : The biopsy of pemphigus foliaceus would show a split in the upper epidermis (subcorneal ), not a suprabasal split.
• Option D. Erythema multiforme:
• Description: Erythema multiforme is an acute, immune-mediated skin condition. It is characterized by target-like lesions and is often triggered by infections (e.g., herpes simplex virus ) or certain medications.
• Reason for exclusion from the diagnosis : The presentation of erythema multiforme is quite distinct with its target-like lesions , and a biopsy would not reveal a suprabasal split.
• Educational Objective:
• Pemphigus vulgaris diagnosed by the presence of flaccid bullae that rupture easily and a suprabasal split on biopsy, indicating an autoimmune process causing loss of epidermal cell adhesion.
• Rook's Textbook of Dermatology -9th Edition Page No 47.1-47.5, 50.1-50.10
explanation.ref: Rook's Textbook of Dermatology -9th Edition Page No 47.1-47.5, 50.1-50.10

subject: Dermatology
question: A 27-year-old female presents to the clinic with complaints of Vesiculo Bullous lesions for the past 14 days. Which of the following diseases is least likely to present with fluid-filled lesions as seen in this patient?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. Impetigo –
• It commonly presents with fluid-filled lesions that can develop into pustules.
• Option B. Diabetes mellitus –
• Bullosis Diabeticorum is characterized by a sudden and spontaneous appearance of vesicles and bullae within normal-appearing, non-inflamed skin of the distal extremities of diabetics. The most frequent site is the feet
• Option C. Porphyria –
• Some types, like porphyria cutanea tarda (PCT) , can present with fluid-filled blisters on sun-exposed areas.
• Flaccid hemorrhagic bulla and tense vesicle with clear fluid on the forefinger , accompanied by crusts and scars in a patient of PCT.
• Educational Objective
• Lupus vulgaris is a disease unlikely to present with vesiculobullous lesions compared to impetigo, diabetes-related skin conditions, and porphyria.
• Rooks Textbook of Dermatology- 9 th Edition Chapter 27 Page No 27.21 27.22
• Bolognia Textbook of Dermatology 4 th Edition Page No 555, 775, 1306
explanation.ref: Rooks Textbook of Dermatology- 9 th Edition Chapter 27 Page No 27.21 27.22

subject: Dermatology
question: A 50-year-old male visits the clinic with complaints of recurrent episodes of oral ulcers, large areas of denuded skin & flaccid vesiculobullous eruptions. Nikolsky sign was found to be positive. Which of the following bedside studies is most likely to confirm the diagnosis?
options: Skin Biopsy and Direct Immuno Fluorescence
Tzanck Smear from the Floor of Bulla

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. Gram staining of blister fluid –
• It is used for bacterial infections, not autoimmune vesiculobullous diseases.
• Option B. Culture and sensitivity –
• This is also used for identifying infections .
• Option C. Skin biopsy and Direct Immunofluorescence –
• Although this is the definitive test, it is not a bedside study and requires laboratory facilities.
• Educational Objective:
• Tzanck smear - rapidly detect acantholytic cells at the bedside in patients with suspected pemphigus .
• Rooks Textbook of Dermatology- 9 th Edition Page No 41.26
explanation.ref: Rooks Textbook of Dermatology- 9 th Edition Page No 41.26

subject: Dermatology
question: A 45-year-old male presents to the dermatology clinic with a sudden appearance of skin lesions on the entire upper half of the body for the past 7 days. On examination, you notice erythematous shallow erosions with blisters and yellowish scales over seborrheic areas, as shown in the image below. The oral mucosa is normal. Histology revealed a subcorneal cleft, and DIF shows IgG deposition in the granular layer of the epidermis and autoantibodies against Desmoglein 1. What is the most likely diagnosis?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. Bullous Pemphigoid
• Typically involves deeper blisters and autoantibodies target the basement membrane , not Desmoglein.
• Option B. Pemphigus Vulgaris –
• Would generally involve the oral mucosa and shows antibodies against Desmoglein 3 > 1.
• Option D. Dermatitis Herpetiformis –
• Associated with gluten sensitivity and IgA deposition, NOT IgG.
• Educational Objective:
• Diagnose Pemphigus foliaceus by the presence of blisters and erosions in seborrheic areas and autoantibodies against Desmoglein 1 with subcorneal clefts on histology.
• IADVL Textbook of Dermatology 2 nd Edition Page No 860
• Bolognia Textbook of Dermatology 4 th Edition Page no 500
explanation.ref: Rooks Textbook of Dermatology- 9 th Edition Page No 50.5

subject: Dermatology
question:
A 35-year-old man presents to the Dermatology OPD with Complaints of Vesiculobullous lesions which are flaccid all over body with mucosal erosions & ulcers. The Senior resident in Dermatology department in which you are posted, does this clinical sign to look for the findings as shown in the image below. What is the name of this Clinical sign being elicited?

[Image: https://cerebellum-web-static.s3.amazonaws.com/media/public/images/2024/02/24/picture55.jpg]

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. Auspitz Sign
• This sign is associated with Psoriasis and involves pinpoint bleeding when scales are scraped away.
• Option B. Buschke Ollendroff Sign –
• Ollendorff probe sign or Buschke-Ollendorff sign refers to the deep dermal tenderness elicited by pressing the Papular lesions of secondary syphilis with a pinhead , when the patient winces with pain.
• Option D. Row of Tombstone Appearance
• This refers to the histological appearance of basal cell layer keratinocytes in pemphigus vulgaris, not a clinical sign.
• Educational Objective:
• Utilize the Nikolsky sign to aid in the clinical diagnosis of diseases with f laccid vesiculobullous lesions such as pemphigus vulgaris.
• Rooks Textbook of Dermatology- 9 th Edition Page No 50.3
explanation.ref: Rooks Textbook of Dermatology- 9 th Edition Page No 50.3

subject: Dermatology
question:
A 45-year-old man presents with recurrent flaccid bullae on an erythematous base on his face and trunk. He had similar lesions in the oral cavity, which are painful. The histopathology of the lesion is shown in the image below. What is the underlying mechanism responsible for the clinical features and histopathological changes in this patient?

[Image: https://cerebellum-web-static.s3.amazonaws.com/media/public/images/2024/02/24/picture57.jpg]

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• It refers to intercellular edema in the epidermis, seen in conditions like eczema.
• It is characterized by thickening of stratum spinosum , not blister formation.
• This is a thickening of the stratum corneum .
• Educational Objective
• Recognize acantholysis as the pathomechanism in pemphigus vulgaris , which presents with flaccid bullae and "row of tombstone" appearance histologically
• Rooks Textbook of Dermatology- 9 th Edition Page No 50.7
• Bolognia Textbook of Dermatology 4 th Edition Page No 499
• IADVL Textbook of Dermatology 2 nd Edition Page No 860-861
explanation.ref: Rooks Textbook of Dermatology- 9 th Edition Page No 50.7

subject: Dermatology
question: Statement 1 : Clinically a patient having flaccid bullae with painful erosions, will show Suprabasal split and inflammatory infiltrate in the blister cleft on histopathological examination. Statement 2 : Row of tombstones appearance is diagnostic of Pemphigus vulgaris
options: Statement 1 is False and Statement 2 is True
Statement 1 is True and Statement 2 is False
Both the statements are True but 2 is not explaining 1
Both the statements are True and 2 is explaining 1

explanation.short: Ans. C) Both the Statements are True but 2 is not explaining 1
explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Incorrect because both statements are true.
• Incorrect because statement 2 is not explaining statement 1, even though both statements are true individually.
• Educational Objective:
• In pemphigus vulgaris , patients clinically present with flaccid bullae and painful erosions, and histologically show suprabasal acantholysis , which on the skin biopsy appears as a "row of tombstones ." NOTE: Pemphigus foliaceous has a subcorneal split, hence Row of Tombstone appearance is NOT SEEN in this condition.
explanation.ref: Rooks Textbook of Dermatology 9 th Edition Page No. 50.1-50.7

subject: Dermatology
question:
The histopathology picture below is suggestive of?

[Image: https://cerebellum-web-static.s3.amazonaws.com/media/public/images/2024/02/24/picture59.jpg]

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. Psoriasis vulgaris:
• This is an autoimmune disease characterized by hyperproliferation of keratinocytes leading to thickened, scaly plaques . Histologically, it shows parakeratosis ( retention of nuclei in the stratum c orneum ), elongation of rete ridges , and a neutrophilic infiltrate ( Munro's microabscesses) as shown in the image below.
• The tombstone appearance is not a feature of psoriasis.
• Option B . Leishmaniasis:
• It's a parasitic infection caused by the Leishmania species. Histologically, it is characterized by macrophages containing the parasite (Leishman-Donovan bodies ) in the dermis as shown in the image below.
• The tombstone appearance is not seen in leishmaniasis.
• Option C . Mycosis fungoides:
• This is a type of cutaneous T-cell lymphoma . Histologically, there is an atypical lymphocytic infiltrate in the epidermis (epidermotropism ), and the formation of Pautrier's microabscesses (collections of l ymphocytes in the epidermis) as shown in the image below.
• It does not exhibit a tombstone appearance .
• Educational Objective:
• Pemphigus vulgaris is characterized by intraepidermal blistering due to acantholysis , or separation of keratinocytes , as a result of autoantibody attack on desmosomal proteins.
• Rook's Textbook of Dermatology - 9th Edition Chapter 50 Page No 50.1-50.8
explanation.ref: Rook's Textbook of Dermatology - 9th Edition Chapter 50 Page No 50.1-50.8

subject: Dermatology
question:
A patient presents with the Tense bullous lesions associated with itching. Histopathology revealed Subepidermal blistering. What is the diagnosis of this condition & what are the antigens against which autoantibodies are most commonly seen in this condition?

[Image: https://cerebellum-web-static.s3.amazonaws.com/media/public/images/2024/02/24/1.jpg]

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option B. Pemphigus vulgaris & Desmoglein 3.
• Pemphigus vulgaris is associated with flaccid blisters , not tense ones, and the blisters are intraepidermal due to acantholysis . The main autoantigen is Desmoglein 3 , a desmosomal protein.
• Option C. Bullous Pemphigoid & BP280 .
• This option is incorrect because BP280 (collagen type VII) is not the primary
• antigen in Bullous Pemphigoid ; BP180 is the correct antigen.
• Option D. Pemphigus vulgaris & Desmoglein 1 .
• While Desmoglein 1 is indeed a target antigen in some cases of Pemphigus vulgaris (usually in combination with Desmoglein 3), the description provided does not match Pemphigus vulgaris, which presents with supra basal intra-epidermal blistering and flaccid bullae .
• Educational Objective:
• In a patient with tense bullae and histopathology showing subepidermal blistering , the diagnosis is Bullous Pemphigoid , and the autoantibodies are most commonly directed against the BP180 antigen .
• Rooks Textbook of Dermatology- 9 th Edition Chapter 50 Page No 50.10
explanation.ref: Rooks Textbook of Dermatology- 9 th Edition Chapter 50 Page No 50.10

subject: Dermatology
question: A 52-year-old man with fragile skin arrives at the Dermatology OPD. On examination, it was observed that he has bullae which are healing with scarring, and erosions on the extensor surfaces of his elbows, hands, and knees. DIF findings revealed antibodies against type VII collagen. What is the most likely diagnosis?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. Epidermolysis bullosa simplex
• It is a condition characterized by skin fragility and blistering, but it is due to mutations affecting keratins in the epidermis , not autoantibodies against type VII collagen.
• Option C. Hailey-Hailey disease also known as familial benign pemphigus
• It is a genetic disorder that results in a blistering skin condition but does not involve autoantibodies against type VII collagen .
• Option D. Darier's Disease
• It is a genetic disorder characterized by greasy, warty papules and plaques , particularly in seborrheic areas, not by bullae that heal with scarring, and it is not associated with antibodies against type VII collagen.
• Educational Objective:
• In a patient presenting with fragile skin, scarring bullae on extensor surfaces, and DIF findings showing antibodies against type VII collagen , the most likely diagnosis is Epidermolysis bullosa acquisita. Feature Epidermolysis Bullosa Acquisita (EBA) Epidermolysis Bullosa Dystrophica (EBD) Etiology Autoimmune (acquired) Genetic (inherited) Target antigen Type VII collagen (autoantibodies) Type VII collagen (mutation) Direct Immunofluorescence (DIF) Positive (IgG at basement membrane zone) Negative (no immune deposition; structural defect)
explanation.ref: Rooks’ Textbook of Dermatology- 9 th Edition Chapter 50 Page No 50.41

subject: Dermatology
question:
A 3-year-old girl child presents to OPD with blisters over perioral area & other periorificial areas which are arranged annularly giving a ‘crown of jewels’ appearance (Image given below). Direct immunofluorescence microscopy revealed linear IgA deposition in Basement membrane zone. What is the most likely diagnosis & what is the drug of choice in this disease?

[Image: https://cerebellum-web-static.s3.amazonaws.com/media/public/images/2024/02/24/5_JWNrTCe.jpg]

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. Bullous pemphigoid
• It is typically a disease of the elderly and is characterized by subepidermal blisters with linear IgG and C3 deposition , not IgA. Dapsone is not the first-line treatment for bullous pemphigoid.
• Option B. Chronic bullous disease of childhood (CBDC), also known as linear IgA disease.
• It is the correct diagnosis based on the description, but azathioprine is not the drug of choice. It is used as a second-line or adjunctive therapy when dapsone is contraindicated or insufficient.
• Option D. Epidermolysis bullosa simplex
• It is a hereditary condition characterized by skin fragility and blistering due to cytoskeletal protein mutations. Genetic counselling may be of value for familial planning , but the condition does not present with linear IgA deposition, nor is it treated with dapsone.
• Educational Objective:
• In a child presenting with annularly arranged periorificial blisters and linear IgA deposition at the basement membrane zone , the most likely diagnosis is Chronic bullous disease of childhood, and the drug of choice is dapsone.
• Rooks Textbook of Dermatology- 9 th Edition Chapter 50 Page N50.33
explanation.ref: Rooks Textbook of Dermatology- 9 th Edition Chapter 50 Page N50.33

subject: Dermatology
question: All of the following are correct statements about Bullous Pemphigoid EXCEPT?
options: Direct Immunofluorescence (DIF) shows Linear IgG Deposits
Acantholysis is Absent as it is a Subepidermal Disorder

explanation.short: Ans. B) Oral Erosions are frequently seen
explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A . The Asboe-Hansen sign may be positive in Bullous Pemphigoid.
• It is also known as the "blister spread sign ," indicating that gentle pressure on the top of an intact blister may cause it to extend into adjacent unblistered skin.
• Option C. Direct immunofluorescence
• In Bullous Pemphigoid typically shows linear IgG and often C3 deposits along the basement membrane zone, which is consistent with a subepidermal blistering process .
• Refers to the loss of keratinocyte-to-keratinocyte adhesion within the epidermis, is not a feature of Bullous Pemphigoid. This disorder is characterized by a subepidermal split without acantholysis.
• Educational Objective:
• Bullous Pemphigoid may show a positive Asboe-Hansen sign and will have linear IgG deposition on DIF, but oral erosions are not a frequent finding, and acantholysis is absent as it is a subepidermal disorder.
explanation.ref: Rooks Textbook of Dermatology- 9 th Edition Chapter 50 Page No 50.10- 50.15

subject: Dermatology
question:
A 28-year-old patient complains of the following intensely itchy rash over her elbows, knees as shown in the image below. She is also a known case of celiac disease & she strictly follows a gluten-free diet, which has resulted in improvement of her diarrhea. However, the following itchy skin lesions have persisted despite her altered diet. What is the Drug of choice for this patient & what is the characteristic DIF finding seen in this patient?

[Image: https://cerebellum-web-static.s3.amazonaws.com/media/public/images/2024/02/24/7.jpg]
options: Dapsone & Granular Deposits of IgA in Dermal Papillae
Steroids & Linear Deposits of IgA in Basement Membrane Zone
Dapsone & Granular Deposits of IgG in Dermal Papillae
Steroids & Linear Deposits of IgG in Basement Membrane Zone

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option B . Steroids & Linear deposits of IgA in Basement membrane Zone.
• While steroids may be used in a variety of dermatological conditions, they are not the first-line treatment for dermatitis herpetiformis. Additionally, linear deposits of IgA at the basement membrane zone are indicative of a different condition, such as linear IgA disease.
• Option C . Dapsone& Granular deposits of IgG in dermal papillae.
• Although Dapsone is the correct treatment, the characteristic DIF finding in dermatitis herpetiformis is granular IgA, not IgG deposits.
• Option D .Steroids & Linear deposits of IgG in Basement membrane Zone.
• This option suggests a diagnosis of Bullous pemphigoid and does not correspond to dermatitis herpetiformis , which is characterized by IgA deposits .
• Educational Objective:
• For a patient with an intensely itchy rash over the elbows and knees and a history of celiac disease , the drug of choice is Dapsone , and the characteristic DIF finding is granular deposits of IgA in the dermal papillae.
• Rooks Textbook of Dermatology- 9 th Edition Chapter 50 Page No 50.52-50.54
explanation.ref: Rooks Textbook of Dermatology- 9 th Edition Chapter 50 Page No 50.52-50.54

subject: Dermatology
question: All of the following are correct statements about Darier’s disease EXCEPT?
options: Mutation of the ATP2A2 gene seen
Corps Ronds & Grains seen in Histopathology

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. Mutation of the ATP2A2 gene is seen in Darier’s disease.
• This gene encodes the SERCA2 calcium pump in the endoplasmic reticulum , and mutations lead to abnormalities in skin cell adhesion and keratinization .
• Option B. Corps ronds& Grains seen in histopathology
• Corps ronds and grains are characteristic histopathological findings in Darier’s disease.
• Option D. DIF will be negative in Darier’s disease,
• It is not an autoimmune disorder but a genetic one. Direct immunofluorescence is typically used to detect deposits of immunoreactants in tissue, which would not be present in Darier’s disease.
• Educational Objective:
• In Darier’s disease, look for a genetic mutation in ATP2A2 , histopathological features of corps ronds and grains , and expect negative DIF . Tense bullae over flexors are not a feature of this condition and represent the incorrect statement in the options provided.
explanation.ref: Rooks Textbook of Dermatology- 9 th Edition Chapter 66 Page No 66.1- 66.9

subject: Dermatology
question: A primigravida in her 3 rd Trimester develops intensely itchy grouped vesicles in the periumbilical area. A diagnosis of Herpes gestation is made based on these features. Which of the following is INCORRECT statement among the following options regarding this condition?
options: It resolves spontaneously in P[ostpartum Period
Herpes Simplex Virus- 1 is responsible for Cross Reaction with Skin Antigens & Antibody Formation
Associated with Antibodies against BP180 Antigen

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. It resolves spontaneously in postpartum period
• The condition resolves spontaneously in the postpartum period , although flare-ups can occur with menstruation or subsequent pregnancies.
• Option C. Associated with antibodies against BP180 antigen
• The condition is associated with antibodies against BP180 antigen , which is a hemidesmosomal protein involved in the structural integrity of the skin.
• Option D. Variant of Bullous Pemphigoid
• Pemphigoid Gestationis is considered a variant of Bullous Pemphigoid , occurring specifically during pregnancy.
• Educational Objective:
• Pemphigoid Gestationis, the autoimmune response targets BP180 antigen , and it is a pregnancy-related variant of Bullous Pemphigoid that typically resolves postpartum . It is not related to Herpes simplex virus infection.
explanation.ref: Bolognia Textbook of Dermatology- 4 th Edition Chapter 27 Page No 554

subject: Dermatology
question:
An 84-year-old woman with a history of ischaemic heart disease is reviewed in the dermatology clinic. Her current medication includes aspirin, simvastatin, bisoprolol, ramipril and isosorbide mononitrate. She has developed tense blistering lesions on her legs as shown in the image below. Each lesion is around 1 to 3 cm in diameter and she reports that they are slightly pruritic. Examination of her mouth and vulva is unremarkable. What is the most likely diagnosis?

[Image: https://cerebellum-web-static.s3.amazonaws.com/media/public/images/2024/02/24/11.jpg]

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A . Pemphigus:
• It is a group of autoimmune blistering disorders characterized by the formation of fragile blisters and erosions on the skin and mucous membranes .
• It typically presents with flaccid blisters and is often associated with involvement of the mouth and mucous membranes, which are not mentioned in the patient's presentation.
• Option B. Drug reaction to Aspirin
• This can cause various skin reactions, including urticaria or fixed drug eruptions , but it is less likely to cause tense blistering lesions like those described in the case.
• Option C. Epidermolysis Bullosa Simplex
• This is a genetic disorder characterized by blistering of the skin and mucous membranes in response to minor trauma or friction . It usually presents in childhood and is not typically associated with the development of tense blisters in an elderly patient without a previous history of the condition.
• Educational objective:
• In an elderly patient with tense blistering lesions on the legs and a history of ischaemic heart disease, the most likely diagnosis is bullous pemphigoid
explanation.ref: Rooks Textbook of Dermatology- 9 th Edition Chapter 50 Page No 50.10

subject: Dermatology
question: Arrange the following blistering diseases based on the level of separation (i.e. site where bulla is formed superficial to deep) Pemphigus Vulgaris Linear IgA Disease Bullous Pemphigoid Epidermolysis Bullosa Dystrophica

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Pemphigus vulgaris involves the superficial epidermis where the desmosomes are affected, leading to intraepidermal blisters due to loss of cell adhesion between keratinocytes. Bullous pemphigoid is deeper than pemphigus vulgaris, with separation occurring within the subepidermal zone due to autoantibodies against hemidesmosomes. Linear IgA disease also features a subepidermal blister but is typically grouped with other immunobullous disorders that have a similar level of separation as bullous pemphigoid; however, it is differentiated by its distinct immunopathological findings. Epidermolysis bullosa dystrophica is the deepest , with separation at the level of the dermis-epidermis junction or within the dermis , resulting in severe blistering and scarring.
• Image depicting structure of DEJ / BMZ
• Educational Objective:
• The pathophysiology of blistering disorders, specifically Pemphigus vulgaris causes superficial blisters within the epidermis, bullous pemphigoid and linear IgA disease cause subepidermal blisters, and epidermolysis bullosa dystrophica involves the deepest level of separation within the dermis.
• Rooks Textbook of Dermatology- 9 th Edition Chapter 50 Page No 50.4-10
• Bolognia Textbook of Dermatology 4 th Edition Page No 512
explanation.ref: Rooks Textbook of Dermatology- 9 th Edition Chapter 50 Page No 50.4-10

subject: Dermatology
question:
A 60-year-old female patient presented with blisters and crusts all over the body. The Histopathology of the patient is shown below. What is the most likely diagnosis of this patient?

[Image: https://cerebellum-web-static.s3.amazonaws.com/media/public/images/2024/02/24/16.jpg]

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option B. Dermatitis herpetiformis :
• This condition typically presents with grouped vesicles as shown in the image below.
• Histology usually shows neutrophilic infiltrates at the tips of dermal papillae , with granular IgA deposits found on direct immunofluorescence.
• Option C. Epidermolysis bullosa acquisita :
• This is a rare, chronic subepidermal blistering disease that usually presents as non-inflammatory blisters on trauma-prone areas, with an onset that can be at any age. Histologically, it can resemble bullous pemphigoid but tends to have a more cell-poor infiltrate and is often associated with trauma . Direct immunofluorescence shows IgG deposits at the basement membrane.
• Option D. Pemphigus vulgaris :
• This autoimmune c ondition presents with supra basal acantholysis leading to intra epidermal blisters .
• Histopathology of Pemphigus vulgaris showing Row of TOMBSTONE appearance. It is characterized by flaccid blisters and erosions, often starting in the oral mucosa , and a positive Nikolsky's sign . Histopathology would show acantholytic keratinocytes above the basal layer of the epidermis.
• Educational Objective:
• The characteristic histological features of bullous pemphigoid , include subepidermal blistering with a variable i nflammatory infiltrate, often containing eosinophils, and its clinical presentation with tense blisters in an elderly patient , which is crucial for accurate diagnosis and management.
• Rooks Textbook of Dermatology- 9 th Edition Page No 50.13
explanation.ref: Rooks Textbook of Dermatology- 9 th Edition Page No 50.13

subject: Dermatology
question: A 30-year-old male had itchy papulovesicular lesions on elbows, knees & buttocks. Direct ImmunoFluorescence (DIF) showed granular IgA deposition at Dermoepidermal Junction (DEJ). What is the most likely diagnosis of this patient?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A . Bullous pemphigoid:
• Typically presents with large, tense blisters in elderly patients and shows linear IgG and/or C3 deposition along the basement membrane zone on DIF , not granular IgA.
• Option C . IgA pemphigus:
• Presents with pustules and shows intercellular IgA deposits within the epidermis on DIF, not at the DEJ.
• Option D . Linear IgA disease:
• Characterized by blisters that can form an annular pattern and shows linear IgA deposition at the DEJ, but the clinical presentation and age of the patient are less typical for this diagnosis compared to dermatitis herpetiformis.
• Image depicting Linear IgA disease showing Annular pattern of lesion.
• Educational Objective:
• Dermatitis herpetiformis typically presents with pruritic papulovesicular lesions on extensor surfaces and is associated with granular IgA deposits at the DEJ on DIF.
• Rooks Textbook of Dermatology- 9 th Edition Page No 50.54
explanation.ref: Rooks Textbook of Dermatology- 9 th Edition Page No 50.54

subject: Dermatology
question:
Match the following blistering disorders in Column A with Column B

[Image: https://cerebellum-web-static.s3.amazonaws.com/media/public/images/2024/02/24/08.jpg]

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Bulla spread sign: Refers to the extension of a blister to adjacent unblistered skin when pressure is put on the lateral border of the bulla as shown in the image below.
• Nikolsky’s sign : On application of tangential pressure over skin on the border of an intact blister → upper layers of epidermis separate from the lower layers → → skin peels off → extension of the blister .
• → Erosion formed → Nikolsky’s sign.
• Bullous pemphigoid (BP) | c. Bulla spread sign + Nikolsky sign Bullous pemphigoid (BP) typically does not exhibit the Nikolsky sign (as acantholysis is not associated with BP); the skin adjacent to the blisters is not easily dislodged by lateral pressure. Pemphigus vulgaris | a. Bulla spread sign + Nikolsky sign + | Pemphigus vulgaris is characterized by both the bulla spread sign and the Nikolsky sign due to acantholysis within the epidermis. Staphylococcal Scalded Skin Syndrome | b. Bulla spread sign - Nikolsky sign + | Staphylococcal Scalded Skin Syndrome (SSSS) typically shows a positive Nikolsky sign , where the top layers of the skin slip away from the lower layers when rubbed. Impetigo | d. Bulla spread sign - Nikolsky sign - | Impetigo, particularly the bullous type, may present with blisters but does not typically show the Nikolsky sign or bulla spread sign.
• Educational Objective:
• Pemphigus vulgaris and bullous pemphigoid can be distinguished by the presence or absence of the Nikolsky sign, with pemphigus vulgaris being positive and bullous pemphigoid being negative for this clinical sign. Additionally, understanding the typical presentations of Staphylococcal Scalded Skin Syndrome and Impetigo is essential for appropriate diagnosis and treatment.
• Rooks’ Textbook of Dermatology- 9 th Edition Page No 50.3, 115.13
• Bolognia Textbook of Dermatology 4 th Edition Page No 499
explanation.ref: Rooks’ Textbook of Dermatology- 9 th Edition Page No 50.3, 115.13

subject: Dermatology
question: 30-year-old male presents to the clinic complaining of persistent skin lesions that he describes as "dirty" and "warty". He mentions that these papules tend to cluster around his hairline, behind his ears, and on his chest and back, particularly in areas that seem to be rich in sebaceous glands. On examination, the papules are greasy, hyperkeratotic, and foul-smelling with some crusting. The patient also reports a family history of similar skin lesions. He is otherwise in good health and takes no medications. Based on the clinical presentation and family history, the most likely diagnosis is ?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option B . Keratosis Pilaris:
• This benign condition typically presents with small, rough bumps on the upper arms, thighs, or buttocks without a foul odour and is not generally found in seborrheic regions.
• Option C . Pemphigus Foliaceous:
• This autoimmune blistering disorder presents with superficial blisters that evolve into crusted erosions , not typically described as "dirty" or "warty".
• Option D . Seborrheic Dermatitis:
• It presents with scaly, flaky, and erythematous plaques in seborrheic areas but does not typically produce the warty papules described in this patient.
• Educational Objective:
• The presentation of Keratosis follicularis (Darier's disease ) malodorous, greasy, hyperkeratotic papules in seborrheic areas, often with a positive family history , which distinguishes it from other keratotic and dermatologic conditions.
• Rooks Textbook of Dermatology- 9 th Edition Page No 66.3
• Bolognia Textbook of Dermatology 4 th Edition Page No 944
explanation.ref: Rooks Textbook of Dermatology- 9 th Edition Page No 66.3

subject: Dermatology
question: A 30-year-old female in her third trimester of pregnancy presents to the dermatology clinic with a 2-week history of itchy, tense blisters centralized on her abdomen, especially around the umbilical area. The blisters are on an erythematous base and she denies any prior history of similar lesions. She has no significant medical history and her pregnancy has been uneventful until these skin changes. Examination does not reveal involvement of the mucous membranes. Based on the clinical presentation, the most likely diagnosis is ?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A . Bullous pemphigoid:
• Incorrect because it typically affects older individuals and is not associated with pregnancy.
• Option B . Dermatitis herpetiformis:
• Incorrect because this condition presents with grouped vesicles and intense pruritus , often on extensor surfaces, and is associated with celiac disease.
• Option C . PUPPP (Pruritic Urticarial Papules and Plaques of Pregnancy):
• Incorrect because PUPPP usually presents with urticarial papules and plaques , starts in the stretch marks of the abdomen, and typically does not involve the formation of blisters.
• Educational Objective:
• Pemphigoid gestationis is a pruritic , vesiculobullous disorder of pregnancy p resenting with tense blisters around the umbilical area , typically occurring in the second or third trimester , without mucosal involvement.
• Rooks Textbook of Dermatology- 9 th Edition Page No 115.13
• Bolognia Textbook of Dermatology 4 th Edition Page No 472
explanation.ref: Rooks Textbook of Dermatology- 9 th Edition Page No 115.13

subject: Dermatology
question:
A 26-year-old presents with a chronic history of vesiculopustular eruptions, macerated plaques, and fissures predominantly in the axillary and inguinal regions. These lesions are recurrent and often exacerbate with heat and sweating. Physical examination reveals well-demarcated, moist, erythematous plaques with multiple small vesicles and superficial erosions. A skin biopsy is performed, which shows supra basal acantholysis with a “dilapidated brick wall” appearance without evidence of granulomas or significant dermal inflammation as shown in the image below. The most likely diagnosis is ?

[Image: https://cerebellum-web-static.s3.amazonaws.com/media/public/images/2024/02/24/25.jpg]

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Incorrect because while it can present with maceration and satellite pustules , the histology would show budding yeast and pseudohyphae .
• Option B . Erythrasma:
• Incorrect because it typically presents as well-demarcated pink to brown patches in the axilla and groin but lacks the histological feature of acantholysis. It is caused by Corynebacterium minutissimum and would fluoresce coral-red under Wood's lamp.
• Option D . Hidradenitis suppurativa:
• Incorrect because this chronic inflammatory condition presents with painful nodules , abscesses, and sinus tracts , primarily affecting apocrine gland-bearing skin . Histopathology would show follicular occlusion and secondary inflammatio n, not acantholysis.
• Educational Objective:
• Hailey-Hailey disease recognized by its clinical presentation of recurrent vesiculopustular lesions in intertriginous areas and histopathological findings of supra basal acantholysis , which is characteristic of the disorder.
• Rooks Textbook of Dermatology- 9 th Edition Page No 66.10
• Bolognia Textbook of Dermatology 4 th Edition Page No 950
explanation.ref: Rooks Textbook of Dermatology- 9 th Edition Page No 66.10

subject: Dermatology
question: A 90-year-old male presented with tense bullae, urticarial lesions all over body along with oral lesions. Which of the following statements is true regarding the disease?
options: Dapsone is the treatment of choice
Split is Intraepidermal at Suprabasal Level

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A . Dapsone is the treatment of choice:
• Dapsone is not the first-line treatment for bullous pemphigoid; systemic corticosteroids and immunosuppressive agents are typically used.
• Option C . Split is Intraepidermal at suprabasal level:
• This description applies to pemphigus vulgaris , not bullous pemphigoid. Bullous pemphigoid is characterized by a subepidermal split at the level of Hemidesmosome & Lamina lucida as shown in the images below.
• Image depicting Split at the level of Hemidesmosome & Lamina lucida in Bullous Pemphigoid.
• Option D . Target antigen is BP 120:
• The primary target antigens in bullous pemphigoid are BP180 (also known as BPAG2or type XVII collagen ) and BP230, not BP120.
• Educational Objective:
• Bullous pemphigoid can present with tense bullae and urticarial lesions , including oral mucosal involvement, and may heal with post inflammatory hyper- or hypo pigmentation. The disease is characterized by a sub epidermal split with auto antibodies targeting BP180 and BP230.
• Bolognia Textbook of Dermatology 4 th Edition Page No 512
explanation.ref: Rooks Textbook of Dermatology- 9 th Edition Page No 50.16

subject: Dermatology
question:
A 40-year-old male presented with a recurrent history of vesiculopustular macerated plaques over the axilla as shown in the image below. Which of the following statements is TRUE about the disease?

[Image: https://cerebellum-web-static.s3.amazonaws.com/media/public/images/2024/02/24/30.jpg]
options: Defect is in Calcium Pump ATP 2A2 Gene
Nails have Red & White Longitudinal Bands

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A . Defect is in the calcium pump ATP 2A2 gene
• This statement is true for Darier's disease, not Hailey-Hailey disease. Hailey-Hailey disease is caused by mutations in the ATP2C1 gene.
• Option C . Nails have red & white longitudinal bands
• This finding is associated with Darier's disease, not Hailey-Hailey disease.
• Option D . Retinoids are the treatment of choice:
• While systemic retinoids may be used in treatment, they are not the primary choice due to potential side effects and are generally considered when other treatments fail.
• Educational Objective:
• Hailey-Hailey disease recognised by its clinical presentation of vesiculopustular, macerated plaques in intertriginous areas and its histopathological examination reveals incomplete acantholysis.
• Rooks Textbook of Dermatology- 9 th Edition Page No 66.10
• Bolognia Textbook of Dermatology 4 th Edition Page No 944
explanation.ref: Rooks Textbook of Dermatology- 9 th Edition Page No 66.10

subject: Dermatology
question:
A 45-year-old male presented with severe excoriations over the elbows, knees, and buttocks. Direct Immuno Fluorescence has IgA deposition in dermal papilla in granular pattern as shown in the image below. You have told the patient what the possible diagnosis for his clinical features could be and DIF findings. After learning about his condition, he googled, and now he wants to know which food he can eat safely. What would you recommend as a safe food item among the following options?

[Image: https://cerebellum-web-static.s3.amazonaws.com/media/public/images/2024/02/24/31.jpg]

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Not recommended as it contains gluten.
• Incorrect because rye also contains gluten .
• Not safe as it contains gluten.
• Pathophysiology of Dermatitis herpetiformis
• Acronym "BROW" helps to remember gluten containing foods:
• B: Barley R: Rye O: Oats (not specifically labelled as gluten-free) W: Wheat
• Oats are inherently gluten-free , they are often contaminated with gluten from other grains during processing , unless they are specifically labeled as gluten-free. Rice, Maize and Millets can be given.
• Educational Objective:
• Choose safe food options for patients with gluten-related skin conditions like D ermatitis herpetiformis, avoiding gluten-containing grains like barley, rye, and wheat.
• Rooks Textbook of Dermatology- 9 th Edition Page No 50.54
• Bolognia Textbook of Dermatology 4 th Edition Page No 528
explanation.ref: Rooks Textbook of Dermatology- 9 th Edition Page No 50.54

subject: Dermatology
question: A patient presented with multiple tense blisters over the body associated with pruritus. On histopathology neutrophils are visible. Which is the most likely antigen causative for this presentation?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option B. BPAG 230:
• This antigen is associated with Bullous Pemphigoid , not LABD.
• Option C. Collagen 7:
• This antigen is related to Epidermolysis Bullosa Acquisita.
• Option D. Desmoglein 3:
• It is associated with Pemphigus Vulgaris.
• Educational Objective:
• BPAG 180 i s the target antigen in LABD , which presents with tense blisters and neutrophil infiltration on histopathology.
• Rooks Textbook of Dermatology- 9 th Edition Page No 50.33
• Bolognia Textbook of Dermatology 4 th Edition Page No 323
explanation.ref: Rooks Textbook of Dermatology- 9 th Edition Page No 50.33

subject: Dermatology
question:
A 65-year-old guy arrives with tense, itchy bullae over his limbs. The roof of the bullae collapsed after a few days. What is the most likely diagnosis shown in the clinical image below?

[Image: https://cerebellum-web-static.s3.amazonaws.com/media/public/images/2024/02/24/34.jpg]

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option B . Pemphigus vulgaris:
• This is another Autoimmune Blistering Disorder . However, the blisters in pemphigus vulgaris are flaccid and easily ruptured as shown in the image below, as opposed to the tense bullae seen in bullous pemphigoid. Additionally, the oral mucosa is frequently involved in pemphigus vulgaris .
• Option C. Dermatitis Herpetiformis :
• This condition is associated with gluten-sensitive enteropathy (celiac disease) and presents with itchy papules, vesicles , and bullae. However, the distribution is typically on extensor surfaces like elbows and knees as shown in the image below, and the appearance differs from what is shown in the image in the MCQ.
• Option D. Linear IgA Dermatosis :
• This is a rare autoimmune blistering disorder . The blisters are typically in a "string of pearls " or annular arrangement as shown in the image below,
• It's different from the presentation described and shown in the image.
• Educational Objective:
• " Bullous Pemphigoid : It is a subepidermal blistering disorder predominantly affecting the elderly , characterized by tense itchy bullae , autoantibodies against BP180 and BP230 , and eosinophilic involvement."
• Rooks Textbook of Dermatology- 9 th Edition Chapter 50, Page No. 50.4-50.37.
explanation.ref: Rooks Textbook of Dermatology- 9 th Edition Chapter 50, Page No. 50.4-50.37.

subject: Dermatology
question:
In the case of a child presenting to the OPD with tense bullae over the torso, and a biopsy of the lesion revealing subepidermal blistering and neutrophil infiltration, what is the preferred drug for treatment?

[Image: https://cerebellum-web-static.s3.amazonaws.com/media/public/images/2024/02/24/38.jpg]

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• This is a monoclonal antibody that targets CD20 on B-cells and is used in a variety of autoimmune diseases, including those with blistering skin manifestations like pemphigus vulgaris . It is not the first-line therapy for Linear IgA disease.
• This is an immunosuppressant that inhibits T-cell activation and is used in severe cases of many autoimmune disorders and in transplant patients. It may be used in refractory cases of Linear IgA disease but is not the first choice due to potential side effects, especially in children.
• This immunosuppressive agent is used as a steroid - sparing drug in various autoimmune conditions. It is not the first-line treatment for Linear IgA disease and is considered in cases that are refractory to dapsone.
• Educational Objective:
• The first - line treatment for LinearIgAdisease ( ChronicBullousDisease of Childhood ) is dapsone , due to its efficacy in managing conditions with neutrophilic infiltrates.
• Fitzpatrick’s Dermatology 9 th Edition Page No 989,998
explanation.ref: Fitzpatrick’s Dermatology 9 th Edition Page No 989,998

subject: Dermatology
question: A 35-year-old female with a clinical history of respiratory distress, persistent gastrointestinal symptoms, notable weight loss, and a duodenal biopsy revealing total villous atrophy and increased intraepithelial lymphocytes, in conjunction with seropositivity for anti-tissue transglutaminase antibodies, is most likely to exhibit which dermatological manifestation?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option B . Erythema Marginatum:
• This type of rash is associated with rheumatic fever and not directly linked to celiac disease, or the antibodies indicated.
• Option C . Erythema Multiforme:
• While erythema multiforme can be associated with various triggers, including infections and medications , it is not specifically linked to celiac disease.
• Option D . Malar Rash:
• The malar rash is characteristic of systemic lupus erythematosus and is not typically seen in the context of celiac disease or dermatitis herpetiformis.
• Educational Objective:
• Dermatitis herpetiformis i s the skin condition associated with celiac disease, characterized by blistering eruptions and confirmed by a positive anti-tissue transglutaminase antibody test.
• IADVL Textbook of Dermatology 2 nd Edition Page No 876
• Rooks Textbook of Dermatology- 9 th Edition Page No 50.54
• Bolognia Textbook of Dermatology 4 th Edition Page No 528
explanation.ref: IADVL Textbook of Dermatology 2 nd Edition Page No 876

subject: Dermatology
question: A 38-year-old woman presents with respiratory difficulty, chronic gastrointestinal issues, significant weight loss, and itchy papulovesicular lesions on her elbows, knees & buttocks. and a duodenal biopsy indicating complete loss of villi and a rise in intraepithelial lymphocytes. She also tests positive for anti-tissue transglutaminase antibodies. Given these clinical features, which treatment would be most appropriate for her dermatological condition?
options: Gluten Free Diet with Minerals & Vitamins

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A . Dapsone:
• While dapsone is used to provide rapid relief of itching and lesions in dermatitis herpetiformis, it is not curative , and a gluten-free diet remains the fundamental treatment.
• Option B . Acyclovir:
• This antiviral medication is not used in the treatment of dermatitis herpetiformis, as the condition is not caused by a virus.
• Option C . Steroids:
• Steroids may be used in the short term for their anti-inflammatory effects but do not address the underlying cause of dermatitis herpetiformis.
• Educational Objective:
• Dermatitis herpetiformis is the skin manifestation of celiac disease, characterized by pruritic papulovesicular lesions and granular IgA deposition at the dermo-epidermal junction , which is best managed with a gluten-free diet and supplementation with minerals and vitamins.
• Rooks Textbook of Dermatology- 9 th Edition Page No 50.54
• Bolognia Textbook of Dermatology 4 th Edition Page No 532
explanation.ref: Rooks Textbook of Dermatology- 9 th Edition Page No 50.54

subject: Dermatology
question: A person undergoing Anti PD1 immunotherapy (Nivolumab) presents with itching, eczema, and hives-like rashes. Upon histopathological analysis, a blister beneath the outer skin layer is observed. The direct immunofluorescence assessment of the affected areas indicates the presence of Linear C3 and IgG along the basement membrane. What is the diagnosis?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. Pemphigus vulgaris
• Is an autoimmune disorder that typically shows intraepidermal blisters and acantholysis. Direct immunofluorescence usually reveals intercellular IgG and C3 deposition in the epidermis , not along the basement membrane.
• Option C. Dermatitis herpetiformis
• Is associated with celiac disease and is characterized by granular IgA deposits at the tips of the dermal papillae on direct immunofluorescence, not linear deposition along the basement membrane. Dermatitis Herpetiformis showing Excoriations over extensors, Papulo vesicles as primary lesions & granular IgA deposits at the tips of the dermal papillae on direct immunofluorescence
• Option D. Pemphigus foliaceous
• Is another type of pemphigus which also shows intraepidermal blisters and acantholysis but affects the superficial epidermis producing Subcornealsplit . The immunofluorescence pattern is similar to pemphigus vulgaris and not linear along the basement membrane.
• Educational Objective:
• The diagnosis of bullous pemphigoid is suggested by clinical presentation and confirmed by histopathology showing subepidermal blisters and direct immunofluorescence indicating linear C3 and IgG deposition along the basement membrane.
explanation.ref: Rooks Textbook of Dermatology 9 th Edition Page No 50.1-50.22, 50.52-50.55

subject: Dermatology
question: A male baby, born to a non-consanguineous couple, presents with blisters on areas of friction. Their previous baby also experienced similar issues and unfortunately passed away at the age of 2 weeks. What is the probable diagnosis?

explanation.detail:
• Educational Objective:
• The likely diagnosis in a neonate presenting with blistering at areas of friction , especially with a similar family history and early demise in a sibling , is Epidermolysis bullosa, a genetic condition causing extremely fragile skin leading to blister formation with minimal trauma. The fact that the parents are non-consanguineous does not rule out a recessive genetic disorder , as both parents may still be asymptomatic carriers of the same mutated gene by chance.
explanation.ref: Rook's Textbook of Dermatology - 9th Edition - Volume I Chapter 71 Page no 71.4-71.16

subject: Dermatology
question:
A 45-year-old patient presented with Erythema & purplish tinge on upper eyelids & Erythema over shawl area as shown in the image below. She also had significantly elevated serum Creatinine phosphokinase levels. Identify the FALSE statement regarding this disease?

[Image: https://cerebellum-web-static.s3.amazonaws.com/media/public/images/2024/01/30/picture1_BsYJsky.jpg]
options: Dilated Capillary Loops in Nail Fold can be seen
Heliotrope Rash seen in this disease is associated with Anti-Mi-2 Antibodies.
Mechanic's Hands can be seen in this disease
Muscle Tenderness is common in this condition

explanation.short: Ans. D) Muscle Tenderness is common in this condition
explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. Nail fold changes with periungual erythema with visible dilated capillary loops in the proximal nail fold .
• Option B. Gottron's papule, heliotrope rash, and shawl sign are associated with anti-Mi2 antibodies .
• Option C. Mechanic's hand : Roughening and cracking of the fingertip and side skin that results in scuffed , crooked lines that resemble those of a mechanic or manual labourer .
• The following list includes several dermatomyositis symptoms :
• Heliotrope rash around eyelids with a lilac hue . Gottron's papules are violaceous , erythematous papules that can appear over the elbow , knee , dorsal interphalangeal or metacarpophalangeal joints . The Gottron's sign is a distribution of symmetric , non-scaling , violaceous , erythematous macules or plaques that are frequently atrophic . Shawl sign or V-sign : Fixed macular erythema spanning the shoulders and outer arms ( Shawl distribution )
• Proximal Myopathy : Patients have trouble performing everyday activities like climbing stairs , getting up , and lifting their arms above their heads.
• Screening : Approximately 20 to 40% of patients have an underlying malignancy (eg, lung, ovarian, breast) and hence screening should be done at the time of diagnosis.
• Diagnosis : Laboratory evaluation of patients with dermatomyositis will reveal elevated serum creatinine kinase and antinuclear antibodies .
• Mechanic hand, Myositis, and ILD (Interstitial Lung Disease) are associated with anti-histidyl tRNA synthetase (anti-Jo-1) antibodies.
• Prognosis : The presence of dysphagia points to a poorer prognosis .
• Educational Objective
• In dermatomyositis, muscle tenderness is not a common finding; instead, patients typically present with muscle weakness , a heliotrope rash associated with anti-Mi-2 antibodies , mechanic's hands, and dilated capillary loops in the nail fold.
explanation.ref: Rook's Textbook of Dermatology - Volume II-9th Edition, Chapter 53 Page no’s 53.5, 53.6 and 53.8

subject: Dermatology
question: All of the following statements are correct regarding Discoid Lupus Erythematosus except:
options: Face and Scalp are the most frequently involved sites.
Non Scarring Alopecia is seen associated with Discoid Lupus Erythematosus.
Topical Steroids are the First Line treatment for Localised Lesions
Antimalarials can be used for treatment

explanation.short: Ans. B) Non Scarring Alopecia is seen associated with Discoid Lupus Erythematosus.
explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option B. Non scarring alopecia is seen associated with Discoid Lupus Erythematosus.
• A benign inflammatory skin condition called discoid lupus erythematosus usually affects the face and scalp , associated scarring alopecia (Option B) is seen. It has distinct, red, scaly patches that can vary in size. These patches heal with atrophy, scarring, and pigmentary changes . The most common type of disease is the circumscribed or discoid form , which mainly affects the cheeks, nose bridge , ears, side of the neck, and scalp. ( Option A)
• The scalp-specific discoid lupus erythematosus with follicular plugging (Scarring alopecia) is depicted in the image below.
• The skin lesions in discoid or chronic cutaneous lupus erythematosus are well-defined erythematous patches with adherentscaling and peripheral hyperpigmentation .
