The underside of the scale reveals horny plugs that have occupied dilated pilosebaceous canals when the scale is removed. This sign is known as the "tin-tack" sign. The first-line treatment for localized lesions is topical corticosteroids. (Option C) Hydroxychloroquine and chloroquine are oral antimalarial drugs that can be used along with oral steroids and have a good response rate. (Option D)
• Educational Objective
• Discoid lupus erythematosus (DLE) typically presents with scarring alopecia when it affects the scalp, distinguishing it from non-scarring forms of alopecia.
explanation.ref: Rook's Textbook of Dermatology 9th Edition, Chapter 51, Page no’s 51.4-51.10

subject: Dermatology
question:
A young woman arrived at the OPD with the following Annular Polycyclic Rash. She also complained of Myalgia, Arthralgia, and Hair Loss. Her medical history was significant for sun aggravating her Skin complaints. A diagnosis of SCLE is considered. Which of the following is the most common cutaneous presentation of this condition?

[Image: https://cerebellum-web-static.s3.amazonaws.com/media/public/images/2024/01/30/picture2_E0ejbOV.jpg]

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. Scarring papulosquamous eruption :
• While cutaneous lesions in systemic lupus erythematosus (SLE) can sometimes scar , particularly in the case of discoid lupus , SCLE typically presents with non-scarring lesions .
• Option C. Annular polycyclic lesions :
• This option is not the most common presentation , making it incorrect for the purpose of this question. Although annular polycyclic lesions are characteristic of SCLE , they occur in a smaller proportion of patients (about one-third) compared to the papulosquamous eruption.
• Option D. Vesiculobullous lesions :
• Vesiculobullous lesions are not typically seen in SCLE. These types of lesions are more commonly associated with other dermatological conditions like bullous pemphigoid .
• Educational Objective
• The most common cutaneous presentation of subacute cutaneous lupus erythematosus (SCLE ) is a non-scarring papulosquamous eruption, which is seen in the majority of patients, while a smaller percentage presents with annular polycyclic lesions.
explanation.ref: Rook's Textbook of Dermatology 9th Edition, Chapter 51, Page no 51.11-13

subject: Dermatology
question: All of the following statements are correct regarding SLE except?
options: Photosensitivity is the most common Cutaneous Manifestation
Salmon Rash is seen in this condition
Anti-Smith (anti-Sm) Antibodies are considered to be most specific for SLE
Anti-ds DNA correlates with Disease Activity

explanation.short: Ans. B) Salmon Rash is seen in this condition
explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option B: Salmon rash is seen in this condition
• Salmon rash is seen in adult-onset of Still's disease , not SLE.
• The most common cutaneous manifestation of Systemic Lupus Erythematosus is photosensitivity .
• In decreasing order, the cutaneous manifestations in SLE are:
• 1. Photosensitivity (Option A)
• 4. Chronic urticaria >36 hrs
• 6. Painless ulcers over palate
• SLE is more prevalent in women (7:1 to 15:1). It is connected to the classic erythematous rash known as malar/butterfly rash, which typically spares the nasolabial folds .
• There may also be discoid rashes that resemble discoid lupus erythematosus .
• Malar / Butterfly Rash.
• Some important points regarding Antibodies in SLE :
• Anti-Smith (anti-Sm) antibodies (Option C): Most specific for SLE & included in ACR criteria. (Its levels do NOT correlate with disease activity) ANA antibodies: Most sensitive antibodies. Rim pattern of ANA positivity is specific for SLE. Anti-ds DNA (Option D) levels correlates with disease activity. Anti-Ro antibodies are associated with photosensitivity.
• Glomerular Capillary Loops appearing as circumferentially thickened "Wire Loop" lesion , is classically seen in class IV or diffuse proliferative Lupus Nephritis .
• Adult-Onset of Still's Disease, is a rare systemic inflammatory disease characterized by the classic triad of:
• Persistent high Spiking Fever Joint Pain Salmon Rash (Option B)
• Educational Objective
• 'Salmon Rash' is not a feature of systemic lupus erythematosus (SLE) but is seen in adult-onset Still's disease , while SLE commonly presents with photosensitivity and may show specific antibodies like Anti-Sm and Anti-dsDNA, which correlates with disease activity.
explanation.ref: Rook's Textbook of Dermatology - Volume II-9th Edition, Chapter 51 Page no 51.15, 51.21, 51.34

subject: Dermatology
question: Which of the following statements is true regarding Neonatal Lupus Erythematosus?
options: Approximately 10% of the Infants Manifest Cutaneous Lesions
Rash Resolves over few months with Scarring
Phototherapy can be safely given to this Neonate
The most common Cutaneous finding is Raccoon Sign

explanation.short: Ans. D) The most common Cutaneous finding is Raccoon Sign
explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option D. The most common Cutaneous finding is raccoon sign
• An erythematous, slightly scaly eruption on the face and periorbital skin (also known as the "Raccoon Sign," "Owl Eye," or "Eye Mask") is the most typical symptom of Neonatal Lupus Erythematosus (NLE).
• NLE is a well-known subtype of lupus erythematosus that is thought to be brought on by maternal antibodies crossing the placenta. Skin lesions and congenital heart block (CHB) are the most common clinical manifestations . Skin lesions , which may be present at birth or develop in the first few weeks of life, are present in about 50% of new-borns (NOT 10%). (Option A) UV exposure may make the eruption worse (So Phototherapy , if needed, has to be administered with Caution). (Option C) The rash gets better over the first few months of life and usually goes away without leaving any scars by the age of twelve months . Skin conditions are frequently minor and do not need to be treated. (Option B)
• The image given below shows typical raccoon eyelid lesions in neonatal lupus erythematosus.
• Educational Objective
• Neonatal lupus erythematosus , the most common cutaneous manifestation is the 'raccoon sign', presenting as erythematous, slightly scaly lesions on the face and periorbital skin , which typically resolve without scarring.
explanation.ref: Rook's Textbook of Dermatology 9th Edition, Chapter 51, Page no’s 51.37 and 51.38

subject: Dermatology
question:
A patient comes to the OPD with the following Skin Manifestation of a Disease associated with Raynaud Phenomenon. What is the manifestation?

[Image: https://cerebellum-web-static.s3.amazonaws.com/media/public/images/2024/01/30/picture6_jBW2wUn.jpg]

explanation.short: Ans. B) Salt and Pepper Pigmentation
explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. Poikiloderma is a skin disorder characterized by: (ATP)
• Atrophy Telangiectasia s Pigment abnormalities Hypo and hyper
• It is seen in ionizing radiation-damaged skin and dermatomyositis.
• Option C . Sometimes the colour of the hair can change to white in the amelanotic lesions of vitiligo . It is known as Leucotrichia.
• Option D . Sharply demarcated depigmented macules are a hallmark of vitiligo . It results from the loss of melanocytes.
• Educational Objective
• 'Salt and pepper' pigmentation i s a skin manifestation associated with systemic sclerosis , a condition commonly accompanied by Raynaud's phenomenon . This pattern includes both hyperpigmented and hypopigmented areas interspersed on the skin.
explanation.ref: Rooks Textbook of Dermatology, 9 th Edition, Chapter 56 Page No 56.1 – 56.23

subject: Dermatology
question:
All of the following are correct about the below given condition except?

[Image: https://cerebellum-web-static.s3.amazonaws.com/media/public/images/2024/01/30/picture7_BC1RZJr.jpg]
options: Erythema Multiforme Minor is the most common form
The Distribution of Target Lesions is mainly over Acral Sites
NSAIDs are the most common Triggering Factor for EMF

explanation.short: Ans. D) NSAIDs are the most common Triggering Factor for EMF
explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Educational Objective
• Erythema Multiforme is often triggered by infections , particularly herpes simplex virus , rather than NSAIDs, and it commonly presents as Erythema Multiforme minor with target lesions concentrated on acral sites.
• Ref s: Rook's Textbook of Dermatology 9th Edition Chapter 47, Page no 47.1-5
• s: Rook's Textbook of Dermatology 9th Edition Chapter 47, Page no 47.1-5

subject: Dermatology
question: A 19-year-old patient with neurocysticercosis develops generalised peeling of skin except palms & soles & severe oral & genital involvement starting after taking seizure medication. What is the most likely diagnosis?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. Steven Johnson Syndrome (SJS) :
• SJS is a severe mucocutaneous reaction , often to medication s, but it is characterized by less extensive skin detachment than TEN ( less than 10% body surface area ). Given the " generalised peeling of skin ", SJS is less likely than TEN.
• Option C. Fixed drug eruption :
• This condition typically presents with solitary or a few well-demarcated plaques that recur at the same site with re-exposure to the offending drug, which does not match the described extensive peeling and mucosal involvement.
• Option D. Pemphigus vulgaris :
• Pemphigus vulgaris is an autoimmune blistering disorder that involves the skin and mucous membranes, but it does not typically present with generalised skin peeling as a reaction to medication.
• Educational Objective
• Toxic Epidermal Necrolysis i s a severe drug reaction characterized by widespread skin peeling , including severe oral and genital mucosal involvement.
• Refs : Rook's Textbook of Dermatology 9th Edition, Chapter 119 Page Nos 119.13-119.14

subject: Dermatology
question:
A 55 -year-old woman presents with rash over her lips as shown in image below, after she had taken NSAIDs for her joint pain. She had reported that she developed the same rash in the same location 5 months ago when she took the same drugs for back pain. What is the most likely diagnosis?

[Image: https://cerebellum-web-static.s3.amazonaws.com/media/public/images/2024/01/30/picture12_61cJM38.jpg]

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. Chikungunya fever :
• The nose and cheeks show signs of post-chikungunya hyperpigmentation on the Centro-facial face (so-called " chik sign "). After the fever has subsided , the pigmentary changes appear and could last for three to six months . However, the question stem describes how pigmentary changes start to manifest after a few days. Additionally, during the acute febrile phase , these patients will not experience any preceding erythema or eruption over the affected areas.
• Option B. Post-inflammatory hyperpigmentation (PIH )
• Option B. Post-inflammatory hyperpigmentation (PIH
• This is an acquired form of hyper melanosis that develops as a result of cutaneous injury or inflammation , such as impetigo, atopic dermatitis, or acne vulgaris.
• Option D. Drug hypersensitivity
• This is an immune-mediated reaction to a drug of which symptoms ranging from mild to severe include rash, anaphylaxis, and serum sickness.
• Educational Objective
• Fixed drug eruption by the recurrence of a localized rash at the same site upon re-exposure to an offending drug, such as NSAIDs.
• Ref s: Rooks Textbook of Dermatology, 9 th Edition, Chapter 118 Page No 118.12

subject: Dermatology
question:
A 35-year-old woman presents to the clinic with a 5-day history of a spreading rash. She reports that it began as a small red spot on her thigh that has expanded progressively and now measures approximately 15 cm in diameter. The lesion is not painful but feels warm to the touch. She recalls hiking in a heavily wooded area in the North-eastern United States two weeks prior. On examination, you observe a target like lesions as shown in the image below. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

[Image: https://cerebellum-web-static.s3.amazonaws.com/media/public/images/2024/01/30/picture14_hryUQNN.jpg]

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. Erythema Annulare : It is characterized by ring-shaped lesions shown in the image below but lacks the Bulls eye appearance and is not associated with a history of tick exposure.
• Option C. Erythema Marginatum : It is associated with rheumatic fever and presents with pink rings on the trunk and inner surfaces of the limbs as shown in the image below, not the expanding lesion described.
• Option D. Erythema Multiforme : It is a hypersensitivity reaction associated with certain infections , medications , and other triggers and presents with multiple target lesions mainly involving extremities as shown in the image below.
• Educational Objective:
• Erythema Migrans is the characteristic rash of Lyme disease , typically presenting as a circular rash with central clearing following a tick bite in endemic areas.
• Bolognia Textbook of Dermatology 4 th edition Page no 326
explanation.ref: Rooks textbook of dermatology- 9 th Edition page no 50.70

subject: Dermatology
question:
A 30-year-old woman presents to the Derma OPD with complaints of a rash on the palms and trunk for the past 2 weeks. One week prior to the onset of her symptoms, she was diagnosed with a urinary tract infection, for which she was prescribed Trimethoprim sulfamethoxazole. Now, on general physical examination her BP is 120/80, temperature is 100.2 degrees F, pulse is 98/minute, and her respiration is 16/minute. On examination of her skin, maculopapular rash having a dusky center with multiple rings is noted as shown in the image below. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

[Image: https://cerebellum-web-static.s3.amazonaws.com/media/public/images/2024/01/30/picture18.jpg]

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. Erythema Annulare: This term is less commonly used and may refer to a pattern seen in several conditions, but the description of rings with dusky centers is more typical of erythema multiforme .
• Option B. Erythema Marginatum: This type of erythema is most often associated with rheumatic fever and is characterized by non-pruritic, pink rings on the trunk and inner aspects of the limbs , which do not fit the description provided.
• Option D. Erythema Nodosum: Presents as tender red nodules , usually on the shins , and is not described as having multiple rings or a dusky center.
• Educational Objective:
• Erythema multiforme is characterized by target lesions —a d usky central area surrounded by a paler ring and a dark outermost ring —and is often a reaction to certain medications or infections .
• Bolognia Textbook of Dermatology 4 th edition Page no 334
explanation.ref: Rooks textbook of dermatology- 9 th Edition page no 47.11- 47.17

subject: Dermatology
question:
A 45-year-old patient with a history of antiepileptic medication use presents with widespread bullous skin lesions with extensive denudation of skin covering over 60% of body surface area as shown in the image below, mucosal involvement, and a positive Pseudo Nikolsky sign. Laboratory investigations reveal the following abnormalities. Which of the following serological findings is most useful in assessing the prognosis?

[Image: https://cerebellum-web-static.s3.amazonaws.com/media/public/images/2024/01/30/picture19_G6EJhLt.jpg]

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. Alanine aminotransferase >40 IU/L –
• Elevated liver enzymes may be seen in some cases due to systemic involvement, but they are not the primary indicator for assessing the prognosis in TEN.
• Option C. Potassium > 2.5 mEq/L –
• Elevated potassium levels can occur in TEN due to tissue damage and cell breakdown but are not as useful in assessing the prognosis compared to renal function.
• Option D. Sodium 140 mEq/L –
• Normal sodium levels do not provide specific information about the prognosis in TEN.
• Educational Objective:
• Elevated urea levels (>10 mmol/L) are a valuable serological finding for assessing the prognosis in toxic epidermal necrolysis (TEN) because they indicate the presence of acute kidney injury , a significant predictor of poor outcomes in this severe skin condition.
• Bolognia Textbook of Dermatology 4 th edition Page No 341
explanation.ref: Rooks Textbook of Dermatology- 9 th Edition Page No 119.3- 119.20

subject: Dermatology
question:
A 50-year-old patient presents with severe bullous skin lesions with extensive denudation of skin covering over 60% of the body surface area, mucosal involvement as shown in the image below, and a positive Pseudo Nikolsky sign. On further evaluation, a thorough medication history reveals recent use of antibiotics for gastrointestinal complaints. What is the most likely aetiology of this patient's condition?

[Image: https://cerebellum-web-static.s3.amazonaws.com/media/public/images/2024/01/30/picture20.jpg]

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A) Bacterial Infection –
• Bacterial infections can cause skin conditions , but they are not the primary cause of TEN. TEN is typically associated with medications and, less commonly, viral infections.
• Option B) Viral Infection –
• Although viral infections can lead to various skin manifestations, they are not the primary cause of TEN. Medications and drug-induced reactions , such as antibiotics, are more common triggers .
• Option D) Idiopathic –
• Idiopathic means the cause is unknown , but in the majority of TEN cases , a specific trigger can be identified, such as medication use or, less commonly, viral infections.
• Educational Objective:
• Toxic Epidermal Necrolysis (TEN) is most commonly drug-induced , often triggered by medications like antibiotics . Identifying the causative drug is crucial for management and preventing further exposure.
• Bolognia Textbook of Dermatology 4 th edition Page No 341
explanation.ref: Rooks Textbook of Dermatology- 9 th Edition Page No 119.3- 119.20

subject: Dermatology
question: Which of the following statements is False regarding Scleredema?
options: Can occur in association with Diabetes
Associated with Sclerodactyly and Raynaud’s Phenomenon
The Dermis is 3-4 times thicker than normal
Erythema and Peaud'orange Appearance of the Skin can be seen

explanation.detail:
• Educational Objective:
• S cleredema , a connective tissue disorder associated with diabetes and characterized by dermal thickening , does not typically present with sclerodactyly and Raynaud’s phenomenon , which are features of systemic sclerosis.
• Rooks’ Textbook of Dermatology- 9 th Edition Chapter 59 Page No 59.09 - 59.11.
explanation.ref: Bolognia 4 th Edition, Chapter 46, Page No 870

subject: Dermatology
question:
Match the following Drugs in Column A with the Colour of pigmentation associated in Column B?

[Image: https://cerebellum-web-static.s3.amazonaws.com/media/public/images/2024/01/30/screenshot-2024-01-30-151917.jpg]

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• 1. Arsenic exposure can lead to raindrop pigmentation of the skin, which presents as Small Areas of Hyperpigmentation resembling Raindrops.
• 2. Minocycline, a tetracycline antibiotic , can cause blue-black pigmentation in Acne Scars, on the Face, and in areas of previous Inflammation.
• 3. Chlorpromazine , an Antipsychotic medication, can lead to Blue-Grey Pigmentation typically occurring after prolonged use.
• 4. Clofazimine , used to treat Leprosy , is known to cause Brown Pigmentation of the Skin.
• Systemic causes of hyperpigmentation
• Cushing Disease, Addisons, Grave's Disease Hemochromatosis Megaloblastic Anemia Pernicious Anemia Pregnancy Renal Failure Systemic Sclerosis
• Educational Objective:
• The clinical recognition of drug-induced skin pigmentation requires matching the characteristic color change with the causative agent : Arsenic causes raindrop pigmentation ; Minocycline leads to blue-black pigmentation ; Chlorpromazine results in blue-grey pigmentation ; and Clofazimine causes brown pigmentation .
explanation.ref: Rooks Textbook of Dermatology- 9 th Edition Page no 88.17, 88.25

subject: Dermatology
question:
A 37-year-old female presented with lesions on face and concha as shown in the pictures below. The lesions are associated with scaling and are non-pruritic. Which of the following is a characteristic finding in this condition?

[Image: https://cerebellum-web-static.s3.amazonaws.com/media/public/images/2024/01/30/screenshot-2024-01-30-154647_aCTuesv.jpg]
options: Lifting the Scale Reveals Spikes on Under Surface
Pinpoint Bleeding is visible on Removal of Scales
Scratching the Lesion will make the Scales more Apparent
Stretching the Skin, Scales tend to Fold along the Line of Stretch

explanation.short: Ans. A) Lifting the Scale Reveals Spikes on Under Surface.
explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option B. Pinpoint bleeding is visible on removal of scales :
• Pinpoint bleeding upon removal of scales , known as the Auspitz sign , is typically seen in psoriasis when the thin layers overlying the dilated dermal papillae are removed
• Option C. Scratching the lesion will make the scales more apparent :
• The grattage test , where scratching a psoriatic lesion leads to more prominent scales , is suggestive of psoriasis , not DLE.
• Option D. Stretching the skin, scales tend to fold along the line of stretch :
• The collarette scale , which is a fine scale that seems to fold along the line of stretch , is characteristic of pityriasis rosea , not DLE.
• Educational Objective
• In Discoid Lupus Erythematosus , lifting the scale reveals spikes on the undersurface, which are the remnants of follicular plugs , distinguishing this condition from psoriasis and pityriasis rosea which have different characteristic scale findings .
• Bolognia Textbook of Dermatology 4 th Edition Page No 1166
explanation.ref: Rooks Textbook of Dermatology- 9 th Edition Page No 51.5

subject: Dermatology
question:
A 35-year-old woman presents to the clinic complaining of a widespread rash that has been worsening over the past month. She notes that the rash gets more pronounced when she spends time in the sun. Physical examination reveals multiple annular, erythematous plaques with central clearing and slight scale, distributed over her chest and back as shown below. She denies any joint pain or systemic symptoms. Laboratory tests are ordered, and her serological studies are pending. Which of the following antibodies is most characteristic of this condition?

[Image: https://cerebellum-web-static.s3.amazonaws.com/media/public/images/2024/01/30/picture11_ajEg3gJ.jpg]

explanation.short: Ans. A) Anti-Ro/SSA and Anti-La/SSB Antibodies:
explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option B. Anti-Double-Stranded DNA (dsDNA) Antibodies : Anti-dsDNA antibodies are highly specific for Systemic Lupus Erythematosus (SLE) and are typically associated with more severe disease, including renal involvement , which is not indicated in this patient's presentation.
• Option C. Anti-Centromere Antibodies : Anti-centromere antibodies are most commonly associated with limited cutaneous systemic sclerosis (lcSSc) , previously known as CREST syndrome , which does not present with the annular plaques seen in SCLE.
• Option D. Anti-Smooth Muscle Antibodies : Anti-smooth muscle antibodies are often associated with autoimmune hepatitis , which does not present with the cutaneous findings described for this patient.
• Antibodies and Associated Diseases in SLE and Other Conditions
• Educational Objective
• In Subacute Cutaneous Lupus Erythematosus (SCLE), Anti-Ro/SSA and Anti-La/SSB antibodies are characteristic serological findings , and these patients often present with photosensitive annular plaques with central clearing .
• Bolognia Textbook of Dermatology 4 th edition Page No 668
explanation.ref: Rooks Textbook of Dermatology- 9 th Edition Page No 51.13

subject: Dermatology
question: A 28-year-old woman presents to the dermatology clinic with complaints of hair loss and skin lesions. She reports that the skin lesions have been present for several months and often feel tender. She has also noticed sores in her mouth. Her medical history is significant for joint pain and fatigue. On examination, you note several round, well-defined plaques with erythema, scale, and central scarring on her scalp. The patient also has diffuse, non-scarring alopecia. No lymphadenopathy is palpable. Which of the following findings is most characteristic of systemic lupus erythematosus (SLE) in this patient?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. Anagen Effluvium
• Anagen effluvium is a form of hair loss that occurs during the anagen or growth phase of the hair cycle , usually due to chemotherapy. This is not a characteristic finding in SLE.
• Option C. Painful Oral Ulcers :
• Oral ulcers in SLE are painless , which helps differentiate them from other types of ulcers.
• While painful oral ulcers can occur in SLE, they are not as specific or characteristic as discoid lesions.
• Option D. Raccoon Eyes :
• Raccoon eyes, or bilateral periorbital ecchymosis , are typically associated with Neonatal SLE , basal skull fractures or certain types of amyloidosis , not in adult forms of SLE.
• Educational Objective
• Discoid lesions is characteristic cutaneous manifestations of SLE , which present as well-defined , erythematous plaques with scaling and central scarring on the scalp.
• Bolognia Textbook of Dermatology 4 th edition Page no 672
explanation.ref: Rooks textbook of dermatology- 9 th Edition page no 51.15

subject: Dermatology
question:
The clinical finding in the 10-year-old boy includes fever, joint pain, and a hand lesion as shown in the image below. What is the likely diagnosis based on these findings?

[Image: https://cerebellum-web-static.s3.amazonaws.com/media/public/images/2024/01/30/picture44.jpg]

explanation.short: Ans. A) Gottron's Papules - Juvenile Dermatomyositis.
explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option B. Rheumatoid Nodules - Juvenile Rheumatoid Arthritis:
• Juvenile Rheumatoid Arthritis is an Autoimmune disorder causing chronic joint inflammation in children. Rheumatoid Nodules are Firm, Painless , Subcutaneous nodules that usually occur in areas of pressure but are not typically seen in the hands in this form.
• Rheumatoid arthritis and rheumatoid nodules. Note the periarticular location of the skin- coloured nodules . The lesion in the image doesn't match the typical appearance of rheumatoid nodules .
• Option C. Sclerodactyly - Systemic Sclerosis:
• Systemic Sclerosis is a connective tissue disease characterized by skin thickening and hardening (scleroderma). Sclerodactyly refers to thickening and tightening of the skin on the fingers and hands , leading to a claw-like appearance . The lesion shown doesn't indicate the skin thickening or tightness characteristic of sclerodactyly .
• Option D. Mechanic's Hand - Systemic Lupus Erythematosus (SLE):
• SLE is an autoimmune disease affecting multiple organ systems. Mechanic's hand is a sign associated more with dermatomyositis than SLE, characterized by rough, cracked skin on the sides of the fingers, mimicking the hands of a manual labourer . The presentation of mechanic's hand in SLE is less common and the symptoms described do not align well with SLE.
• Educational Objective:
• In a case involving fever, joint pain, and hand lesions in a child , the presence of Gottron's papules is indicative of Juvenile dermatomyositis .
• Harrison’s Principles of Internal Medicine 21 st Edition Page 2820-2821
explanation.ref: Rooks Textbook of Dermatology- 9 th Edition Chapter 53 Page no 53.1-53.12

subject: Dermatology
question: A 22-year-old male presents to the emergency department with a rapidly developing rash. He reports a recent history of herpes simplex virus infection and has been taking a sulphonamide antibiotic for a urinary tract infection. Physical examination reveals multiple round lesions with a typical target or "iris" appearance, centrally darkened with concentric rings, located on his palms and soles. Some of the lesions are also noted on his trunk and face. The patient denies any respiratory or gastrointestinal symptoms. All are true about EMF except?

explanation.short: Ans. D) EM Minor has Mucosal Involvement
explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. Associated with Herpes Simplex : Erythema Multiforme (EM) is frequently associated with herpes simplex virus (HSV) infections . HSV is one of the most common precipitating factors for EM.
• Option B. Associated with Sulphonamides : EM can be associated with reactions to certain medications, including sulphonamides , which are known to be a common drug trigger.
• Option C. Lesions are Symmetrical : The lesions in EM are typically symmetrical and often present with a classic target or iris appearance , as described in the vignette.
• Educational Objective
• Erythema multiforme minor is characterized by target lesions without mucosal involvement, unlike erythema multiforme major, which does include mucosal lesi ons. EM can be triggered by infections such as Herpes Simplex Virus and medications like Sulphonamides and the lesions are typically symmetrical.
• Bolognia Textbook of Dermatology 4 th Edition Page no 323
explanation.ref: Rook's Textbook of Dermatology 9th Edition Chapter 47, Page no 47.1-5

subject: Dermatology
question: All of the following are associated with atopic dermatitis EXCEPT:

explanation.short: Ans. D) None of the above
explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Signs associated with Atopic Dermatitis .
• Dennie Morgan infraorbital fold
• Educational Objective :
• Atopic dermatitis is a multifactorial disease characterized by intense itching , chronic or relapsing eczematous lesions , and a personal or family history of atopic diseases . Recognizing the various signs associated with atopic dermatitis , such as the antenna sign , headlight sign , and Hertoghe's sign , is crucial for diagnosis and management
explanation.ref: Rooks Textbook of Dermatology- 9th Edition Volume 3 Chapter 149 Page No 149.21

subject: Dermatology
question: Identify the INCORRECT statement about Dermatitis among the following?
options: All Eczema is Dermatitis, but not all dermatitis is Eczema
Nummular Eczema is the other name for Discoid Eczema
Pityriasis Versicolor is a form of Dermatitis & it is associated with Atopy

explanation.short: Pityriasis Versicolor is a form of Dermatitis & it is associated with Atopy
explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A: Dermatitis is an umbrella term , covering many skin conditions associated with inflammation of skin including Eczema , so All eczema is dermatitis , but not all dermatitis is eczema .
• Option B: The hallmark symptom of eczema is intense pruritus.
• The image below shows eczema .
• Option C: Nummular dermatitis / Nummular eczema / Discoid Eczema presents as a well demarcated coin shaped erythematous plaque with vesicles surrounding it.
• Common locations involved are:
• Trunk Extensor surfaces of extremities:
• Men - pretibial areas
• Women – Dorsal aspects of hand
• The image below shows nummular dermatitis .
• Educational Objective
• Pityriasis Alba (NOT versicolor) is a form of dermatitis & it is associated with atopy .
explanation.ref: Rooks Textbook of Dermatology- 9th Edition Volume 2 Chapter 39 Page No 39.1-3, 39.7-8, 39.25

subject: Dermatology
question:
A 10-year-old child presented to the dermatology clinic with itchy and hyperpigmented plaques in cubital and popliteal fossa as shown in image. Her personal history reveals a chronic & recurrent course of these lesions; family history is positive for asthma. Identify the FALSE statement related to this patient?

[Image: https://cerebellum-web-static.s3.amazonaws.com/media/public/images/2023/04/07/picture4_MDg49a8.jpg]
options: UK refinement of Hanifin & Rajka criteria is used in the diagnosis of AD
It has a Flexural distribution in Infantile Phase
Face is the First Site of involvement of AD in infants

explanation.short: Ans. C) It has a Flexural Distribution in Infantile Phase
explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option B: UK refinement of Hanifin & Rajka’s diagnostic criteria of atopic dermatitis (Eczema):
• An itchy skin condition (or parental report of scratching or rubbing in a child ) Plus three or more of the following:
• Onset below age 2 years (not used if child is under 4 years) History of skin crease involvement (including cheeks in children under 10 years) History of a generally dry skin Personal history of other atopic disease (or history of any atopic disease in a first degree relative in children under 4 years ) Visible flexural dermatitis (or dermatitis of cheeks / forehead and outer limbs in children under 4 years )
• Dennie morgan fold under lower eyelid Allergic shiners – periorbital pigmentation Allergic salute – horizontal groove on dorsum of nasal bridge
• Staphylococcus aureus is the most common organism to cause secondary infections in the lesions of atopic dermatitis .
• Educational Objective:
• Atopic dermatitis in infants typically involves the face and extensor surfaces rather than flexural areas , which are more commonly affected in older children and adults .
• Rooks Textbook of Dermatology- 9 th Edition V olume 3 Chapter 149 Page No 149.21
explanation.ref: Rooks Textbook of Dermatology- 9th Edition Volume 2 Chapter 41 Page No 41.1, 41.2, 41.13-19

subject: Dermatology
question: A Systemic Disease known to be associated with Seborrheic Dermatitis?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option B. COPD (Chronic Obstructive Pulmonary Disease)
• It is a lung disease characterized by chronic obstruction of lung airflow that interferes with normal breathing . There is no direct association between COPD and seborrheic dermatitis.
• Option C. Multiple myeloma
• It is a cancer of plasma cells . While it can cause a variety of systemic symptoms , it is not typically associated with seborrheic dermatitis.
• It is a chronic infectious disease caused by Mycobacterium leprae , affecting the skin and peripheral nerves . It is not commonly associated with seborrheic dermatitis.
• Educational Objective:
• Seborrheic dermatitis is commonly associated with Parkinson’s disease due to possible autonomic dysregulation affecting the skin , particularly the sebaceous glands .
explanation.ref: Rooks Textbook of Dermatology- 9th Edition Volume 2 Chapter 40 Page No 40.1-40.4

subject: Dermatology
question: All of the following statements are correct EXCEPT?
options: Berloque Dermatitis is due to Perfumes
Meyerson Nevus is also known as Halo Dermatitis
Esteatotic eczema is also called as Eczema Craquele
Lymphomas are most common cause for Erythroderma

explanation.short: Lymphomas are most common cause for Erythroderma
explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. Berloque Dermatitis
• This is caused by exposure to certain chemicals found in perfumes . The chemicals, primarily bergapten, are photosensitizing agents that can cause a rash on areas of the skin exposed to the perfume and then to sunlight.
• Option B. Meyerson Nevus , or Halo Dermatitis
• This is a benign skin condition where an Eczematous Halo develops around a Mole (Nevus). This is a correct association.
• Option C. Eczema Craquele / Asteatotic Eczema:
• Itusually develops in the elderly . It affects the legs, arms & hands of elderly people in the context of very dry skin.
• The illness is typically chronic, relapsing in the winter and going away in the summer , but it eventually becomes irreversible. It might be a presenting symptom in myxoedema . It might also be brought on by a lack of zinc .
• Educational Objective:
• The most common cause of Erythroderma is Eczema , not lymphomas. Lymphomas may cause erythroderma , but they are not the most frequent cause.
• Online resource: https://www.uptodate.com/contents/erythroderma-in adults#:~:text=Erythroderma%20(literally%2C%20%22red%20skin
explanation.ref: Rooks Textbook of Dermatology- 9th Edition Chapter 39 Page No 39.10, 39.25, 39.31, Chapter 88 Page No 88.29

subject: Dermatology
question:
A woman who is a regular user of hair dye presented as shown in the image below. What is the allergen responsible for this condition?

[Image: https://cerebellum-web-static.s3.amazonaws.com/media/public/images/2023/04/07/picture8_ZCxLe8P.jpg]

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A . Para-tertiary Butyl Phenol (PTBP)
• It is less commonly used in hair dyes and is not the most frequent cause of allergic contact dermatitis related to hair dye use.
• Option C . Nickel
• It is a common allergen associated with jewellery and metal closures on clothing but is not typically found in hair dye products.
• Option D. Mono Benzyl Ether of Hydroquinone (MBEH)
• It is used in skin lightening creams and is not a common ingredient in hair dyes.
• Substances & the allergens in them causing ACD
• Educational Objective:
• Para-Phenylene Diamine (PPD) is a common allergen in hair dyes that can cause allergic contact dermatitis , presenting with an erythematous, pruritic, and vesicular rash on areas of application.
explanation.ref: Rooks Textbook of Dermatology- 9th Edition Chapter 128 Page No 128.74

subject: Dermatology
question: A 45-year-old person, who is taking care of cleaning utensils at his home since covid lockdown, presents to the dermatology clinic with intensely pruritic lesions for 6 days. History reveals, the lesions began to appear after he recently changed the cleaning detergent. Considering the likely diagnosis, which statement is True about the given condition?
options: It can be diagnosed with Patch Test
It is a delayed type of Hypersensitivity
Due to Direct Toxic Effects of a strong chemical

explanation.short: Due to Direct Toxic Effects of a strong chemical
explanation.detail:
• Educational Objective:
• Irritant contact dermatitis is caused by the direct toxic effect of chemicals on the skin and does not require prior sensitization. It can be differentiated from allergic contact dermatitis by the lack of a requirement for sensitization , immediate onset after exposure , and negative patch testing .
explanation.ref: Rooks Textbook of Dermatology- 9th Edition Chapter 129 Page No 129.4-8

subject: Dermatology
question: Identify the INCORRECT statement from the following?
options: Photo Patch Test is used to diagnose Photoallergic Contact Dermatitis
Gold Standard Diagnostic Test for Airborne Contact Dermatitis (ABCD) is a Patch Test
In Patch Testing, the patch is removed after 48 hours to examine the site of reaction
Patch Test Works on the Principle of Type III Hypersensitivity Reaction

explanation.short: Ans. D) Patch Test Works on the Principle of Type III Hypersensitivity Reaction
explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. Photo patch test
• This is indeed used to diagnose photoallergic contact dermatitis. This test involves applying a substance to the skin and then exposing the area to ultraviolet light to see if a reaction occurs.
• Option B. The Gold standard Diagnostic test for Airborne contact dermatitis (ABCD) & Allergic Contact Dermatitis (ACD) is a patch test.
• The image below shows a patch test.
• Option C. After the patch is removed , the area is examined for Type IV /delayed type of hypersensitivity.
• 48 hours: patch removal for initial reading to detect delayed hypersensitivity reaction 72-96 hours : final reading done for confirmation
• Educational Objective:
• Patch tests are used to diagnose Allergic contact dermatitis and work on the principle of a Type IV hypersensitivity reaction, which is T cell-mediated and does not involve immune complex deposition as seen in Type III hypersensitivity.
• Fitzpatrick’s Dermatology 9th Edition Page No 406
explanation.ref: Rooks Textbook of Dermatology- 9th Edition Volume I Chapter 4 Page No 4.23

subject: Dermatology
question: All of the following are characteristics of Acute Spontaneous urticaria EXCEPT?
options: Wheals are seen in Urticaria & they are Evanescent Lesions
Most of the times cause is not known
Darier's Sign is seen in Urticaria

explanation.short: Ans. C) Darier’s Sign is seen in Urticaria
explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A . Wheals are the hallmark of urticaria and are characterized by transient , elevated , and often itchy swellings of the skin , which are indeed evanescent, meaning they typically resolve within hours .
• Option B . It is true that in many cases of acute spontaneous urticaria , the cause is not known, making this condition idiopathic in nature.
• Option D . Urticaria usually subsides without leaving pigmentation , as it does not involve the deeper skin layers where pigment cells are located, which is a characteristic feature of this condition.
• While both conditions involve mast cells , urticaria is a hypersensitivity reaction (IgE-mediated or non-IgE), whereas urticaria pigmentosa is a form of mastocytosis , involving increased mast cell proliferation in the skin .
• Educational Objective:
• Darier's sign , which is the rapid appearance of a wheal after stroking the lesional skin , is associated with mastocytosis and not acute spontaneous urticaria. Urticaria is characterized by evanescent wheals that often have an idiopathic cause and resolve without pigmentation.
• Bolognia Textbook of Dermatology 4 th Edition Page No 309
explanation.ref: Rook's Textbook of Dermatology - Volume II-9th Edition Chapter 42, Page no 42.1-4, 42.8-10

subject: Dermatology
question:
A child presented with multiple blemishes and light brown macules located on the trunk as shown in the below image. On scratching of lesions, erythema develops around the lesion which is known as _____ & seen in______?

[Image: https://cerebellum-web-static.s3.amazonaws.com/media/public/images/2023/04/07/picture10_rkBu28c.jpg]

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A . Pseudodarier's sign , which is associated with smooth muscle hamartoma , typically manifests as a localized elevation of the skin or "goosebump" appearance when stroked , rather than erythema around the lesion .
• Option B . Koebner's phenomenon
• This is observed in conditions like lichen planus , psoriasis and vitiligo where new lesions appear at the site of skin trauma .
• However, this is different from the erythematous reaction described in the question.
• Option C . Darier's sign is not associated with Darier's disease (a genetic keratinization disorder).
• In Darier's disease, skin findings include greasy, scaly papules and plaques in seborrheic areas , not the response described.
• Educational Objective:
• Darier's sign , characterized by the development of erythema and swelling around a lesion upon scratching , is a diagnostic feature of urticaria pigmentosa , a form of cutaneous mastocytosis .
explanation.ref: Rook's Textbook of Dermatology 9th Edition Chapter 46, Page No 46.1-6

subject: Dermatology
question: Which of the following statements about angioneurotic edema is FALSE?
options: C1 Esterase Inhibitor Deficiency can cause it
Presents as Angioedema without Wheals & Itching
Pitting Edema of Face, Lips &Mucous Membranes

explanation.short: Ans. D) Pitting Edema of Face, Lips &Mucous Membranes
explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A . C1 esterase inhibitor deficiency is a well-known cause of hereditary angioedema , which leads to recurrent episodes of severe swelling (angioedema ). This type of edema is not pitting in nature and is not accompanied by wheals or pruritus (itching).
• Option B . Angioneurotic edema is indeed also referred to as Quincke’s disease . This is a synonym for angioedema , particularly when it affects the face and lips.
• Option C . Angioedema typically presents as swelling of the deeper dermis and subcutaneous or submucosal tissue , often without wheals and itching , which are characteristic of urticaria ( hives ).
• Educational Objective:
• Angioedema , also known as Angioneurotic Edema or Quincke’s disease, presents as Non-Pitting E dema of the Deeper Dermis and Subcutaneous or Submucosal tissue without wheals or itching and can be caused by C1 Esterase Inhibitor Deficiency .
explanation.ref: Rook's Textbook of Dermatology - Volume II- 9th Edition Chapter 43, Page No 43.2

subject: Dermatology
question:
A 6-year-old child came with white lesions over his face as shown in the image below. Which of the following statements is INCORRECT regarding this condition?

[Image: https://cerebellum-web-static.s3.amazonaws.com/media/public/images/2024/02/21/picture76.jpg]
options: History of Atopy might be present in the Family
Indeterminate Leprosy is an important differential for this presentation
It is a Variant of Vitiligo

explanation.short: Ans. D) It is a Variant of Vitiligo
explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. History of atopy might be present in the family –
• Option A. History of atopy might be present in the family
• Pityriasis alba is often associated with a history of atopic dermatitis or other atopic diseases , which may have a familial occurrence.
• Option B . Mostly happens in children –
• Pityriasis alba predominantly occurs in children and is considered a benign condition that usually resolves with age.
• Option C. Indeterminate leprosyis an important differential for this presentation –
• When evaluating hypopigmented lesions, especially in areas where leprosy is endemic, it is crucial to consider leprosy in the differential diagnosis.
• Indeterminate leprosy presents with a single hypopigmented macule and can be a consideration before the disease progresses to more definitive forms .
• Educational Objective:
• Recognize pityriasis alba as a common cause of hypopigmented lesions in children, which may be confused with other conditions such as vitiligo or indeterminate leprosy. Understanding the clinical presentation and associated conditions, like atopy, is essential for accurate diagnosis and appropriate management.
explanation.ref: Rook's Textbook of Dermatology -9th Edition Page No 39.25, 88.34

subject: Dermatology
question:
An infant presents with facial eczema as shown in the image below. Which of the following is true statement regarding this condition?

[Image: https://cerebellum-web-static.s3.amazonaws.com/media/public/images/2024/02/21/picture79.jpg]
options: Family History of Asthma should be inquired
Patch Test should be done to confirm the diagnosis
Flexural Eczema is classical in infants affected with this condition

explanation.short: Ans. A) Family History of Asthma should be inquired
explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option B. Improves in winters –
• AD often worsens in the winter due to drier conditions, which can exacerbate the skin dryness and irritation associated with AD.
• Option C. Patch test should be done to confirm the diagnosis –
• Patch testing is not routinely used to diagnose AD. Instead, AD is usually diagnosed based on clinical criteria (Hanifin Rajka) , including a personal or family history of atopy , the presence of typical lesions , and chronic or relapsing dermatitis .
• Option D. Flexural eczema is classical in infants affected with this condition
• This statement is generally incorrect for infants. While flexural eczema , which affects the bends of elbows, behind the knees , and other folded areas of the skin, is common in older children and adults with AD, infants more commonly have eczema on their face and extensor surfaces like knees & elbows dueto damage of skin barrier function due to trauma by crawling, as shown in the image below.
• Educational Objective:
• Atopic dermatitis is a common inflammatory skin condition associated with the atopic triad (eczema, asthma, allergic rhinitis) and can be supported by a family history of these conditions.
• Bolognia Textbook of Dermatology 4 th Edition Page No 208
explanation.ref: Rook's Textbook of Dermatology -9th Edition Page No 41.17

subject: Dermatology
question:
A 35-year-old man presents with widespread, red, scaly, and flaky skin that covers more than 90% of his body surface area, as depicted in the image. He reports that the rash started on his scalp and has progressively involved his entire body over the past few weeks. He also complains of severe itching and scaling. His medical history is significant for mild dandruff. He denies any recent infections, drug intake, or history of blistering disorders. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

[Image: https://cerebellum-web-static.s3.amazonaws.com/media/public/images/2024/02/21/picture81.jpg]

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. Pemphigus vulgaris
• Pemphigus vulgaris is a blistering autoimmune disorder characterized by intraepidermal blisters and erosions but is not typically associated with the widespread erythematous and scaling rash seen in the patient.
• Option C. Stevens-Johnson Syndrome
• SJS is an acute and severe mucocutaneous reaction , usually to drugs , characterized by blistering and epidermal detachment resembling burns , mucosal involvement, and systemic symptoms , which is not consistent with the gradual progression and symptoms described.
• Option D. Toxic Epidermal Necrolysis
• TEN is a more severe form of SJS with extensive detachment of the epidermis and mucous membranes , high mortality , and is typically drug-induced . The clinical picture described does not include the acute, severe blistering or systemic symptoms typical of TEN.
• Educational Objective:
• Diagnose seborrheic dermatitis when presented with a patient with a history of dandruff and widespread erythematous, scaly, and flaky skin , particularly when it starts on the scalp and progresses to cover a significant area of the body.
• Bolognia Textbook of Dermatology 4 th Edition Page No 208
explanation.ref: Rook's Textbook of Dermatology -9th Edition Page No 39.31

subject: Dermatology
question:
Match the Eczemas in Column A with the characteristic features given in Column B:

[Image: https://cerebellum-web-static.s3.amazonaws.com/media/public/images/2024/02/21/screenshot-2024-02-21-173130.jpg]

explanation.detail:
• Educational Objective:
• Patch tests are used to diagnose allergic contact dermatitis , not atopic dermatitis (which is instead associated with subcapsular cataract and Keratoconus ). Sensitization phase is absent in irritant contact dermatitis, not in bindi dermatitis, which is typically an allergic reaction confirmed by patch testing.
• Bolognia Textbook of Dermatology 4 th Edition Page No 208, 262, 1109
explanation.ref: Rook's Textbook of Dermatology -9th Edition Page No 41.20, 128.51, 129.3

subject: Dermatology
question:
A 65-year-old male patient presents with chronic, itchy, and erythematous rashes on his lower legs and feet, as shown in the image. He reports that the rashes often weep and have developed over areas where he feels a heavy sensation after standing for long periods. His medical history is significant for varicose veins and deep vein thrombosis. On examination, there is pitting edema around the ankles and hyperpigmentation of the affected skin. What is the most likely cause of the eczematous changes in this patient's lower extremities?

[Image: https://cerebellum-web-static.s3.amazonaws.com/media/public/images/2024/02/21/picture85.jpg]

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A . Atopy - Incorrect .
• While atopy refers to the genetic tendency to develop allergic diseases and can lead to atopic dermatitis, the patient's presentation is more consistent with stasis eczema due to underlying venous insufficiency , not atopy.
• Option B . Drugs - Incorrect .
• Drug reactions can cause various skin changes , including rashes , but the patient's symptoms and signs are more suggestive of a chronic process related to venous insufficiency rather than a drug-induced cause .
• Option C . Leather shoes - Incorrect .
• Contact dermatitis can be caused by leather shoes due to allergens like chromates ; however, the distribution and chronicity of the rash , along with the patient's history, support a diagnosis of stasis eczema due to venous insufficiency rather than an external allergen like leather.
• Educational Objective:
• The clinical presentation of stasis eczema i s a result of chronic venous insufficiency.
• Bolognia Textbook of Dermatology 4 th Edition Page No 236
explanation.ref: Rook's Textbook of Dermatology -9th Edition Page No 39.19

subject: Dermatology
question:
A 40-year-old male construction worker presents with itchy, red, and weeping plaques on his palms and forearms, as depicted in the image. He reports that the rash developed shortly after he started working with wet cement on a new project. He denies any known allergies or atopic history. On examination, the skin lesions are erythematous, with vesicles and some crusting, localized to areas of contact with cement. What is the most appropriate next step in the management of this patient?

[Image: https://cerebellum-web-static.s3.amazonaws.com/media/public/images/2024/02/21/picture86.jpg]
options: Perform Patch Test to confirm the Diagnosis

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. Check IgE levels - Incorrect.
• Elevated IgE levels are associated with atopic dermatitis and allergies , but this does not have any role in management of the given patient.
• Option B. Perform patch test to confirm the diagnosis - Incorrect.
• While a patch test is useful in identifying specific allergens in allergic contact dermatitis, the immediate management should focus on alleviating the patient's symptoms and preventing further skin damage. Patch test should NOT be performed when active lesions are present.
• Option C. Start with Antihistamines - Incorrect.
• Antihistamines can be used to alleviate itching but do NOT address the underlying inflammation , which is the primary pathology in contact dermatitis. Therefore, steroids are more appropriate for initial management.
• Educational Objective:
• The management of contact dermatitis , particularly in occupational settings like construction work where cement exposure is common. Treatment with topical steroids should be initiated to reduce inflammation and provide symptomatic relief . Medical students should be aware that while patch testing is useful for diagnosis, the immediate treatment of the dermatitis should NOT be delayed for testing.
• Bolognia Textbook of Dermatology 4 th Edition Page No 243, 254
explanation.ref: Rook's Textbook of Dermatology -9th Edition Page No 128.22, 128.41

subject: Dermatology
question:
A 55-year-old male patient presents to the ENT outpatient department for a routine dressing. Shortly after the dressing is applied, he develops an erythematous and oozing reaction around his ear, as shown in the accompanying image. The patient denies previous similar reactions. The dressing material contained povidone-iodine, which the patient has been exposed for the first time now. What is TRUE about the patient's reaction to the dressing change?

[Image: https://cerebellum-web-static.s3.amazonaws.com/media/public/images/2024/02/21/picture88_1jgmtl3.jpg]
options: Underlying principle is a Cell-Mediated Immunity
It is associated with severe burning rather than Itching
Patch Test can confirm the diagnosis once the Acute Phase is managed

explanation.short: Ans. B) It is associated with severe burning rather than Itching
explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. The underlying principle is cell-mediated immunity - Incorrect.
• This would be true for allergic contact dermatitis , not irritant contact dermatitis, which is a non-immune direct toxic effect of the irritant on the skin .
• Option C. Memory T cells are involved - Incorrect.
• Memory T cells are involved in allergic reactions , not in the non-immunologic reactions of ICD.
• Option D. Patch testing can confirm the diagnosis once the acute phase is managed - Incorrect.
• Patch testing is used to diagnose allergic contact dermatitis, not ICD. However, it may still be used if an allergic component is suspected after the acute phase of ICD .
• Educational Objective:
• To distinguish between irritant contact dermatitis and allergic contact dermatitis based on symptoms and underlying mechanisms. ICD is characterized by a burning sensation and results from direct toxicity , not an immune-mediated response.
• Bolognia Textbook of Dermatology 4 th Edition Page No 268
explanation.ref: Rook's Textbook of Dermatology -9th Edition Page No 129.3

subject: Dermatology
question:
A 38-year-old man presents to the dermatology clinic with a six-month history of multiple asymptomatic, pigmented moles over his trunk and limbs. He also mentions a recent change in one of the moles, which now has a reddish halo and very mild itching around it. There is no history of similar skin changes in the family, and he denies any systemic symptoms. On examination, the mole is 3 mm in diameter with a symmetrical border, uniform color, and an erythematous, eczematous halo surrounding it as shown in the image below. There is no palpable lymphadenopathy, and the rest of the skin examination is unremarkable. What is the most appropriate next step in the management of this patient?

[Image: https://cerebellum-web-static.s3.amazonaws.com/media/public/images/2024/02/21/picture89_PmbJJTA.jpg]
options: Urgent Referral for Excision of the Mole
Immediate Full Blood Count and Biochemistry Panel
Prescribe a Potent Topical Steroid for the Eczematous Halo

explanation.short: Ans. B) Reassurance and Routine Follow-Up
explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. Urgent referral for excision of the mole - Incorrect.
• The urgent excision would be indicated if there were signs suggestive of melanoma , which are not present in this scenario.
• Option C. Immediate full blood count and biochemistry panel - Incorrect.
• There is no indication from the history or clinical findings that suggest systemic disease; therefore, these tests are not warranted.
• Option D. Prescribe a potent topical steroid for the eczematous halo - Incorrect.
• While topical steroids could be used to treat the eczematous halo if symptomatic , the most appropriate next step is reassurance and routine follow-up , as the Meyerson phenomenon is benig n.
• Educational Objective:
• Meyerson phenomenon , a benign reaction characterized by an eczematous halo around a nevus, and to understand that r eassurance and routine dermatological follow-up are appropriate management.
• Bolognia Textbook of Dermatology 4 th Edition Page No 566
explanation.ref: Rook's Textbook of Dermatology -9th Edition Page No 39.27

subject: Dermatology
question: After administering the patch test to a 10-year-old boy with a history of allergic contact dermatitis, when should you schedule his return for patch removal?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. 24 hours
• This time frame is too short for most delayed-type hypersensitivity reactions to occur. Removing the patches at 24 hours would likely result in missing many positive reactions .
• Option B. 72 hours –
• Option B. 72 hours
• It is not typically when the patches are first removed. The patches are usually removed at 48 hours , and the skin is then inspected for any reactions.
• Option D. 96 hours
• This is a common time for a follow-up reading and not the initial removal of the patches. Some protocols suggest readings at this time to ensure that late-appearing reactions are not missed.
• The image below shows a patch test:
• Educational Objective:
• In patch testing , the patch is removed after 48 hours to examine the site of reaction.
• 48 hours: Patch removal for initial reading to detect early hypersensitivity reactions 72-96 hours: Final reading done for confirmation and for detecting late reactions .
• Fitzpatrick’s Dermatology 9 th Edition Page No 406
explanation.ref: Rooks Textbook of Dermatology - 9 th Edition Volume I Chapter 4 Page No 4.23

subject: Dermatology
question: Topical Steroids are most effective in:
options: Vesicular Lesions due to Herpes Simplex Infection

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A . Dermal atrophy
• Dermal atrophy is actually a side effect of prolonged or inappropriate use of topical steroids , not a condition that is treated with them. It is thinning of the skin which can lead to transparency , easy bruising , and other skin changes as shown below.
• Option C . Vesicular lesions due to Herpes simplex infection
• Topical steroids are contraindicated in herpes simplex infections because they can exacerbate the condition by suppressing the local immune response, which is necessary to control viral infections.
• Herpes labialis: Vesicular lesions due to Herpes simplex
• Option D . Rosacea
• Topical steroids may temporarily reduce inflammation associated with rosacea, but their use is generally avoided as they can lead to a worsening of the condition over time, particularly by exacerbating flushing and potentially leading to a condition known as steroid-induced rosacea.
• Educational Objective:
• Topical steroids are effective in treating eczematous dermatitis due to their anti-inflammatory properties, but they should not be used for conditions like herpes simplex infections or rosacea and can cause dermal atrophy if used inappropriately.
explanation.ref: Fitzpatrick’s Dermatology, 9 th Edition Page Number 377

subject: Dermatology
question:
The possible diagnosis for a child whose mother had a history of bronchial asthma & child developed itchy plaques bilaterally over the popliteal fossa, & cubital fossa (as shown below) could be?

[Image: https://cerebellum-web-static.s3.amazonaws.com/media/public/images/2024/02/21/picture95.jpg]

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option B. Dermatitis Herpetiformis:
• It is frequently observed in individuals in their 40s . This is an intensely pruritic condition predominantly affecting extensor surfaces of the body , rather than the flexures and associated with gluten sensitivity and is characterized by grouped vesicles on an erythematous base . It is not related to a history of atopy.
• Option C. Pemphigus Vegetans:
• A variant of pemphigus vulgaris , pemphigus vegetans presents with vegetating plaques , usually in the intertriginous areas . It is not associated with a familial history of atopy and does not predominantly affect the popliteal and cubital fossae.
• Psoriasis is a chronic autoimmune skin condition characterized by well-demarcated, scaly plaques . While psoriasis can affect any area of the body but more commonly extensor involvement is seen, it does not typically present as pruritic plaques in the popliteal and cubital fossae in association with a history of atopy.
• Educational Objective:
• A child with a familial history of atopy presenting with itchy plaques over the popliteal and cubital f ossae is most likely suffering from atopic dermatitis.
• Rooks Textbook of Dermatology- 9 th Edition Volume 3 Chapter 149 Page No 149.21
explanation.ref: Rooks Textbook of Dermatology- 9 th Edition Volume 2 Chapter 41 Page No 41.1, 41.2, 41.13-19,

subject: Dermatology
question: Case scenario: A 45-year-old woman presents to the clinic with a two-week history of itchy, red, scaly patches on her hands. She reports that she has been using a new brand of hand lotion since 2 weeks. Physical examination reveals erythematous, lichenified plaques on the dorsal aspects of both hands. She has no significant medical history and is currently not taking any medications. Question: The dermatologist decides to perform an investigation to determine the cause of the patient's contact dermatitis. Which of the following statements is CORRECT regarding the investigation likely to be used in this scenario?
options: Documents Delayed type of Hypersensitivity Reaction

explanation.short: Ans. D) All of the above
explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. Documents Delayed type of hypersensitivity reaction - Correct.
• The patch test identifies delayed-type hypersensitivity reactions to specific allergens, which are mediated by T-cells and occur hours to days after exposure.
• Option B. Investigation of choice in ACD - Correct.
• The patch test is the standard method for identifying the causative allergen(s) in ACD , helping to establish the diagnosis.
• Option C. Documents Type 4 hypersensitivity reaction - Correct.
• Type 4 hypersensitivity is a delayed-type reaction mediated by T-cells, which the patch test is designed to document.
• Educational Objective:
• The patch test is used to document Type 4 hypersensitivity reactions and is the investigation of choice for diagnosing allergic contact dermatitis. It is an important tool for medical students to be familiar with, as it assists in identifying the specific allergens responsible for a patient's dermatitis, which is crucial for effective management and prevention of recurrence.
explanation.ref: Rooks Textbook of Dermatology- 9 th Edition Page No 128.64

subject: Dermatology
question:
A 24-year-old woman presents to the clinic with a skin condition that has been present for several months. She reports that whenever she scratches her skin, even lightly, she develops raised, red lines that are itchy and last for about 30 minutes to an hour. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

[Image: https://cerebellum-web-static.s3.amazonaws.com/media/public/images/2024/02/21/picture98.jpg]

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. Contact Dermatitis:
• This condition usually presents with red, itchy rashes at the site of contact with an irritant or allergen . However, it does not typically produce raised lines or welts that appear and disappear so quickly after scratching.
• Psoriasis can manifest as raised, red, scaly plaques on the skin .
• It does not generally present with rapid onset of transient lines or welts following scratching.
• Option D. Atopic Dermatitis:
• Also known as eczema, atopic dermatitis typically presents with itchy, red, and inflamed skin . While scratching can worsen the condition, it does not lead to the immediate appearance of raised lines or welts characteristic of Dermatographic urticaria.
• Educational Objective:
• Dermatographic urticaria is the likely diagnosis in patients who develop transient, raised, red lines on the skin following minor physical trauma such as scratching. This condition is characterized by the appearance of wheals or welts in response to physical stimuli .
• Bolognia Textbook of Dermatology 4 th Edition Page No 309
explanation.ref: Rook's Textbook of Dermatology - Volume II-9th Edition Chapter 42, Page No 42.1-4, 42.8-10

subject: Dermatology
question: Identify the INCORRECT match related to types of icthyosis & their characteristic features among the following options?
options: X-Linked Recessive Ichthyosis - Steroid Sulfatase Deficiency
Autosomal Recessive Congenital Ichthyosis - Lamellar Ichthyosis
Harlequin Icthyosis - Mutations of the ABCA12 Genes

explanation.short: Ans. D) Collodion membrane- Icthyosis Vulgaris
explanation.detail:
• Educational Objective:
• A collodion membrane at birth is associated with lamellar ichthyosis , part of the group of autosomal recessive congenital ichthyoses , and not with ichthyosis vulgaris.
explanation.ref: Rook's Textbook of Dermatology 9th edition Page no. 65.4 to 65.10

subject: Dermatology
question:
CT brain shows tram-track cortical calcifications as shown in the below image in which of the following diseases?

[Image: https://cerebellum-web-static.s3.amazonaws.com/media/public/images/2023/05/31/untitled-92.jpg]

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. Von Hippel-Lindau syndrome :
• It is characterized by hemangioblastomas of the central nervous system and retina, renal cell carcinoma , pheochromocytoma , and other tumors, not by tram-track calcifications on brain imaging.
• Option C. Tuberous sclerosis :
• It is typically associated with cortical tubers, sub-ependymal nodules , and sub-ependymal giant cell astrocytomas , not the tram-track calcifications seen in Sturge-Weber syndrome.
• It is a condition associated with Cerebellar Ataxia, Oculocutaneous Telangiectasias, Immunodeficiency , and increased risk for Malignancies , but not with the type of calcifications seen in Sturge-Weber syndrome.
• Educational Objective:
• Tram-Track Cortical Calcifications on a CT brain scan are indicative of Sturge-Weber Syndrome .
• Rook's Textbook of Dermatology - Volume II - 9th Edition Chapter 73, Page No 73.2
• Rook's Textbook of Dermatology - 9th Edition - Volume III Chapter 116 Page Nos 116.1-116.10
explanation.ref: Journal: https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC4225308/

subject: Dermatology
question:
A young girl having low intelligence presented as shown below. There is history of epilepsy. Diagnosis?

[Image: https://cerebellum-web-static.s3.amazonaws.com/media/public/images/2023/05/31/untitled-94.jpg]

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Neurofibromatosis typically presents with café-au-lait spots, neurofibromas, and Lisch nodules , not the facial angiofibromas seen in the image, which are commonly associated with tuberous sclerosis.
• Option C. Systemic Lupus Erythematosus (SLE) :
• SLE is an autoimmune condition characterized by a broad spectrum of clinical presentations including malar rash, photosensitivity, oral ulcers, and systemic involvement, but not the specific facial lesions shown in the image.
• Option D. Systemic Sclerosis :
• Systemic sclerosis ( scleroderma ) is associated with skin thickening, fibrosis , and in some cases, t elangiectasia , but not with the facial angiofibromas and neurological symptoms seen in tuberous sclerosis.
• Educational Objective:
• The clinical triad of Facial Angiofibromas, Epilepsy, and Cognitive Impairment is indicative of Tuberous Sclerosis .
explanation.ref: Rook's Textbook of Dermatology 9th Edition Chapter 80, Page Nos 80.10-80.11

subject: Dermatology
question: Identify the INCORRECT statement regarding Neurofibromatosis-I (NF-I) among the given options?
options: Lisch's Nodules are an important Ocular Manifestation
Ash Leaf Macules can be seen

explanation.short: Ans. B) Ash Leaf Macules can be seen
explanation.detail:
• Explanation
• Neurofibromatosis-I (NF-I) / Von Recklinghausen's disease: Autosomal dominant disorder. For diagnosis of NF-I , two or more of the following should be present: 6 or more café-au-lait Spots 2 or more Neurofibromas or one Plexiform Neuroma Freckling i n the axillary or inguinal region 2 or more Lisch nodules Dysplasia of the sphenoidal or tibial bones First degree relative with NF1 Optic Gliomas Neurofibromas:
• Option A: Lisch Nodules
• Lisch Nodules: These are Hamartomas which are melanocytic , and they are typically brown or yellow in color . They are typically visible with the unaided eye and appear as rounded elevations on the iris surface. Slit-lamp examination , however, provides the most accurate evaluation. They do not produce visual impairment. The image below shows Lisch's nodules , the most common ocular manifestation of neurofibromatosis type 1.
• Optic Glioma in NF-1
• Option C : Axillary Freckling
• The image below shows neurofibromatosis: Axillary Freckling (Crowes sign ) and Café-au-lait spots.
• Option D: Buttonhole Sign
• Buttonhole Sign is seen in type 1 neurofibromatosis. Buttonhole Sign: Lesion gets invaginated by finger pressure (On applying pressure the lesion goes back in the skin and comes out on removing the pressure.
• This sign is also positive in anetoderma and dermatofibroma.
• Educational Objective:
• While Lisch Nodules , Axillary Freckling , and the Button-Hole Sign are associated with Neurofibromatosis-I , Ash Leaf Macules are not a feature of NF-I and instead are indicative of Tuberous Sclerosis Complex .
explanation.ref: Rook's Textbook of Dermatology - 9th Edition - Volume III Chapter 80 Page Nos 80.1-80.5

subject: Dermatology
question: Identify the FALSE statement regarding Ichthyosis vulgaris?

explanation.detail:
• Educational Objective:
• Ichthyosis vulgaris is an autosomal dominant disorder associated with a defect in filaggrin protein , presenting with hyperlinear palms and fish-like scales .
explanation.ref: Rook's Textbook of Dermatology - 9th Edition - Volume I, Chapter 65 Page No 65.2, 65.4, 65.5

subject: Dermatology
question:
A patient presented with Multiple Soft Nodules and Hyperpigmented Macules as shown in the picture. Which of the following statement is CORRECT regarding the disease?

[Image: https://cerebellum-web-static.s3.amazonaws.com/media/public/images/2024/04/18/image_nnNqFx7.png]
options: Freckling should be checked in the Groin
It is due to a Mutation in Neurofibromin on Chromosome 18
Lisch Nodules can cause Vision Loss in this patient
3 or more Café-au-lait Macules should be present to confirm the Diagnosis

explanation.short: Freckling should be checked in the Groin
explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option B. It is due to a mutation in neurofibromin on chromosome 18 :
• The mutation associated with NF1 is in the NF1 gene on chromosome 17 , not chromosome 18. This gene is responsible for producing neurofibromin, a protein that suppresses tumour growth .
• Option C. Lisch nodules can cause vision loss in this patient :
• Lisch nodules, which are iris hamartomas found in NF1, are generally asymptomatic and do not typically result in vision loss .
• They are, however, useful in the diagnosis of NF1 .
• Option D. 3 or more café-au-lait macules should be present to confirm the diagnosis :
• The diagnostic criteria for NF1 require six or more café-au-lait macules of a specific size, depending on the age of the patient ( over 5 mm in diameter pre-puberty and over 15 mm in diameter post-puberty ).
• Educational Objective:
• The diagnosis of neurofibromatosis type 1 (NF1) can be supported by the presence of inguinal or axillary freckling , in addition to other criteria such as six or more café-au-lait macules .
• Bolognia Textbook of Dermatology 4 th Edition Page No 985
explanation.ref: Rooks Textbook of Dermatology- 9 th Edition Page No 80.3

subject: Dermatology
question: All of the following statements are correct regarding Neuro-Fibromatosis Type 2 EXCEPT?
options: Affected Gene is present on Chromosome 22

explanation.short: Ans. C) Hamartomas in Kidney are seen
explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. It has Autosomal dominant inheritance :
• Neurofibromatosis Type 2 (NF2) is an autosomal dominant disorder characterized by the development of bilateral vestibular schwannomas, meningiomas, ependymomas , and other nervous system tumors.
• Option B. Axillary Freckling is absent :
• Axillary freckling is a feature of Neurofibromatosis type 1 (NF1), not NF2. NF2 has different clinical manifestations, primarily related to the central nervous system.
• Option D. Affected gene is present on Chromosome 22 :
• The gene associated with NF2 is located on chromosome 22 and is responsible for producing a protein called Merlin ( or Schwannomin ) that suppresses Tumors.
• Educational Objective:
• Renal Hamartomas are not associated with Neurofibromatosis type 2 ( NF2) but are a feature of Tuberous Sclerosis Complex (TSC). NF2 is characterized by Bilateral Vestibular Schwannomas and other CNS Tumors , with the Affected Gene located on Chromosome 22.
explanation.ref: Bolognia Textbook of Dermatology 4 th Edition Page No 993

subject: Dermatology
question: Which of the following icthyosis presents with a Collodion membrane?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. Ichthyosis Vulgaris :
• Ichthyosis Vulgaris typically presents with fine scaling , particularly on the extensor surfaces of the limbs , without a collodion membrane at birth. It is the most common form of ichthyosis and is often associated with atopy .
• Option B. X-Linked Recessive Ichthyosis :
• This condition presents with large, dark scales and is seen in males due to its X-linked inheritance . It does not typically present with a collodion membrane at birth.
• Option C. Bullous Ichthyosiform Erythroderma :
• This condition, also known as epidermolytic hyperkeratosis , does present at birth , it is characterized by widespread erythema, blistering, and erosions rather than a collodion membrane.
• Educational Objective:
• Lamellar Ichthyosis as the ichthyosis subtype that presents with a collodion membrane at birth, which is a tight, shiny casing that is eventually shed to reveal scaly skin beneath.
explanation.ref: Rooks Textbook of Dermatology- 9 th Edition Page No 65.7

subject: Dermatology
question:
A child was born with membranes around the body and had ectropion and eclabium. He is brought to the OPD with lesions covering his face, trunk, and extremities. Which of the following is an unlikely diagnosis?

[Image: https://cerebellum-web-static.s3.amazonaws.com/media/public/images/2024/02/23/screenshot-2024-02-23-140028.jpg]

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option B. Lamellar Ichthyosis
• Description: Infants with lamellar ichthyosis are often born with a collodion membrane , which sheds after a few weeks . The skin then becomes darkly pigmented with large, plate-like scales . Ectropion and eclabium can also be present.
• Option C . Bathing Suit Ichthyosis
• Description: This is a rare type of ichthyosis where the scaling is restricted to the trunk , sparing the extremities and face . It's called "Bathing Suit " due to the distribution pattern resembling the Area covered by a Bathing Suit.
• Option D . Harlequin Ichthyosis
• Description: This is a severe form of ichthyosis , where infants are born with very thick skin that forms large , diamond-shaped plates separated by deep fissures as seen in the below image.
• Features like ectropion, eclabium , and respiratory difficulties are common.
• Autosomal Recessive Congenital Ichthyosis (ARCI) includes the following:
• Lamellar Ichthyosis (LI) Bathing Suit Ichthyosis (BSI) Harlequin Ichthyosis (HI) Congenital Ichthyosiform Erythroderma (CIE) Self-Limiting Congenital Ichthyisis (SLCI) Collodion Baby
• Educational Objective:
• Ichthyosis vulgaris is an unlikely diagnosis in a new-born presenting with a Collodion Membrane, Ectropion, Eclabium , and Extensive Skin Lesions, given that Ichthyosis Vulgaris is typically a Milder Form and less likely to present with such severe symptoms at Birth.
explanation.ref: Rook's Textbook of Dermatology - 9th Edition - Volume I, Chapter 65 Page No 65.4, 65.6-65.10

subject: Dermatology
question:
An infant presented with generalized dry skin and fish-like scales, which appeared 3 months after birth. The child's mother also reports that the infant seems to be uncomfortable in hot environments. On physical examination, the palms display accentuated skin markings as shown below. What additional finding is likely to be present in this patient?

[Image: https://cerebellum-web-static.s3.amazonaws.com/media/public/images/2024/02/23/screenshot-2024-02-23-140558.jpg]

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Erythroderma, which is a generalized redness of the skin, is not typically a finding associated with Ichthyosis Vulgaris. It can be seen in other more severe forms of ichthyosis or different dermatological conditions.
• Option C. Lid Eversion :
• Lid eversion, or ectropion , is not a typical finding in Ichthyosis Vulgaris.
• Ectropion is more commonly associated with other types of ichthyosis, such as Harlequin ichthyosis.
• Option D. Undescended Testes :
• Undescended testes are associated with X Linked Recessive Ichthyosis. Undescended testes are not associated with Ichthyosis Vulgaris. This finding is not related to the condition and would prompt an evaluation for other issues , such as congenital or endocrinological abnormalities.
• Educational Objective:
• Ichthyosis Vulgaris is commonly associated with Keratosis Pilaris , which presents as Small, Rough Bumps on the Upper Arms and T highs , along with the characteristic Dry Skin with Fish-Like Scales and increased Skin Markings on the palms.
explanation.ref: Rooks Textbook of Dermatology- 9 th Edition Page No 65.4

subject: Dermatology
