question: A 63-year-old man with a history of hyperlipidaemia presents to the dermatology clinic with a 6-month history of progressively worsening dry skin and scaling. He describes the scales as resembling fish skin and reports they are most prominent on his lower legs. His medications include atorvastatin, which was started approximately 7 months ago. He denies any history of similar skin conditions in himself or his family. Physical examination reveals diffuse, fine, white scaling on the lower extremities without erythema. The rest of the examination is unremarkable. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Psoriasis typically presents with well-demarcated , erythematous plaques with silvery scales and is commonly found on extensor surfaces, the scalp , and the sacral area , which is not described in this patient's presentation.
• Hypothyroidism can cause dry, coarse skin and may mimic ichthyosis , but there is no indication of other symptoms of hypothyroidism in the patient's history or presentation.
• Option D. X-Linked Ichthyosis :
• X-Linked Ichthyosis is a genetic condition that typically presents in early life , not acutely in adulthood. Moreover, it is inherited in an X-linked recessive pattern, affecting primarily males , with the mother being the carrier .
• Educational Objective:
• Drug-induced acquired ichthyosis is a potential side effect of statin therapy , particularly when the skin changes develop after the initiation of the medication and there is no prior personal or family history of similar skin conditions.
explanation.ref: Rooks Textbook of Dermatology- 9 th Edition Page No 87.1

subject: Dermatology
question: A 2-year-old boy is brought to the clinic due to a seizure. He has a history of a red-purplish discoloration on his face since birth. His mother reports that he has been experiencing episodes of eye redness and tearing, which an ophthalmologist diagnosed as increased intraocular pressure. A brain MRI is ordered, which shows gyriform calcifications. Which of the following statements is INCORRECT regarding the suspected diagnosis?
options: Tram Track Calcifications seen on MRI Brain
Bilateral Port Wine Stain seen over Face

explanation.short: Bilateral Port Wine Stain seen over Face
explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. Tram Track calcifications seen on MRI Brain :
• In Sturge-Weber syndrome, MRI of the brain can show "tram track" calcifications that correspond to the areas of Leptomeningeal Angioma.
• MRI axial T2-weighted fast-field echo sequence showing subcortical calcifications with characteristic ‘tram-track’-like appearance involving the left frontal and parietal regions .
• Option B. Seizures seen :
• Seizures are a common neurological manifestation in Sturge-Weber syndrome due to the involvement of leptomeningeal angiomas.
• Option C. Glaucoma is an Eye finding :
• Glaucoma is a common ocular finding in Sturge-Weber syndrome, often caused by increased episcleral venous pressure due to associated angiomas.
• Educational Objective:
• In Sturge-Weber syndrome , the port wine stain is typically unilateral and associated with "tram track" calcifications on brain MRI, seizures, and glaucoma , distinguishing it from other neurocutaneous disorders.
explanation.ref: Rooks’ Textbook of Dermatology- 9 th Edition Page No 73.2

subject: Dermatology
question:
Match the following Genodermatoses in Column A with Associated Symptoms / Signs in Column B:

[Image: https://cerebellum-web-static.s3.amazonaws.com/media/public/images/2024/02/23/screenshot-2024-02-23-142818.jpg]

explanation.detail:
• Explanation
• 1. Neurofibromatosis-1 (c Crowe’s sign) :
• Neurofibromatosis type 1 (NF1), also known as von Recklinghausen disease , can present with Crowe’s sign , which is axillary or inguinal freckling , one of the diagnostic criteria for NF1.
• 2. Sturge-Weber Syndrome (a Glaucoma) :
• Sturge-Weber syndrome is associated with glaucoma due to elevated episcleral venous pressure . It is a neurocutaneous disorder characterized by a facial port-wine stain , leptomeningeal angiomas , and ocular abnormalities.
• 3. Bourneville’s Disease (b Ash Leaf Macules) :
• Bourneville’s disease, also known as Tuberous Sclerosis Complex (TSC ), is associated with ash leaf macules , which are hypopigmented lesions typically found on the trunk and extremities .
• 4. Neurofibromatosis-2 (d Bilateral Acoustic Neuromas) :
• Neurofibromatosis type 2 (NF2) is associated with Bilateral Acoustic Neuromas (Vestibular Schwannomas ), which are Benign Tumors that affect the nerve responsible for hearing and balance .
• Educational Objective:
• Genodermatosis with its associated symptom/sign: Neurofibromatosis-1 with Crowe’s sign , Sturge-Weber syndrome with glaucoma , Bourneville’s Disease with ash leaf macules , and Neurofibromatosis-2 with bilateral acoustic neuromas .
explanation.ref: Rooks Textbook of Dermatology- 9 th Edition Page No 73.2, 78.2, 80.3, 80.11

subject: Dermatology
question:
A 24-year-old female presented with lesions over her fingers since past 10 years as shown in the image below. What is the most likely diagnosis associated with these nail changes and which of the following chromosomes can be affected in this condition?

[Image: https://cerebellum-web-static.s3.amazonaws.com/media/public/images/2024/02/23/picture6_MpJUXzZ.jpg]

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. Neurofibromatosis -1 and 18, 22 :
• The cutaneous manifestations of Neurofibromatosis Type 1 (NF1) include café-au-lait spots , Neurofibromas , and Axillary Freckling , not periungual fibromas, and the NF1 gene is located on chromosome 17 , not 18.
• Option B. Bourneville’s Disease and 9, 22 :
• TSC is associated with Bourneville’s disease; the affected genes are TSC1 on chromosome 9 and TSC2 on chromosome 16 , not 22.
• Option C. Neurofibromatosis -1 and 17, 22 :
• The nail changes shown are not typically seen with NF1, which is associated with chromosome 17 . NF2 is associated with chromosome 22 and is characterized by Bilateral Acoustic Neuromas.
• Educational Objective:
• Periungual Fibromas is a sign of Tuberous Sclerosis Complex (TSC) , which is associated with mutations in the TSC1 gene on chromosome 9 and the TSC2 gene on chromosome 16.
explanation.ref: Rooks Textbook of Dermatology- 9 th Edition Page No 80.10

subject: Dermatology
question: A patient had irregular hyperpigmented patches on the trunk and multiple small hyperpigmented macules in the axillae and groins since early childhood. There were no other skin lesions. Which is the most likely investigation to support the diagnosis?

explanation.short: Ans. D) Slit Lamp Examination of Eye
explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. Retinal Artery Angiography :
• This investigation is used to visualize the blood vessels in the retina , not the iris, and is not typically indicated for the diagnosis of NF1.
• Option B. Examination of Fundus :
• For the specific diagnosis of NF1 , as the fundus examination focuses on the internal surfaces of the eye , such as the retina , and would not typically reveal Lisch nodules.
• Option C. Measurement of Intraocular Tension :
• This test is used primarily for the diagnosis and monitoring of glaucoma , which is not a direct diagnostic feature of NF1.
• Educational Objective:
• Slit lamp examination is crucial for diagnosing Neurofibromatosis Type 1 as it allows for the visualization of Lisch nodules, which are melanocytic iris hamartomas associated with the condition.
explanation.ref: Rooks Textbook of Dermatology- 9 th Edition Page No 80.3

subject: Dermatology
question: All of the following are seen associated with Tuberous Sclerosis EXCEPT:

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. Hypomelanotic Macules :
• Hypomelanotic macules, often referred to as "Ash Leaf Spots," are a common skin finding in Tuberous Sclerosis Complex (TSC) and are usually present from birth.
• Option B. Shagreen Patch :
• A Shagreen patch is a connective tissue nevus typically found on the lower back and is another characteristic cutaneous manifestation of TSC .
• Option C. Koenen's Tumors :
• Koenen's Tumors, or periungual fibromas , are benign fibrous growths that typically appear around the nails and are commonly associated with TSC.
• Educational Objective:
• While Hypomelanotic Macules , Shagreen patches , and Koenen's Tumors are characteristic of Tuberous Sclerosis Complex , Axillary Freckles are not associated with TSC and are instead a feature of Neurofibromatosis Type 1.
explanation.ref: Rooks Textbook of Dermatology- 9 th Edition Page No 80.3

subject: Dermatology
question: Shagreen Patch is usually found in:

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Shagreen patches are not typically found on the face. Facial Angiofibromas , also known as Adenoma Sebaceum , are the more common Facial Lesions associated with Tuberous Sclerosis Complex ( TSC ).
• Option B. Cervical Region :
• Shagreen patches are classically located in the lumbosacral region rather than the cervical region .
• Shagreen patches are usually not found on the limbs. Hypomelanotic macules or ash leaf spots can be found on the limbs and are associated with TSC.
• Educational Objective:
• Shagreen Patches are typically located in the Lumbosacral Region and are a major diagnostic feature of Tuberous Sclerosis Complex .
explanation.ref: Rooks Textbook of Dermatology- 9 th Edition Page No 80.11

subject: Dermatology
question: Which of the following findings is a Pathognomonic Sign of Neurofibromatosis 1?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. Café-au-lait Macules :
• Café-au-lait macules are not pathognomonic for Neurofibromatosis type 1 ( NF1 ) as they can be seen in several other conditions , including McCune-Albright syndrome and Legius syndrome.
• Option C. Shagreen Patch :
• Shagreen patches are associated with Tuberous Sclerosis Complex ( TSC ), not NF1.
• Option D. Bilateral Acoustic Neuromas :
• Bilateral Acoustic Neuromas are pathognomonic for Neurofibromatosis Type 2 ( NF2 ), not NF1.
• Educational Objective:
• Axillary Freckling (Crowe's Sign ) is a pathognomonic sign of Neurofibromatosis type 1 ( NF1 ), which is one of the diagnostic criteria for this genetic disorder.
explanation.ref: Rooks Textbook of Dermatology- 9 th Edition Page No 80.2

subject: Dermatology
question:
What is the treatment of choice for the condition shown in the image below?

[Image: https://cerebellum-web-static.s3.amazonaws.com/media/public/images/2024/02/23/screenshot-2024-02-23-142235_S1yfeEv.jpg]

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option B. Nd YAG laser :
• Nd YAG laser penetrates deeper into tissue and is typically used for vascular lesions that are resistant to PDL or for deeper vessels. However, it carries a higher risk of scarring and pigment changes compared to PDL.
• Option C. CO 2 Laser :
• CO 2 laser is primarily used for skin resurfacing and cutting , not for treating vascular lesions like port wine stains. It has a high risk of scarring and pigmentary changes when used for vascular lesions.
• Option D. Q-switched Ruby Laser :
• This type of laser is mainly used for pigmented lesions and tattoo removal , not typically for vascular lesions like port wine stains.
• Educational Objective:
• Pulsed dye laser is the treatment of choice for port wine stains due to its effectiveness and lower risk of adverse effects compared to other laser options.
explanation.ref: Rooks Textbook of Dermatology- 9 th Edition Page No 23.8

subject: Dermatology
question: ___________________ Demarcates Hair into Upper & Lower Hair Follicles?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. Stem Cells :
• They are primarily located in the bulge region of the hair follicle, play a crucial role in the regeneration and growth cycle of the hair but do not serve as a demarcating boundary between the upper and lower segments of the hair follicle .
• Option B . Huxley’s Layer:
• It is part of the inner root sheath and is involved in the structural support and shaping of the growing hair shaft . It does not demarcate the hair follicle into upper and lower segments.
• Huxley’s & Henley’s layers are part of Inner Root Sheath (IRS)
• As can be seen in the below image.
• Option D. Adamson’s fringe:
• This refers to the point where the inner root sheath disintegrates and the hair shaft emerges, marking the transition from the non - keratinized to the keratinized part of the hair shaft within the follicle . It is not considered a demarcation point between the upper and lower hair follicle.
• Educational Objective:
• Arrector pili muscle's insertion point demarcates the boundary between the upper and lower segments of the hairfollicle .
• Rooks Textbook of Dermatology - 9 th Edition Chapter 89 Page No 89.3
explanation.ref: Rooks Textbook of Dermatology - 9 th Edition Chapter 89 Page No 89.3

subject: Dermatology
question: Identify the FALSE statement among the following statements regarding Hair Cycle?
options: Anagen is the period of Active Growth of Hair
80-90% of Hair is in Anagen at One Point of Time
Kenogen is a State of Latency

explanation.short: Ans. C) Telogen Lasts for 3 Years.
explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Anagen is indeed the active growth phase of hair follicles and can last several years .
• Option B . Anagen.
• At any given time, about 80-90% of hair follicles are in the anagen phase , which can last from two to six years or longer for scalp hair .
• Option D . Kenogen:
• It is a phase not always recognized in the literature but refers to the period of the hair cycle where the hair follicle remains empty after hair shedding and before the start of a new anagen phase . This phase is essentially a gap or a latency period in the hair cycle and is characterized by the absence of a hair shaft in the follicle .
• Educational Objective:
• Telogen is the resting phase of the hair cycle, typically lasting about 1 to 4 months , not 3 years, while anagen is the active growth phase , and the majority of hair is in this phase at any point in time. Kenogen represents a state of latency in the hair cycle.
• Rooks Textbook of Dermatology - 9 th Edition Chapter 89 Page No 89.6-8
explanation.ref: Rooks Textbook of Dermatology - 9 th Edition Chapter 89 Page No 89.6-8

subject: Dermatology
question: Identify the INCORRECT statement regarding Types of Hair?
options: Melanin is Absent in Vellus Hair
Vellus Hair Depth extends to the Dermis
Medulla is Thicker in Vellus Hair

explanation.short: Ans. C) Medulla is Thicker in Vellus Hair
explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. Melanin is absent in vellus hair: Correct
• Vellus hair is fine , light-colored , and non-pigmented , which contrasts with terminal hair that contains melanin .
• Option B. Vellus hair depth extends to the dermis: Correct
• This is true as the follicles of vellus hair are present in the dermis , although they are not as deep as the follicles of terminal hair.
• Option D. Lanugo hair is non-medullated: Correct
• Lanugo hair, which is the fine hair that covers the fetus and is usually shed before or shortly after birth, typically does not have a medulla. The term "lanugo" describes the delicate , soft , non medullated, and typically unpigmented hair of the prenatal stage . Normally, between the eighth and ninth month of pregnancy , it is shed in utero . Postnatal hair is of two kinds : Vellus & Terminal
• Educational Objective:
• Vellus hair is non-pigmented and lacks a medulla , whereas the medulla is found in thicker terminal hairs . Lanugo hair is also non-medullated .
explanation.ref: Rooks Textbook of Dermatology - 9 th Edition Chapter 89 Page No 89.2

subject: Dermatology
question: Which among the following is the primary target of action of androgen in the hair follicle?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A . Hair Follicle Epithelium:
• While androgens influence the hair follicle as a whole , the primary and direct target is not the epithelium, but rather the dermal papillae where the androgen receptors are most concentrated .
• The hair bulb , which is the lower part of the hair follicle containing the hair matrix , is influenced by the dermal papillae . However, it is not the primary site of androgen action.
• This is the upper part of the hair follicle that extends from the skin surface to the opening of the sebaceous gland . Androgens do not primarily target the infundibulum in altering hair growth.
• Educational Objective:
• The dermal papillae are the primary target of androgen action in the hair follicle , playing a crucial role in hair growth cycles and conditions such as androgenetic alopecia .
• Rooks Textbook of Dermatology - 9 th Edition Chapter 89 Page No 89.4 & 89.5
explanation.ref: Rooks Textbook of Dermatology - 9 th Edition Chapter 89 Page No 89.4 & 89.5

subject: Dermatology
question: A 57-year-old lady diagnosed with Skin malignancy & she underwent chemotherapy, presents with hair loss after 3 weeks of starting of chemotherapy. Identify the INCORRECT statement about this condition?
options: This is called as Anagen Effluvium
It is an example for Diffuse Hair Loss
Hair does not regrow once the Offending Agent is Stopped
Hair Loss finished within 1-2 Months of Chemotherapy.

explanation.short: Ans. C) Hair does not regrow once the Offending Agent is Stopped.
explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A . This condition is known as Anagen effluvium. Correct:
• It is hair loss that occurs during the anagen or growth phase of the hair cycle, commonly induced by chemotherapy. Hair regrows once offending chemotherapy agent is stopped . It is due to the anagen, or proliferating phase of the hair cycle being adversely affected . Following the start of chemotherapy , hair loss usually starts within 1-3 week s and is finished within 1-2 months.
• Option B . It is an example for Diffuse Hair Loss. Correct :
• It is indeed an example of Diffuse hair loss , where hair loss occurs uniformly across the scalp , often because of systemic treatment like chemotherapy .
• Option D . Hair loss finished within 1-2 months of chemotherapy. Correct :
• It typically occurs rapidly after the start of chemotherapy and can continue for a short period after the therapy has concluded .
• Educational Objective:
• Chemotherapy- induced hair loss , or Anagen effluvium , is a type of Diffuse hair loss that generally resolves after the completion of chemotherapy , with hair regrowth occurring over time.
explanation.ref: Rooks Textbook of Dermatology - 9th Edition Chapter 89 Page No 89.7, 89.48

subject: Dermatology
question:
A 38-year-old female presented with heavy hair loss for 4 months. She gives a history of suffering with COVID 19, 8 months back. What is the likely diagnosis?

[Image: https://cerebellum-web-static.s3.amazonaws.com/media/public/images/2024/02/23/picture12.jpg]

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option B. Androgenetic Alopecia . Incorrect:
• It is characterized by a patterned hair loss and is a genetic condition . It's less likely to be associated with a recent illness.
• Option C. Alopecia Areata . Incorrect:
• It usually presents with patchy hair loss and is an autoimmune disorder . The hair loss is not typically diffuse as described in the scenario.
• Option D. Trichotillomania. Incorrect:
• It is a hair-pulling disorder , which typically results in irregular areas of broken hairs of varying length , usually not consistent with a history of illness.
• Educational Objective:
• Telogen effluvium is a likely diagnosis in patients presenting with diffuse hair loss following a significant stressor such as illness , in this case, COVID-19 . This condition usually resolves spontaneously as the effects of the stressor diminish .
• Rooks Textbook of Dermatology - 9th Edition Chapter 89 Page No 89.25
• Fitzpatrick’s Dermatology 9th Edition Page 1509
explanation.ref: Rooks Textbook of Dermatology - 9th Edition Chapter 89 Page No 89.25

subject: Dermatology
question: A patient presented with patchy hair loss. On histological examination, perifollicular lymphocytic infiltration is seen giving swarms of Bees appearance. All the following can be seen associated with this disease EXCEPT?
options: Alopecia Totalis Denotes Loss of all Scalp Hair
Spontaneous Remission is seen in 10% of the Patients, in case of Limited Alopecia Areata.

explanation.short: Ans. C) Spontaneous remission is seen in 10% of the patients, in case of limited alopecia areata
explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A: Exclamation Mark Hair
• This refers to short extractable broken hairs which are thinner towards the base as shown in the image below
• Option B. Alopecia Totalis:
• It means loss of all scalp hair & alopecia universalis means loss of all body hair.
• Option D. Pigmented hair is more frequently affected in alopecia areata:
• Grey hair in older patients often includes both white and coloured hair . Only coloured hair will be impacted by the disease . If it advances quickly , sparing white hair , this leads to a dramatic change in overall hair colour that is called Turning white overnight.
• The following image sparing of white hair within patches of hair loss .
• Educational Objective:
• Alopecia areata is characterized by patchy hair loss , exclamation mark hair , and potential for complete scalp hair loss ( alopecia totalis ), and while spontaneous remission is common, it occurs in 80% of cases with limited disease.
• Rooks Textbook of Dermatology - 9 th Edition Chapter 89 Page No 89.28-33
explanation.ref: Rooks Textbook of Dermatology - 9 th Edition Chapter 89 Page No 89.28-33

subject: Dermatology
question: What is the initial treatment recommended for limited alopecia areata?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option B . Oral steroids:
• They are not recommended as initial therapy for limited Alopecia areata due to the potential for systemic side effects and lack of evidence for superior efficacy compared to less invasive treatments.
• It is an immunosuppressant that is generally reserved for more severe cases of Alopecia areata or when other treatments have failed, due to its side effect profile and the need for monitoring.
• Option D . Squaric Acid Dibutylester (SABDE):
• It is a topical immunotherapy that is sometimes used in Alopecia areata , but it is not considered a first-line treatment and is typically reserved for extensive cases or when other treatments have not worked.
• Educational Objective
• The initial treatment for limited Alopecia areata is intralesional steroids , which are considered first-line due to their efficacy and localized action with minimal systemic effects .
explanation.ref: Rooks Textbook of Dermatology - 9th Edition Chapter 89 Page No 89.34

subject: Dermatology
question: All of the following conditions cause Non-Cicatricial Alopecia EXCEPT?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. Alopecia Areata :
• It is an autoimmune condition that causes non-cicatricial (non-scarring) alopecia. It presents with round patches of hair loss and does not destroy the hair follicle, thus the hair can regrow.
• Option C. Secondary Syphilis:
• It can present with "moth-eaten" alopecia , which is a non-cicatricial (non- scarring ) alopecia
• The hair loss from syphilis does not typically result in permanent follicle destruction and is reversible with treatment .
• Option D. Trichotillomania (TTM):
• It is a condition caused by the compulsive pulling of one's own hair , leading to non-cicatricial ( non-scarring ) alopecia .
• Clinical Image of a patient suffering from Trichotillomania (TTM) depicting hairs of varying Lengths
• The hair loss pattern is irregular and can improve with Behavioral Interventions.
• Educational Objective
• Scleroderma is the condition associated with cicatricial ( scarring ) alopecia , while Alopecia Areata , Secondary Syphilis , and Trichotillomania are associated with non-cicatricial ( non-scarring ) alopecia .
• Rooks Textbook of Dermatology - 9th Edition Chapter 86 Page No 86.17 Chapter 89 Page No 89.41 89.45
explanation.ref: Rooks Textbook of Dermatology - 9th Edition Chapter 86 Page No 86.17 Chapter 89 Page No 89.41 89.45

subject: Dermatology
question: Which of the following is useful for Diagnosis of Tinea Capitis?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. Tzanck Smear:
• It is used for diagnosing vesicular skin diseases like herpes simplex and varicella-zoster virus infections. It is not useful for fungal infections such as tinea capitis.
• Option B. Gram Stain:
• It is used primarily to detect bacterial organisms, which are categorized as Gram-positive or Gram-negative based on their cell wall structure . It is not appropriate for the diagnosis of fungal infections like tinea capitis.
• Option C. Slit Skin Smear:
• It is commonly used for the diagnosis of leprosy by identifying acid-fast bacilli. It is not used for fungal infections.
• Educational Objective
• KOH mount is the diagnostic test of choice for tinea capitis , as it allows for the visualization of fungal hyphae and spores in hai r shafts and skin scrapings.
• Rooks Textbook of Dermatology - 9 th Edition Chapter 32 Page No 32.8
explanation.ref: Rooks Textbook of Dermatology - 9 th Edition Chapter 32 Page No 32.8

subject: Dermatology
question: Bad Prognostic Factors for Alopecia Areata are all EXCEPT?

explanation.short: Ans. A) Late Age of Onset
explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option B . Ophiasis:
• It is a pattern of Alopecia areata with hair loss in the shape of a band along the sides and lower back of the scalp and is known to be associated with a poorer prognosis .
• Option C . Alopecia Totalis:
• Where there is total hair loss of the scalp , and
• Option D. Alopecia Universalis:
• Which is hair loss of the entire body, are both considered to have a poorer prognosis compared to more limited forms of Alopecia areata .
• Educational Objective
• Early Age of Onset , Ophiasis Pattern , Alopecia Totalis , and Alopecia Universalis are associated with a poorer prognosis in Alopecia areata , whereas Late Age of Onset is generally not considered a negative prognostic factor.
• Rooks Textbook of Dermatology - 9 th Edition Chapter 89 Page No 89.28-33
explanation.ref: Rooks Textbook of Dermatology - 9 th Edition Chapter 89 Page No 89.28-33

subject: Dermatology
question: Identify the FALSE statement regarding Androgenetic Alopecia and its treatment?
options: DHT (Dihydrotestosterone) is responsible for Follicular Regression
Frontal Hairline is maintained in Female Pattern Hair Loss (FPHL)
Finasteride selectively inhibits Type 1, 5 𝛼 -reductase within the Hair Follicle

explanation.short: Ans. D) Finasteride selectively inhibits Type 1,5
explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. DHT (Di Hydro Testosterone):
• It is implicated in the pathogenesis of Androgenetic alopecia , causing progressive follicular miniaturization and regression .
• Option B. Female Pattern Hair Loss (FPHL):
• In Female pattern hair loss (FPHL) , the frontal hairline is typically maintained, which is a key feature that distinguishes it from male pattern hair loss .
• Minoxidil does have a vasodilatory function, which is thought to improve hair growth by increasing blood flow to the hair follicles . Minoxidil increases Androgen Phase & increases size of the Miniaturized hair Follicle
• Educational Objective
• Finasteride targets type 2, 5-alpha Reductase in the treatment of Androgenetic Alopecia , while Minoxidil works through vasodilation , and in FPHL , the frontal hairline remains intact .
• Rooks Textbook of Dermatology - 9 th Edition Chapter 89 Page No 89.9-17
explanation.ref: Rooks Textbook of Dermatology - 9 th Edition Chapter 89 Page No 89.9-17

subject: Dermatology
question: All of the following is the location of stem cells in the skin EXCEPT?

explanation.short: Ans. D) The Base of Apocrine Glands
explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A . The bulge region of the hair follicle:
• It is a well-characterized niche for stem cells . It houses a population of multipotent stem cells that can give rise to the various cellular lineages of the skin , including hair follicles , sebaceous glands , and epidermis .
• Option B . The basal layer of the interfollicular epidermis:
• It is another recognized site for stem cells . These cells are responsible for the continuous regeneration of the epidermis.
• Option C. The base of sebaceous glands:
• It is associated with the hair follicle , also contains stem cells . These stem cells can differentiate into sebaceous gland cells and contribute to the repair of the epidermis.
• Educational Objective:
• The locations of stem cells within the skin - Stem cells are located in the bulge of hair follicles, the basal layer of the interfollicular epidermis, and the base of sebaceous glands, but not at the base of apocrine glands.
• Rooks Textbook of Dermatology - 9 th Edition Chapter 2 Page No 2.44
explanation.ref: Rooks Textbook of Dermatology - 9 th Edition Chapter 2 Page No 2.44

subject: Dermatology
question:
A 27-Year-Old Male presents to the Dermatology Clinic with a complaint of Hair Loss over the past few weeks. He reports that the Hair Loss is Painless. On Examination, Patchy Hair Loss is observed on the Scalp, as shown in the image provided. The Exclamation Mark Sign is Positive. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

[Image: https://cerebellum-web-static.s3.amazonaws.com/media/public/images/2024/02/23/picture29.jpg]

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option B. Anagen Effluvium:
• This typically occurs due to the interruption of the active growth phase of hair follicles , often as a result of chemotherapy or radiation.
• Option C. Telogen Effluvium:
• This is characterized by diffuse hair shedding , often following a stressful event , and does not typically present with patchy hair loss.
• Option D. Androgenic Alopecia:
• It is also known as male pattern baldness , is characterized by a receding hairline and thinning of the hair on the crown . It does not present with patchy, well-demarcated areas of hair loss.
• Educational Objective:
• Alopecia Areata recognized by its classic presentation of Patchy, Well-Demarcated Areas of Hair Loss on the Scalp and a positive Exclamation Mark Sign.
• Rooks Textbook of Dermatology - 9 th Edition Chapter 89 Page No 89.28-33
explanation.ref: Rooks Textbook of Dermatology - 9 th Edition Chapter 89 Page No 89.28-33

subject: Dermatology
question: A 28-year-old patient presenting with Non-Scarring Alopecia in the Vertex Region of the Head and exhibiting histological findings of Hair Loss with haemorrhage into the Follicular Areas and Mild Perinuclear Lymphocytic Infiltrate. What is the most likely diagnosis?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A . Alopecia Areata:
• While it is characterized by non-scarring alopecia and can have l ymphocytic infiltrate around the hair follicles, the hemorrhage is not a typical feature. Generally, presents as well-defined patches of hair loss .
• Option B . Telogen Effluvium:
• It manifests as a diffuse or generalized hair shedding from the entire scalp rather than localized patches. It's commonly due to stress , certain medications , or illnesses . Histopathology would not typically show hemorrhage or the specific perinuclear infiltrate described.
• Option C. Tinea Infection:
• Tinea capitis is a fungal infection of the scalp . It usually presents with scaling and possibly broken-off hairs. Histological examination would show evidence of fungal elements ( hyphae and spores ) rather than hemorrhage or lymphocytic infiltrate .
• Educational Objective:
• In a young patient with non-scarring alopecia in the vertex region and histological findings of hair loss with follicular hemorrhage and mild lymphocytic infiltrate , the diagnosis is likely Trichotillomania .
• Fitzpatrick’s Dermatology 9 th Edition Page No: 1695
explanation.ref: Rooks Textbook of Dermatology - 9 th Edition Chapter 86 & 89, Page Nos 86.17 & 89.31

subject: Dermatology
question: An 8-year-old child presents to the dermatology outpatient department with a complaint of a painful, boggy swelling on the scalp that has developed over the past few days. On examination, the child's hair is easily pluckable, and regional lymph nodes are palpably swollen. Culture of the lesion yields growth of Trichophyton mentagrophytes. Which of the following represents the location beyond which this fungus DOES NOT typically penetrate a hair follicle in this condition?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. The Isthmus :
• It is the part of the hair follicle between the entry of the sebaceous gland duct and the arrector pili muscle insertion .
• Option B. The Infundibulum :
• It is the upper part of the hair follicle extending from the skin surface to the opening of the sebaceous gland. Fungal penetration in tinea capitis usually involves the infundibulum .
• Option D. The stem of the hair follicle :
• It includes the regions above Adamson's fringe , which are typically involved in fungal infections like tinea capitis .
• Educational Objective
• The limit of fungal penetration in the hair follicle in tinea capitis , which is typically Adamson’s fringe.
• Bolognia Textbook of Dermatology - 4 th Edition Page No 1520
• Fitzpatrick’s Dermatology 9 th Edition Page No: Fig 4.1
explanation.ref: Bolognia Textbook of Dermatology - 4 th Edition Page No 1520

subject: Dermatology
question: A 35-year-old male presents with diffuse hair thinning over the crown and frontal scalp without any signs of inflammation. The Frontal hairline is receding backwards which is prominent in the temporal area, and the exclamation mark sign is negative. Based on the presentation, which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

explanation.short: Ans. C) Androgenetic Alopecia (Male pattern Baldness)
explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. Alopecia Areata :
• It is usually characterized by patchy, non-scarring hair loss and may occasionally present with the exclamation mark sign , which is not observed in this case.
• Option B. Anagen Effluvium :
• It is typically a result of an insult to the hair follicle during the anagen phase , often from chemotherapy, which leads to widespread hair loss , not a receding hairline.
• Option D. Androgenetic Alopecia (Female pattern Baldness) :
• This generally presents with diffuse thinning over the vertex with the frontal hairline being maintained , which differs from the pattern seen in this male patient.
• Educational Objective:
• Diagnose Androgenetic Alopecia (Male pattern B aldness ) by recognizing the classic presentation of a receding frontal hairline , particularly in the temporal areas , and diffuse thinning over the crown and frontal scalp , without signs of inflammation .

• Rooks’ Textbook of Dermatology - 9 th Edition Chapter 89 Page No 89.14, 89.17
explanation.ref: Online resources

subject: Dermatology
question: A 25-year-old patient presents to the clinic with sudden, patchy hair loss over the past three months. A diagnosis of Alopecia areata is made. Which of the following findings is NOT associated with alopecia areata?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. Exclamation Mark Hair Sign:
• It is a characteristic feature often observed at the edges of alopecia patches .
• It includes a range of allergic conditions such as asthma, eczema, and allergic rhinitis, can be associated with alopecia areata.
• Option C. Nail Pitting:
• It is a common finding in patients with alopecia areata and may sometimes be the first sign of this disease . Geometrically arranged Fine, Superficial, Regular Pits
• Educational Objective:
• Alopecia areata is associated with exclamation mark hair sign, atopy, and nail pitting but not with geographic tongue.
explanation.ref: Rooks Textbook of Dermatology - 9 th Edition Chapter 89 Page No 89.14, 89.17

subject: Dermatology
question: Identify the cause(s) that result in Non-Scarring Alopecia from the following options. Alopecia Areata Telogen Effluvium Androgenic Alopecia Frontal Fibrosing Alopecia

explanation.detail:
• Educational Objective:
• Non-Scarring Alopecia , which allows for the possibility of hair regrowth , includes Alopecia Areata, Telogen Effluvium, and Androgenic Alopecia. Frontal Fibrosing Alopecia is associated with Scarring Alopecia .

subject: Dermatology
question:
Match the items of Column A with Column B

[Image: https://cerebellum-web-static.s3.amazonaws.com/media/public/images/2024/02/23/picture44.jpg]

explanation.detail:
• Explanation of each option
• Loss of Frontal-Parietal Hairline - e) Androgenic Alopecia Androgenic alopecia commonly manifests as a receding hairline at the temples and can progress to affect the Frontal-Parietal Region .
• Chemotherapy-Induced Hair Loss - d) Anagen Effluvium Anagen effluvium typically occurs due to chemotherapy which affects rapidly dividing hair follicle cells, leading to widespread hair loss .
• Hairs of varying Lengths in Alopecia Patch - c) Trichotillomania Trichotillomania, a hair-pulling disorder , results in patches of hair loss with broken hairs of various lengths .
• Peribulbar Inflammation - a) Alopecia Areata Alopecia areata is characterized by immune-mediated peribulbar inflammation described as swarm of Bees Appearance around Hair Follicles, leading to Patchy Hair Loss.
• Stress-Induced Hair Loss - b) Telogen Effluvium Telogen effluvium is often triggered by stress , causing diffuse hair shedding when hairs enter the telogen (resting) phase prematurely.
• Scarring Alopecia - f) Lichen Planopilaris Lichen planopilaris is a type of scarring alopecia that results in permanent hair loss due to inflammation that destroys the hair follicle
• Educational Objective:

subject: Dermatology
question:
What is the probable diagnosis of this condition affecting Hair?

[Image: https://cerebellum-web-static.s3.amazonaws.com/media/public/images/2024/02/23/picture46.jpg]

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A: Trichorrhexis Nodosa
• It is associated with Node Formation. It may be seen in Arginosuccinic Aciduria . Characterized by classical appearance as if Two Paint Brushes are rubbing against each other described as Thrust Paintbrush Appearance.
• Option B. Trichorrhexis Invaginata:
• Also known as Bamboo hair having Golf tee or Ball & Socket appearance, in which the distal part of the hair shaft is compressed against the dilated and cupped proximal shaft . Seen in Netherton’s syndrome.
• Beaded hair seen in Menkes disease.
• Educational Objective
• " Tigertail " banding of hair under polarized light microscopy is indicative of trichothiodystrophy , a condition characterized by brittle hair with reduced sulfur content and potentially associated with multi system involvement .
• Rooks Textbook of Dermatology 9 th Edition Pages 65.33, 68.21, 78.10 89.53-54

subject: Dermatology
question: Anagen Phase of the Hair Follicle indicates?

explanation.short: Ans. A) The Phase of Activity & Growth
explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option B. The phase of transition:
• Incorrect because this describes the catagen phase (involution) , a transitional stage where the hair follicle begins to shut down.
• Option C. The phase of resting
• Incorrect because this describes the telogen phase, where the hair follicle is at rest and the hair shaft does not grow .
• Option D. The phase of degeneration
• Incorrect because this is not a recognized phase of the hair growth cycle. The Exogen Phase could be considered a phase of shedding or the release of hair , but it is not typically referred to as degeneration.
• Educational Objective:
• The anagen phase is the period of active hair growth in the hair follicle life cycle .
explanation.ref: Bolognia Dermatology 3 rd Edition Page No 1073

subject: Dermatology
question: Identify incorrect match among the following.
options: Hand foot mouth disease: Coxsackie A virus

explanation.detail:
• Explanation
• Option A: Hertoghe's sign (Queen Anne's sign)
• It is defined as loss of lateral one third of eye-brows (superciliary madarosis).
• It is seen in various conditions such as
• Lepromatous leprosy, Atopic dermatitis
• Option B. Hand foot mouth disease: Coxsackie A virus
• Hand foot mouth disease is correctly associated with Coxsackie A virus. It is a common viral illness that usually affects infants and children younger than 5 years old and presents with sores in the mouth and a rash on the hands and feet.
• Option C. Eczema herpeticum: Kaposi varicelliform eruption:
• A common viral skin infection known as eczema herpeticum or Kaposi varicelliform eruption causes localised or mild vesicular eruptions in people who already have a skin condition (most commonly in atopic dermatitis). Defective skin barrier → acute disseminated cutaneous HSV I infection e.g., in patients with atopic eczema, darier disease or Sezary syndrome Umbilicated Vesiculo pustules → rupture to form punched out erosions covered by hemorrhagic crusts
• The implicated viruses in Eczema herpeticum are:
• HSV-1 (most common) HSV-2 VZV Coxsackie A6 and A16 (eczema coxsackium) Vaccinia (eczema vaccinatum) Kaposi varicelliform eruption presents with a high fever and widely disseminated, potentially hemorrhagic vesicles. The skin is painful and generally erythematous. In 4 to 5 days, the fever goes down, and some permanent scarring remains after the pustules crust over and heal.
• Educational Objective:
• Chickenpox is caused by the varicella-zoster virus, not herpes simplex virus type 2.
• Bolognia Textbook of Dermatology 4 th edition Page no 1408
explanation.ref: Rooks textbook of dermatology- 9 th Edition page no 25.24- 25.25, 25.38-25.42

subject: Dermatology
question: What are the diseases / malignancies associated with the virus which is implicated in causing Oral Hairy Leukoplakia?

explanation.detail:
• Educational Objective:
• Epstein-Barr virus is associated with multiple diseases, including Oral Hairy Leukoplakia, Burkitt's lymphoma, glandular fever, and nasopharyngeal carcinoma.
explanation.ref: Rook's Textbook of Dermatology 9th edition, Page nos 25.33-25.34, Bolognia Textbook of Dermatology 4 th edition Page no 1416

subject: Dermatology
question:
A 10-year-old child develops the following lesions over the face. All the following statements are correct about this disease except?

options: Lesions are Not seen in palms & Soles generally

explanation.detail:
• Explanation
• Option C: Pseudo-Koebner phenomenon : New lesions develop along the line of trauma due to Autoinoculation.
• Sexually transmitted molluscum contagiosum may be present over the genital area . Microscopic examination of the lesions shows eosinophilic inclusions called Henderson-Patterson bodies.
• Usually, it is self-limiting and will disappear by itself . Thus, There is no need for treatment Destruction with cantharidin, trichloroacetic acid, diluted liquefied phenol, or surgical removal are a few options.
• Educational Objective:
• Molluscum contagiosum is a skin condition caused by a DNA virus , and understand its common presentations and methods of transmission, with a special note that it is not caused by an RNA virus.

explanation.detail:
• Explanation
• Option A: Ecthyma pyogenicum:
• It is a deeper variant of impetigo , caused by staphylococcus aureus & streptococcus, present with indurated lesions & necrotic crust , which on removal leads to ulcer formation.
• Option C: Erythema infectiosum is caused by Parvovirus B19, characterised by slapped cheek appearance
• Option D: Ecthyma gangrenosum is caused by Pseudomonas aeruginosa.

subject: Dermatology
question:
A 9-month-old child is brought by mother with rash as shown in the following picture. Three days ago, she took her to the emergency after the girl spiked a fever to 104 degrees Fahrenheit and had a seizure at home. Her work-up was normal, and she was sent home with acetaminophen and fluids. This morning the child developed a rash on her trunk, which her mother believes is spreading to involve arms and legs. On examination, palate had red papules. What is the most likely diagnosis?

[Image: https://cerebellum-web-static.s3.amazonaws.com/media/public/images/2024/03/15/picture6_6Q3YNLV.jpg]

explanation.detail:
• Explanation
• Option A: Varicella presents as a vesicular eruption over erythematous base , not as a maculopapular rash as given in this case scenario.
• Option B: Erythema infectiosum / 5th Disease:
• Caused by Human Parvovirus B19 . Slapped cheek appearance can be seen.
• Option C: Measles (RUBEOLA):
• Maculopapular rash begins at the hairline , then involves the face and upper neck . Later involves extremities. Fever, running nose and congested eyes . Infectivity period → 4 days Before ←RASH → 5 Days after. Most characteristic Feature → Koplik's Spots [opposite to lower 2nd molar]
• Educational Objective:
• The classic presentation of Roseola infantum is a high fever that resolves followed by a rash that starts on the trunk and spreads to the extremities in a young child.
• Bolognia Textbook of Dermatology 4 th edition Page no 1420
• Bolognia Textbook of Dermatology 4 th edition Page no
explanation.ref: Rook's Textbook of Dermatology 9th editionPage nos 25.34-25.35

subject: Dermatology
question: All of the following statements are correct regarding Warts and its management except?
options: HPV 2 is the most common causative organism in common warts
Deep palmoplantar warts are also called as Myrmecia warts
HPV 6 is the most common causative organism in anogenital warts

explanation.detail:
• Explanation
• Option A: Verruca vulgaris or common wart is most commonly caused by human papillomavirus-2 (HPV-2) but is also related to types 1, 4, 27, and 57.
• Below image depicts Verruca vulgaris:
• Common warts typically have no symptoms. They appear as firm papules with a rough horny surface and exhibit acanthosis, hyperkeratosis, and papillomatosis . The hands, fingers, and knees of young children are the areas that are most frequently affected. Pseudo-Koebner phenomenon may be observed.
• Option B: Deep palmoplantar warts are also called Myrmecia warts .
• It is usually caused by human papillomavirus-1 (HPV-1). The superficial form of plantar warts is called mosaic warts which are caused by HPV-2.
• Below image depicts Plantar warts
• Various types of warts and their clinical features:
• Verrucous warts present as asymptomatic skin-coloured papules and plaques with rough surfaces. Filiform warts are finger-like projections.
• Verruca Plana are flat surfaced lesions and hence the name. They are mainly due to HPV-3 and - 10.
• Option C: Anogenital Warts are most commonly caused by human papillomavirus 6 ( HPV-6 > HPV-11 ).
• Educational Objective:
• A doctor administers this treatment. Liquid nitrogen, carbon dioxide snow, and dimethyl ether can all cause thermal injury to the skin. The coldest freeze is produced by liquid nitrogen , which is frequently employed in practice. A cotton wool bud or a cryospray is used to apply it until a rim of iced tissue forms around the wart (visible as a white discoloration). Depending on the size and location of the wart, the freezing is maintained for 5 to 30 seconds. With freezing times under 30 seconds, scarring is unlikely. Cryotherapy is safer than imiquimod, podophyllin, and podofilox , which are teratogenic & so contraindicated during pregnancy.
• Bolognia Textbook of Dermatology 4 th edition Page no 1214
• Bolognia Textbook of Dermatology 4 th edition Page no
• Andrew's Diseases of the Skin: Clinical Dermatology - 11th Edition Chapter 19, Page no 401
explanation.ref: Rook's Textbook of Dermatology 9th editionPage no 25.43, 25.47, 25.48, 25.52

subject: Dermatology
question:
Child presents with sores in the mouth and a rash on the hands and feet as shown in the image below. What is the causative organism for it?

[Image: https://cerebellum-web-static.s3.amazonaws.com/media/public/images/2024/03/15/picture14_zhmw8qO.jpg]

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option B. Pox virus :
• Poxviruses cause diseases such as smallpox and molluscum contagiosum , which are characterized by distinct lesions but not the oral sores and hand-foot rash combination seen in HFMD.
• Option C. Herpes virus :
• While certain herpes viruses can cause oral lesions (like HSV-1 ) and others can cause systemic symptoms (like varicella-zoster virus ), they do not typically cause the hand-foot-mouth disease pattern.
• Option D. Human papillomavirus (HPV) :
• HPV causes warts and is not typically associated with the systemic presentation of hand, foot, and mouth disease with oral sores.
• Educational Objective:
• Coxsackie virus A16 is the causative organism of hand, foot, and mouth disease , characterized by oral sores and a rash on the hands and feet.
• Bolognia Textbook of Dermatology 4 th edition Page no1426
explanation.ref: Rook's Textbook of Dermatology 9th edition Page no 117.6

subject: Dermatology
question:
This 37-year-old woman developed these grouped vesicles near her lips after developing fever. All of the following statements are true about this disease EXCEPT

[Image: https://cerebellum-web-static.s3.amazonaws.com/media/public/images/2024/03/15/picture15_QdC41FI.jpg]
options: It is Herpes labialis or Cold sore
Foscarnet is used to treat Acyclovir resistant cases
It is commonly caused by herpes simplex virus type 1 (HSV-1)
Once disease is treated with antivirals, Recurrences are uncommon

explanation.detail:
• Educational Objective:
• Herpes labialis , caused by HSV-1, can be treated with antivirals like acyclovir and foscarnet for resistant cases, but despite treatment, recurrences are common due to the laten t nature of the virus.
• Bolognia Textbook of Dermatology 4 th edition Page no 1402
explanation.ref: Rook's Textbook of Dermatology - 9th Edition Page no 25.12, 25.17-19, 25.20

subject: Dermatology
question: Identify the incorrect statement regarding Herpes zoster (Shingles)?
options: Varicella-zoster virus remains latent in Dorsal root ganglion
Post-herpetic neuralgia is most frequent when the trigeminal nerve is involved.
Mucous membranes within the affected dermatomes are involved.

explanation.short: Ans. B. Maculopapular rash is seen
explanation.detail:
• Educational Objective:
• The presentation of herpes zoster (shingles) is a vesicular rash within a single dermatome and recognize the virus's ability to remain latent in the dorsal root ganglia , with potential complications including post-herpetic neuralgia , especially with trigeminal nerve involvement.
• Harrison's Principles of Internal Medicine 20th EditionPage no 106

subject: Dermatology
question: Slapped cheek appearance is associated with which of the following?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation
• Option B: Herpes zoster
• Herpes zoster, also known as shingles , is caused by the reactivation of the varicella-zoster virus and presents as a painful rash with blisters in a dermatomal fashion , usually in a band on one side of the body or face .
• It does not cause the slapped cheek appearance.
• Option C: Kaposi sarcoma
• Kaposi sarcoma is a type of cancer that forms in the lining of blood and lymph vessels .
• It is associated with HHV 8 infection. The lesions are typically red or purple and can occur anywhere on the body, including the skin and internal organs, not presenting as a slapped cheek rash.
• Option D: Molluscum contagiosum
• Molluscum contagiosum is a viral skin infection that causes raised, umbilicated papules or nodules on the skin.
• These are not associated with the slapped cheek appearance.
• Educational Objective:
• Erythema infectiosum (Fifth disease) is identified by its classic clinical presentation of a "slapped cheek" rash on the face, which is a hallmark sign of this viral illness in children. It's important for medical students and clinicians to recognize this distinctive rash for proper diagnosis and reassurance, as this condition is usually self-limiting and requires no specific treatment beyond symptomatic relief.
• Rooks textbook of dermatology- 9 th Edition page no25.66
• Bolognia Textbook of Dermatology 4 th edition Page no 1430
explanation.ref: Rooks textbook of dermatology- 9 th Edition page no25.66

subject: Dermatology
question: Which of the following does not present with maculopapular rash?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option B: Rubella : Presents with a maculopapular rash as shown in the image below, which consists of minute, discrete macules .
• The rash appears within 24 hours of onset of prodromal symptoms.
• Rubella virus is a RNA virus which belongs to Togaviridae family.
• Option C: Dengue (during febrile phase) : Dengue fever can present with a maculopapular rash during its febrile phase .
• Option D: Measles : Measles is characterized by fever and a maculopapular skin rash , which is accompanied by immune clearance of measles virus (MV)-infected cells
• Educational Objective:
• Zoster (shingles) does not present with a maculopapular rash but rather with a painful, vesicular rash in a dermatomal distribution. In contrast, rubella, dengue during the febrile phase, and measles are classic causes of maculopapular rashes .
explanation.ref: Rook's Textbook of Dermatology 9th edition, Page no. 4.13, 25.27-25.28 Harrison's Principles of Internal Medicine 20th Edition, Page no 106

subject: Dermatology
question: Rubella virus belongs to which family?

explanation.detail:
• Educational Objective:
• When classifying viruses, it is important to identify the correct family based on the virus's genome type , envelope presence, and disease association. The rubella virus is classified under the Togaviridae family due to its positive-sense single-stranded RNA genome and association with rubella disease.
• Bolognia Textbook of Dermatology 4 th edition Page no 1428
explanation.ref: Rooks textbook of dermatology- 9 th Edition page no 25.3

explanation.detail:

• Diseases/Viruses with Intra-cytoplasmic Inclusion Bodies

subject: Dermatology
question:
A child presents with multiple skin lesions over the inner thigh and genital areas. Some lesions have central umbilication. The representative of the histology of the lesion is shown below. What would be found on biopsy?

[Image: https://cerebellum-web-static.s3.amazonaws.com/media/public/images/2024/03/15/picture24.jpg]

explanation.detail:
• Explanation
• Option A: Donovan bodies
• They are intracytoplasmic inclusions seen in granuloma inguinale caused by Klebsiella granulomatis . These are not associated with umbilicated skin lesions.
• They are indicative of HPV infection and are typically associated with changes in the skin and mucous membranes that do not usually present with umbilicated lesions.
• Option D: Tzanck cells
• They are Acantholytic cells seen in vesiculobullous disorders like pemphigus and herpes infections , such as herpes simplex and varicella-zoster virus , which typically present with vesicular lesions rather than umbilicated nodules.
• Some important findings on Tzanck smear are as follows:
• Image : Tzanck smear, showing multinucleated giant cells in center
• Educational Objective:
• Henderson-Patterson bodies on biopsy is diagnostic for Molluscum contagiosum , a viral skin infection that presents with characteristic central umbilication . Recognizing the histopathological features of common skin lesions is essential for accurate diagnosis and management in dermatology.
• Bolognia Textbook of Dermatology 4 th edition Page no 1440
explanation.ref: Rooks textbook of dermatology- 9 th Edition page no 25.13

subject: Dermatology
question: Vesicular rash is seen in which of the following?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation
• Dengue fever typically presents with high fever, headache , severe muscle and joint pain , and a rash. The rash associated with dengue fever is not typically vesicular but can be morbilliform (measles-like).
• Option C: Roseola infantum –
• Option C: Roseola infantum
• Also known as sixth disease and caused by human herpesvirus 6 (HHV-6), Roseola presents with a sudden high fever that lasts for several days, followed by a rash as the fever subsides. The rash is not vesicular but rather maculopapular.
• Option D: Zika virus –
• Option D: Zika virus
• The Zika virus can cause fever, rash, joint pain, and red eyes , but the rash associated with Zika is not vesicular.
• Educational Objective:
• When assessing a patient with a vesicular rash , it is essential to consider HFMD, especially in children. This disease presents with characteristic vesicles on the hands , feet, and mouth and is often associated with fever and malaise .
• Rooks textbook of dermatology- 9 th Edition page no 25.81
• Bolognia Textbook of Dermatology 4 th edition Page no 1425
explanation.ref: Rooks textbook of dermatology- 9 th Edition page no 25.81

subject: Dermatology
question:
An elderly person presented with lesions as shown in the image below. What is the most likely diagnosis?

[Image: https://cerebellum-web-static.s3.amazonaws.com/media/public/images/2024/03/15/picture27.jpg]

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Chickenpox, caused by the varicella-zoster virus , typically presents with a pruritic , vesicular rash that starts on the face and trunk and spreads to the rest of the body. The lesions appear in crops and are at different stages of healing . The rash in the image does not display this typical distribution or the various stages of healing.
• Option B: Contact dermatitis
• Contact dermatitis is an i nflammatory skin condition caused by contact with a substance that triggers an allergic or irritant response. It presents with erythema, edema, and sometimes vesicles, but these are not typically in a dermatomal distribution.
• Option D: Lichen planus .
• Lichen planus is a chronic inflammatory condition of the skin and mucous membranes . It presents with pruritic, flat-topped, violaceous papules and plaques with fine white lines ( Wickham's striae ) on the surface.
• The rash in the image does not exhibit these features.
• Educational Objective:
• Herpes zoster can be identified by its distinctive clustered vesicular eruption within a single dermatome , which is typically unilateral and does not cross the midline . This presentation is especially common in elderly or i mmunocompromise d individuals due to the reactivation of the varicella-zoster virus .
• Bolognia Textbook of Dermatology 4 th edition Page no 4, 1412
explanation.ref: Rooks textbook of dermatology- 9 th Edition page no25.27

subject: Dermatology
question:
HIV patient with following mucosal lesion came for consultation to a dermatology OPD. What can be the most likely diagnosis?

[Image: https://cerebellum-web-static.s3.amazonaws.com/media/public/images/2024/03/15/picture29.jpg]

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A: Basal cell carcinoma
• It usually presents as a pearly nodular lesion with telangiectasia and rarely occurs in the oral cavity.
• Option C : Melanoma in the oral cavity is rare and typically presents as a rapidly enlarging dark lesion.
• Option D : Squamous cell carcinoma
• It tends to present as an ulcerative lesion with indurated margins and can be more common in the oral cavity but does not match the description of firm, violaceous plaques.
• Educational Objective:
• When an HIV patient presents with firm, violaceous plaques in the oral cavity and skin, Kaposi's sarcoma should be suspected. Diagnosis can be confirmed with a biopsy showing spindle-cell neoplasm positive for CD34 and HHV-8. Treatment includes antiretroviral therapy and, if necessary, chemotherapy.
• Bolognia Textbook of Dermatology 4 th edition Page no 32, 2022
explanation.ref: Rooks textbook of dermatology- 9 th Edition page no 25.37

subject: Dermatology
question:
An adult female HIV patient with hilar lymphadenopathy & lung infiltration comes to you with these lesions over body as shown. What is your diagnosis?

[Image: https://cerebellum-web-static.s3.amazonaws.com/media/public/images/2024/03/15/picture30.jpg]

explanation.detail:
• Explanation
• This typically presents with cutaneous lesions in HIV patients, they are associated with umbilicated papules . Cryptococcal infections more commonly manifest as meningitis or disseminated disease with cutaneous lesions that can be molluscum-like , but other systemic signs would be expected.
• Option C. Disseminated molluscum contagiosum
• This can present with umbilicated papules in immunocompromised patients. However, the presence of systemic symptoms is not consistent with molluscum contagiosum.
• Option D. Pneumocystis carinii pneumonia / Pneumocystis jirovecii pneumonia (PCP):
• This does not typically present with cutaneous manifestations. It presents with respiratory symptoms and is diagnosed with chest imaging and identification of the organism in respiratory specimens , not skin lesions.
• Diseases which can manifest with umbilicated papules over skin surface:
• Educational Objective:
• To differentiate between various opportunistic infections in HIV patients, focusing on their cutaneous manifestations, systemic involvement, and specific h istopathological findings. Skin lesions in HIV patients can indicate systemic opportunistic infections, and the clinical presentation combined with histopathology is crucial for accurate diagnosis and management.
• Rooks textbook of dermatology- 9 th Edition page no 31.26
• Bolognia Textbook of Dermatology 4 th edition Page no 1352
explanation.ref: Rooks textbook of dermatology- 9 th Edition page no 31.26

subject: Dermatology
question:
What is correct regarding the diagnosis based on the given image?

[Image: https://cerebellum-web-static.s3.amazonaws.com/media/public/images/2024/03/15/picture16_ovkoAiw.jpg]
options: Anterior nerve roots are more commonly involved
Mucous membranes within the affected dermatomes are involved
Trigeminal dermatome is most commonly affected

explanation.short: Ans. B. Mucous membranes within the affected dermatomes are involved
explanation.detail:
• Educational Objective:
• Herpes zoster , commonly involves reactivation of the varicella-zoster virus within the dorsal root ganglion , leading to painful cutaneous eruptions that can affect mucous membranes within the involved dermatomes . Herpes zoster lesions are infectious , particularly to those who are not immune to varicella-zoster virus , and thoracic dermatome is the most frequently affected area.
• Harrison's Principles of Internal Medicine 20th Edition, Page no 106
• Rook's Textbook of Dermatology 9th edition, Page no 4.13
explanation.ref: Rook's Textbook of Dermatology 9th edition, Page no. 25.27-25.28

subject: Dermatology
question:
A child was admitted with complaints of fever, neck rigidity (meningitis features) with associated oral erosions and skin manifestations as shown in the picture. CSF analysis show normal glucose, proteins and lymphocytes increased. What is the possible diagnosis?

[Image: https://cerebellum-web-static.s3.amazonaws.com/media/public/images/2024/03/15/picture15_gBShaQ8.jpg]

explanation.detail:
• Explanation
• Option A. Bacterial meningitis
• This typically presents with high fever, neck rigidity, and altered mental status. CSF analysis often shows elevated white cells (usually neutrophils ), low glucose, and high proteins , which is not the case here.
• Option B. Hand, foot, and mouth disease
• It is usually caused by Coxsackieviru s and primarily affects children. It presents with oral ulcer s and a rash on the hands and feet , but it is not commonly associated with meningitis.
• Option C. Tubercular meningitis
• It would generally present with a chronic course of fever and neck rigidity. CSF analysis in tubercular meningitis often shows low glucose , elevated proteins , and lymphocytosis, which does not align with the findings presented.
• Educational Objective:
• To diagnose herpes simplex infection in a pediatric patient presenting with fever, neck rigidity, oral erosions , characteristic skin manifestations, and CSF findings consistent with viral meningitis . The recognition of oral and skin lesions typical of herpes simplex is crucial in differentiating it from other causes of meningitis.
• Bolognia Textbook of Dermatology 4 th edition Page no 1368
explanation.ref: Rooks textbook of dermatology- 9 th Edition page no 25.16

subject: Dermatology
question:
A 6-month-old male child experienced an abrupt high-grade fever lasting several days, followed by its sudden cessation. After the fever resolved, he developed a rash that began on his trunk and then spread to his arms and legs as shown in the image below. The child also had a brief febrile seizure during the period of high fever. The rash is not pruritic and the child is otherwise well-appearing. Based on this clinical presentation, what is the most likely diagnosis?

[Image: https://cerebellum-web-static.s3.amazonaws.com/media/public/images/2024/03/15/picture34.jpg]

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. Fixed Drug Eruption
• It typically presents as well-demarcated , round, reddish-brown plaques that recur at the same site with the re-administration of the offending drug. This is unlikely in a 6-month-old infant who likely has not had multiple drug exposures.
• Option B. Erythema Infectiosum
• It is also known as fifth disease , is caused by Parvovirus B19 and typically presents with a "slapped cheek" facial rash followed by a lacy, reticulated body rash.
• It does not typically present with high fever or febrile seizures.
• It typically starts with a high fever , cough, coryza, and conjunctivitis , followed by a spreading rash from the face to the rest of the body, along with Koplik's spots inside the mouth.
• The clinical presentation in the question does not mention these features.
• Educational Objective:
• Diagnose Roseola based on the classic clinical triad of high-grade fever, subsequent rash, and potential febrile seizure in a young infant. The sequence is fever followed by rash (after fever subsides), in the absence of other systemic symptoms, is key to identifying Roseola over other pediatric exanthems.
• Rooks textbook of dermatology- 9 th Edition page no 25.34
• Bolognia Textbook of Dermatology 4 th edition Page no 1420
