subject: Dermatology
question:
An immunocompromised individual presents with a corrugated white lesion on the side of the tongue as shown in the image below, which does not wipe off. The most likely causative agent is?

[Image: https://cerebellum-web-static.s3.amazonaws.com/media/public/images/2024/03/15/picture36.jpg]

explanation.detail:
• Explanation
• It typically causes oral thrush , which presents as white plaques that can be wiped off, unlike the corrugated lesion shown in the image.
• Option C. Human Herpes Virus (HHV7)
• This is not commonly associated with oral lesions in immunocompromised patients. HHV-7 more often reactivates to cause roseola in infants or is asymptomatic in adults.
• Option D. Human Herpes Virus 8 (HHV8)
• This is associated with Kaposi's sarcoma, not corrugated white oral lesions. Kaposi's sarcoma typically presents as red or purple lesions in the oral cavity or on the skin.
• Educational Objective:
• Oral hairy Leukoplakia in immunocompromised patients and understand its association with EBV. Recognizing that these lesions do not wipe off is a key diagnostic feature distinguishing them from other conditions like candidiasis.
• Bolognia Textbook of Dermatology 4 th edition Page no 1370
explanation.ref: Rooks textbook of dermatology- 9 th Edition page no 33.31

subject: Dermatology
question: A 26-year-old primigravida is living with her nephew, who has recently developed varicella. She has no history of varicella exposure or vaccination, and serological testing reveals that she is not immune to the varicella-zoster virus (VZV). Based on this information, which of the following is true?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. is Incorrect because zoster, or shingles , occurs in individuals who have had chickenpox in the past and the virus reactivates . Since the patient has never had chickenpox, she is not at risk for zoster until she has had a primary VZV infection.
• Option C. is Incorrect. Immunity to zoster implies that the patient has had a prior infection with VZV, which is not the case here as evidenced by the negative antibody test.
• Option D. is Incorrect because the negative varicella antibody test indicates that the patient does not have immunity to chickenpox.
• Educational Objective:
• The absence of varicella antibodies in an individual with no past history of infection or vaccination indicates susceptibility to primary varicella-zoster virus infection , which presents as chickenpox.
• Bolognia Textbook of Dermatology 4 th edition Page no 1408
explanation.ref: Rooks textbook of dermatology- 9 th Edition page no 25.25

subject: Dermatology
question:
A 30-year-old man presents to the clinic with painful vesicles on his lip that erupted two days after he felt a tingling sensation in the area. He has had similar episodes in the past, usually during periods of stress or sun exposure. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

[Image: https://cerebellum-web-static.s3.amazonaws.com/media/public/images/2024/03/15/whatsapp-image-2024-03-15-at-65732-pm.jpeg]

explanation.detail:
• Explanation
• This is caused by the Coxsackie virus and is characterized by small, painful vesicles and ulcers on the posterior oropharynx , not on the lips.
• Patients often present with a sore throat, fever, and malaise , which are not described in this vignette.
• Option B. Molluscum contagiosum
• It presents with small, flesh-colored, dome-shaped papules with a central umbilication , typically not painful , caused by a poxvirus infection .
• This condition does not match the description of painful lip vesicles that are recurrent and associated with prodromal symptoms.
• This is a superficial skin infection characterized by honey-colored crusts and can be caused by Staphylococcus aureus or Streptococcus species.
• While it can affect the face, the presence of grouped vesicles with a history of recurrence and a tingling prodrome is more suggestive of herpes labialis.
• Educational Objective:
• The clinical presentation of herpes labialis, includes the characteristic painful vesicles on the lips that often recur, triggered by factors such as stress o r sun exposure . Understanding the etiology and triggers is crucial for appropriate management and counselling of patients with recurrent herpes labialis.
• Bolognia Textbook of Dermatology 4 th edition Page no 1402
explanation.ref: Rook's Textbook of Dermatology - 9th Edition Page no 25.12, 25.17-19, 25.20

subject: Dermatology
question: Identify the INCORRECT match of disease & organism among the following?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation
• Option A: Erysipelothrix rhusiopathiae .
• It is a Gram-positive bacillus that feeds primarily on dead animal matter , causes an acute infection known as Erysipeloid. It infects many animals, particularly pigs, with diseases. Fishermen, farmers, farmers, butchers, cooks, veterinarians , and farmers are typically affected. Erysipeloid depicted in the image below:
• A hot, tender, violaceous erythema that spreads centrifugally forms around the inoculation site about three days after the inoculation. Without treatment, healing normally occurs spontaneously in 2 weeks without desquamation or suppuration. Antibiotic therapy : Penicillin, erythromycin, and ciprofloxacin
• Option B:  Pitted keratolysis.
• It is caused most commonly due to infection by Corynebacterium / Kytococcus sedentarius. The pits are produced by serine proteases that can degrade keratin in the horny layer.
• It is usually associated with hyperhidrosis. Treatment of hyperhidrosis slowly brings the condition under control including potassium permanganate soaks, aluminium chloride, and iontophoresis. Topical Fucidin ointment may be used.
• Option C: Scrub typhus
• Aetiology : Orientia tsutsugamushi Vector : trombiculid mite Infective form of mite : chiggers (larval form ) Clinical presentation : presents to Causality with fever, CNS symptoms. Lesion : Eschar (erythema surrounding black scab).
• Eschar is seen in the below image with black scab at centre surrounded by erythema.
• Differential diagnosis of Eschar:
• Cutaneous anthrax Brown recluse spider bite (poisonous spider) Scrub typhus
• Educational Objective:
• Erysipelas is an infection commonly caused by Group A Streptococcus , not Pseudomonas aeruginosa.
• Rook's Textbook of Dermatology - 9th Edition - Volume I
• Chapter 26, Page nos 26.42-26.43, 26.46
• Bolognia Textbook of Dermatology 4 th edition Page no 1276
explanation.ref: Rook's Textbook of Dermatology - 9th Edition - Volume I

subject: Dermatology
question: A 2-month-old infant presents to the clinic with several fluid-filled blisters on the skin and some areas with a varnish-coloured crust. The infant does not appear to be in significant discomfort, and the rest of the physical examination is unremarkable. Given the clinical presentation and the age of the infant, which of the following statements is INCORRECT regarding the most likely diagnosis?
options: Bullous impetigo is the other name for this disease
Staphylococcus aureus is the causative organism
Golden yellow or honey-coloured crusts are seen associated with this disease
Superficial intraepidermal bulla are formed as desmoglein-1 is targeted by the exfoliative toxins

explanation.short: Ans. C. Golden yellow- or honey-coloured crusts are seen associated with this disease
explanation.detail:
• Educational Objective:
• Pemphigus neonatorum , or bullous impetigo , typically presents with varnish-like crusts rather than golden yellow or honey-colored crusts after bullae rupture.
• Chapter 26, Page nos 26.14-26.15
explanation.ref: Rook's Textbook of Dermatology 9th edition

subject: Dermatology
question: All of the following are bacterial infections of skin EXCEPT:

explanation.detail:
• Explanation
• Option A: Ecthyma pyogenicum
• It is a pyogenic infection of the skin characterized by the formation of adherent crusts, beneath which ulceration occurs. It most commonly results from mixed infection of group A streptococci > staphylococci and causes a deeper infection than impetigo. The buttocks, thighs, and legs are most affected.
• Extremes of age I mmunocompromise d Diabetes Poor hygiene.
• Treatment: Topical Fucidin, topical mupirocin , and systemic cloxacillin.
• Option B: Ecthyma gangrenosum
• It is caused by Pseudomonas aeruginosa , which manifests as hemorrhagic pustules that evolve into a necrotic (black) ulcer which can be seen in the image below.
• Option D: Trichomycosis axillaris (now referred to as Trichobacteriosis axillaris )
• It is caused by Corynebacterium tenuis. It is a superficial infection of axillary and pubic hairs with the formation of yellow, black, or red nodular concretions on the hair shaft. Trichobacteriosis. Tan-yellow concretions on the hair shafts of the axilla.
• Depending on the colour of the concretions, the axillary sweat may be yellow, black, or red , and the clothing may be stained. Since the condition frequently has no symptoms , the patient is frequently unaware of its existence. The use of an effective antiperspirant such as aluminium chloride is a rapid means of therapy.
• Educational Objective:
• Impetigo herpetiformis , despite its name, is not a bacterial infection but a form of pustular psoriasis seen in pregnancy.
• Rook's Textbook of Dermatology - Volume II-9th Edition
• Chapter 26, Page no 26.16-26.17, 26.41, 26.51
• Fitzpatrick’s dermatology textbook 9 th edition Chapter 105 page no 1771
explanation.ref: Rook's Textbook of Dermatology - Volume II-9th Edition

subject: Dermatology
question: True about Paronychia are all EXCEPT?
options: Topical steroids have an important role in treatment of chronic paronychia.
It is Infection of nail folds.
Candida is the most common causative organism in chronic paronychia.
Streptococcus pyogenes is the most common causative organism in acute paronychia.

explanation.detail:
• Educational Objective:
• Staphylococcus aureus , not Streptococcus pyogenes, is the most common causative organism of acute paronychia.
explanation.ref: Fitzpatrick’s Textbook of Dermatology 9th edition chapter 150 page no 2725

subject: Dermatology
question: Staphylococcus aureus causes or associated with all of the following EXCEPT?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation
• It is a misnomer. It is an infection brought on by bacteria , usually staphylococcus . It is a chronic granulomatous reaction to bacterial infection that manifests as multiple skin abscesses with mucopurulent discharge through multiple sinuses . The discharge contains granules that resemble the sulphur granules found in actinomycosis and does not reveal filamentous organisms when stained with Gomori methenamine silver (GMS negative).
• Below image depicts Botryomycosis with multiple skin abscesses with mucopurulent discharge through multiple sinuses
• Etiological agents for Botryomycosis include:
• Staphylococcus aureus (most common ) Pseudomonas Escherichia coli Proteus Streptococcus Most lesions are on the limbs, but other sites including the perianal region and the face are affected. Treatment: Flucloxacillin or erythromycin .
• It is a deep-seated cutaneous infection around a group of hair follicles with sieve-like multiple openings discharging pus. Carbuncle is caused by Staphylococcus aureus
• Below image depicts Carbuncle
• A subacute or chronic infection that affects the entire depth of the follicle is known as sycosis . It affects the area around the beard . An oedematous erythematous follicular papule or pustule centred on a hair is the primary lesion.
• Image shown below depicts Sycosis barbae:
• Educational Objective:
• Scarlet fever is caused by Streptococcus pyogenes , not Staphylococcus aureus, distinguishing it from other skin conditions like botryomycosis, carbuncle, and sycosis barbae , which are associated with Staphylococcus aureus.
• Chapter 26, Page nos 26.16-26.17, 26.72
• Andrew's Diseases of the Skin: Clinical Dermatology - 11th Edition Chapter 14, Page nos 245 - 246
explanation.ref: Rook's Textbook of Dermatology 9th edition

subject: Dermatology
question: A child came to dermatological OPD with complaints of an itchy lesion over face. On microbiological examination Gram positive cocci arranged in pairs & chains are seen. All of the following are possible complications EXCEPT?

explanation.detail:
• Educational Objective
• Rheumatic fever is not a complication typically associated with streptococcal skin infections but rather with streptococcal pharyngitis.
explanation.ref: Rook's Textbook of Dermatology 9th edition Chapter 26, Page no 26.13

subject: Dermatology
question: A 12-month-old male infant developed conjunctivitis which was managed with topical & oral antibiotics. A few days later, he developed fever, irritability, and skin tenderness followed by extensive peeling of the skin. On examination mucosa of eyes, oral cavity & genitalia were normal. What is the most likely diagnosis?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation
• Option A: Syphilitic pemphigus:
• Vesiculo-bullous lesions seen in early congenital syphilis.
• Option B & C: In Stevens Johnson Syndrome & Toxic Epidermal Necrolysis
• Mucosal involvement is prominent with Hemorrhagic crusts.
• Educational Objective
• Staphylococcal scalded skin syndrome , characterized by fever, irritability, skin tenderness , and skin peeling , with an absence of mucosal involvement, in infants and young children .
explanation.ref: Rook's Textbook of Dermatology 9th edition Chapter 26, Page no 26.17

subject: Dermatology
question:
A 45-year-old came to Dermatological OPD with a hyperpigmented asymptomatic rash over the axilla as shown below. Woods lamp examination shows coral pink fluorescence. What is the most likely diagnosis?

[Image: https://cerebellum-web-static.s3.amazonaws.com/media/public/images/2024/03/18/picture14_Zsb40mU.jpg]

explanation.detail:
• Educational Objective:
• Erythrasma by its typical presentation of a well-demarcated, hyperpigmented rash in intertriginous areas and coral red or pink fluorescence under Wood's lamp examination.
• Rook's Textbook of Dermatology - Volume II-9th Edition
• Chapter 26 Page no 26.40
explanation.ref: Rooks textbook of dermatology- 9 th Edition volume I Chapter 4 page no 4.20

subject: Dermatology
question:
A 1-year-old child is brought to the clinic with complaints of fever, irritability, poor oral intake, and skin lesions. Vitals examination revealed increased Heart rate (tachycardia present) and a temperature of 101 F. On examination of the skin, diffuse erythema is present along with flaccid bullae. The skin is moist, erythematous, and shiny. Some areas of the skin are desquamated, as shown in the image below. The culture from the lesions is positive for Staphylococcus aureus. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

[Image: https://cerebellum-web-static.s3.amazonaws.com/media/public/images/2024/03/18/picture19_oNRwlZo.jpg]

explanation.detail:
• Explanation
• Option A. Congenital syphilis
• It presents with various signs including snuffles , vesiculobullous rash, Hepatosplenomegaly, Hutchinson teeth and bony abnormalities . Positive bacterial culture makes SSSS more likely diagnosis, though vesiculobullous lesions can be seen in both these conditions.
• Option B. Pemphigus vulgaris
• It is an autoimmune disease that presents with flaccid bullae and erosions, but it is extremely rare in children and does not typically present with systemic symptoms such as fever or a positive bacterial culture.
• Option C. Epidermolysis bullosa
• It is a group of genetic disorders that present with blistering of the skin and mucous membranes in response to minor trauma since birth . It would not cause fever , systemic symptoms, or a positive bacterial culture.
• Educational Objective
• Staphylococcal Scalded Skin Syndrome (SSSS) based on the clinical presentation of fever, irritability, poor oral intake, diffuse erythema, flaccid bullae , shiny erythematous skin, desquamation, and a positive Staphylococcus aureus culture, especially in the paediatric population.Top of Form
• Bolognia Textbook of Dermatology 4 th edition Page no 1265
explanation.ref: Rooks textbook of dermatology- 9 th Edition page no 26.13-26.16

subject: Dermatology
question: A military soldier presents with rashes all over the body sparing the palms & soles. On examination, fever and lice were noted. Which of the following is responsible for his condition?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. Rickettsia akari
• It causes rickettsialpox , not epidemic typhus. Rickettsialpox is transmitted by mite bites and is characterized by an eschar at the site of the bite, followed by a vesicular rash and mild systemic symptoms.
• Option B. Rickettsia conorii
• It is the agent responsible for Mediterranean spotted fever , also known as Boutonneuse fever . It is transmitted by dog ticks and presents with fever, a rash that also involves the palms and soles , and a characteristic tache noire (black spot) at the site of the tick bite.
• Option D. Rickettsia typhi
• It causes murine or endemic typhus , which is transmitted by flea bites. The clinical presentation can be similar to epidemic typhus but is typically milder, and the rash also often involves the palms and soles.
• Educational Objective
• Rickettsia prowazekii is the causative agent of epidemic typhus , which presents with fever, a rash sparing palms and soles , and lice infestation, conditions that can be prevalent in crowded military environments.
• Bolognia Textbook of Dermatology 4 th edition Page no 1319, 1511
explanation.ref: Rooks textbook of dermatology- 9 th Edition page no 26.76

subject: Dermatology
question:
A 28-year-old male presents to the clinic with a one-week history of fever, headache, and myalgia. He reports a recent hiking trip in a rural area of Southeast Asia. On physical examination, he is found to have a maculopapular rash on his trunk and extremities and an eschar on his back, as shown in the image. Laboratory tests reveal thrombocytopenia and elevated hepatic transaminases. He denies any respiratory symptoms, gastrointestinal symptoms, or a history of similar episodes. Which of the following diseases is most likely depicted by the image?

[Image: https://cerebellum-web-static.s3.amazonaws.com/media/public/images/2024/03/18/picture20_FPhtkYV.jpg]

explanation.detail:
• Explanation
• Option A. COVID 19
• It typically presents with respiratory symptoms such as cough, shortness of breath, and fever. The skin manifestations of COVID-19, if present, do not usually include an eschar.
• It is characterized by cyclical fever , chills, and sweats. It is caused by Plasmodium species and transmitted by Anopheles mosquitoes. Malaria does not present with an eschar.
• Option C. RMSF (Rocky Mountain spotted fever)
• It is caused by Rickettsia rickettsii , is transmitted by tick bites and presents with fever, headache, abdominal pain, and a rash that may start on the wrists and ankles before spreading; however, it does not typically present with an eschar.
• Educational Objective
• S crub typhus by its clinical presentation, including the presence of an eschar at the site of a chigger bite , fever, headache, myalgia, and a maculopapular rash in a patient with a history of travel to an endemic area.
• Bolognia Textbook of Dermatology 4 th edition Page no 1326
explanation.ref: Rooks textbook of dermatology- 9 th Edition page no 26.79

subject: Dermatology
question: A six-year-old child presents to the clinic with a complaint of a rapidly spreading rash around the mouth. The mother notes that it started as a small red area that quickly erupted into blisters before forming the characteristic honey-coloured crusts. The child has no significant medical history and is up to date with vaccinations. On examination, there are no signs of systemic infection, and the lesions are localized around the mouth. The mother denies recent exposure to anyone with similar symptoms. Based on the clinical presentation, which of the following statements is most accurate regarding this disease?
options: It is also known as pemphigus neonatorum.
Post streptococcal glomerulonephritis is a serious complication associated with this disease

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. Bacteria targeting desmoglein 1: It is characteristic of Bullous impetigo, not non bullous form.
• Option B. It is also known as pemphigus neonatorum: Pemphigus neonatorum is the other name for Bullous Impetigo.
• Option C. Mostly involves trunk: Nonbullous impetigo commonly presents around the mouth and nose, not predominantly on the trunk.
• Educational Objective
• Nonbullous impetigo is a common pediatric skin infection characterized by honey-coloured crusts usually around the mouth and nose, understanding that it is caused by Staphylococcus aureus and Streptococcus and can occasionally lead to post-streptococcal glomerulonephritis as a serious complication.
• Bolognia Textbook of Dermatology 4 th edition Page no 1260
explanation.ref: Rooks textbook of dermatology- 9 th Edition page no 26.14

subject: Dermatology
question:
A 32-year-old male presents with an abscess containing pus. Gram stain of the organism obtained from the pus is shown in the image below. Which of the following diseases is NOT caused associated with this organism?

[Image: https://cerebellum-web-static.s3.amazonaws.com/media/public/images/2024/03/18/picture22_C3WRag5.jpg]

explanation.detail:
• Explanation
• Option A. Acute paronychia :  Staphylococcus aureus can cause acute paronychia, an infection of the nail fold, commonly seen in individuals who bite their nails or have frequent exposure to moisture.
• Option C. Sycosis barbae: Staphylococcus aureus can indeed cause sycosis barbae, which is a bacterial infection of the beard area.
• Option D. Toxic shock syndrome :  Staphylococcus aureus, specifically strains producing toxic shock syndrome toxin-1 (TSST-1 ), is a well-known cause of toxic shock syndrome.
• Involvement of Staphylococcus aureus in cutaneous disease:
• Involvement of streptococci (mostly group A) in cutaneous disease
• Skin conditions associated ONLY WITH STREPTOCOCCI.
• Educational Objective
• Scarlet fever is NOT caused by Staphylococcus aureus but rather by group A Streptococcus (Streptococcus pyogenes).
• Bolognia Textbook of Dermatology 4 th edition Page no 1268
explanation.ref: Rooks textbook of dermatology- 9 th Edition page no 26.6

subject: Dermatology
question: A 9-year-old child presents with a severe blistering skin condition. On examination, you observe extensive skin denudation with a positive Nikolsky sign. Which among the following findings in history and/or examination is in favour of the diagnosis of Staphylococcal Scalded Skin Syndrome (SSSS) and helps rule out the diagnosis of Toxic Epidermal Necrolysis (TEN)?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation
• Option A. Presence of systemic symptoms:
• Both SSSS and TEN can present with systemic symptoms such as fever, malaise, and weakness. This finding doesn't specifically differentiate between the two conditions.
• Option B. Skin denudation:
• Both SSSS and TEN are characterized by skin denudation or blistering . This finding is common to both conditions and doesn't help distinguish between them.
• Option D. Antibiotic usage:
• SSSS is often associated with a primary Staphylococcus aureus infection, whereas TEN is frequently triggered by medications, particularly antibiotics. The presence of antibiotic usage is more suggestive of TEN.
• Educational Objective:
• Differentiating between Staphylococcal Scalded Skin Syndrome (SSSS) and Toxic Epidermal Necrolysis (TEN) is crucial in managing these severe skin conditions - The absence of oral mucosal involvement is a characteristic feature of SSSS , while TEN often presents with mucosal involvement.
• Bolognia Textbook of Dermatology 4 th edition Page no 1266
explanation.ref: Rooks textbook of dermatology- 9 th Edition page no 26.28

subject: Dermatology
question:
Gram stain revealed the following organism as shown in the image below. Which of the following diseases is caused by it?

[Image: https://cerebellum-web-static.s3.amazonaws.com/media/public/images/2024/03/18/picture12_Wtk6lWI.jpg]

explanation.detail:
• Explanation
• Image depicts Angular infarct & petechiae seen in Meningococcal infection caused by Neisseria meningitidis.
• Image depicts Eschar surrounded by small vesicles (Malignant Pustule) seen in Anthrax Caused by Bacillus anthracis.
• Image depicts Folliculitis caused by Staphylococcus aureus
• Involvement of Staphylococcus aureus in cutaneous disease
• Involvement of streptococci (mostly group A) in cutaneous disease
• Skin conditions associated ONLY WITH STREPTOCOCCI.
• Educational objective :
• Gram stain findings of bacterium with chain like arrangement is Streptococcus and remember the diseases caused by gram positive organisms like Staphylococcus & Streptococcus .
• Bolognia Textbook of Dermatology 4 th edition Page no 1265
explanation.ref: Rooks textbook of dermatology- 9 th Edition page no 26.17

subject: Dermatology
question:
A 40-year-old male patient presented with well demarcated redness over leg as shown in the image below. It was extremely painful. Which of the following statement is true regarding it?

[Image: https://cerebellum-web-static.s3.amazonaws.com/media/public/images/2024/03/18/picture31_5N43mZh.jpg]
options: Can be caused by both staphylococcus and streptococcus
It extends deep into subcutaneous tissues

explanation.detail:
• Explanation
• Option A. Can be caused by both Staphylococcus and Streptococcus:
• Erysipelas is caused by Streptococcus, especially Group A Streptococcus ( Streptococcus pyogenes ). Staphylococcus is not a causative agent for erysipelas. Therefore, this statement is not true .
• Option B. It extends deep into subcutaneous tissues:
• Erysipelas typically involves the upper layers of the skin and the superficial dermis but does not extend deep into subcutaneous tissues. It is more superficial compared to cellulitis. Therefore, this statement is not true .
• Option D. It never involves the ear:
• Erysipelas can affect various parts of the body, including the ear (Milian's Ear Sign). Ear involvement is called Milian's ear sign , and it is a feature distinguishing erysipelas from cellulitis, because the pinna has no deeper dermis and subcutaneous tissue .
• It is not restricted to any specific anatomical location. Therefore, this statement is not true .
• Educational Objective
• Erysipelas is an acute bacterial skin infection, primarily caused by Streptococcus , and is characterized by well-demarcated, painful, warm , and often firm or raised skin lesions . It can affect various parts of the body, including the ear.
• Bolognia Textbook of Dermatology 4 th edition Page no 1269
explanation.ref: Rooks textbook of dermatology- 9 th Edition page no 26.19

subject: Dermatology
question:
A 6-year-old female presented with complaints of fever associated with chills, sore throat, and facial rash for 5 days. Physical examination reveals an erythematous pharynx without exudates and perioral honey-coloured crusts as shown in the image below. What is the most appropriate therapy?

[Image: https://cerebellum-web-static.s3.amazonaws.com/media/public/images/2024/03/18/picture34.jpg]

explanation.detail:
• Explanation
• Option A. Isotretinoin ointment:
• Isotretinoin is not indicated for the treatment of bacterial infections like impetigo.
• Option B. Topical steroids:
• Topical steroids are contraindicated in impetigo because they can exacerbate bacterial skin infections .
• Option C. Topical salicylic acid:
• Topical salicylic acid is not indicated for the treatment of bacterial infections like impetigo.
• Educational Objective
• I mpetigo is a common bacterial skin infection, and the treatment of choice is topical antibiotics . The use of topical antibiotics helps to target and eliminate the causative bacteria, such as Staphylococcus aureus or Streptococcus pyogenes.
• Bolognia Textbook of Dermatology 4 th edition Page no 1261
explanation.ref: Rooks textbook of dermatology- 9 th Edition page no 26.15

subject: Dermatology
question:
A 45-year-old butcher presents with a painful, erythematous, and slightly swollen lesion on his left hand as shown in the image below. He reports that the symptoms started a few days after he handled some raw fish. The lesion has well-defined borders and does not present with lymphangitic streaking. His vital signs are stable, and he has no fever. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

[Image: https://cerebellum-web-static.s3.amazonaws.com/media/public/images/2024/03/18/picture35.jpg]

explanation.detail:
• Explanation
• It is a superficial skin infection caused by Staphylococcus aureus or Streptococcus pyogenes, characterized by honey-colored crusts . It commonly affects children and is not typically associated with occupational exposure .
• It is a deeper form of impetigo penetrating into the dermis, often presenting with ulcerative lesions covered by a crust . It is not consistent with the patient’s occupational history or clinical presentation.
• Option D. Scalded skin syndrome:
• It is caused by exfoliative toxins of Staphylococcus aureus, typically presents with widespread erythema, fever, and skin sloughing . The localized nature of this patient's lesion and the absence of systemic symptoms make this diagnosis unlikely.
• Educational Objective
• Identify Erysipeloid as a localized skin infection often associated with occupational exposure to fish, meat, or poultry , presenting with well-defined, erythematous lesions, usually without systemic symptoms.
• Bolognia Textbook of Dermatology 4 th edition Page no 1278
explanation.ref: Rooks textbook of dermatology- 9 th Edition page no 26.46

subject: Dermatology
question:
A 30-year-old male marathon runner presents to the clinic with complaints of malodour and multiple small, crater-like depressions on the weight-bearing parts of his feet as shown in the image below. He mentions the problem seems to worsen with his running schedule, especially in his occlusive footwear. Physical examination reveals numerous, discrete, 1-3 mm pits on the soles of his feet. There is no scaling or interdigital involvement. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

[Image: https://cerebellum-web-static.s3.amazonaws.com/media/public/images/2024/03/18/picture36.jpg]

explanation.detail:
• Explanation
• Option A. Tinea pedis
• It is a fungal infection that typically presents with scaling and maceration , especially between the toes , and might not have the distinct pitted appearance.
• Option B. Superficial plantar warts
• It is caused by human papillomavirus , often have a cauliflower-like appearance and may contain small black thrombosed capillaries as shown in the image below.
• It tends to affect warm, moist areas like skin folds and the interdigital spaces , and is often associated with erythema and scaling , unlike the presentation in this scenario.
• Educational Objective
• Pitted keratolysis clinical presentation - S mall, crater-like depressions on the soles of the feet, often with an associated foul odor, and understand that it is typically seen in individuals with excessive foot s weating and those who wear occlusive footwear.
• Bolognia Textbook of Dermatology 4 th edition Page no 1276
explanation.ref: Rooks textbook of dermatology- 9 th Edition page no 26.42

subject: Dermatology
question:
A 40-year-old female with acute myeloid leukemia presents to the emergency department with a two-day history of fever, chills, and a painful skin lesion. On physical examination, she has a rapidly expanding erythematous lesion on her chest with a black necrotic centre as shown in the image below. She is currently neutropenic due to recent chemotherapy. Which of the following is the most likely etiology of her skin lesion?

[Image: https://cerebellum-web-static.s3.amazonaws.com/media/public/images/2024/03/18/picture39.jpg]

explanation.detail:
• Explanation
• Option B. Hansen’s disease or leprosy:
• It is a chronic infection caused by Mycobacterium leprae and presents with skin and peripheral nerve involvement over months to years , not acutely.
• Option C. Parapox virus septicaemia:
• It is not a recognized medical condition. Parapox virus infections are typically associated with orf, which presents with Nodular lesions , weeping pustules on hands/fingers in individuals who have contact with sheep and goats .
• Option D. Staphylococcal septicaemia:
• It can cause a variety of skin manifestations, including abscesses or cellulitis , but the distinctive necrotic lesions are more characteristic of Pseudomonas infection in the context of ecthyma gangrenosum .
• Educational Objective
• Ecthyma gangrenosum i s a cutaneous sign of Pseudomonas aeruginosa septicaemia , particularly in immunocompromised patients such as those with neutropenia secondary to chemotherapy .
• Bolognia Textbook of Dermatology 4 th edition Page no 1281, 1437
explanation.ref: Rooks textbook of dermatology- 9 th Edition page no 30.49

subject: Dermatology
question:
Match the wood lamp findings with the respective diseases.

[Image: https://cerebellum-web-static.s3.amazonaws.com/media/public/images/2024/03/18/screenshot-2024-03-16-155851.jpg]

explanation.detail:
• Educational Objective :
• The Wood's lamp uses UVA light with a wavelength of 365nm ( 320 - 400 nm) for the diagnosis of various skin conditions based on the fluorescence characteristics of the skin under this specific range of ultraviolet light.
• Rook's Textbook of Dermatology -9th Edition, Chapter 26 Page no 26.40
explanation.ref: Rooks textbook of dermatology- 9 th Edition volume I Chapter 4 page no 4.20

question:
In a diabetic patient, the following lesions are present in the armpit. What would be observed during a Wood's lamp examination?

[Image: https://cerebellum-web-static.s3.amazonaws.com/media/public/images/2024/03/18/picture45.jpg]

explanation.detail:

• The Wood's lamp uses UVA light with a wavelength of 365nm ( 320 - 400 nm) for the diagnosis of various skin conditions based on the fluorescence characteristics of the skin under this specific range of ultraviolet light.
• Rook's Textbook of Dermatology -9th Edition, Chapter 26 Page no 26.40

subject: Dermatology
question:
Which of the following differentials should not be considered for the lesion seen in the image developed in a forest officer?

[Image: https://cerebellum-web-static.s3.amazonaws.com/media/public/images/2024/03/18/picture51.jpg]

explanation.detail:
• Explanation
• Option A: Scrub typhus (chigger-borne typhus)
• The infective agent of scrub typhus, Orientia tsutsugamushi (Previously known as Rickettsia tsutsugamushi ), is conveyed to humans from its natural rodent reservoir by the bites of the Trombiculid mite .
• Humans are infected when they are bitten by the mite larvae known as chiggers . The incubation period is 1- 3 weeks . The primary lesion, or eschar, is a firm papule up to 1 mm in diameter surmounted by a vesicle, which dries to form a black crust.
• Option C: Cutaneous anthrax ( Hide porters’ disease )
• Anthrax is a zoonotic infection with Bacillus anthracis. Between 1 and 5 days after infection, an irritable papule develops at the site of inoculation. A bulla on a red oedematous base soon follows. The bulla ruptures and forms a haemorrhagic crust which will undergo necrosis . Surrounding this several satellite lesions filled with serum or yellow fluid are arranged around a central necrotic lesion which is covered by a black eschar. This is called as Malignant pustule as shown in the image below .
• Option D: Healing brown recluse spider bite.
• Bite of Loxosceles reclusa (Loxoscelism ) → Target lesion develops → Development of eschar → Heals with Ulcer f ormation.
• Diseases / Skin conditions presenting with ESCHAR formation:
• Educational Objective
• Kyasanur Forest Disease tick-borne viral hemorrhagic fever, primarily presenting with haemorrhagic and neurological symptoms, should not be considered for skin lesion, Eschar, unlike the other mentioned differentials.
• Ananthanarayan & Paniker’s textbook of Microbiology 10 th edition Page no 249, 250, 415, 530
explanation.ref: Rooks textbook of dermatology- 9 th Edition page no 26.79, 34.33

subject: Dermatology
question: A 32-year-old male with a history of diarrhoea and neurogenic bladder requiring intermittent self-catheterization presents to the clinic with a complaint of a new rash around his groin. On examination, you note a patch of erythematous skin with multiple pustules. A swab of the area grows a gram-negative, facultatively anaerobic bacterium. Considering the patient's medical history and the typical skin flora that can be found in the groin area due to faecal contamination, which organism is most likely responsible for this presentation?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. Staphylococcus epidermidis and B. Staphylococcus aureus
• They are part of the normal skin flora. S. aureus can cause skin infections, but the presence of a gram-negative organism in the culture points away from these bacteria.
• Option C. Candida albicans: It is a common cause of fungal infections in skin folds but would not present as a gram-negative bacterium in cultures.
• Educational Objective
• E. coli is not typically part of the normal skin flora, it can be present in certain areas such as the groin due to Fecal contamination , particularly in patients with conditions that affect normal bowel or bladder function.
explanation.ref: Rooks textbook of dermatology- 9 th Edition page no 2.13, 26.3

subject: Dermatology
question:
A 40-year-old female presents with red, subcutaneous violaceous swelling over the cervical region from the last several months. Some of the lesions ulcerated & underwent spontaneous healing leaving typical cord like scars. Infection of the underlying cervical lymph node is present with multiple sinuses. Histology showed marked caseous necrosis. Labs revealed CD4 count< 50 cells/mm3. What is the most likely diagnosis?

[Image: https://cerebellum-web-static.s3.amazonaws.com/media/public/images/2024/02/21/picture1_dy7uzDg.jpg]

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option B. Lupus vulgaris :
• Lupus vulgaris is a chronic form of cutaneous tuberculosis with apple jelly nodules , typically not associated with deep nodules , ulceration , or sinus tracts as in this case.
• Option C. Acne vulgaris:
• Acne vulgaris is a common skin condition characterized by comedones, papules, and pustules , not by ulceration or sinus tract formation, and is not associated with caseous necrosis or immunocompromised states.
• Option D. Erythema nodosum :
• Erythema nodosum presents as tender red nodules , typically on the lower legs , and does not lead to ulceration or sinus tract formation. It is also not associated with caseous necrosis.
• Educational Objective:
• The presence of red, subcutaneous violaceous swelling that ulcerates and heals with scarring, coupled with infected cervical lymph nodes and sinus tract formation , in an i mmunocompromised patient with a low CD4 count and histology showing caseous necrosis , is indicative of scrofuloderma , a cutaneous form of tuberculosis.
explanation.ref: Rooks Textbook of Dermatology- 9 th Edition Chapter 27 Page No 27.13

subject: Dermatology
question: All of the following statements are true about tuberculosis verrucosa cutis EXCEPT?
options: Anatomist's Wart is the other name
Post Primary Tuberculosis with poor immunity
Antitubercular Therapy is used in the management

explanation.short: Post Primary Tuberculosis with poor immunity
explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Tuberculosis verrucosa cutis —
• Tuberculosis verrucosa cutis (TBVC, also known as prosecutor’s Wart, Anatomist's Wart (OptionA), Verruca Necrogenica, and Warty Tuberculosis ) is a form of Cutaneous TB that occurs after direct inoculation of the mycobacteria into the skin of a previously sensitized host with good immunity against the bacillus. (OptionB) Inoculation typically occurs from an exogenous source. In adults , TBVC most frequently develops on the Acral Extremities ; the Fingers and Dorsum of the hands are commonly affected. The Ankles or Buttocks are frequent sites for lesion occurrence in children.
• The skin lesions are usually solitary and manifest as painless, violaceous, or brown , red , indurated warty plaques that range from 1 to 5 cm in diameter . TBVC grows via peripheral extension .
• Due to the strong immune response , the lesion is paucibacillary (OptionC) . Histology shows hyperkeratosis with no granulomas .
• The treatment of cutaneous TB is the same as that for systemic TB . Antitubercular therapy is used in the management (OptionD)
• Educational Objective:
• Tuberculosis Verrucosa Cutis, also known as Anatomist's Wart is a paucibacillary cutaneous tuberculosis that occurs in individuals with a good or moderat e level of immunity and is managed with Antitubercular Therapy .
explanation.ref: Rooks Textbook of Dermatology- 9 th Edition Chapter 27 Page No 27.19

subject: Dermatology
question: Identify Tuberculids among the following options?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation
• Option A. Lupus Vulgaris:
• This is a form of cutaneous tuberculosis characterized by reddish-brown papules or nodules with apple jelly nodules . It is not a tuberculid but rather a specific form of cutaneous tuberculosis.
• Scrofuloderma is not a tuberculid but a form of cutaneous tuberculosis characterized by skin ulcers and abscesses . It is not related to hypersensitivity reactions.
• Option D. Warty Tuberculosis:
• Tuberculosis Verrucosa Cutis —Tuberculosis Verrucosa Cutis (TBVC, also known as prosecutor’s wart, Anatomist's wart, verruca necrogenica, and warty tuberculosis ) is a form of cutaneous TB that occurs after direct inoculation of the mycobacteria into the skin of a previously sensitized host with good immunity against the bacillus.
• Educational Objective:
• Tuberculids , such as lichen scrofulosorum , represent cutaneous hypersensitivity reactions to mycobacterial antigens in individuals with a high degree of immunity to tuberculosis and no active mycobacterial infection at the site of skin lesions.
• Rooks Textbook of Dermatology- 9 th Edition Chapter 27 Page No 27.25 27.28
explanation.ref: Rooks Textbook of Dermatology- 9 th Edition Chapter 27 Page No 27.25 27.28

subject: Dermatology
question: Buruli Ulcer is caused by?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. M. Avium-Intracellulare :
• This bacterium is associated with infections in immunocompromised individuals, particularly those with HIV/AIDS. It is not the cause of Buruli ulcer.
• Option C. M. Kansasii :
• M. kansasii , which is another mycobacterial species but not the cause of Buruli ulcer.
• Option D. M. Tuberculosis:
• Mycobacterium tuberculosis is the causative agent of tuberculosis (TB) but is not responsible for Buruli ulcer.
• Educational Objective:
• Buruli ulcer, a chronic skin infection resulting in large ulcers primarily on the limbs, is caused by Mycobacterium ulcerans .
explanation.ref: Rooks Textbook of Dermatology- 9 th Edition Chapter 27 Page No 27.38

subject: Dermatology
question: Identify the true statement among the following options?
options: Fish Tank Granuloma is caused by Mycobacterium Ulcerans
Eating infected Tuna Fishes causes Fish Tank Granuloma
Erythema Induratum of Bazin is due to Hypersensitivity reaction to M. Tuberculosis
Erythema Nodosum is an example for Lobular Panniculitis

explanation.short: Ans. C) Erythema induratum of Bazin is due to Hypersensitivity reaction to M. tuberculosis.
explanation.detail:
• Educational Objective:
• Erythema induratum of Bazin is a hypersensitivity reaction to Mycobacterium tuberculosis and is classified as a tuberculid. It typically presents with tender nodules on the back of the legs .
explanation.ref: Rooks Textbook of Dermatology- 9 th Edition Chapter 27 Page No 27.29 27.31

subject: Dermatology
question:
A 6-year-old boy has tiny pinhead sized flesh-colored papules over his elbow & penis. Histopathology of the lesion reveals the following feature. Which of the following is NOT TRUE about this condition?

[Image: https://cerebellum-web-static.s3.amazonaws.com/media/public/images/2024/02/21/picture5_IbkKHeL.jpg]
options: Presents with tiny pinhead sized papules mainly on forearms
Most commonly seen in elderly individuals

explanation.short: Ans. B) Most commonly seen in elderly individuals
explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A: The dorsum of the forearms or penis is where Lichen Nitidus most commonly manifests as tiny pinhead-sized flesh-coloured papules.
• In early lesions, Lichen Nitidus and Lichen planus share a similar histological profile.
• Option D: Mucous membrane involvement is quite rare .
• Option C: True Koebners phenomenon is evident.
• Lesions are self-limiting & no treatment is required.
• Ball and Claw Arrangement :
• The Ball is comprised of well circumscribed granulomatous infiltrate of lymphocytes, epithelioid cells & occasional Langhan’s giant cells that are clutched by surrounding hyperplastic rete ridges , giving the overall claw clutching ball appearance. Lesions are typically asymptomatic and infrequently need medical attention.
• The histology of Lichen Nitidus is shown in the image below, where a well-defined granuloma is surrounded by rete pegs and appears to be claw clutching a ball .
• Educational Objective:
• Lichen nitidus, characterized by tiny pinhead-sized flesh-colored papules , commonly affects children and young adults , can exhibit Koebner's phenomenon , and rarely involves mucous membranes. It is not most commonly seen in elderly individuals.
• Rooks Textbook of Dermatology- 9 th Edition Chapter 37 Page No 37.10 37.11
• Fitzpatrick’s Dermatology 9 th Edition Page No 554
explanation.ref: Rooks Textbook of Dermatology- 9 th Edition Chapter 37 Page No 37.10 37.11

subject: Dermatology
question:
A 24-years man presents with a tender erythematous plaque in the groin. It has been there for 4 years and slowly progressing. Examination revealed well demarcated indurated annular shaped plaque with irregular borders which was progressing towards one side & scarring on another border. There was no regional lymphadenopathy, and systemic examination revealed no abnormalities. Considering the above features, a diagnosis of most common type of Cutaneous tuberculosis in adults is made. Which of the following statements is FALSE about this condition?

[Image: https://cerebellum-web-static.s3.amazonaws.com/media/public/images/2024/02/21/picture8_uA8hUd4.jpg]
options: It occurs in previously sensitised patients
Apple Jelly Nodules can be seen

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Diagnosis is Lupus Vulgaris & it is a Paucibacillary condition.
• Points in favour are
• Age (24 years) - LV is more common in children and young adults History of exposure to tuberculosis Chronic presentation (4 years) Progressive nature Annular erythematous plaque with irregular borders. Induration Healing with scarring
• All of the above findings are in favour of lupus vulgaris.
• Lupus vulgaris is a chronic form of skin TB seen in patients with moderate immunity to TB bacilli It is seen more common in females .
• It occurs due to hematogenous , lymphatic , or contiguous spread from elsewhere in the body, or from exogenous inoculation. It occurs in previously sensitized hosts with good immunity, hence Paucibacillary. (OptionB)
• It presents as single or few Erythematous / Violaceous Annular Plaques with Central Scarring and extending Centrifugally.(OptionC) The periphery shows erythematous deep-seated nodules which on diascopy may stand out as Apple jelly nodules . (OptionD) They are most commonly seen in buttocks , upper extremities , and face. The center is depigmented with paper-thin scar. Papules may recur in the scarred areas , which enlarge and coalesce. This leads to the formation of large, firm, elevated plaques.
• Histologic examination & a positive culture for M. tuberculosis/ bovis confirm the diagnosis. Most prominent histopathologic feature: Typical granulomas with scanty or absent caseation.
• Secondary scarring& squamous cell carcinomas may develop rarely.
• Educational Objective:
• Lupus vulgaris is a paucibacillary condition occurring in previously sensitized patients, characterized by slow progression , healing with central scarring , and the potential presence of apple jelly nodules on diascopy.
• Rooks Textbook of Dermatology- 9 th Edition Chapter 27 Page No 27.21 27.22
• Bolognia Textbook of Dermatology 4 th Edition Page No 1306
explanation.ref: Rooks Textbook of Dermatology- 9 th Edition Chapter 27 Page No 27.21 27.22

subject: Dermatology
question:
A 25-year-old patient presented with multiple pinpointed dome shaped skin lesions over hands as shown in the image below. What is TRUE about the diagnosis of the patient?

[Image: https://cerebellum-web-static.s3.amazonaws.com/media/public/images/2024/02/21/picture9_N1D8mAv.jpg]
options: It’s a form of id reaction.
The presence of Wickhams Striae is classical for this condition.

explanation.short: Ans. D) Mucous Membrane is not involved.
explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. An id reaction is a secondary skin eruption occurring in response to a primary infection or inflammation elsewhere in the body. Lichen nitidus is not classified as an id reaction.
• Option B . Koebnerization, or the Koebner phenomenon , can occur in lichen nitidus, where new lesions develop in areas of skin trauma.
• Option C . Wickham's striae are classically associated with lichen planus , not lichen nitidus, and are not a feature of this condition.
• Educational Objective:
• Lichen nitidus is characterized by numerous small, shiny papules primarily on the skin, with the mucous membranes generally not involved . It is important to differentiate it from lichen planus , which can involve the mucous membranes and present with Wickham's striae.
• Bolognia Textbook of Dermatology 4 th Edition Page No 203
explanation.ref: Rooks Textbook of Dermatology- 9 th Edition Page No 37.10

subject: Dermatology
question:
A 20-year-old woman presents with a chronic, progressive skin lesion on her buttock. On physical examination, a well-demarcated, erythematous plaque with deep-seated nodules is noted which was progressing at one end and becoming atrophied at the other end as shown in the image below. Diascopy of the nodules reveals an "apple jelly" color. The patient has a history of a positive tuberculin skin test but no history of active tuberculosis. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

[Image: https://cerebellum-web-static.s3.amazonaws.com/media/public/images/2024/02/21/picture10_9EK7eih.jpg]

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• This typically presents as well-demarcated, erythematous plaque s with silvery scales , most commonly on extensor surfaces, and scalp .
• It is associated with systemic manifestations such as psoriatic arthritis and does not produce apple jelly nodules on diascopy.
• It is a multisystem granulomatous disorder that can present with skin manifestations known as lupus pernio, which can appear similar to lupus vulgaris but is associated with systemic symptoms such as dyspnea, cough, and ocular involvement.
• It can present with apple jelly nodules on diascopy , but rest of the features which are in favor of Lupus vulgaris help us to rule out this option.
• Option D. Granuloma Aannulare
• This is a benign, self-limited dermatological condition that presents with annular plaques which are Non itchy and lack any scaling , presenttypically over joints and is not associated with the apple jelly nodules seen in cutaneous tuberculosis.
• Educational Objective:
• The clinical features of Lupus Vulgaris , a form of cutaneous tuberculosis , including the well-demarcated plaque , deep-seated nodules , and the characteristic "Apple Jelly" Colour on diascopy. Understand the association with a positive tuberculin skin test and the chronic, progressive nature of the condition.
• Bolognia Textbook of Dermatology 4 th Edition Page No 1306
explanation.ref: Rooks Textbook of Dermatology- 9 th Edition Chapter 27 Page No 27.21 27.22

subject: Dermatology
question:
A six-year-old child presents with a single warty plaque on the elbow. Upon examination, the lesion is well-demarcated, raised, and verrucous. The patient has no significant past medical history and reports no recent fevers, weight loss, or night sweats. A biopsy of the lesion is performed, revealing non-caseating granulomas. No other skin lesions are noted on physical examination. Given the clinical and histopathological findings, which of the following statements is true about the condition?

[Image: https://cerebellum-web-static.s3.amazonaws.com/media/public/images/2024/02/21/picture16_ND5CnJ9.jpg]
options: Apple jelly nodules can be seen on biopsy
It is a type of post-primary inoculation tuberculosis
Treatment is cautery of the lesion

explanation.short: It is a type of post-primary inoculation tuberculosis
explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. Apple jelly nodules can be seen on biopsy :
• Apple jelly nodules are observed on clinical examination as translucent yellow-brown nodules after diascopy. They are seen in Lupus vulgaris, Leishmaniasis and Sarcoidosis.
• Option C. The patient has low immunity :
• The development of non-caseating granulomas in response to Mycobacterium tuberculosis suggests a competentcell-mediated immune response . In immunocompromised patients , tuberculosis lesions may not form granulomas due to an inadequate immune response.
• Option D. Treatment is cautery of the lesion:
• The mainstay of treatment for tuberculosis verrucosa cutis is anti-tuberculous therapy , not cautery.
• Regression of lesion after 3months of ATT. Cautery may be used for certain skin conditions, but not typically for infectious diseases like tuberculosis.
• Educational Objective:
• The correct identification of a warty plaque with non-caseating granulomas on biopsy in a child with no systemic symptoms suggests tuberculosis verrucosa cutis , a form of post-primary inoculation tuberculosis.
• Bolognia Textbook of Dermatology 4 th Edition Page No 1306
explanation.ref: Rooks Textbook of Dermatology- 9 th Edition Page No 27.19

subject: Dermatology
question:
A 14-year-old girl presents with chronic facial lesions, characterized by erythematous plaques with central scarring and an advancing edge, as shown in the image. There is no history of systemic symptoms such as fever or weight loss, and the patient reports a gradual progression of the lesions over several months. The patient lives in an urban setting with no known exposure to armadillos or endemic areas for leprosy. A biopsy is performed, which is expected to show granulomatous inflammation. Which of the following treatment will be started in this patient?

[Image: https://cerebellum-web-static.s3.amazonaws.com/media/public/images/2024/02/21/picture18_UkuyqNN.jpg]

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. Amphotericin B:
• Amphotericin B is an antifungal medication used for serious systemic fungal infections. It is not indicated in the treatment of cutaneous tuberculosis.
• Itraconazole is an antifungal agent that is commonly used for fungal infections of the skin like dermatophytosis . It would not be effective against mycobacterial infections.
• Minocycline is a tetracycline antibiotic with anti-inflammatory properties, often used for acne vulgaris. It is not the treatment of choice for mycobacterial skin infections.
• Educational Objective:
• The appropriate treatment for lupus vulgaris , a form of cutaneous tuberculosis , is antituberculous therapy which includes rifampicin as part of a combination regimen.
• Bolognia Textbook of Dermatology 4 th Edition Page No 1311
explanation.ref: Rooks Textbook of Dermatology- 9 th Edition Page No 27.21

subject: Dermatology
question: Which of the following statement is TRUE regarding Tuberculid?
options: Erythema Nodosum occurs over Anterior Leg.
Lichen Scrofulosorum presents as Pruritic Lichenoid Papules over Trunk.
Multiple Bacilli can be seen in Skin Biopsy.
Patient has good response to ATT.

explanation.short: Patient has good response to ATT.
explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. Erythema nodosum occurs over anterior leg:
• While erythema nodosum typically presents on the anterior aspect of the legs , it is not classified as a True tuberculid . Erythema nodosum is a form of septalpanniculitis that can be associated with various systemic diseases, including tuberculosis.
• Option B. Lichen scrofulosorum presents as pruritic lichenoid papules over trunk:
• Lichen scrofulosorum does present as lichenoid papules , but they are typically not pruritic and are considered a tuberculid.
• Option C. Multiple bacilli can be seen in skin biopsy:
• Tuberculids are paucibacillary , meaning there are few or no detectable bacilli within the lesions. They represent a hypersensitivity reaction to mycobacterial antigens rather than a direct bacterial infection.
• Educational Objective:
• The true statement about tuberculids is that patients have a good response to antituberculous therapy ( ATT ), as tuberculids are a hypersensitivity reaction to mycobacterial antigens in individuals with generally competent immune systems.
• Bolognia Textbook of Dermatology 4 th Edition Page No 1307
explanation.ref: Rooks Textbook of Dermatology- 9 th Edition Page No 27.25

subject: Dermatology
question:
A young man presents with the following skin lesions as shown in the image below. Which of the following organisms can least likely spread through dermal and subcutaneous lymphatics?

[Image: https://cerebellum-web-static.s3.amazonaws.com/media/public/images/2024/02/21/picture19_3ITy0MW.jpg]

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. Mycobacterium Marinum
• It can cause a chronic condition known as "aquarium granuloma " or "fish tank granuloma," which can spread along the lymphatics.
• It can cause cutaneous nocardiosis , which may manifest as lymphocutaneous disease, with spread along lymphatic channels.
• Option D. Sporothrix schenckii
• It is the causative agent of sporotrichosis , which classically spreads via lymphatics, leading to a nodular lymphangitis.
• Important OrganismsTransmittedin Linear/Sporotrichoid Pattern
• Educational Objective:
• Staphylococcus aureus typically does not spread through the dermal and subcutaneous lymphatics as commonly as the other organisms listed, which are known for this pattern of dissemination.
• Bolognia Textbook of Dermatology 4 th Edition Page No 9
explanation.ref: Rooks Textbook of Dermatology- 9 th Edition Page No 105.51

subject: Dermatology
question: All of the following are types of skin tuberculosis EXCEPT?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. Lupus Vulgaris:
• Lupus vulgaris is indeed a type of skin tuberculosis . It is a chronic, progressive form that typically presents with reddish-brown nodules and plaques that can ulcerate or scar.
• Scrofuloderma is a type of skin tuberculosis that occurs due to direct extension from an underlying focus of infection, such as an infected lymph node or bone.
• Option D. Lichen Scrofulosorum:
• Lichen scrofulosorum is a tuberculid, a type of skin condition that represents a hypersensitivity reaction to tuberculosis or its antigens .
• Educational Objective:
• Lichen planus is not a type of skin tuberculosis but a separate chronic inflammatory skin disorder. Lupus vulgaris, Scrofuloderma, and lichen scrofulosorum are all associated with tuberculosis.
• Bolognia Textbook of Dermatology 4 th Edition Page No 1307
explanation.ref: Rooks Textbook of Dermatology- 9 th Edition Page No 27.25

subject: Dermatology
question:
A 15-year-old boy presents to the clinic with a large painless ulcer on the lower extremity as shown in the image below. The ulcer has a necrotic base and undermined edges. He has no systemic symptoms. His family reports that they live near a wetland area in a region known to have cases of similar ulcers. There has been no response to standard wound care and antibiotic treatment for common bacterial skin infections. A smear from the lesion shows acid-fast bacilli.What is the most likely diagnosis?

[Image: https://cerebellum-web-static.s3.amazonaws.com/media/public/images/2024/02/21/picture20_yHDB4ev.jpg]

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option B. Cutaneous Tuberculosis:
• Although cutaneous tuberculosis is caused by an acid-fast bacillus (Mycobacterium tuberculosis ), it typically presents in areas with a high prevalence of tuberculosis.
• Option C. Cutaneous Leishmaniasis :
• Cutaneous leishmaniasis causes skin ulcers , but these are typically painful and often associated with travel to endemic areas , such as the Middle East or South America . A smear would show Leishmania parasites, not acid-fast bacilli.
• Option D. Diabetic Ulcer:
• Diabetic ulcers are typically found on pressure points of the feet, are associated with neuropathy and vascular disease , and do not usually present with undermined edges or a necrotic base unless secondarily infected.
• Educational Objective:
• The presentation of a large, painless ulcer with a necrotic base and undermined edges in a patient from a wetland area , with a smear showing acid-fast bacilli , is characteristic of Buruli ulcer , caused by Mycobacterium ulcerans .
explanation.ref: Rooks Textbook of Dermatology- 9 th Edition Chapter 27 Page No 27.38

subject: Dermatology
question: All the statements are true about scabies EXCEPT?
options: Burrows are present mainly in Stratum Corneum
Animal Scabies is characterized by Burrows
5% Permethrin Cream from Neck to Toe in Adults & from Head to Toe in Children is Topical Agent of Choice

explanation.short: Ans. C) Animal Scabies is characterised by Burrows.
explanation.detail:
• Explanation
• Scabies is an ectoparasitic infestation caused by the Sarcoptesscabei var hominis / Itch mite / Sarcoptesscabei
• The incubation period of Scabies is 3-4 weeks ( 21-28 days ) However, Reinfection of a previously cured individual may provoke immediate symptoms . Burrows in scabies are typically found in the stratum corneum as depicted in the below image.
• In most people the trails of the burrowing mites appear brownish linear or s shaped tracks in the skin & occur as a result of excavation of the adult mite in the epidermis . S shaped track , pointed with black arrows in the below image.
• Lesions are found in the Circle of Hebra (imaginary line formed by connecting the sites of involvement in scabies like, web spaces, feet, ventral wrists, elbows, cack, breasts, buttocks & external genitals).
• Itching worsens at night & when the patient is warm. Nocturnal pruritus is the most common symptom associated with scabies.
• Water-washed diseases , such as scabies , are infections brought on by poor personal hygiene and a lack of access to clean water . The distinctive signs include intense itching and shallow burrows .
• A single overnight application of 5% permethrin cream from neck to toe in adults & from head to toe in children is the drug of choice for the treatment of scabies in all ages including infants ( at least 2 months old ) & pregnant women.
• Educational Objective
• While human scabies involves burrow formation in the skin , animal scabies does not typically lead to burrow formation when transmitted to humans .
explanation.ref: Rooks textbook of dermatology- 9th Edition Chapter 34 page no 34.39-34.47

subject: Dermatology
question:
Causative Agent & Drug of Choice for the condition shown below would be?

[Image: https://cerebellum-web-static.s3.amazonaws.com/media/public/images/2024/02/21/picture24_BxEOorG.jpg]

explanation.detail:
• Explanation
• The serpiginous / serpent-like skin lesions seen are cutaneous larva migrans or larva currens . The hookworms Ancylostoma duodenale and Necator americanus are the least likely to result in cutaneous larva migrans in humans . Non-human hookworms like Ancylostomabraziliense and Ancylostoma caninum that are acquired through direct skin contact with soil contaminated by dog or cat faeces are the cause of cutaneous larva migrans . It is common after visiting tropical or endemic regions , and symptoms usually appear within the first two weeks . First line treatment: oral ivermectin at 200 microgram/kg or albendazole. An inflamed papule , frequently found in the space between the toes , indicates the point of entry . Later, as the larvae migrate through the epidermis , serpentine or linear single-track lines become visible . The growth of the lesions can reach 1 to 2 cm per day .
• Larva Currens Skin Lesions
• Educational Objective
• Cutaneous larva migrans , presenting with serpiginous skin lesions , is commonly caused by Ancylostomabraziliense , and the treatment of choice is Albendazole .
• Rooks Textbook of Dermatology- 9 th Edition Volume I Chapter 4. Page No 4.13-14
explanation.ref: Rooks Textbook of Dermatology- 9 th Edition Volume I Chapter 4. Page No 4.13-14

subject: Dermatology
question: Chiclero ulcer /Gum tree harvester’s ulcer is caused by?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. Enterobius vermicularis :
• Enterobius vermicularis , commonly known as the pinworm, is a nematode parasite that causes enterobiasis , primarily affecting the gastrointestinal tract, particularly the anus and rectum , and does not cause ulcers. Most frequent in children associated with nocturnal pruritus ani.
• Ancylostomabraziliense is a species of hookworm that can cause cutaneous larva migrans , presenting as serpiginous skin lesions , not ulcers.
• Option D. Strongyloides stercoralis :
• Strongyloides stercoralis is an intestinal roundworm that causes strongyloidiasis, which can lead to severe systemic infection , especially in immunocompromised hosts, but is not associated with Chiclero’s ulcer.
• Educational Objective
• Chiclero’s ulcer , also known as Gum tree harvester’s ulcer , is caused by Leishmania Mexicana , which is transmitted by the bite of an infected sandfly.
explanation.ref: Rooks Textbook of Dermatology- 9 th Edition Chapter 33 Page No 33.47

subject: Dermatology
question: Maculae Ceruleae are seen in?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. Pediculosis capitis:
• Pediculosis capitis, or head lice infestation, does not typically present with maculae ceruleae. The primary symptom is itching of the scalp , and the findings include lice and nits (lice eggs) on the hair and scalp.
• Option B. Pediculosis corporis:
• Pediculosis corporis, or body lice infestation, is characterized by itching , especially along the seams of clothing where lice tend to reside . Maculae ceruleae are not a characteristic finding in this condition.
• Scabies, caused by the mite Sarcoptes scabiei , presents with intense itching , especially at night, and a rash that includes burrows but not maculae ceruleae.
• Educational Objective
• Maculae Ceruleae, Bluish-Gray Skin Macules , are characteristic of an infestation with Pthirus pubis , the pubic louse. These macules are a result of louse feeding and are not seen in other common parasitic infestations like pediculosis capitis, pediculosis corporis, or scabies.
• Rooks Textbook of Dermatology- 9th Edition Chapter 34 Page No 34.21- 34.22
• Harrison’s Principles of Internal Medicine 19th Edition Page No 2746
• Andrew’s Diseases of the Skin Clinical Dermatology – 12th Edition Page No 441
• Online resources: https://dermnetnz.org/topics/pubic-lice https://www.cdc.gov/parasites/lice/pubic/index.html
explanation.ref: Rooks Textbook of Dermatology- 9th Edition Chapter 34 Page No 34.21- 34.22

subject: Dermatology
question: An 8-month-old child presented with itchy exudative papulovesicular lesions on face, palms, soles. Father of the child also has similar complaints associated with nocturnal itching. A diagnosis of scabies is made. In this disease, what part of the baby’s body can be affected, but is UNLIKELY to show lesions in the father?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Multiple itchy eczematous exudative papulovesicular lesions in the face palms soles & trunk are characteristic of infantile scabies . Mites prefer HAIRY skin & areas of low sebum production , so Face , Palms , soles are spared in adults but characteristically involved in infants .
• Educational Objective
• Scabies in infants can present with lesions on the face, palms, and soles, which are areas less commonly affected in adults .
explanation.ref: Rooks Textbook of Dermatology- 9 th Edition Chapter 34 Page No 34.40

subject: Dermatology
question: Which of the following statements is TRUE about Scabies?
options: Number of Lesions correspond to Number of Mites
50% Topical Permethrin is used for treatment
Ivermectin is an Oral Medication used in the treatment
Lesions in Nodular Scabies are not Pruritic

explanation.short: Ans. C) Ivermectin is an Oral Medication used in the Treatment.
explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A : The number of lesions does not correspond to the number of mites .
• Option B : Topical 5% permethrin (NOT 50%) is used in the treatment of scabies.
• Option D : An unusual scabies manifestation is nodular scabies . It is extremely itchy .
• Educational Objective
• Ivermectin is an oral medication used in scabies treatment .
• Rooks Textbook of Dermatology- 9 th Edition Chapter 34 Page No 34.40 & 34.42
• Fitzpatrick’s Dermatology 9 th Edition Page No 3275
explanation.ref: Rooks Textbook of Dermatology- 9 th Edition Chapter 34 Page No 34.40 & 34.42

subject: Dermatology
question: Most Severe Form of Scabies among the following?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. Nodular Scabies :
• While nodular scabies can be persistent and itchy , it is not the most severe form of scabies. Nodular scabies results from a hypersensitivity reaction to the mites and their feces , leading to itchy nodules that may persist even after the mites are eradicated .
• Option B. Animal Scabies :
• Animal scabies may cause a transient skin reaction in humans after contact with infested animals , but it is not the most severe form as the mites do not reproduce on humans and the condition is self-limiting .
• Option C. Genital Scabies :
• Genital scabies is a localized infestation of the genital area and, while it may cause significant discomfort , it is not classified as the most severe form of scabies.
• Educational Objective
• The most severe form of scabies is Norwegian scabies , which presents with extensive crusting and scaling , and contains a large number of mites , making it highly contagious and severe , particularly in immunocompromised patients.
explanation.ref: Rooks Textbook of Dermatology- 9th Edition Chapter 34 Page No 34.47

subject: Dermatology
question: Identify the INCORRECT statement regarding vagabonds disease?
options: It is also called as Pediculosis Capitis
The Causative Organism is a Louse
The Causative Organism is a Vector for Bartonella Quintana
Advice thorough Washing of the Body & Clothes & Application of Permethrin

explanation.short: Ans. A) It is also called Pediculosis Capitis.
explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option B: The Causative Organism is a Louse:
• Vagabond's disease is caused by body lice (specifically pediculus corporis)
• Option C. The causative organism is a vector for Bartonella Quintana :
• The human body louse can transmit Bartonella Quintana , the causative agent of Trench Fever. Important diseases like Relapsing Fever (Borrelia Recurrentis), Trench Fever (Bartonella Quintana ), and Epidemic Typhus are spread by Body Lice
• Option D. Advice thorough washing of the body & clothes & application of permethrin :
• Clothes and Bed Linens should be thoroughly decontaminated . Although this is typically sufficient in most situations, a thorough soap wash followed by the application of pyrethrins, pyrethroids, or malathion for 8–2 hours can also be advised.
• Educational Objective
• Vagabond's disease is caused by an infestation of body lice, not head lice, and is properly referred to as pediculosis corporis.
• Rooks Textbook of Dermatology- 9 th Edition Chapter 34 Page No 34.21- 34.22
• Andrew’s Diseases of the Skin Clinical Dermatology – 12th Edition Page No 437
explanation.ref: Rooks Textbook of Dermatology- 9 th Edition Chapter 34 Page No 34.21- 34.22

subject: Dermatology
question: Romana Sign is seen with?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. African Trypanosomiasis :
• In early phase shows Posterior cervical lymphadenopathy (Winter bottom sign)
• Option C. Leishmania Mexicana :
• Leishmania Mexicana causes cutaneous leishmaniasis , which can present with skin ulcers but not Romana's sign.
• Option D. Sarcoptes scabiei :
• Sarcoptes scabiei causes scabies, a condition characterized by intense itching and a rash with burrows in the skin, not Romana's sign.
• Educational Objective
• Romana's sign, characterized by unilateral painless swelling of the eyelids, is seen in American trypanosomiasis ( Chagas disease ) caused by Trypanosoma cruzi .
• Rooks Textbook of Dermatology- 9th Edition Chapter 33 Page No 33.39
explanation.ref: Rooks Textbook of Dermatology- 9th Edition Chapter 33 Page No 33.39

subject: Dermatology
question:
In a 45-year-old male patient from Bihar with Normo-Anesthetic Hypopigmented Lesions and a History of Prolonged Fever in Childhood, what is the probable clinical diagnosis, considering the Absence of Thickened Nerves?

[Image: https://cerebellum-web-static.s3.amazonaws.com/media/public/images/2024/02/21/picture39_sWpFSV8.jpg]

explanation.short: Ans. D) Post Kala-Azar Dermal Leishmaniasis.
explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. Tuberculoid leprosy:
• This form of leprosy presents with hypopigmented patches with well-defined borders. However, it is typically associated with thickened nerves , which is absent in the patient described.
• Option B. Lepromatous leprosy:
• This is a more advanced and disseminated form of leprosy with multiple nodules, plaques, and thickened nerves . It doesn't fit the presentation described.
• Option C. Mycosis fungoides:
• It is a type of cutaneous T-cell lymphoma . The initial presentation can be patches , which might resemble the described lesions, but the history of prolonged fever in childhood and the geographic location (Bihar ) make PKDL more probable.
• Educational Objective:
• PKDL i s a probable diagnosis in a patient with a history of prolonged fever in childhood presenting with normo-anesthetic hypopigmented lesions without any thickened nerves, especially from the Bihar region.
explanation.ref: Rooks Textbook of Dermatology- 9 th Edition Page No 33.50-33.51, 140.10

subject: Dermatology
question: A 39-year-old man complains of widespread pruritus for the past two weeks. The itching is particularly bad at night. He has no history of note and works in the local car factory. On examination he has noted to have a number of linear erythematous lesions in between his fingers. What is the most likely diagnosis?

explanation.detail:
• Educational Objective:
• In a patient with widespread pruritus , particularly worse at night , and linear erythematous lesions between the fingers (Burrows) , the most likely diagnosis is scabies.
explanation.ref: Rooks Textbook of Dermatology- 9th Edition Chapter 34 Page No 34.39-34.47

subject: Dermatology
question: What is the Most Effective Treatment of Vagabonds Disease?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. 5% Permethrin below neck application :
• While permethrin is an effective treatment for lice , 5% permethrin is typically used for the treatment of scabies. For pediculosis corporis ( body lice or Vagabond's disease ), the concentration used is different, and the focus is on clothing disinfection because body lice live in clothing .
• Option B. 1% Permethrin whole body application :
• Although 1% permethrin is used for head lice , it is not the most effective treatment for Vagabond's disease, where lice infestation is primarily in the clothes , not on the body itself.
• Clotrimazole is an antifungal medication and would not be effective against lice, as it does not have pediculicidal properties.
• Educational Objective
• The most effective treatment for Vagabond's disease (pediculosis corporis ) is disinfection of clothing , as the body louse lives and lays eggs on clothing , not on the body itself.
• Bolognia Textbook of Dermatology 4 th edition Page No 1511
explanation.ref: Rooks Textbook of Dermatology- 9 th Edition Page No 34.21

subject: Dermatology
question: A 53-year-old man from Bihar, India, presents with Multiple Symmetrical, Hypopigmented, Normoanesthetic Macules on his Face, Arms, and Upper Back. He recalls a severe Fever a year prior that required Hospitalization and Injectable Medications. No Nerve Enlargement is observed. A Slit-Skin Smear from the Macules is Negative for Acid-Fast Bacilli. What is the most appropriate next step in the diagnosis of this patient?
options: Crush Tissue Preparation Stained with Giemsa
Repeat Slit Smear including Nasal Septum and Earlobes

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option B. PCR for lepra bacilli :
• PCR for Mycobacterium leprae is a highly sensitive technique for detecting leprosy bacilli but given the patient's geographic location and history suggestive of visceral leishmaniasis , it is not the most appropriate choice for this case.
• Option C. Skin biopsy :
• While a skin biopsy is generally the next step in cases suspicious for leprosy with a negative AFB smear , the patient's history and presentation are more suggestive of PKDL, a sequelae of leishmaniasis rather than leprosy.
• Option D. Repeat slit-skin smear including nasal septum and earlobes :
• This option would be more appropriate if leprosy were still being considered as a primary diagnosis . However, the clinical context provided points toward PKDL .
• Educational Objective
• In a patient from an endemic area with a history suggestive of visceral leishmaniasis and presenting with hypopigmented macules negative for AFB , the most appropriate diagnostic step is a crush tissue preparation stained with Giemsa to detect Leishman bodies.
• Bolognia Textbook of Dermatology 4 th Edition Page No 1107, 1473
explanation.ref: Rooks Textbook of Dermatology- 9 th Edition Page No 33.50-33.51, 140.10

subject: Dermatology
question: Which of the following hair is NOT infested by Pthirus Pubis?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. Pubic Hair :
• Pthirus pubis, commonly known as crab louse or pubic louse , typically infests the hair in the pubic region. This is the most common site for an infestation.
• Option C. Axillary Hair :
• Pthirus pubis can also infest axillary hair . The body hair in the axillary region can provide a suitable environment for the lice to live and lay eggs .
• Although less common, Pthirus pubis can infest eyelashes , where they are associated with a condition known as pthriasis Palpebrarum .
• Educational Objective
• Pthirus pubis does not typically infest scalp hair, which is more commonly affected by Pediculus humanus capitis causing pediculosis capitis.
• Bolognia Textbook of Dermatology 4 th Edition Page No 1507
explanation.ref: Rooks Textbook of Dermatology- 9 th Edition Page No 34.17, 34.16, 34.22,

subject: Dermatology
question: A 35-year-old man from a rural area in India presents to the dermatology clinic with complaints of persistent skin lesions. He was treated for visceral leishmaniasis (Kala-Azar) two years ago. On examination, he has multiple hypopigmented macules and nodules on his face, arms, and trunk. A skin biopsy reveals amastigotes within macrophages. What is the most appropriate treatment for his condition?

explanation.detail:
• Educational Objective
• The treatment of choice for Post Kala Azar Dermal Leishmaniasis (PKDL) is Miltefosine
