• ↳ Online references: https://ncvbdc.mohfw.gov.in/Doc/PKDL-Guidelines-220512.pdf
• ↳ Rooks textbook of dermatology- 9 th Edition Page no 33.51
• ↳ Bolognia Textbook of Dermatology 4 th edition Page no 1476

subject: Dermatology
question: Topical or Intralesional Steroids are indicated in which type of Scabies?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Animal scabies, caused by mites that typically infest animals , may cause a self-limiting dermatitis in humans but is not typically treated with steroids, as it resolves when the human host is no longer in contact with the animal.
• Infantile scabies presents similarly to adult scabies , but with a higher propensity for widespread involvement and pustular lesions. The treatment is with Antiscabetic agents , and steroids are not typically used unless there is a significant inflammatory component.
• Norwegian , or crusted, scabies is a severe form of scabies with extensive crust formation and high mite load . The mainstay of treatment is aggressive ant scabietic therapy , and steroids are not a primary treatment modality for the scabies itself, though they may be used to manage associated inflammatory skin reactions .
• Educational Objective
• Topical or intralesional steroids are indicated in nodular scabies to reduce the hypersensitivity reaction and inflammation associated with persistent nodules , alongside anti scabetic treatment.
• Rooks Textbook of Dermatology- 9 th Edition Page No 34.47
• Bolognia Textbook of Dermatology 4 th Edition Page No 1506
explanation.ref: Rooks Textbook of Dermatology- 9 th Edition Page No 34.47

subject: Dermatology
question:
A 7-year-old intellectually disabled boy was brought with large warty crusts on the trunk, hands & feet as shown below. Severe fissuring & scaling of the skin noted over the soles. What is the possible diagnosis?

[Image: https://cerebellum-web-static.s3.amazonaws.com/media/public/images/2024/02/21/picture48.jpg]

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. Scabies Incognito :
• This refers to atypical presentations of scabies that result from treatments with corticosteroids or other immunosuppressive agents . The term " incognito " is used because the typical clinical features of scabies might be masked , making diagnosis challenging . While it might cause atypical skin changes , it doesn't usually present with the extensive crusting described in the case.
• Option B. Animal Scabies :
• Humans can be transiently infested with mites from animals , which cause a condition often referred to as " pseudo-scabies ." The itch mites from animals typically do not reproduce on human skin , so the infestation tends to be self-limited . The presentation is usually pruritic papules and not extensive crusting.
• Option C. Nodular Scabies :
• This variant presents with persistent pruritic nodules , often found on covered areas like the axilla or groin . These nodules are an immunological reaction to the mites or their products .
• Educational Objective:
• Norwegian scabies by its hallmark presentation of extensive crusting and scaling , particularly in patients with compromised immune systems or sensory deficits , as it require prompt and aggressive treatment to prevent outbreaks.
• Rooks Textbook of Dermatology- 9 th Edition Chapter 34 Page No 34.42, 34.43, 34.45, 34.47
explanation.ref: Rooks Textbook of Dermatology- 9 th Edition Chapter 34 Page No 34.42, 34.43, 34.45, 34.47

subject: Dermatology
question: A 26-year-old male had multiple small hypopigmented macules on the upper chest & back for the last 3 months. The macules were circular, arranged around follicles & many had coalesced to form large sheets. The surface showed fine scaling. He had similar lesions one year ago which subsided with treatment. Identify the INCORRECT statement about the given disease?
options: Scratch sign is positive in this condition
Topical Terbinafine is the first-line treatment
Wood's lamp examination shows coral red fluorescence
10% KOH mount shows Spaghetti & Meat ball appearance.

explanation.detail:
• Explanation
• Option A. Besnier's sign (coup d'ongle sign or scratch sign or stroke of the nail):
• When the fine scales are not visible, an important diagnostic clue may be the loosening of barely perceptible scales with a fingernail, which is called the scratch sign. The most commonly affected site is the upper trunk.
• Option B. Terbinafine 1% cream twice daily for 2-3 weeks
• Second-line treatment: Oral itraconazole, 200 mg daily for 5 days
• Option D. Potassium hydroxide (KOH) mount shows Spaghetti & Meat ball appearance .
• ﻿﻿Topical azoles twice daily for 2-3 weeks
• Educational Objective:
• The correct diagnosis of Tinea Versicolor involves recognizing the clinical presentation of hypopigmented macules with fine scaling and the typical KOH mount appearance. Wood's lamp examination may aid in diagnosis but does not typically show coral red fluorescence in Tinea Versicolor. Treatment involves antifungals that target the yeast Malassezia , with topical azoles being first line, rather than Terbinafine.
explanation.ref: Rook's Textbook of Dermatology 9th edition Chapter 32, Page no’s 32.11-32.13

subject: Dermatology
question: All of the following statements are true about Dermatophytic infections EXCEPT?
options: They infect & survive in Stratum corneum
Aspergillus is associated with tinea cruris
Microsporum infects only hair and skin
Annular lesions are seen in these infections

explanation.detail:
• Explanation
• Option A. Dermatophytes infect and survive only in the dead keratin layers of skin (stratum corneum), hair and nails as they are Keratinophilic.
• Option B. Aspergillus species are not dermatophytes & are not associated with tinea infections.
• Educational Objective:
• Dermatophytic infections are caused by a group of fungi that infect keratinized tissues such as the stratum corneum , hair , and nails . These infections are characterized by annular lesions and do not include infections by Aspergillus species.
• Ananthanarayan and Paniker's Textbook of Microbiology 9th edition Page no 599
explanation.ref: Rooks textbook of dermatology- 9 th Edition Page no 4.11, 4.13, 32.20

subject: Dermatology
question: A 10-year-old boy presented with painful boggy swelling of scalp, multiple sinuses with purulent discharge, easily pluckable hair & enlarged lymph nodes in occipital region. which one of the following would be most helpful for initial diagnosis?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation
• Option A. While biopsy and culture
• This can provide a definitive diagnosis, but they are not the most immediate or least invasive initial tests. They are generally reserved for cases where initial less invasive testing is inconclusive or when the infection does not respond to typical treatments.
• Option B. Bacterial culture : This would not be the most helpful initial diagnostic test given the presentation strongly suggests a fungal infection rather than a bacterial one.
• Option D. Patch testing : It is used for diagnosing Allergic contact dermatitis , not infections, and would not be useful in this scenario.
• Educational Objective:
• The correct initial diagnostic approach for a suspected fungal infection of the scalp is to perform a KOH mount . This test is quick, cost-effective , and minimally invasive , and it can provide rapid evidence of fungal hyphae or spores , which is critical for the timely initiation of antifungal treatment.
explanation.ref: Rook's Textbook of Dermatology - Volume II-9th Edition Chapter 32 Page no 32.39

subject: Dermatology
question:
A farmer having trauma history in lower limb following which a cauliflower-like growth developed on that site. On HPE copper penny bodies are seen. What is the diagnosis?

[Image: https://cerebellum-web-static.s3.amazonaws.com/media/public/images/2024/03/18/picture17.jpg]

explanation.detail:
• Explanation
• Option B. While mycetoma can also present as a growth on the site of trauma, it is differentiated by the presence of draining sinuses with granules and does not typically show copper penny bodies on HPE.
• It is a subacute or chronic infection caused by the dimorphic fungus Sporothrix schenckii, commonly presenting as nodular lesions along lymphatic channels.
• It does not show copper penny bodies but rather asteroid bodies in tissue.
• This describes a group of infections caused by dematiaceous (darkly pigmented) fungi. While it can present with various clinical manifestations, copper penny bodies are not a characteristic finding.
• Educational Objective:
• The identification of copper penny bodies on histopathology is indicative of Chromoblastomycosis , a fungal infection that leads to characteristic verrucous skin lesions. This finding, along with the clinical history of a cauliflower-like growth following trauma in a farmer , solidifies the diagnosis and guides appropriate antifungal therapy.
explanation.ref: Rook's Textbook of Dermatology - Volume II-9th Edition Chapter 32 Page no 32.76

subject: Dermatology
question:
A Rose gardener presents with the following lesion after traumatic inoculation. Identify the causative organism?

[Image: https://cerebellum-web-static.s3.amazonaws.com/media/public/images/2024/03/18/picture21.jpg]

explanation.detail:
• Explanation
• Option A. Mycobacterium scrofulaceum
• It is a nontuberculous mycobacterium typically associated with cervical lymphadenitis in children, not the lesion seen here which is indicative of a subcutaneous infection following a puncture injury .
• Option C. Madurella mycetomatis :
• It is commonly associated with mycetoma, also known as "Madura foot," which is characterized by a chronic granulomatous infection of the skin and underlying tissues with multiple draining sinuses , not the pattern of spread along lymphatics as seen here.
• Option D. Phialophora verrucosa
• It is associated with c hromoblastomycosis , which presents with verrucous skin lesions that are warty and can become disfiguring over time. The clinical presentation would not typically include the lymphatic spread pattern as with sporotrichosis.
• Educational Objective:
• The clinical presentation of sporotrichosis , typically involving a series of nodular lesions that follow the lymphatic drainage from a site of traumatic inoculation, often associated with gardening. The diagnosis is confirmed by the presence of the fungus Sporothrix schenckii in culture or histopathology.
• Chapter 32 Page no 32.71
explanation.ref: Rook's Textbook of Dermatology - Volume II-9th Edition

subject: Dermatology
question: A farmer comes to skin OPD with a painful, swollen foot which has discharging sinuses & dark grains coming out of sinuses. Diagnosis of this case scenario would be?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation
• It is a chronic bacterial infection that typically presents with granulomatous lesions but is not known for producing dark grains as described.
• It is caused by filamentous bacteria , not fungi, and typically produces lighter-colored grains in discharging sinuses.
• It usually presents as a nodular lymphangitis , with lesions along the lymphatic channels rather than the presentation of a swollen foot with discharging sinuses and dark grains .
• Educational Objective:
• Eumycetoma is diagnosed by the presence of characteristic dark granules in discharges from sinuses on the affected area. These grains are aggregates of the causative fungi, such as Madurella mycetomatis , and differentiate eumycetoma from actinomycetoma , which is caused by filamentous bacteria and typically presents with paler granules. Treatment involves long-term antifungal therapy and sometimes surgery.
explanation.ref: Rook's Textbook of Dermatology - Volume II - 9th Edition Chapter 32 Page no 32.75

subject: Dermatology
question: Identify the False statement about various types of Tinea infections?
options: Corticosteroids are associated with Tinea incognito
Tinea pedis is caused by Trichophyton rubrum
Tinea cruris is also called as Dhobis itch

explanation.detail:
• Explanation
• Option A. Tinea incognito
• It is an uncommon clinical lesion of tinea that is typically brought on by improper application of a topical corticosteroid to treat a fungal infection. Steroids reduce inflammation without removing the fungus, which results in a smaller, less raised margin, lost scaling, and decreased inflammation . As a result, diagnosis becomes challenging. Pharmacological treatment of fungal tinea infection depends on the extent of the disease.
• ﻿﻿ Topical terbinafine twice daily for 2 weeks ﻿﻿ Topical azole once or twice daily for 2-4 weeks
• ﻿﻿First-line - oral terbinafine 250 mg/day 2-3 weeks or oral itraconazole 100 mg/day 2-4 weeks ﻿﻿Second line - oral griseofulvin 1 g/day for 4 weeks
• Option B. Tinea pedis or ringworm of foot is most commonly caused by Trichophyton rubrum infections (70%) > Trichophyton interdigitale infections (15%), Epidermophyton floccosum infections (<10%), and mixed infections (5%).
• Option C. A type of dermatophyte causes Dhobi's itch/tinea cruris , an infection of the groin. It is also referred to as eczema marginatum or groin ringworm.
• The primary cause is T. rubrum , but T. interdigitale and E. floccosum are also responsible for some cases.
• Educational Objective:
• Tinea unguium refers to a fungal infection of the nail plate itself, not the nail folds. It's important to differentiate between the various tinea infections as they affect different parts of the body and may require different approaches to treatment.
explanation.ref: Rook's Textbook of Dermatology 9th Edition Page no 32.38-32.50

subject: Dermatology
question: Which type of oral candidiasis does not present with a white patch?

explanation.detail:
• Educational Objective:
• When diagnosing types of oral candidiasis , it is important to recognize that chronic atrophic candidiasis does not present with a white patch but is characterized by red, atrophic areas usually associated with dental prosthesis wear . In contrast, other forms of oral candidiasis, such as pseudomembranous and hyperplastic types, are typically associated with white patches or plaques .
explanation.ref: Rook's Textbook of Dermatology 9th edition Page nos 32.61-32.63

subject: Dermatology
question:
A child was brought with complaints of hair loss in a patchy form over the scalp associated with easy pluckability of hair. Physical examination reveals black spots on the patch of hair loss as shown in the image below. Which of the following oral medications is used in the treatment of this condition?

[Image: https://cerebellum-web-static.s3.amazonaws.com/media/public/images/2024/03/18/picture2_SUq8W0R.jpg]

explanation.detail:
• Explanation
• Option A. Amphotericin B
• It is an antifungal medication used to treat serious systemic fungal infections and is not typically used for fungal infections of the scalp.
• It is used to treat a variety of fungal infections , but it is not the first-line treatment for tinea capitis. Fluconazole can be used when griseofulvin is not tolerated or ineffective.
• It is an antiparasitic medication used for conditions such as scabies and some worm infestations , not fungal infections.
• Educational Objective:
• Griseofulvin is the oral medication of choice for the treatment of tinea capitis, a dermatophyte infection of the scalp that presents with patchy hair loss and black dot appearance due to broken hair shafts at the scalp surface.
• Bolognia Textbook of Dermatology 4 th edition Page no 1339
explanation.ref: Rooks textbook of dermatology- 9 th Edition page no 32.40

subject: Dermatology
question:
A young man presents with the following skin lesions as shown in the image below. Which of the following organisms can least likely spread through dermal and subcutaneous lymphatics?

[Image: https://cerebellum-web-static.s3.amazonaws.com/media/public/images/2024/03/18/picture4_8caQ39w.jpg]

explanation.detail:
• Explanation
• Option A. Mycobacterium marinum: It can cause a chronic condition known as "aquarium granuloma " or "fish tank granuloma," which can spread along the lymphatics.
• Option C. Nocardia: It can cause cutaneous nocardiosis , which may manifest as lymphocutaneous disease, with spread along lymphatic channels.
• Option D. Sporothrix schenckii: It is the causative agent of sporotrichosis , which classically spreads via lymphatics, leading to a nodular lymphangitis.
• Important Organisms Transmitted in Linear / Sporotrichoid Pattern
• Educational Objective:
• Staphylococcus aureus typically does not spread through the dermal and subcutaneous lymphatics as commonly as the other organisms listed, which are known for this pattern of dissemination.
• Bolognia Textbook of Dermatology 4 th edition Page no 9
explanation.ref: Rooks textbook of dermatology- 9 th Edition page no 105.51

subject: Dermatology
question:
A forest worker developed skin lesions over the forearm. Histology of the nodules shows the following findings. Which are the following is true regarding this condition?

[Image: https://cerebellum-web-static.s3.amazonaws.com/media/public/images/2024/03/18/picture5.jpg]
options: These bodies are formed by engulfment of the dead fungi by the macrophages
Organism does not invade underlying facia tendons and muscles
It is a dimorphic Organism, and east forms are seen in clinical sample
Angioinvasion is common especially in people with haematological malignancies

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. Incorrect because the bodies seen in the image are sclerotic or copper penny bodies typical of chromoblastomycosis, which are not formed by engulfment of fungi by macrophages but are actually the characteristic form of the fungus in the tissue.
• Differential Diagnosis of Parasitized Macrophages:
• Option C. Incorrect because the organism causing chromoblastomycosis is not dimorphic and the yeast forms mentioned are not associated with this disease.
• Dimorphic Fungi and Associated Diseases
• Option D. Incorrect . Angioinvasion is not a common feature of chromoblastomycosis; it is more associated with other invasive fungal infections in immunocompromised patients.
• Educational Objective:
• Chromoblastomycosis , is a chronic fungal infection of the skin and subcutaneous tissue caused by dematiaceous fungi, characterized by sclerotic bodies seen on histology and typically NOT invading deeper structures like fascia, tendons, and muscles.
• Bolognia Textbook of Dermatology 4 th edition Page no 1347, 1353
explanation.ref: Rooks textbook of dermatology- 9 th Edition page no 32.76

subject: Dermatology
question:
A forest worker has a cauliflower like lesion on the foot as shown in the image below. The histopathology from the lesion is given below. What is the most likely finding shown in the histopathology?

[Image: https://cerebellum-web-static.s3.amazonaws.com/media/public/images/2024/03/18/picture7_HQWfYNK.jpg]

explanation.detail:
• Explanation
• Option B. Splendore Hoeppli phenomenon / Asteroid bodies:
• This phenomenon is characterized by intensely eosinophilic material ( antigen-antibody complexes) that radiates out from a bacteria, parasites and fungal organisms, creating a starry appearance.
• Conditions Associated with Splendore Hoeppli Phenomenon
• This is not typically seen in chromoblastomycosis.
• Option C. Encapsulated budding yeast :
• This description fits the histopathology of cryptococcosis more than chromoblastomycosis. Cryptococcus is encapsulated and can show a clear halo around the yeast cells in tissue samples.
• Option D. Macrophage ingesting fungi :
• While macrophage activity is present in fungal infections, the characteristic feature of chromoblastomycosis is the presence of copper penny bodies, not necessarily the ingestion of fungi by macrophages. Differential Diagnosis of Parasitized Macrophages
• Educational Objective:
• The correct identification of copper penny bodies in a histopathological sample is indicative of chromoblastomycosis , which is consistent with the clinical presentation of cauliflower-like lesions in a forest worker.
• Bolognia Textbook of Dermatology 4 th edition Page no 1347, 1353
explanation.ref: Rooks textbook of dermatology- 9 th Edition page no 32.76

subject: Dermatology
question:
A farmer presents with a swollen foot and multiple sinuses as shown in the image below. What is the most likely diagnosis?

[Image: https://cerebellum-web-static.s3.amazonaws.com/media/public/images/2024/03/18/picture9_4JN690N.jpg]

explanation.detail:
• Educational Objective:
• The correct diagnosis of mycetoma is based on the presentation of a swollen foot with multiple sinuses and granules, often associated with an occupational history of exposure to soil. Recognizing this presentation is important for the management and initiation of appropriate antimicrobial therapy.Top of FormBottom of Form
• Bolognia Textbook of Dermatology 4 th edition Page no 1350
explanation.ref: Rooks textbook of dermatology- 9 th Edition page no 32.73

subject: Dermatology
question:
A child presents with complaints of itching over scalp. On examination a patch of hair loss, with redness is noted as shown in the image below. Which of the following investigations would help to diagnose the most probable diagnosis in this child?

[Image: https://cerebellum-web-static.s3.amazonaws.com/media/public/images/2024/03/18/picture10_sdchXRm.jpg]

explanation.detail:
• Explanation
• Option A. Scalp biopsy: It is a more invasive test that's used for complex cases where the diagnosis is not clear. It's not usually the first step.
• Option B. Gram stain : It is used to identify bacterial infections, which are less likely the cause of itchy, red bald patches.
• Option C. Slit skin smears are used for certain skin infections, like leprosy , which do not typically present as an itchy, red bald patch on the scalp.
• Educational Objective:
• For a child with an itchy scalp and a patch of hair loss , a KOH mount of the scalp can help diagnose a fungal infection , often the most likely cause. This test is simple, quick, and a good first step in such cases.
• Bolognia Textbook of Dermatology 4 th edition Page no 1339
explanation.ref: Rooks textbook of dermatology- 9 th Edition page no 32.39

subject: Dermatology
question:
A 38-year-old farmer presents with swelling of the foot, multiple sinuses discharge over foot. Microscopy of the granule is shown below. Which of the following is true regarding this condition?

[Image: https://cerebellum-web-static.s3.amazonaws.com/media/public/images/2024/03/18/picture11_XGqZsN5.jpg]
options: Caused by both Bacteria and fungi
Hematogenous spread is the most common mode of systemic involvement
Involves only superficial tissue and spares deep tissues
Splendore Hoeppli phenomenon is characteristic of this condition

explanation.detail:
• Explanation
• Option B. Hematogenous spread.
• This is not the typical mode of systemic involvement for mycetoma. The infection typically spreads locally through direct extension from the site of inoculation.
• Option C. Mycetoma involves both superficial and deep tissues , often extending to the muscle and bone , and is not confined to superficial layers of the skin.
• Option D. While the Splendore Hoeppli phenomenon can be seen in mycetoma, it is not exclusive to it and is also characteristic of other infections. It refers to the presence of bright eosinophilic material surrounding microorganisms or parasites, seen in various inflammatory conditions.
• Splendore Hoeppli phenomenon / Asteroid bodies:
• This phenomenon is characterized by intensely eosinophilic material ( antigen-antibody complexes) that radiates out from a bacteria, parasites and fungal organisms, creating a starry appearance.
• Conditions Associated with Splendore Hoeppli Phenomenon
• Educational Objective:
• Mycetoma is a chronic, progressive infection that can be caused by both bacteria and fungi . This condition is characterized by subcutaneous swelling with sinus tract formation and discharge containing granules .
• Bolognia Textbook of Dermatology 4 th edition Page no 1349
explanation.ref: Rooks textbook of dermatology- 9 th Edition page no 32.73

subject: Dermatology
question:
A female patient presents with complaints of thick curdy white vaginal discharge as shown in the image below. Microscopy shows budding yeast cells with pseudohyphae. Which medium is used to differentiate the species of the causative agent?

[Image: https://cerebellum-web-static.s3.amazonaws.com/media/public/images/2024/03/18/picture14_2axhbXy.jpg]

explanation.detail:
• Explanation
• Option A. LJ medium, or Löwenstein-Jensen medium , is primarily used for the culture of Mycobacterium species , not for differentiating yeast species.
• Option B. Brain heart infusion agar is a broad-spectrum medium that supports the growth of a variety of organisms but does not offer species-level differentiation for yeasts.
• Option D. SDA, or Sabouraud Dextrose Agar , is commonly used for cultivating fungi due to its low pH . While it promotes fungal growth , it lacks the differentiation capabilities found in CHROMagar .
• Educational Objective:
• Employ CHROMagar for the differentiation of yeast species in clinical samples, such as those from patients with candidiasis. This medium is particularly valuable because it provides a color-coded identification system that allows clinicians to quickly and accurately identify the causative agent and ensure appropriate antifungal therapy.
• Bolognia Textbook of Dermatology 4 th edition Page no
explanation.ref: Rooks textbook of dermatology- 9 th Edition page no

subject: Dermatology
question:
What type of tinea is likely affecting the 10-year-old child who presents with patchy hair loss as shown in the image below, along with the presence of black dots in the areas of hair loss?

[Image: https://cerebellum-web-static.s3.amazonaws.com/media/public/images/2024/03/18/picture16_yWRdmsT.jpg]

explanation.detail:
• Educational Objective:
• The presence of patchy hair loss with black dots in the areas of hair loss in a child is indicative of an endothrix type of tinea capitis , where the fungal spores are located inside the hair shaft , causing it to break at the scalp level.
explanation.ref: Rook's Textbook of Dermatology - Volume II-9th Edition Chapter 32 Page no 32.8, 32.38-32.40

subject: Dermatology
question: A patient had a history of thorn prick while working in a rose garden and later observed linear spread proximally till his shoulder. Which of the following is the most likely underlying cause for this clinical feature?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option B (Blaschkitis): Blaschkitis, or lichen striatus, is an inflammatory skin condition that follows Blaschko's lines ; it does not occur due to infections and does not present with linear red streaking from an infected wound.
• Option C (Abscess): An abscess is a collection of pus that has built up within the tissue of the body and typically presents as a localized swelling, redness, and pain , not as linear red streaking.
• Option D (Furuncle): A furuncle, or boil, is a deep folliculitis , infection of the hair follicle , that forms a pus-filled nodule . It does not cause red streaks along the lymphatic vessels.
• Educational Objective:
• Lymphangitis is characterized by red streaking on the skin , which represents inflammation of the lymphatic channels , usually occurring secondary to an infectious process spreading from a distal site of injury, such as a thorn prick.
explanation.ref: Rook's Textbook of Dermatology - Volume II-9th Edition Chapter 32 Page no 32,71

subject: Dermatology
question: A drug used to treat Tinea cruris that is experiencing an increasing development of widespread resistance is?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• While Itraconazole is effective against dermatophytoses, , but there's no specific indication of increasing resistance against it in the context of Tinea cruris. Mechanism of Action: Itraconazole inhibits the enzyme cytochrome P450 14α-demethylase , which is necessary for the conversion of lanosterol to ergosterol in the fungal cell membrane. This leads to accumulation of toxic intermediates and membrane dysfunction.
• This drug is used for treatment, but there's no specific indication of increasing resistance against it in the context of Tinea cruris. Mechanism of Action: Griseofulvin binds to tubulin , disrupting the microtubule function and thereby inhibiting mitosis in fungal cells. It specifically targets dividing fungal cells.
• Mechanism of Action: Similar to Itraconazole, Voriconazole also inhibits the cytochrome P450 enzyme involved in the production of ergosterol for the fungal cell membrane. It has a broad spectrum of antifungal activity. But it is not routinely used for treating superficial fungal infections.
• Educational Objective:
• Terbinafine is increasingly encountering resistance in the treatment of Tinea cruris , likely due to mutations in the fungal enzyme squalene epoxidase , which is the drug's target .
• https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC148991 HYPERLINK "https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC148991/" /
• ncbi.nlm.nih.gov - Management of tinea corporis, tinea cruris, and tinea pedis
• ncbi.nlm.nih.gov - Update on terbinafine with a focus on dermatophytoses
• [ijdvl.com - III. Antifungal resistance and treatment options https://ijdvl.com/the-unprecedented-epidemic-like-scenario-of-dermatophytosis-in-india-iii-antifungal-resistance-and-treatment-options/

subject: Dermatology
question:
What is the diagnosis seen in the image below?

[Image: https://cerebellum-web-static.s3.amazonaws.com/media/public/images/2024/03/18/picture27_jeepamY.jpg]

explanation.detail:
• Explanation :
• Option A. Candidal paronychia
• This refers to an infection of the nail fold , leading to a painful and red swelling around the nail. It can be due to bacterial or candidal infection.
• Option C. Diabetic foot ulcer
• This is an open sore or wound commonly located on the bottom of the foot in people with diabetes. Factors leading to diabetic foot ulcers include neuropathy, peripheral arterial disease, foot deformities, and trauma . The skin around the ulcer may be calloused or discolored but not typically macerated.
• Option D. Staphylococcal scalded skin syndrome (SSSS)
• This is a serious skin disorder caused by the exotoxins of Staphylococcus aureus. It primarily affects infants and young children . It is characterized by blistering and the appearance that the top layer of skin is scalded or burned. SSSS affects larger areas of the body and often starts with facial erythema , widespread redness, and blisters , which then rupture, leaving raw areas . Maceration of toe web spaces would not be a primary symptom.
• Educational Objective :
• Candidal intertrigo can be identified by the classic presentation of erythema, maceration , and possible white exudate in skin folds , such as the web spaces between fingers, distinguishing it from conditions that affect the nail folds, feet, or involve widespread skin desquamation.
explanation.ref: Rook's Textbook of Dermatology 9th edition Chapter 32, Page nos 32.61-32.63

subject: Dermatology
question:
A 35-year-old female presented with lesions over the trunk as shown in the picture below. Which of the following treatment should be started in this patient?

[Image: https://cerebellum-web-static.s3.amazonaws.com/media/public/images/2024/03/18/picture29_Q8uMvly.jpg]

explanation.detail:
• Explanation
• Option A. Griseofulvin : This is a treatment option for dermatophyte infections of the scalp and nails but is not the first-line treatment for the condition likely depicted in the image, which resembles Tinea Versicolor .
• Option C. Ketoconazole is another antifungal used for Tinea Versicolor , but due to potential hepatotoxicity and drug interactions, it is often reserved for topical use rather than systemic treatment.
• Option D. Terbinafine is effective against dermatophyte infections but is not the first-line treatment for Tinea Versicolor.
• Educational Objective
• The appropriate treatment for Tinea Versicolor , as indicated by the hypopigmented and hyperpigmented macules on the trunk, is a short course of an oral antifungal such as Itraconazole .
• Bolognia Textbook of Dermatology 4 th edition Page no 1332
explanation.ref: Rooks textbook of dermatology- 9 th Edition page no 32.13

subject: Dermatology
question:
A 10-year-old male child complains of mild painful swelling over the scalp for 3 months as shown in the image below. He also has a pet dog at his home. What is the diagnosis of the given condition?

[Image: https://cerebellum-web-static.s3.amazonaws.com/media/public/images/2024/03/18/picture19_fHZMXpb.jpg]

explanation.detail:
• Explanation
• Description : Also known as a boil , a furuncle is a deep folliculitis , an infection of the hair follicle. Reason for exclusion from the diagnosis : Furuncles are typically more localized and painful and would not be described as "boggy." Also, they don’t have a direct association with having a pet dog.
• Option B. Epidermoid cyst:
• Description : These are benign cysts originating from the epidermis . They are filled with keratin and have a characteristic central punctum as seen in the image below .
• Reason for exclusion from the diagnosis : Epidermoid cysts are typically non-painful unless infected . They do not usually present as boggy swellings and are not related to having a pet.
• Description: Folliculitis is the inflammation or infection of one or more hair follicles. Reason for exclusion from the diagnosis : Folliculitis on the scalp would typically present as multiple small, itchy, and red pustules . It wouldn't create a boggy swelling as described in the question.
• Educational Objective:
• kerion is a boggy, tender scalp swelling associated with hair loss and potentially linked to animal exposure, indicative of a fungal infection rather than a bacterial cause like furuncle or folliculitis, or a benign lesion such as an epidermoid cyst.
explanation.ref: Rook's Textbook of Dermatology 9th Edition Page no 26.22, 26.23-26.24, 32.38, 32.39, 32.40, 90.27, 108.11

subject: Dermatology
question:
A patient presents with a BT Leprosy Lesion on the Face as shown in the image below. Which of the following Nerves is most commonly involved in this condition?

[Image: https://cerebellum-web-static.s3.amazonaws.com/media/public/images/2024/02/21/1.jpg]

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option B. Trigeminal nerve:
• The trigeminal nerve ( fifth cranial nerve ) primarily innervates the face and is responsible for sensations such as touch and pain. While it can be affected in leprosy, it is not the most commonly involved nerve in BT leprosy lesions on the face.
• Option C. Abducens nerve:
• The abducens nerve (sixth cranial nerve) controls the movement of the lateral rectus muscle of the eye. In leprosy, it is less commonly involved compared to the facial nerve, especially in BT leprosy.
• Option D. Optic nerve:
• The optic nerve ( second cranial nerve ) is responsible for vision and is not commonly affected in leprosy lesions on the face.
• Educational Objective:
• In patients presenting with leprosy lesions on the face, the most commonly involved nerve is the facial nerve.
• Harrison’s Principles of Internal Medicine 20 th Edition Page No 1385, 1386, 1388
explanation.ref: Rook's Textbook of Dermatology, 9th Edition, Chapter 28, Page No 28.1, 28.3, 28.12

subject: Dermatology
question: All of the following are correct statements regarding leprosy EXCEPT
options: Borderline Tuberculoid is the most common type of Leprosy in India
Borderline Borderline is the most unstable form of Leprosy
Earliest Sign of Leprosy is Loss of Temperature
Facial Nerve is the most common nerve involved in Leprosy

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option B. Borderline Borderline is the most unstable form of leprosy:
• Presenting with punched out anesthetic lesions, which have Geographical or Map like appearance. Inverted saucer & punched out lesions are characteristic of Borderline Borderline leprosy. It is rarely seen as it rapidly upgrades towards tuberculoid or downgrades towards lepromatous pole , based on immune status of the individual.
• Pure Neuritic leprosy (PNL)is not included in the Ridley Jopling classification. Mononeuritis is the most common form of presentation of PNL. Nerve biopsy is the gold standard method for the diagnosis of PNL.
• Earliest sign of leprosy is loss of temperature , seen over he hands & feet . Sensations lost in the following order:
• Temperature > Fine Touch > Pain > Deep Touch or Pressure
• Educational Objective:
• Ulnar Nerve is the most affected nerve in Leprosy , not the facial nerve. Early signs of leprosy include Loss of Temperature Sensation, and Borderline Borderline Leprosy is the most immunologically unstable form .
• https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC4318064/ , Harrison’s Principles of Internal Medicine – 19 th Edition Page 1124,. Harrison’s Principles of Internal Medicine 20 th Edition Chapter 174 Leprosy, Andrew’s Diseases of the Skin: Clinical Dermatology – 12 th Edition Page No 336, Rooks Textbook of Dermatology 9th Edition Page 28.10 – 28.11
explanation.ref: Journal: https://ijdvl.com/current-scenario-of-leprosy-at-tertiary-care-level-hospital-of-rural-central-india/

subject: Dermatology
question: Which among the following is CORRECT regarding the healing of leprosy if left untreated?
options: Half of the cases Resolve Spontaneously After Bacilli Exposure
Spontaneous Remission is more common in Children than in Adults
Indeterminate Leprosy has more Remission than Tuberculoid Leprosy

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Even in the absence of specific treatment , a majority (80-90%) of patients tend to get cured spontaneously.
• Spontaneous remission in children is variable when compared to adults.
• Educational Objective:
• Spontaneous remission occurs even in the absence of specific treatment in majority (almost 80-90%) of the patients, but when compared to adults, in children spontaneous cure is very much variable. If left untreated, Indeterminate leprosy has a higher rate of spontaneous remission compared to tuberculoid leprosy .
• Parks Textbook of Preventive & Social Medicine 26 th Edition Pg No. 359
explanation.ref: Rook's Textbook of Dermatology 9th Edition, Page No 28.7

subject: Dermatology
question:
In a 45-year-old male patient from Bihar with a normo-anesthetic hypopigmented lesion and a history of prolonged fever in childhood, what is the probable clinical diagnosis, considering the absence of thickened nerves?

[Image: https://cerebellum-web-static.s3.amazonaws.com/media/public/images/2024/02/21/4.jpg]

explanation.short: Ans. D) Post Kala-Azar Dermal Leishmaniasis
explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. Tuberculoid leprosy :
• This form of leprosy presents with hypopigmented patches with well-defined borders . However, it is typically associated with thickened nerves , which is absent in the patient described.
• Option B. Lepromatous leprosy :
• This is a more advanced and disseminated form of l eprosy with multiple nodules, plaques, and thickened nerves . It doesn't fit the presentation described.
• Option C. Mycosis fungoides :
• It is a type of cutaneous T-cell lymphoma . The initial presentation can be patches , which might resemble the described lesions, but the history of prolonged fever in childhood and the geographic location (Bihar ) make PKDL more probable .
• Educational Objective:
• PKDL i s a probable diagnosis in a patient with a history of prolonged fever in childhood presenting with normo-anesthetic hypopigmented lesions without any thickened nerves, especially from the Bihar region.
explanation.ref: Rooks Textbook of Dermatology- 9 th Edition Chapter 33 Page No 33.50-33.51, 140.10

subject: Dermatology
question:
The Below-Given Image is characteristically seen in which of the following type of Leprosy?

[Image: https://cerebellum-web-static.s3.amazonaws.com/media/public/images/2024/02/21/3_f3Flb2X.jpg]

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A . Borderline Tuberculoid Leprosy
• It is characterized by fewer and more well-demarcated lesions than borderline leprosy , and there is a finding called as Satellite lesions which can be seen in BT leprosy .
• Option B . Lepromatous leprosy
• It presents with numerous lesions that are more symmetrical and have less sensory loss ; it represents one polar end of the leprosy spectrum with a high bacterial load .
• Option D . Histoid leprosy
• It is a rare form of lepromatous leprosy presenting with firm , nodular lesions that typically do not resemble the "inverted saucer" appearance.
• Educational Objective
• The clinical presentation of borderline leprosy ( BB Leprosy ) and distinguish it from other forms of leprosy . It highlights the importance of recognizing the characteristic " inverted saucer " appearance of the cutaneous lesions and understanding the spectrum of leprosy classifications , which is essential for appropriate diagnosis and management
explanation.ref: Rooks Textbook of Dermatology 9 th Edition Page No 28.3, 28.5, 28.10

subject: Dermatology
question: Which nerve is biopsied for the diagnosis of Pure Neuritic leprosy?

explanation.short: Ans. B) Superficial Branch of Radial Nerve.
explanation.detail:
• Explanation
• Superficial branch of radial nerve is most commonly used for nerve biopsy in pure neuritic leprosy. Pure neuritic leprosy is characterized by asymmetrical involvement of peripheral nerve trunks without visible skin lesions . To confirm the diagnosis of pure neuritic leprosy, nerve biopsy is considered the gold standard. It is essential to biopsy only sensory nerves and avoid motor or major nerve trunks, as well as mixed nerves. For nerve biopsy in neuritic leprosy , the superficial sensory branch of the radial nerve is typically utilized.
• The biopsy sample should be taken from a purely sensory thickened peripheral nerve , such as the superficial sensory branch of the radial nerve at the wrist , ulnar cutaneous nerve near the ulnar border of the base of the hand , or sural nerve near the ankle .
• Educational Objective:
• For the diagnosis of Pure Neuritic Leprosy , the superficial branch of the radial nerve is the preferred site for biopsy due to its accessibility, lower risk of complications, and high diagnostic yield.
explanation.ref: Rook's Textbook of Dermatology -9th Edition Chapter 28 Page No 28.11 28.14, Sensory reanimation of the hand by transfer of the superficial branch of the radial nerve to the median and ulnar nerve - Scientific Figure on ResearchGate. Available from: https://www.researchgate.net/figure/Transfer-of-the-superficial-branch-of-the-radial-nerve-SBRN-to-the-median-nerve-MN_fig5_309138539 [accessed 9 Nov, 2023]

subject: Dermatology
question: A patient presented with Incomplete Closure of Left Eye. On examination Orbicularis Oculi weakness was found, and Cornea shows Decreased Sensation. Which of the following organisms is most likely associated with these findings?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. Mycobacterium tuberculosis :
• Mycobacterium tuberculosis is the causative agent of tuberculosis , not typically associated with nerve involvement that would lead to orbicularis oculi weakness or corneal anaesthesia .
• Option C. Mycobacterium marinum :
• Mycobacterium marinum causes a skin disease known as "aquarium granuloma ," which typically does not lead to the neural or ocular symptoms described.
• Option D. Onchocerca volvulus :
• Onchocerca volvulus is a parasitic worm that causes onchocerciasis , also known as river blindness . It is associated with severe itching, visual impairment and permanent blindness.
• Educational Objective:
• Mycobacterium leprae is the causative organism of leprosy (Hansen's disease ), which can lead to nerve involvement manifesting as orbicularis oculi weakness and decreased corneal sensation .
explanation.ref: IAL Textbook of Leprosy, 2 nd Edition Chapter 27 Page No 378

subject: Dermatology
question:
Match the following Leprosy types in Column A with their clinical features in Column B:

[Image: https://cerebellum-web-static.s3.amazonaws.com/media/public/images/2024/02/21/screenshot-2024-02-21-174804.jpg]

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option 1. Indeterminate leprosy (IL) (c No Nerve Involvement) :
• Indeterminate leprosy is an early form of the disease and often presents with nonspecific skin lesions without definite nerve involvement.
• Option 2. Lepromatous Leprosy (LL) (a Leonine Facies) :
• Lepromatous leprosy is characterized by diffuse infiltration of the skin , which can lead to thickening of the skin and the classic leonine facies (lion-like appearance).
• Option 3. Borderline Borderline (BB) (b Punched Out Lesions) :
• Punched out lesions with well-defined inner border and ill-defined outer border can be seen in BB hansens disease.
• Option 4. Tuberculoid (TT) (d Saucer right way up lesions) :
• Tuberculoid leprosy presents with well-demarcated , hypopigmented , and anesthetic skin lesions which have well defined border and the elevated border slopes down to flat centre , which is described as “ Saucer right way up ” lesion .
• Educational Objective:
• The clinical features associated with different types of leprosy: indeterminate leprosy presents without nerve involvement, lepromatous leprosy can present with leonine facies, and tuberculoid leprosy presents with Saucer right way up skin lesions.
explanation.ref: IAL Textbook of Leprosy, 2 nd Edition Chapter 15 Page No 240-245

subject: Dermatology
question: A 38-years-old male presents with multiple hypopigmented and hypo anaesthetic lesions over his body, which are almost symmetrical. Which of the following leprosy is present in this patient?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option B. Borderline tuberculoid:
• In Borderline tuberculoid leprosy, the number of lesions is fewer and they are non-symmetrical . There may also be a characteristic "satellite lesion " near a larger patch .
• Lepromatous Leprosy presents with diffuse, symmetrical skin involvement that includes macules, patches, and nodular lesions . The lesions are more numerous and widespread than in the borderline forms . Also, sensations over lesions are mostly intact in LL , as opposed to mentioned in this clinical vignette .
• Tuberculoid leprosy presents with a single or few well-demarcated , hypopigmented, and anesthetic patches . These lesions have a "saucer-shaped" appearance and represent a strong immune response that limits the number of lesions .
• Educational Objective:
• The presence of multiple annular or punched-out lesions with almost symmetrical distribution suggests borderline lepromatous leprosy . This form shows features intermediate between lepromatous and tuberculoid leprosy, with a higher number of lesions than borderline tuberculoid leprosy and less symmetry than lepromatous leprosy. Top of Form
explanation.ref: IAL Textbook of Leprosy, 2 nd Edition Chapter 15 Page No 244

subject: Dermatology
question: Which of the following types of leprosy is most seen India?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. Borderline Lepromatous (BL) :
• Borderline lepromatous leprosy does occur in India, it is not the most common form. It is characterized by multiple hypopigmented patches that tend towards symmetry .
• Option B. Lepromatous Leprosy (LL) :
• Lepromatous leprosy presents with numerous diffuse nodules and plaques and tends to have a symmetric distribution , but it is not the most common type of leprosy in India.
• Option D. Borderline (BB) :
• Borderline (BB) leprosy represents an intermediate form between tuberculoid and lepromatous leprosy and is less common than BT leprosy. It is characterized by punched-out lesions . It is the most unstable form of leprosy.
• Educational Objective:
• Borderline tuberculoid (BT) leprosy is the most common form of leprosy in India , typically presenting with a single or few hypopigmented patches with a characteristic satellite lesion.
explanation.ref: IAL Textbook of Leprosy, 2 nd Edition Chapter 15 Page No 242

subject: Dermatology
question:
A 6-year-old child came with white lesions over his face as shown in the image below. Which of the following statements is INCORRECT regarding this condition?

[Image: https://cerebellum-web-static.s3.amazonaws.com/media/public/images/2024/02/21/9.jpg]
options: History of Atopy might be present in the Family
Indeterminate Leprosy is an important differential for this presentation
It is a Variant of Vitiligo

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. History of atopy might be present in the family :
• Pityriasis alba is often associated with a history of atopic dermatitis or other atopic diseases, which may have a familial occurrence.
• Option B . Mostly happens in children:
• Pityriasis alba predominantly occurs in children and is considered a benign condition that usually resolves with age.
• Option C. Indeterminate leprosyis an important differential for this presentation:
• When evaluating hypopigmented lesions, especially in areas where leprosy is endemic, it is crucial to consider leprosy in the differential diagnosis.
• Indeterminate leprosy presents with a single hypopigmented macule and can be a consideration before the disease progresses to more definitive forms . Biopsy is the IOC; Peri-neural, Periappendageal Lymphyocytosis seen.
• Educational Objective:
• Pityriasis alba is a common cause of hypopigmented lesions in children, which may be confused with other conditions such as vitiligo or indeterminate leprosy. Understanding the clinical presentation and associated conditions, like atopy, is essential for accurate diagnosis and appropriate management .
explanation.ref: Rook's Textbook of Dermatology -9th Edition Page No 39.25, 88.34

subject: Dermatology
question: A 45-year-old man from a Leprosy-Endemic Region presents with Painless Ulcers on the soles of his Feet. He has a history of symmetrically distributed Skin Nodules, and on examination, you notice Thickened Peripheral Nerves. The ulcers have a punched-out appearance with a pale base and undermined edges. Which type of leprosy is most commonly associated with these findings?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation
• Option A. Borderline Lepromatous (BL) Leprosy
• It is a less severe form than LL and typically presents with less extensive skin lesions and nerve involvement .
• Option C. Borderline Tuberculoid (BT) Leprosy
• It presents with skin lesions that are fewer in number and more well-demarcated than BL or LL , with more significant nerve involvement leading to anaesthesia .
• Option D. Borderline (BB) leprosy
• It is an intermediate form and can present with skin lesions that have an " inverted saucer " appearance , but without the extensive nodule formation seen in LL.
• Educational Objective:
• Lepromatous leprosy (LL) is the type of leprosy that commonly presents with trophic ulcers due to its extensive nerve damage , leading to loss of sensation and unrecognized trauma, particularly in pressure areas like the soles of the feet.
explanation.ref: Jopling’s Handbook of Leprosy 6 th Edition Page no 326

subject: Dermatology
question: Which of the following types of leprosy is NOT included in Ridley Jopling Classification?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. Borderline Lepromatous (BL) :
• Borderline lepromatous is a part of the Ridley-Jopling classification of leprosy . It represents an immunological form of leprosy that falls between lepromatous and tuberculoid leprosy on the spectrum .
• Option B. Lepromatous Leprosy (LL) :
• Lepromatous leprosy is one end of the spectrum in the Ridley-Jopling classification , characterized by a large number of bacilli and diffuse lesions.
• Option D. Borderline (BB) :
• Borderline (BB) leprosy, also known as mid-borderline or borderline-borderline leprosy , is included in the Ridley-Jopling classification and represents an intermediate form between BL and BT leprosy .
• Educational Objective:
• Pure Neuritic Leprosy is not part of the Ridley-Jopling classification of leprosy, as this classification system is based on the spectrum of disease with cutaneous lesions and immunological response , whereas Pure Neuritic Leprosy presents only with nerve involvement without the skin lesions typically seen in leprosy.
explanation.ref: Jopling’s Handbook of Leprosy 6 th Edition Page No 8, 9

subject: Dermatology
question:
A 10-year-old boy from Bihar presents with a 9 Month History of an Ill-Defined, Hypopigmented Slightly Atrophic Patch on the face as shown in the image below. The most likely diagnosis is:

[Image: https://cerebellum-web-static.s3.amazonaws.com/media/public/images/2024/02/21/12.jpg]

explanation.detail:
• Explanation
• Option A. Pityriasis alba :
• Pityriasis alba typically presents as multiple, ill-defined , hypopigmented patches primarily on the face and is often associated with a history of atopy .
• The lesions are mildly scaly and non-atrophic , and sensation is intact .
• Option C. Atopic Dermatitis :
• Atopic dermatitis usually presents with pruritic, eczematous lesions, often in flexural areas, not typically as a single hypopigmented atrophic patch.
• Option D. Niacin Deficiency :
• Niacin deficiency, or pellagra, typically presents with dermatitis in sun-exposed areas , diarrhea, and dementia , not as a single hypopigmented atrophic patch on the face.
• Educational Objective:
• Diagnose indeterminate leprosy by identifying a single , hypopigmented , slightly atrophic patch on the skin , with possibly impaired sensation , especially in a patient from an endemic area .
• Jopling’s Handbook of Leprosy 6 th Edition Page no 16
explanation.ref: Jopling’s Handbook of Leprosy 6 th Edition Page no 16

subject: Dermatology
question: A 40-years-old male presented with multiple hypopigmented macules on trunk. On examination, the lesions were non-scaly, shiny and hypo aesthetic. Which of the following tests should be performed to confirm the diagnosis?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Diascopy is used to determine the nature of erythema or lesions by applying pressure with a glass slide. It helps to differentiate erythema from purpura but is not useful for diagnosing hypopigmented, anesthetic lesions in leprosy.
• Option B. Lepromin Test :
• Lepromin test is not a diagnostic test for leprosy. It is used to determine the type of immune response (cell-mediated immunity ) a person has to Mycobacterium leprae and can help classify the form of leprosy and to give estimate of the prognosis of the patients , but does not confirm the presence of the disease itself.
• Option D. Woods Lamp :
• Woods lamp is used to highlight areas of skin depigmentation , such as in vitiligo or some fungal infections. It is not useful for diagnosing leprosy, as the causative organism does not fluoresce under UV light.
• Educational Objective:
• Perform a slit skin smear to diagnose leprosy by detecting Mycobacterium leprae in skin lesions , particularly when the clinical presentation includes multiple hypopigmented , non-scaly , shiny , and hypoesthetic macules on the trunk .
explanation.ref: IAL Textbook of Leprosy, 2 nd Edition, Page No 278, 299

subject: Dermatology
question: Which of the following sensations should be checked first in a patient suffering from Leprosy?

explanation.short: Ans. C) Hot and Cold Differentiation
explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• In leprosy, the first sensation to be lost is the ability to differentiate between hot and cold temperatures , which is an early sign of nerve involvement.
• Sensations lost in the following order:
• Temperature ( Hot and Cold differentiation) > Fine Touch > Pain > deep touch or pressure
• Educational Objective:
• In leprosy, the differentiation between hot and cold temperatures is the first sensory modality affected due to nerve involvement. This early clinical sign should be checked first in patients suspected of having leprosy .
• IAL Textbook of Leprosy, 2 nd Edition Chapter 28 Page No 442
explanation.ref: IAL Textbook of Leprosy, 2 nd Edition Chapter 11 Page No 175

subject: Dermatology
question: All of the following statements are TRUE in relation to Lepromatous Leprosy, EXCEPT?
options: Lepromin Test is Negative as Cell Mediated Immunity is very Low
Facial Nerve involvement may lead to Blindness
Anaesthetic Skin Patches and Tender Nerves Seen

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. Patient is infective :
• Patients with lepromatous leprosy are considered i nfective due to the high bacterial load in their lesions.
• Option B. Lepromin test is negative as cell mediated immunity is very low :
• The lepromin skin test is typically negative in lepromatous leprosy because of the poor cell-mediated immune response against Mycobacterium leprae.
• Option C. Facial nerve involvement may lead to Blindness :
• Involvement of the facial nerve can lead to lagophthalmos (inability to close the eyelids completely), which can result in corneal ulcers and potential blindness if not treated.
• Educational Objective:
• In lepromatous leprosy, the hallmark features include extensive non-tender and non-anesthetic skin lesions due to the high number of bacteria and poor cell-mediated immunity , which also results in a negative lepromin test . Facial nerve involvement can lead to complications such as blindness , but tender nerves are not a feature of this type of leprosy.
explanation.ref: Jopling’s Handbook of Leprosy 6 th Edition Page no 29

subject: Dermatology
question: Which of the following is the commonest deformity seen in leprosy?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. Ape thumb deformity :
• Ape thumb deformity (also known as simian hand ) can occur in leprosy due to thenar muscle atrophy ; it is not the most common deformity.
• Option B. Leonine facies :
• Leonine facies describes the facial appearance often seen in lepromatous leprosy due to infiltrative lesions , not a deformity of the hands or feet.
• Option D. Foot drop :
• Foot drop, resulting from damage to the common peroneal nerve , is a common deformity in leprosy but not as frequent as claw hand.
• Educational Objective:
• Claw hand deformity is the most common deformity seen in leprosy. It results from ulnar nerve damage , a frequent occurrence in leprosy due to the bacillus's affinity for cooler temperatures , where nerves near the skin surface are affected.
explanation.ref: Jopling’s Handbook of Leprosy 6 th Edition Page no 331

subject: Dermatology
question: All of the following statements are true related to Mycobacterium leprae EXCEPT?
options: It infects Skin, Peripheral Nervous System and Upper Respiratory System
It is transmitted via Respiratory Tract
Solid, Fragmented and Granular Bacteria are all viable

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. It infects skin, peripheral nervous system and upper respiratory system :
• Mycobacterium leprae targets the skin, peripheral nerves , and the mucosa of the upper respiratory tract. It can also infect Eyes, Testes, Muscles and Bones . It has a predilection for cooler areas of the body.
• Option B. It is transmitted via Respiratory tract :
• The most widely accepted mode of transmission for Mycobacterium leprae is via the respiratory route , from nasal and other secretions of patients with untreated , multibacillary leprosy . It can also spread through skin-to-skin contact , via trans placental and through breast milk.
• Option C. It spares CNS, Uterus :
• Mycobacterium leprae does not typically infect the central nervous system or the uterus , as the organism prefers cooler temperatures than those found in these locations. CVS, GIT, Lungs, Prostate, Major blood vessels are also spared.
• Educational Objective:
• In leprosy, only solid-staining bacilli of Mycobacterium leprae are considered viable , whereas fragmented and granular forms are not. Recognizing the viability of bacterial forms is essential for understanding the infectiousness and treatment response of leprosy.
explanation.ref: IAL Textbook of Leprosy, 2 nd Edition Chapter 9 Page No 147

subject: Dermatology
question: Which of the following options is the classical descriptive term used for the lesions of Borderline Leprosy and also Borderline Lepromatous Leprosy?
options: Dew Drop on Rose Petal Appearance of Lesions

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. Saucer right way up lesions :
• "Saucer right way up lesions" are seen in Tuberculoid leprosy .
• Option C. Christmas tree appearance of lesions :
• The " Christmas tree " appearance is not typically used to describe leprosy lesions but is more commonly associated with other dermatological conditions like pityriasis rosea .
• Option D. Dew drop on rose petal appearance of lesions :
• This description is characteristically used for the vesicles seen in varicella (chickenpox) , not leprosy.
• Educational Objective:
• The "inverted saucer shaped lesions" is a descriptive term used for the lesions of Borderline (BB) and Borderline Lepromatous (BL) Leprosy, indicative of the specific morphological appearance of these lesions in the disease spectrum.
• IAL Textbook of Leprosy, 2 nd Edition Chapter 14 Page No 214
explanation.ref: Jopling’s Handbook of Leprosy 6 th Edition Page No 21, 25

subject: Dermatology
question:
Histopathology of a tissue sample from a patient with leprosy is shown below. All the following are possibilities EXCEPT.

[Image: https://cerebellum-web-static.s3.amazonaws.com/media/public/images/2024/02/22/1.jpg]

explanation.detail:
• Explanation
• Option B. Lepromatous Leprosy –
• Lepromatous leprosy represents the polar opposite of the leprosy spectrum with poor cell-mediated immunity leading to a multibacillary state . Lepromatous leprosy exhibits the Grenz zone most obviously because there are not many granulomas to fill the empty spaces below the epidermis. The Virchow cells with globi as indicated in the image, which are large macrophages filled with mycobacteria , are a characteristic feature of lepromatous leprosy . Additionally, it appears on the borderline spectrum .
• Option C. Borderline Leprosy –
• Borderline leprosy falls within the middle of the leprosy spectrum , with histological features that can fluctuate between those seen in tuberculoid and lepromatous forms. It can show a grenz zone and foamy macrophages, which makes it a possibility in this case.
• Option D. Borderline Lepromatous leprosy –
• Borderline Lepromatous leprosy is a subtype of leprosy that falls between the lepromatous and tuberculoid forms. It would show features of both, with numerous bacteria present as well as some immune response limiting the spread. Therefore, the histological findings seen in the image could correspond to this type, making it a possible option.
• Educational Objective:
• The absence of a grenz zone in histopathological samples is associated with tuberculoid leprosy , while the presence of a grenz zone and Virchow cells with globi are indicative of lepromatous forms of the disease .
explanation.ref: Rooks Textbook of Dermatology 9 th Edition Page No. 28.5

subject: Dermatology
question: All of the following are correct statements regarding management of Leprosy EXCEPT:
options: Multibacillary Leprosy cases need treatment with Multi Drug Therapy (MDT) for 12 Months
Dapsone acts as Anti Leprosy Medication by Inhibiting Folate Synthesis.
Lepromin Testing is used for Diagnosis of Leprosy
Clofazimine causes Deposition of Crystals in Intestinal Mucosa

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. Multibacillary leprosy cases need treatment with Multi Drug Therapy (MDT) for 12 months.
• Revised Treatment Regimen of Leprosy:
• Same Drug Dosages and Regimens for both Paucibacillary Leprsoy & Multibacillary Leprosy except Duration ; Paucibacillary Leprosy for 6 months & Multibacillary Leprosy for 12 months.
• Dapsone (DDS) has good oral absorption , is weakly bactericidal due to the inhibition of dihydropteroate synthase , which inhibits folate synthesis . It is a part of multidrug therapy for leprosy. Adverse effects of Dapsone are:
• Hemolysis & anemia Psychosis DDS syndrome or 5-week dermatitis - occurs 5-6 weeks after starting dapsone, which presents with:
• Erythroderma Hepatosplenomegaly Lymphadenopathy Fever Hepatitis
• It has a weak bactericidal activity against mycobacterium leprae & causes deposition of crystals in intestinal mucosa, liver, lymph nodes & spleen. Other adverse effects of Clofazimine are: Icthyosis of skin. Skin discoloration (red to purple black)
• Educational Objective:
• Lepromin test is used to assess the type of immune response in leprosy and is not a diagnostic test, while Multi Drug Therapy for 12 months , the mechanism of action of dapsone , and the side effects of clofazimine are important aspects of leprosy management.
• Rooks Textbook of Dermatology 9 th Edition Chapter 28 Page No. 28.1- 28.15
explanation.ref: Rooks Textbook of Dermatology 9 th Edition Chapter 28 Page No. 28.1- 28.15

subject: Dermatology
question: All of the following are correct statements regarding Reactions in leprosy EXCEPT:
options: Type 1 Lepra Reaction presents with new Red Painful Lesions
Systemic Symptoms are a feature of Type 2 Lepra Reaction
Type 2 Lepra Reaction is due to Immune Complex Deposition
Steroids are Drug of Choice in both Type 1 & 2 Lepra Reactions

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Type 2 lepra reaction is associated with Type III hypersensitivity reaction ( Immune complex deposition) & clinically it presents with new red painful lesions, and also systemic features like fever, arthralgias, anorexia, keratitis, iritis, nephritis, synovitis, orchitis.
• Educational Objective:
• In leprosy, Type 1 reactions involve inflammation of existing lesions rather than the appearance of new lesions, while Type 2 reactions may present with systemic symptoms and are related to immune complex deposition, with steroids being the treatment of choice for both types of reactions .
• Rooks Textbook of Dermatology 9 th Edition Chapter 28 Page No. 28.1- 28.16
explanation.ref: Rooks Textbook of Dermatology 9 th Edition Chapter 28 Page No. 28.1- 28.16

subject: Dermatology
question: Which diagnostic investigation is utilized to identify the condition characterized by a 40-year-old man displaying abrupt Erythematous Painful Lesions on the Trunk and Face, Joint Pain, and Glove and Stock Anesthesia? 1. Nerve examination 2. Skin biopsy 3. Slit skin smear 4. Mantoux test

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• The clinical scenario points to a case of leprosy . The diagnosis of this condition requires nerve examination, skin biopsy, and a slit skin smear (Option B - 1, 2, 3). The clinical presentation described suggests a condition called leprosy or Hansen's disease, which can cause skin lesions , nerve damage resulting in anaesthesia , and joint pain .
• Nerve examination - An essential part of the diagnosis of leprosy involves the examination of nerves for enlargement and tenderness , which can indicate nerve damage due to the disease. Skin biopsy - A biopsy of the affected skin can be taken and histologically examined for the presence of Mycobacterium leprae , the bacterium that causes leprosy . Slit skin smear - This test involves taking a sample from the skin (usually from the ear lobes or from lesions) and staining it for acid - fast bacilli , which is a characteristic of Mycobacterium leprae .
• The Mantoux Test (Option 4)
• The Mantoux test is used to diagnose tuberculosis , not leprosy. It involves the Tuberculin Skin Test ( TST ) to detect Mycobacterium Tuberculosis Infection. Mycobacterium Tuberculosis and Mycobacterium Leprae are both acid - fast Bacilli. The Mantoux test is specific for TB and does not directly aid in the diagnosis of leprosy
• Characteristic clinical features of Hansen’s Disease / Leprosy
• Mycobacterium leprae is the cause of the chronic granulomatous disease known as leprosy . The typical incubation period for leprosy is 2 to 12 years. It frequently manifests as a visible skin lesion, such as a hypopigmented or erythematous macule, or as a numb spot on the skin . The face, buttocks, trunk, and extensor aspects of the limbs are frequently affected by lesions . Skin, cutaneous, and peripheral nerves , as well as mucous membranes , are all impacted . The Ulnar nerve followed by the posterior tibial nerve , median and lateral popliteal nerves , are the peripheral nerves that are most frequently affected . The facial nerve is the cranial nerve that is involved most frequently. Both nerve function and hair growth are compromised by the lesions . Burns or ulcers on the amputation limb may also be present in the patients.
• Classification Systems of Leprosy:
• Ridley-Jopling Classification Tuberculoid (TT) Borderline tubercular (BT) Borderline (BB) Borderline lepromatous (BL) Lepromatous (LL)
• WHO Classification: Paucibacillary Multibacillary
• Cardinal Signs for the Diagnosis of Leprosy:
• The presence of two of the three signs can establish the diagnosis of leprosy . Other tests done for diagnosis of leprosy include nerve biopsy in pure neural leprosy and PGL-1 antibody in cases with low bacterial load . Mantoux test is not useful in diagnosis of leprosy . This is the test which helps to explain the prognosis of leprosy.
• Educational Objective:
• The appropriate diagnostic investigations for a suspected case of leprosy include nerve examination, skin biopsy , and slit skin smear to detect the presence of Mycobacterium leprae and to assess the extent of nerve damage .
• Harrisons Principles of Internal Medicine, 21 st Edition 1385, 1386, 1388
explanation.ref: Rooks Textbook of Dermatology- 9 th Edition Chapter 28 Page No 28.1, 28.3, 28.12

subject: Dermatology
question: In a pregnant woman at 12 weeks of gestation, who is currently undergoing multidrug therapy (MDT) for leprosy, she now presents with type 2 lepra reaction. What is the preferred treatment for this patient?
options: Stop MDT and Start Oral Steroids
Continue MDT and Add Oral Steroids

explanation.short: Ans. D) Continue MDT and Add Oral Steroids
explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. Stop MDT and start oral steroids:
• Stopping MDT is not advisable because it can lead to the proliferation of Mycobacterium leprae and risk the development of drug resistance.
• While antibiotics are a part of MDT to treat the bacterial infection , they alone are not sufficient to address the acute inflammatory symptoms of a Type 2 lepra reaction.
• Thalidomide is an effective treatment for Type 2 lepra reactions due to its potent anti-inflammatory properties. However, it is absolutely contraindicated in pregnant women due to its teratogenic effects , which can cause severe birth defects as seen in the below image .
• Educational Objective:
• In a pregnant woman with leprosy who presents with a Type 2 lepra reaction, the preferred treatment is to continue multidrug therapy and add oral steroids to manage the acute inflammatory symptoms . Thalidomide is c ontraindicated in pregnancy due to its teratogenicity.
• Harrison’s Principles of Internal Medicine 21 st Edition Page No 1387, 1390
explanation.ref: Rook's Textbook of Dermatology -9th Edition Chapter 28 Page No 28.16

subject: Dermatology
question: In a case where a patient undergoing Multi-Drug Therapy (MDT) experiences a deterioration of existing lesions and nerve complications, what course of action would you consider taking next?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. Stop MDT give thalidomide.
• Thalidomide is an immunomodulatory drug that may be used in certain conditions, like leprosy and its complications. However, stopping MDT is not recommended as it is crucial to continue the treatment of the primary disease. Thalidomide is not used as treatment for Type 1 lepra reaction in leprosy .
• Option B. Continue MDT, start thalidomide –
• While MDT should be continued, thalidomide is not typically used for nerve complications. It's used primarily for severe or recurrent erythema nodosum leprosum (ENL) Type 2 Lepra eaction, which is an immune-mediated complication of leprosy.
• Option C. Stop MDT, start systemic corticosteroids –
• Stopping MDT is not advised as the antibiotics in MDT are essential for treating the bacterial infection causing the disease . The MDT should be continued to prevent the development of drug resistance and to adequately treat the underlying infection .
• Educational Objective:
• In the case of nerve complications in a patient undergoing multi-drug therapy for conditions like leprosy , the appropriate course of action is to continue the anti-microbial therapy and start systemic steroids to manage the inflammatory reaction causing nerve damage.
explanation.ref: Rook's Textbook of Dermatology -9th Edition Chapter 28 Page No 28.5, 28.12, 28.16

subject: Dermatology
question:
A 35-year-old patient presents to the outpatient department with a satellite skin lesion. On examination, the patient has one hypopigmented lesion and one thickened nerve. The images of two blister packs are provided. Based on the WHO / NLEP Guidelines, which treatment is most appropriate for this patient?

[Image: https://cerebellum-web-static.s3.amazonaws.com/media/public/image_q7gUPBV.jpeg]

explanation.detail:
• ↳ Jopling’s handbook of leprosy, 6 th edition, page no 10
• ↳ Andrew’s diseases of the skin: clinical dermatology 13 th edition page no 338, 339, 345

subject: Dermatology
question: Chemoprophylaxis for Leprosy should be given to which of the following persons? Sharing the Same Clothes, Towels Living with a patient with Leprosy for > 6 Month Contact of Age more than 2 Years Contact for more than 20 Hours per Week.

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Chemoprophylaxis for leprosy should be given to contacts 2, 3 and 4.
• Chemoprophylaxis for leprosy should be given to the following contacts:
• Being identified as a contact, i.e., a person who has been in close contact with the index case for 20 hours ( option 4 ) or more per week for more than 3 months (Option 2) (Exception is blanket approach where no link with an index case needs to be established.) Age: More than 2 years (Option 3); if younger than 2 years , the child can be given SDR at the age of 2 , in follow-up visits of contacts (if meeting all other inclusion criteria.) Consent of the contact obtained . Depending on the country situation, this can be written or verbal consent . In the case of children, consent should be obtained from the parent or guardian, and sometimes an assent from the grown-up children. Sharing the same clothes and towels, while a potential route of transmission , is not generally considered a primary criterion for chemoprophylaxis.
• A single dose of 600 mg kills more than 99% of the susceptible organisms present at the start of treatment, that is as much as 3 to 6 months of daily treatment with the combination dapsone plus Clofazimine. Rifampicin has become established as a highly potent bactericidal drug , and it acts by inhibiting the bacterial DNA dependent RNA polymerase . Single Dose of Rifampicin (SDR) is used in chemoprophylaxis. Chemoprophylaxis must be initiated only 4 weeks after treatment for leprosy has begun in the patient to ensure the patient is non-infectious .
• Educational Objective
• Persons with possible signs and/or symptoms of Leprosy. Persons with possible signs and/or symptoms of TB or Confirmed with TB. Persons with a history of Liver or Kidney Disorders . Pregnancy ; SDR can be given after the delivery . Persons who have received Rifampicin in the last two years e.g. as treatment of TB, leprosy or as prophylaxis (e.g. contact of another index case). Persons with history of Allergy to Rifampicin . Refusal to take SDR.
• Leprosy/Hansen disease: Contact Tracing and Post-Exposure ;Prophylaxis
• https://www.who.int/publications/i/item/9789290228073 (Page 23, 24)
explanation.ref: Jopling’s Handbook of Leprosy 6 th Edition Page no 242

subject: Dermatology
question: Dapsone syndrome in Hansen disease treatment is seen after how many weeks of therapy initiation?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Dapsone syndrome, also known as "five-week dermatitis," usually occurs five to six weeks after the initiation of dapsone treatment in Hansen's disease (leprosy ). It is characterized by symptoms such as erythroderma, generalized lymphadenopathy, hepatosplenomegaly, fever, and hepatitis , and typically subsides upon cessation of the drug.
• Educational Objective:
• Dapsone syndrome , a hypersensitivity reaction characterized by a range of symptoms including erythroderma and hepatosplenomegaly , typically occurs about five to six weeks after the initiation of dapsone therapy in the treatment of Hansen's disease.
explanation.ref: Jopling’s Handbook of Leprosy 6 th Edition Page No 245

subject: Dermatology
question: A 40-year-old patient with a suspected case of leprosy undergoes a lepromin skin test. The test is administered to assess the patient's type of leprosy and potential response to treatment. Which of the following statements is INCORRECT regarding the lepromin test?
options: It is used to define the Immune Status of the patient.
It may be used for Prognosis.

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. It is used to define the immune status of the patient :
• The lepromin test is used to determine the patient's immune response to Mycobacterium leprae . A positive test suggests a good cell-mediated immune response, typically seen in tuberculoid leprosy.
• Option C. It is a cell-mediated response :
• The lepromin test measures a delayed-type hypersensitivity reaction, which is a cell-mediated immune response to the antigens of Mycobacterium leprae .
• Option D. It may be used for prognosis :
• A positive lepromin test generally indicates a better prognosis and is associated with a stronger cell-mediated immune response, which is typical of tuberculoid leprosy .
• Educational Objective:
• The lepromin test is used to assess the immune status of a patient with leprosy and is not a diagnostic test. It measures the cell-mediated immune response and can provide prognostic information , with a positive test indicating a better prognosis.
explanation.ref: Jopling’s Handbook of Leprosy 6 th Edition Page No 11

subject: Dermatology
question: A 12-year-old child presented with multiple hypopigmented patches on back & lower limb. On examination the patches were hypo aesthetic & bilateral ulnar and lateral popliteal nerves were enlarged. Which of the following drugs and their dosages are to be given on Day 1 of supervised drug administration according to National Leprosy Eradication Programme (NLEP) Guidelines?
options: Rifampicin 450 mg Clofazimine 150 mg Dapsone 50 mg
Rifampicin 600 mg Clofazimine 300 mg Dapsone 100 mg
Rifampicin 450 mg Clofazimine 150 mg Dapsone 100 mg
Rifampicin 300 mg Clofazimine 150 mg Dapsone 50 mg

explanation.short: Ans. A) Rifampicin 450 mg Clofazimine 150 mg Dapsone 50 mg
explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• According to the National Leprosy Eradication Programme (NLEP) Guidelines , for a child aged 9-14 years , the Day 1 supervised dose of medication for Multi-Bacillary (MB) leprosy includes Rifampicin 450 mg, Clofazimine 150 mg, and Dapsone 50 mg .
• Option B. Rifampicin 600 mg Clofazimine 300 mg Dapsone 100 mg :
• Incorrect because this dosage is for adults with MB leprosy , not for children.
• Educational Objective:
• The correct dosages of multi-drug therapy for a child with Multi-Bacillary (MB) leprosy on Day 1 of supervised treatment according to NLEP Guidelines , which for a child aged 9-14 years includes Rifampicin 450 mg, Clofazimine 150 mg, and Dapsone 50 mg .
explanation.ref: Jopling’s Handbook of Leprosy 6 th Edition Page No 252

subject: Dermatology
