question: A patient of leprosy on multidrug treatment came to OPD after 5 weeks of treatment with rash over the body. On examination he has high grade fever, jaundice, and an itchy rash all over the body. There was no evidence of any red tender nodules over the extremities or elsewhere over the body. What is the likely diagnosis of this patient?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option B. Erythema nodosum leprosum (ENL) :
• ENL, or Type II lepra reaction , presents with tender red nodules, systemic involvement like fever, malais e, and enlarged tender nerves .
• The absence of red tender nodules and nerve tenderness makes ENL less likely in this case.
• Option C. Lucio phenomenon :
• Lucio phenomenon is a rare reaction seen in diffuse lepromatous leprosy presenting with painful erythematous plaques , typically on the extremities , that can progress to necrosis and ulceration . It does not typically present with a generalized itchy rash or jaundice.
• Option D. Reversal reaction (Type I reaction) :
• Reversal reaction is characterized by the acute inflammation of pre-existing leprosy skin lesions and nerve enlargement without systemic symptoms like fever or jaundice. The pruritic rash and systemic symptoms described are not typical of a reversal reaction.
• Educational Objective:
• Dapsone Hypersensitivity syndrome in a leprosy patient who presents with fever, jaundice , and a widespread itchy rash without tender nodules or nerve enlargement , approximately 5 weeks after starting multidrug therapy . This serious reaction requires immediate attention and discontinuation of dapsone .
• IAL Textbook of Leprosy, 2 nd Edition Chapter 30 Page No 442
explanation.ref: IAL Textbook of Leprosy, 2 nd Edition Chapter 30 Page No 442

subject: Dermatology
question: Identify the INCORRECT statement regarding Treatment of a Multi Bacillary Leprosy patient?
options: 3 Drugs are given in the regimen
Treatment should be Completed in 18 Months

explanation.short: Ans. B) Treatment Duration is 18 Months
explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. 3 drugs are given in the regimen :
• The Standard Multi-Drug Therapy (MDT) regimen for multi-bacillary (MB) leprosy involves a combination of three drugs : Rifampicin, Dapsone, and Clofazimine .
• Option C. Treatment should be completed in 18 months:
• NLEP guidelines stipulate a 12-month treatment course for MB leprosy . This course can be completed in 18 months , otherwise patient is termed as Defaulter
• Option D. Follow up period is 24 months :
• The follow-up period after the completion of treatment for MB leprosy is typically 24 months to monitor for any signs of relapse or late reactions.
• Educational Objective:
• The treatment duration for multi-bacillary leprosy according to NLEP guidelines is 12 months, not 18 months, with a follow-up period of 24 months post-treatment to ensure there are no relapses or complications .
explanation.ref: Jopling’s Handbook of Leprosy 6 th Edition Page No 252

subject: Dermatology
question: Foam Cells are seen in Abundance in which type of Leprosy?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. Borderline lepromatous (BL ):
• BL leprosy can have foam cells , they are most abundant in LL leprosy where there is widespread infiltration of the skin with numerous bacilli .
• Option C. Pure Neuritic Leprosy:
• Pure Neuritic Leprosy primarily affects nerves , and foam cells are more typically associated with cutaneous lesions.
• Option D. Borderline (BB) :
• In BB leprosy, foam cells may be present but are not as prominent as in LL leprosy. BB leprosy has features between tuberculoid and lepromatous leprosy .
• Educational Objective:
• Lepromatous leprosy (LL) is the form of Hansen's disease most associated with the presence of abundant foam cells , which are macrophages filled with undigested M. leprae . These foam cells are indicative of the multibacillary form of the disease and are used as a diagnostic histopathological feature .
explanation.ref: Jopling’s Handbook of Leprosy 6 th Edition Page No 123

subject: Dermatology
question: Which of the following medications is used only for treating patients with Type II Lepra reaction but NOT used in Type I Lepra reaction?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Steroids are used in the management of both Type I and Type II lepra reactions. They are effective in reducing inflammation and immune reactions in both types of reactions .
• Azathioprine may be used as a steroid-sparin g agent in the treatment of both Type I and Type II lepra reactions, although it is not the first-line treatment for either.
• Chloroquine, an antimalarial medication , has been used in the treatment of Erythema Nodosum Leprosum (ENL) , which is a Type II reaction , but it is also used for Type I reactions .
• Educational Objective:
• Thalidomide is uniquely used for Type II lepra reactions ( erythema nodosum leprosum) and is not indicated for Type I lepra reactions. Thalidomide's effectiveness is based on its ability to inhibit TNF-alpha , a cytokine involved in the inflammatory process of ENL .
explanation.ref: Jopling’s Handbook of Leprosy 6 th Edition Page No 217

subject: Dermatology
question: What is the predominant Histological finding in a patient who has Tuberculoid leprosy?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. Foam Cells :
• Foam cells, or Virchow's cells , are typically found in lepromatous leprosy, not tuberculoid leprosy.
• Option C. Caseous Necrosis :
• Caseous necrosis is a characteristic feature of t uberculosis , not leprosy. In leprosy, especially the tuberculoid form, granulomas are predominantly seen and caseous necrosis is only seen with Nerve granulomas.
• Acantholysis refers to the loss of connections between keratinocytes in the epidermis and is seen in conditions such as pemphigus vulgaris , not in leprosy.
• Educational Objective:
• Granulomas is the predominant histopathological feature in tuberculoid leprosy, reflecting a strong cell-mediated immune response that contains the infection and prevents widespread dissemination.
explanation.ref: IAL Textbook of Leprosy, 2 nd Edition Chapter 14 Page No 136

subject: Dermatology
question: Which of the following statements is FALSE in relation to Type 2 Lepra Reaction?
options: Seen in Borderline Lepromatous Leprosy and Lepromatous Leprosy patients.
MDT should be Continued, and Steroids are to be added.
Underlying Pathogenesis is Increased Cell Mediated Immunity.
Patients present with High Grade Fever, Red Tender Nodules over Body.

explanation.short: Ans. C) Underlying Pathogenesis is Increased Cell Mediated Immunity.
explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. Seen in Borderline Lepromatous Leprosy and Lepromatous Leprosy patients :
• Type 2 Lepra Reaction, also known as Erythema Nodosum Leprosum (ENL), is typically seen in patients with Borderline Lepromatous Leprosy and Lepromatous Leprosy , which are at the multibacillary end of the leprosy spectrum.
• Option B. MDT should be continued, and Steroids are to be added :
• During a Type 2 reaction, the mainstay treatment includes the continuation of Multi-Drug Therapy (MDT) for leprosy along with the addition of steroids to manage the inflammatory reaction.
• Option D. Patients present with High grade fever, Red Tender nodules over body :
• The clinical presentation of ENL includes systemic symptoms such as high-grade fever and red tender nodules , known as erythema nodosum leprosum.
• Educational Objective:
• Type 2 Lepra Reaction (Erythema Nodosum Leprosum ) is associated with immune complex deposition rather than increased cell-mediated immunity . It presents with systemic symptoms and cutaneous manifestations requiring the continuation of MDT and the addition of anti-inflammatory treatment , typically steroids.
explanation.ref: IAL Textbook of Leprosy, 2 nd Edition Chapter 29 Page No 424

subject: Dermatology
question: A 36-year-old male presented multiple hypopigmented patches all over the body which are distributed almost symmetrically. On examination multiple thickened nerves almost symmetrically enlarged are palpable. What type of leprosy does the patient has?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. Pure Neuritic :
• Pure Neuritic leprosy presents with nerve involvement without the skin lesions, which is not the case in the scenario described.
• Option C. Borderline tuberculoid :
• Borderline tuberculoid leprosy would typically present with fewer and asymmetric skin lesions , and while nerve involvement is common, it does not typically present with symmetry as described.
• Indeterminate leprosy is an early and unstable form , often presenting as a single or a few hypopigmented macules without definite nerve enlargement.
• Educational Objective:
• Borderline Lepromatous leprosy as a type characterized by multiple almost symmetrically distributed skin lesions and multiple thickened nerves almost symmetrically enlarged .
explanation.ref: IAL Textbook of Leprosy, 2 nd Edition Chapter 15 Page No 244

subject: Dermatology
question: Identify the FALSE statement among the following options in relation to Thalidomide in the treatment of leprosy?
options: Also Useful in Type 1 Lepra Reaction

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. Teratogenic drug :
• Thalidomide is highly teratogenic and can cause severe birth defects , which is why its use is highly regulated and contraindicated during pregnancy.
• Option B. Causes Peripheral neuropathy :
• A known side effect of thalidomide is peripheral neuropathy , which can be a significant complication requiring monitoring during treatment.
• Option C. Useful in Type 2 Lepra reaction :
• Thalidomide is particularly effective in treating Type 2 Lepra Reactions , also known as Erythema Nodosum Leprosum (ENL) , due to its Anti-Inflammatory Properties .
• Educational Objective:
• Thalidomide is a Teratogenic Drug used in the treatment of Type 2 Lepra Reactions (Erythema Nodosum Leprosum ) and can cause peripheral neuropathy as a side effect , but it is not used for Type 1 Lepra reactions.
explanation.ref: IAL Textbook of Leprosy, 2 nd Edition Chapter 30 Page No 472

subject: Dermatology
question: Which among the following medications can cause orange discolouration of urine?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Dapsone does not typically cause orange discoloration of bodily fluids. It can cause hemolysis and methemoglobinemia as side effects , especially in patients with Glucose-6-Phosphate Dehydrogenase (G6PD) Deficiency.
• Thalidomide does not cause orange discoloration of urine; its significant side effects include teratogenicity and peripheral neuropathy .
• Clofazimine can cause skin and bodily fluids to turn pink to brownish black , but it is not typically associated with orange discoloration of urine.
• Educational Objective:
• Rifampicin can cause orange-red discoloration of bodily fluids , including urine, which is a harmless side effect and does not indicate harm or the need to stop therapy.
explanation.ref: IAL Textbook of Leprosy, 2 nd Edition Chapter 30 Page No 442

subject: Dermatology
question: Which of the following is associated with inflammatory manifestations of Erythema Nodosum Leprosum?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option B. Interferon Gamma (IFN‐ γ ) :
• While IFN-γ is a pro-inflammatory cytokine involved in the immune response against Mycobacterium leprae, it is not specifically implicated in the pathogenesis of ENL as prominently as TNF-α.
• Option C. Interleukin 10 (IL-10) :
• IL-10 is an anti-inflammatory cytokine , and while it plays a role in the immune response , it is not primarily responsible for the inflammatory manifestations seen in ENL.
• Option D. Transforming growth factor beta (TGF -β) :
• TGF-β is generally considered an anti-inflammatory and regulatory cytokine and is not typically associated with the inflammatory symptoms of ENL.
• Educational Objective:
• Tumour Necrosis Factor Alpha (TNF-α) is the cytokine closely associated with the inflammatory manifestations of Erythema Nodosum Leprosum in leprosy , and management often includes medications that modulate the activity of TNF-α.
• Jopling’s Handbook of Leprosy 6 th Edition Page No 220
explanation.ref: Jopling’s Handbook of Leprosy 6 th Edition Page No 220

subject: Dermatology
question: Which among the following is a anti tubercular medication which is also a most potent anti leprotic drug?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• While isoniazid is a key component of the anti-tubercular regimen , it is not the most potent drug against Mycobacterium leprae, the causative agent of leprosy.
• Ethambutol is used as part of the treatment for tuberculosis , but it does not have the same level of potency against Mycobacterium leprae as rifampicin.
• Ofloxacin is a fluoroquinolone antibiotic that can be used in the treatment of leprosy , particularly in cases of drug resistance . However, it is not as potent as rifampicin in the anti-leprotic regimen .
• Educational Objective:
• Rifampicin is the most potent anti-leprotic drug that is also used in the treatment of tuberculosis . It is a corner stone drug in the Multi-Drug Therapy (MDT) for leprosy due to its bactericidal action against Mycobacterium leprae.
explanation.ref: IAL Textbook of Leprosy, 2 nd Edition Chapter 31 Page No 449

subject: Dermatology
question: Which among the following is a anti leprotic drug which is also useful in treatment of Type 2 lepra reaction?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• While rifampicin is a potent anti-leprotic drug used in multi-drug therapy (MDT) for leprosy, it is not specifically used for the treatment of Type 2 lepra reaction (Erythema Nodosum Leprosum, ENL).
• Dapsone is an anti-leprotic drug used in MDT for leprosy , but it is not the treatment of choice for Type 2 lepra reactions. It is mainly used for its antibacterial effects against Mycobacterium leprae.
• Ofloxacin is used as a second-line agent for the treatment of leprosy , particularly in cases of drug resistance . However, it is not typically used for the treatment of Type 2 lepra reactions.
• Educational Objective:
• Clofazimine is an anti-leprotic medication that is also beneficial in the treatment of Type 2 lepra reactions due to its anti-inflammatory effects , in addition to its role in the multi-drug therapy for leprosy .
explanation.ref: Jopling’s Handbook of Leprosy 6 th Edition Page No 211

subject: Dermatology
question: A 15-year-old boy presents with patchy non scarring hair loss over scalp with exclamation mark hairs at margins. Which of the following is the most likely expected nail finding?
options: V Shaped Nicks on the Edge of the Nail

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option B. Thinning of nail :
• Thinning of the nail, is not typically associated with alopecia areata. It is seen in lichen planus .
• Option C. V shaped nicks on the edge of the nail :
• V-shaped nicks on the edge of the nail are typically associated with Dariers disease .
• Option D. Green nails :
• Green nails are indicative of a Pseudomonas aeruginosa infection or green nail syndrome , are not associated with alopecia areata.
• Educational Objective:
• N ail pitting is a common nail finding in patients with alopecia areata , an autoimmune disorder that also presents with patchy hair loss and characteristic exclamation mark hairs .
• Rooks Textbook of Dermatology- 9 th Edition Chapter 89 Page No 89.28-33
explanation.ref: Rooks Textbook of Dermatology- 9 th Edition Chapter 89 Page No 89.28-33

subject: Dermatology
question: Nail Pitting in Psoriasis indicates involvement of?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Pits develop when the proximal nail matrix keratinized abnormally in a few spots, resulting in a concentration of parakeratotic cells in the superficial nail plate.
• They appear as small punctate depressions of the superficial nail plate, which progress distally and often become more evident with nail growth.
• Educational Objective:
• Nail Pitting in Psoriasis indicates involvement of Proximal Nail Matrix
• Fitzpatrick’s Dermatology 8 th Edition Page No 1014
explanation.ref: Fitzpatrick’s Dermatology 8 th Edition Page No 1014

subject: Dermatology
question: Koilonychia is seen in all the following conditions EXCEPT?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Megaloblastic anemia does not result in koilonychia. Koilonychia describes a flattening or concavity of the nail's dorsal portion, creatingthe appearance of a spoon . It can occur in the following conditions:
• Iron deficiency anaemia Hemochromatosis Raynaud’s disease.
• The following image also shows koilonychia or spoon shaped nails :
• Educational Objective:
• Koilonychia is seen in Iron Deficiency Anaemia, Hemochromatosis, Raynaud’s Disease
• Rooks Textbook of Dermatology- 9 th Edition Chapter 95 Page No 95.7
explanation.ref: Rooks Textbook of Dermatology- 9 th Edition Chapter 95 Page No 95.7

subject: Dermatology
question: The following lines are NOT a pattern of apparent leukonychia?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. Muehrcke’s lines have multiple transverse white bands , parallel to lunula . They are specific finding of hypoalbuminemia. They are reversible with albumin infusion .
• Option C. Lindsay nails or half & halfnails . The proximal portion of the nail is white & the distal half is pink with a sharp line of demarcation between the two halves . They are specific for renal failure.
• Option D. Terry’s nails that are entirely white except for a small band of pink or brown at the tip are Terry's nails .
• Educational Objective:
• Muehrcke’s Lines, Lindsay Nails, Terrys Nails are examples of Apparent Leukonychia In Apparent Leukonychia, Pathology is predominant in the nail bed. These lines do not migrate with the growth of the nail.
• Rooks Textbook of Dermatology- 9th Edition Chapter 95 Page No 95.11, 95.14
explanation.ref: Rooks Textbook of Dermatology- 9th Edition Chapter 95 Page No 95.11, 95.14

subject: Dermatology
question: FALSE about Yellow Nail Syndrome & its associations?

explanation.detail:
• Educational Objective:
• Pitting edema is not a feature of Yellow Nail Syndrome ; instead, the syndrome is characterized by yellow dystrophic nails, an increased risk of malignancy, pleural effusion , and typically non-pitting lymphedema.
• Rooks Textbook of Dermatology- 9 th Edition Chapter 95 Page No 95.14
explanation.ref: Rooks Textbook of Dermatology- 9 th Edition Chapter 95 Page No 95.14

subject: Dermatology
question: Which of the following statements is INCORRECT about our nails?
options: Fingernails grow at speed of 3 mm/month
Lunula represents Distal Portion of Nail Matrix
Proximal Matrix forms Upper Nail Bed
Dorsal Pterygium is seen in Lichen Planus

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. Fingernails grow at a speed of 3 mm/month :
• The average rate of fingernail growth is approximately 3 mm per month Toenails grow even more slowly, typically 1 mm per month . Hair grows at a rate of 10 mm per month.
• Option B. Lunula represents the distal portion of the nail matrix :
• The lunula is the visible part of the nail matrix which is the whitish , crescent-shaped base of the visible nail.
• Lunula is present between proximal nail fold and nail bed and represents distal portion of nail matrix
• Option D. Dorsal pterygium is seen in lichen planus :
• Dorsal pterygium can be a consequence of the scarring process in lichen planus , a chronic inflammatory disease of the skin and mucous membranes . Ventral pterygium is seen in connective tissue disorders like SLE and Systemic sclerosis.
• Educational Objective
• The proximal matrix forms the upper part of the nail plate , not the upper nail bed, which is a key point in the anatomy of the nail.
• Rooks Textbook of Dermatology- 9 th Edition Page No 95.3, 95.10
explanation.ref: Rooks Textbook of Dermatology- 9 th Edition Page No 95.3, 95.10

subject: Dermatology
question: What is the nail finding seen in a patient of Hand Foot Mouth Disease (HFMD)?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Clubbing involves the swelling of the soft tissue at the nail base , leading to a change in the angle between the nail and the nail bed .
• It is commonly associated with chronic cardiopulmonary conditions and is not a feature of HFMD.
• Onycholysis is distal or lateral separation of nail plate from nail bed . It can result from trauma , infection , or psoriasis but is not typically seen in HFMD.
• Dorsal pterygium can be a consequence of the scarring process in lichen planus , a chronic inflammatorydisease of the skin and mucous membranes .
• Ventral pterygium is seen in connective tissue disorders like SLE and Systemic sclerosis.
• It is not a feature of HFMD.
• Educational Objective
• Onychomadesis , the separation of the proximal nail plate from the nail matrix and bed , is a nail finding that can follow Hand Foot Mouth Disease (HFMD).
• Rooks Textbook of Dermatology- 9 th Edition Page No 95.11
explanation.ref: Rooks Textbook of Dermatology- 9 th Edition Page No 95.11

subject: Dermatology
question: Identify the CORRECT statement regarding Onychomycosis among the following options?
options: Proximal Subungual Onychomycosis is the Most common form
Investigation of Choice is Nail Culture
Distal Lateral Subungual Onychomycosis is commonly associated with HIV Patients

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. The causative organism is Microsporumcanis :
• Microsporumcanis primarily affects the hair and skin and is not typically the causative organism in onychomycosis as it spares Nails . Onychomycosis is most commonly caused by dermatophytes like Trichophyton species but can also be due to yeasts such as Candida or non-dermatophyte molds.
• Option B. Proximal Subungual Onychomycosis is the Most common form :
• The most common form of onychomycosis is Distal Lateral Subungual Onychomycosis (DLSO) , which usually presents with Yellowing, Thickening, and Subungual hyperkeratosis starting at the distal and lateralnail bed and progressing proximally .
• Only subungual hyperkeratosis without tunnelling is seen in psoriasis
• Option D. Distal Lateral Subungual Onychomycosis is commonly associated with HIV patients :
• While Proximal Subungual Onychomycosis (PSO) is indeed more common in immunocompromised individuals , such as those with HIV/AIDS , DLSO can occur in a variety of patients, not specifically associated with HIV.
• Educational Objective
• The investigation of choice for diagnosing onychomycosis is nail culture , which helps identify the specific causative fungal organism.
• Rooks Textbook of Dermatology- 9 th Edition Page No 32.48
explanation.ref: Rooks Textbook of Dermatology- 9 th Edition Page No 32.48

subject: Dermatology
question:
Match the following Nail findings in Column A with Associated Causes / Diseases in Column B:

[Image: https://cerebellum-web-static.s3.amazonaws.com/media/public/images/2024/02/21/screenshot-2024-02-21-112446.jpg]

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option 1. Mees' lines (c Arsenic poisoning) :
• Mees' lines are horizontal white lines on the nails and are classically associated with arsenic poisoning .
• Option 2. Terry's Nails (a Cirrhosis) :
• Terry's nails are characterized by a white nail with a distal band of reddish-brown and can be seen in cirrhosis .
• Option 3. Lindsay's Nails (b Chronic renal failure) :
• Lindsay's nails, also known as half-and-half nails , have a proximal white area and a distal pink or brown band and are commonly associated with chronic renal failure .
• Option 4. Koilonychia (d Anemia) :
• Koilonychia, also known as spoon nails , is where the nails are concave in shape and can be associated with Iron Deficiency Anemia .
• Educational Objective
• Mees' lines with arsenic poisoning, Terry's nails with cirrhosis, Lindsay's nails with chronic renal failure, and koilonychia with anemia .
• Rooks Textbook of Dermatology- 9 th Edition Page No 95.7, 95.14
explanation.ref: Rooks Textbook of Dermatology- 9 th Edition Page No 95.7, 95.14

subject: Dermatology
question:
What is the level of pathology in the below image?

[Image: https://cerebellum-web-static.s3.amazonaws.com/media/public/images/2024/02/21/picture21_gieYiZK.jpg]

explanation.detail:
• Option B: Lindsay's nails,
• proximal portion being white
• Explanation:
• Option A . Nail Plate :
• Image depicts Lindsay nails / Half and Half nails which is an example for apparent leukonychia and is seen associated with Chronic renal failure and the pathology is in the nail bed not Nail plate.
• Option C. Distal Nail Matrix :
• Incorrect as pathology in the distal nail matrix would affect the formation of the nail plate itself, leading to changes in the texture or thickness of the nail plate as it grows out .
• Option D. Proximal Nail Matrix :
• Incorrect because changes in the proximal nail matrix would typically affect the mostsuperficial part of the nail plate as it forms, not the nail bed.
• Educational Objective:
• The pathology in Lindsay's nails , known as half -and -half nails , is located in the nail bed , which manifests as a bicolored appearance of the nails .
• Rooks Textbook of Dermatology- 9 th Edition Page No 95.14
explanation.ref: Rooks Textbook of Dermatology- 9 th Edition Page No 95.14

subject: Dermatology
question: Regular Pitting of Nails is seen in:

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• While nail pitting can indeed be seen in psoriasis, the pitting is typically irregular, deep, and coarse .
• Psoriasis can cause a variety of nail changes, like onycholysis (nail bed separation), and subungual hyperkeratosis.
• Option C. Lichen Planus :
• Incorrect because lichen planus typically causes longitudinal ridging and thinning of the nail , pterygium . Lichen planus can also lead to nail dystrophy or even loss of the nail plate .
• Option D. Herpes Zoster :
• Incorrect as herpes zoster, or shingles, affects the skin with vesicular eruptions following a dermatomal distribution . It is not typically associated with nail changes.
• Educational Objective
• Regular, Superficial, and Fine Pitting of the nails is characteristic of Alopecia Areata , whereas Irregular, Deep, and Coarse Pitting is more indicative of psoriasis.
explanation.ref: Rooks Textbook of Dermatology- 9 th Edition Page No 89.28-33

subject: Dermatology
question: Trachyonychia is seen associated with all of the following conditions EXCEPT?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. Lichen Planus :
• Trachyonychia , also known as "T wenty-nail dystrophy / Sand paper Nails ," is commonly associated with lichen planus . It is characterized by rough, opaque, and brittle nails as shown below.
• Trachyonychia can also be seen in patients with psoriasis, a condition that can cause a multitude of nail changes including pitting, onycholysis, and the "oil drop" sign.
• Option D. Alopecia Areata :
• Trachyonychia is often seen in patients with alopecia areata and can be a clue to the diagnosis when hair loss is subtle or not yet apparent.
• Educational Objective
• Trachyonychia is associated with conditions such as Lichen Planus, Psoriasis, and Alopecia Areata but not typically with pemphigus.
• Rooks Textbook of Dermatology- 9 th Edition Page No 37.12, 89.32, 95.11
explanation.ref: Rooks Textbook of Dermatology- 9 th Edition Page No 37.12, 89.32, 95.11

subject: Dermatology
question: Which of the following is the most common manifestation of Nail Psoriasis?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Onycholysis is the separation of the nail from the nail bed and is seen in nail psoriasis , but it is not the most frequent feature. It typically occurs in more severe cases and can be quite distressing to patients due to the functional impairment it can cause.
• Option B. Yellow Discoloration :
• Yellow discoloration is less common than pitting. It represents a build-up of psoriatic material in the nail bed.
• Option D. Subungual Hyperkeratosis :
• Subungual hyperkeratosis is the accumulation of scales beneath the distal nail plate , which can lead to nail plate elevation and is seen in nail psoriasis , but pitting is more commonly the initial finding .
• Educational Objective
• Nail pitting is the most common clinical manifestation of nail psoriasis , characterized by small, pinpoint depressions on the nail plate .
• Fitzpatrick’s Dermatology 8 th Edition Page No 1014
explanation.ref: Fitzpatrick’s Dermatology 8 th Edition Page No 1014

subject: Dermatology
question: A 24-year-old woman presents to the clinic with a 3-day history of pain and redness around her right thumbnail. She reports that the area is tender to touch and has progressively worsened. She denies any trauma or prior similar episodes. She works as a bartender and often has her hands in water. Examination reveals erythema, edema, and tenderness of the proximal nail fold, with some purulent discharge. Her temperature is 37.8°C (100°F). Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option B. Chronic Paronychia :
• Incorrect because chronic paronychia is a prolonged inflammation of the periungual tissues, often multifactorial, associated with frequent immersion in water, irritants , or allergens. It is characterized by less intensesymptoms over weeks to months , without significant purulent discharge.
• Option C. Alopecia Areata :
• Incorrect because alopecia areata is an autoimmune condition characterized by sudden hair loss in round patches on the scalp and other body parts, not associated with nail fold infection.
• Incorrect because psoriasis is a chronic autoimmune skin disease with characteristic findings of silvery scales on erythematous plaques , which can involve nails causing pitting, onycholysis, or subungual hyperkeratosis , but not usually acute inflammation or purulent discharge.
• Educational Objective:
• Diagnose acute paronychia based on the clinical presentation of rapid onset of pain, redness, and tenderness at the proximal nail fold , often with purulent discharge, typically associated with water exposure or trauma .
• Rooks Textbook of Dermatology- 9 th Edition Page No 95.35
explanation.ref: Rooks Textbook of Dermatology- 9 th Edition Page No 95.35

subject: Dermatology
question: A 56-year-old male with a history of diabetes mellitus presents to the clinic complaining of discoloration of his fingernails over the past several months. He mentions that the nails have turned a greenish hue. He is a retired plumber and enjoys woodworking in his spare time. He denies any pain, trauma, or previous similar episodes but notes a mild, persistent odour. On examination, the nails are thickened with a greenish-black discoloration. There is no significant erythema or warmth, and the surrounding skin appears intact. What is the most likely diagnosis?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. Acute Paronychia :
• Acute paronychia presents with rapid onset of pain, erythema, and swelling , often with purulent discharge, which is not consistent with the chronic discoloration and absence of acute inflammatory signs described in the vignette.
• Onychomycosis typically presents as thickening, yellowing, and crumbling of the nail , without the characteristic greenish discoloration that is described in the patient's presentation.
• Option D. Subungual Hematoma :
• Subungual hematoma typically presents with a painful, red to black discoloration under the nail following trauma , which is not consistent with the chronic, painless, greenish discoloration in this patient.
• Educational Objective:
• Diagnose green nail syndrome based on a characteristic greenish nail discoloration, potentially with a mild Odor , without significant inflammatory signs, in a patient with risk factors such as prolonged water exposure or occupations such as plumbing.
• Rooks textbook of dermatology- 9 th Edition page no 26.52
explanation.ref: Rooks textbook of dermatology- 9 th Edition page no 26.52

subject: Dermatology
question:
A 42-year-old woman with a history of chronic kidney disease presents to the clinic for a routine follow-up. She has no new complaints and feels well with her current management plan. On examination, you notice several transverse white bands across her fingernails as shown below and blanch when pressure is applied to the nail plate. She is concerned about the appearance of her nails and asks if this signifies a worsening of her condition. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

[Image: https://cerebellum-web-static.s3.amazonaws.com/media/public/images/2024/02/21/picture26.jpg]

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. Terry's nails:
• Terry’s nails typically present as a white nail plate with a distal band of reddish-brown and are associated with liver cirrhosis , which is not the pattern described.
• Option B. Beau's lines:
• Beau's lines are transverse indentations that move distally with nail growth and can be associated with any condition that causes a temporary cessation of nail growth , such as severe illness .
• Onychomycosis is a fungal infection of the nail plate t hat presents with thickening, discoloration, and sometimes separation of the nail from the nail bed, which does not describe the transverse white bands seen in this patient. Why it is not Lindsay Nails (Half-and-Half Nails): Although Lindsay nails are associated with chronic kidney disease , this image is not consistent with Lindsay nails because: Lindsay nails show a proximal white area and a distal brownish-red band , often separated by a sharp line. In contrast, this image shows multiple paired white bands across the nail, not the half-and-half appearance.
• Educational Objective:
• Muehrcke’s bands is paired , stationarywhite lines on the nails , indicative of underlying systemic conditions like hypoalbuminemia, particularly in patients with chronic kidney disease.
explanation.ref: Rooks Textbook of Dermatology- 9 th Edition Page No 95.14

subject: Dermatology
question: A 34-year-old woman presents to the clinic with a 6-month history of redness and swelling around her nail folds. She reports tenderness, especially when pressure is applied, and occasional pus drainage from around her nails. She works as a dishwasher and frequently has her hands in soapy water. Physical examination reveals erythema and edema around several nail folds, and the cuticle is no longer visible. There is no evidence of fever or deep tissue infection. What is the most likely diagnosis?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. Acute Paronychia :
• Acute paronychia is a rapid-onset condition , usually painful, and often associated with pus accumulation . It typically resolves quickly with treatment , which does not align with the chronic course described.
• Onychomycosis is a fungal infection of the nails , generally characterized by thickening, discoloration, and nail dystrophy , not primarily by inflammation of the nail folds.
• Option D. Herpetic Whitlow :
• Herpetic whitlow is a painful viral infection caused by herpes simplex virus, presenting with grouped vesicles on an erythematous base , typically on the fingertip , not as a chronic condition affecting the nail folds.
• Educational Objective:
• The signs and symptoms of chronic paronychia , includes persistent inflammation of the nail folds , tenderness , and possible pus discharge , often related to prolonged exposure to moisture , as seen in individuals who frequently have their hands in water .
• Rooks Textbook of Dermatology- 9 th Edition Page No 95.36
explanation.ref: Rooks Textbook of Dermatology- 9 th Edition Page No 95.36

subject: Dermatology
question: A 45-year-old man comes to the clinic for a follow-up visit after being hospitalized for severe pneumonia 2 months ago. He has recovered well and has no respiratory symptoms. However, he has noticed horizontal indentations across all of his fingernails, which he reports appeared after his hospitalization. He is concerned about these new nail changes and is curious about what they might indicate. Examination reveals transverse grooves across the nails, which the patient confirms are non-painful. Examination of rest of the body did not show any evidence of skin lesions. Which of the following is the most likely cause of the nail changes in this patient?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. Chronic Renal Failure :
• While chronic renal failure can cause various nail changes, including half-and-half nails (Lindsay’s nails), it is not typically associated with the formation of transverse grooves or lines across the nails.
• Psoriasis can cause nail changes, including pitting, onycholysis, and subungual hyperkeratosis , but the transverse grooves described, known as Beau's lines , are not specific to psoriasis and are typically related to an interruption in nail growth due to systemic illness .
• Option D. Fungal Nail Infection :
• Fungal nail infection, or onychomycosis, usually presents as thickening, discoloration, and separation of the nail from the nail bed , not as transverse grooves across the nails.
• Educational Objective:
• Beau's lines is transverse indentations that can appear on the nails following a temporary interruption of nail growth due to systemic illnesses , such as severe infections.
• Rooks Textbook of Dermatology- 9 th Edition Page No 95.11
explanation.ref: Rooks Textbook of Dermatology- 9 th Edition Page No 95.11

subject: Dermatology
question: Which of the following drugs used as nail lacquer belongs to morpholines?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• This is an azole antifungal drug . Azoles work by inhibiting the enzyme lanosterol 14-α-demethylase , which is crucial in converting lanosterol to ergosterol .
• Option C. Ciclopirox Olamine:
• Ciclopirox is a hydroxypyridone antifungal agent, not a morpholine.
• It acts by chelating polyvalent cations , resulting in the inhibition of the metal-dependent enzymes responsible for the degradation of peroxides within fungal cells .
• Tioconazole is another azole antifungal agent and, like oxiconazole, works by inhibiting ergosterol synthesis.
• Educational Objective:
• Amorolfine is the only morpholine derivative among the options given, used as a nail lacquer for the treatment of fungal infections .
• Rook's Textbook of Dermatology - 9th Edition - Volume I, Chapter 18 Page No 18.12
explanation.ref: Rook's Textbook of Dermatology - 9th Edition - Volume I, Chapter 18 Page No 18.12

subject: Dermatology
question:
A 38-year-old male comes with complaints in his fingernail. On examination, nail folds were seen to be erythematous & with pus collection as shown in the picture. The most likely diagnosis is?

[Image: https://cerebellum-web-static.s3.amazonaws.com/media/public/images/2024/02/21/picture32.jpg]

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option B. Yellow Nail Syndrome :
• Description : A rare condition characterized by slow growing, thickened, yellow nails as shown in the below image.
• Symptoms : Besides the characteristic yellow nails, the syndrome may also present with lymphedema (swelling due to lymphatic obstruction) and respiratory manifestations . Causes : The exact cause is unknown, but it's associated with chronic sinusitis, rheumatoid arthritis, and certain cancers.
• Description : A chronic inflammatory skin condition. Symptoms : Nail psoriasis can cause pitting, abnormal nail growth, and discoloration.
• Option D. Alopecia Areata:
• Description : An autoimmune disease where the body attacks its own hair follicles, resulting in hair loss . Symptoms : Sudden patchy hair loss in small, round patches on the scalp or body. Nails can also be affected, showing pits (which are superficial regular & Fine as shown in the below image) , white spots, roughness, or a sandpaper-like texture .
• Educational Objective:
• Paronychia is a condition presenting with Erythematous and Edematous nail folds , often accompanied by pus collection , commonly resulting from bacterial infection .
• Fitzpatrick’s Textbook of Dermatology 9th Edition Chapter 150 Page No 2725
• Andrews Diseases of the Skin Clinical Dermatology 11 th Edition Chapter 14, Page No 248
• Rook's Textbook of Dermatology - 9th Edition Chapter 95 Page No 95.35
explanation.ref: Fitzpatrick’s Textbook of Dermatology 9th Edition Chapter 150 Page No 2725

subject: Dermatology
question: Irregular pitting of the nails with subungual hyperkeratosis is seen in?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. Lichen Planus :
• Lichen planus can affect the nails and produce nail changes, but the common manifestations are nail thinning, ridging , or even complete loss of the nail . Pterygium formation , where there's scarring of the cuticle area leading to the nail's progressive loss , can also be seen. While lichen planus can cause nail pitting , it's typically not associated with subungual hyperkeratosis , a feature more consistent with psoriasis .
• Vitiligo is a disorder where there's a loss of pigment-producing cells ( melanocytes ) leading to white patches on the skin . It doesn't typically produce pitting of nails.
• Option D. Alopecia Areata :
• Geometric fine stippled pitting (Superficial, Regular, Fine) of the nails is the most likely expected finding as shown below.
• Educational Objective:
• Psoriasis is the condition most commonly associated with irregular nail pitting and subungual hyperkeratosis among the options provided.
• Rooks Textbook of Dermatology- 9 th Edition Page No 4.9, 35.4-35.34, 37.12, 41.1, 41.21 89.28-33
• Harrison’s Principles of Internal Medicine 20 th Edition Page No 333
explanation.ref: Rooks Textbook of Dermatology- 9 th Edition Page No 4.9, 35.4-35.34, 37.12, 41.1, 41.21 89.28-33

subject: Dermatology
question:
Which of the following is commonly associated with the condition shown below?

[Image: https://cerebellum-web-static.s3.amazonaws.com/media/public/images/2024/02/21/picture38.jpg]

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option C: The given scenario suggests alopecia areata in which geometric fine stippled pitting (Superficial, Regular, Fine) of nail is the most likely expected finding as shown below
• Alopecia areata is a chronic Autoimmune disease that causes non scaring patches of hair loss . It is a T cell mediated inflammatory disease that occurs in genetically predisposed individuals. Pigmented hair is more frequently affected in alopecia areata . Grey hair in older patients often includes both white and coloured hair . Only coloured hair will be impacted by the disease if it advances quickly , sparing white hair . This leads to a dramatic change in overall hair colour that is called going white overnight.
• The following image sparing of white hair within patches of hair loss .
• Educational Objective:
• Nail changes are common in alopecia areata , with nail pitting being the most frequent finding. Nail pitting appears as small depressions on the nail surface.
• Rooks Textbook of Dermatology- 9 th Edition Chapter 89 Page No 89.28-33
explanation.ref: Rooks Textbook of Dermatology- 9 th Edition Chapter 89 Page No 89.28-33

subject: Dermatology
question:
The image shown below is a pathognomonic finding in Psoriasis. What is it called?

[Image: https://cerebellum-web-static.s3.amazonaws.com/media/public/images/2024/02/21/picture39.jpg]

explanation.short: Ans. B) Subungual Oil Drop Sign
explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option D. Nail Pterygium :
• It is the most specific nail change in Lichen planus . Fusion of proximal nail fold and nail bed with destruction of nail plate , leads to pterygium as shown in the below image.
• Educational Objective:
• Subungual oil drop sign is a specific clinical finding in nail psoriasis, which presents as a yellow to orange discoloration under the nail plate , distinguishing it from other nail changes such as pitting , thinning , or pterygium .
• Rooks Textbook of Dermatology- 9 th Edition Chapter 35 Page No 35.4-35.6, 35.9
• Harrison’s Principles of Internal Medicine 20 th Edition Page No 333
explanation.ref: Rooks Textbook of Dermatology- 9 th Edition Chapter 35 Page No 35.4-35.6, 35.9

subject: Dermatology
question:
A 35-year-old male presents with the following skin finding. Which of the following clinical signs will aid in Diagnosis?

[Image: https://cerebellum-web-static.s3.amazonaws.com/media/public/images/2023/04/07/picture9_lUw0oDC.jpg]

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. Skin Biopsy:
• It is a diagnostic procedure that can help to differentiate between various skin conditions by providing histopathological evidence. However, it is not a clinical sign but rather a diagnostic tool and may not be necessary for classic presentations of psoriasis.
• Option C. KOH Smear:
• It is used to diagnose fungal infections by revealing fungal elements in the skin flakes when examined under a microscope. This would not aid in diagnosing psoriasis, which is not a fungal infection.
• Option D. Besnier’s Sign/Scratch Sign/Stroke of the Nail/Coup d’ongle Sign:
• The fine branny scales of pityriasis versicolor may become visible after scratching the lesions.
• Educational Objective:
• The Auspitz sign is a clinical indicator of psoriasis , characterized by pinpoint bleeding when scales from a psoriatic plaque are scraped away . This sign can be useful in the clinical diagnosis of psoriasis without the need for invasive testing .
explanation.ref: Rooks Textbook of Dermatology- 9 th Edition Chapter 4 Page No 4.17-18

subject: Dermatology
question: Which of the following statements is NOT true regarding Psoriasis?
options: Nail Pitting seen is of Deep Irregular & Coarse Type
Joint involvement is seen in 5-30% of Patients

explanation.short: Ans. D) Deficient TH1 & TH17 Cells
explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. Nail Pitting
• It is a common clinical feature of psoriasis. The pitting is typically deep , irregular , and coarse , which helps differentiate it from the finer pitting seen in conditions like alopecia areata .
• Option B. Genetic Predisposition
• The HLA-C*06:02 allele is strongly associated with psoriasis , particularly with early-onset psoriasis .
• Option C. Joint involvement
• Psoriatic arthritis, which involves joint inflammation, occurs in 5-30% of patients with psoriasis .
• Nail Changes in Psoriasis:
• Salmon patches or subungual oil drop sign is extremely specific pathognomonic sign of nail psoriasis. It looks like an oil droplet and is a yellowish , reddish discoloration in the nail bed. However, the most frequent symptom is nail pitting . Individual pits are irregular in shape and uniform in depth . These nail pits, also referred to as Thimblepits , are brought on by the proximal nail matrix's involvement. Subungual hyperkeratosis , splinter hemorrhages , and distal onycholysis are additional symptoms of nail bed disease. There might also be leukonychia .
• Pitting affecting the fingernails.
• Onycholysis along with pitting and salmon patches in fingernails
• Leukonychia in a patient with psoriasis
• Nail plate thickening and crumbling resulting in complete nail dystrophy. Features of Nail Psoriasis - Mnemonic "Nail - POLISH" P – Pitting (Most common feature) O - Oil drop sign (Pathognomonic), Onycholysis L - Leukonychia I - Irregular pitting pattern S - Splinter Hemorrhage H - Hyperkeratosis (Subungual)
• Educational Objective:
• Psoriasis is associated with an increased Activity of Th1 and Th17 Helper Cells , contrary to a Deficiency, and recognize the clinical features and genetic predispositions linked to the Disease.
• Harrison’s Principles of Internal Medicine 20th Edition Page No 333
explanation.ref: Rooks Textbook of Dermatology- 9th Edition Chapter 35 Page No 35.4-35.6

subject: Dermatology
question:
A patient presented to her primary care physician with an itchy scalp & dandruff. O/E the following finding was seen & True Koebner's phenomenon was present. The disease is LEAST likely to affect the?

[Image: https://cerebellum-web-static.s3.amazonaws.com/media/public/images/2023/04/07/picture6_ZKQMbgd.jpg]

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option B. Gluteal Cleft
• Psoriasis can affect the gluteal cleft .
• Option C. Lumbosacral Area
• The lumbosacral area is a common site for plaque psoriasis , which can be exacerbated by factors like friction or pressure .
• Option D. Elbows & Knees
• These are classic sites for plaque psoriasis due to the frequent trauma and pressure experienced by these areas.
• Educational Objective:
• A xilla is the least likely location to be affected by psoriasis among the options provided, as psoriasis tends to occur in areas subjected to frequent pressure or trauma , which is less characteristic of the axillary region.
• Rooks Textbook of Dermatology- 9 th Edition Chapter 35 Page No 35.5
explanation.ref: Harrison’s Principles of Internal Medicine 19 th Edition Page No 347

subject: Dermatology
question: Histopathology of Psoriasis is unlikely to show which of the following?
options: Camel Foot Appearance of Rete Ridges

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. Camel Foot Appearance of Rete Ridges
• Psoriasis histology often shows elongation of the rete ridges in a pattern resembling the two toes of a camel's foot.
• Image: Regular Acanthosis with Rete Peg Elongation giving Rete Ridges a "Camel Foot" Appearance
• Option B. Supra Papillary Thinning.
• In psoriasis, the epidermis above the dermal papillae is thinned , which is referred to as suprapapillary thinning .
• Option C. Kogoj Spongiform Pustules
• This refers to clusters of neutrophils within the epidermis.
• Educational Objective:
• Psoriasis histopathology typically includes Elongated Rete Ridges, Suprapapillary Thinning, and sometimes the presence of Kogoj Spongiform Pustules , but not wedge-shaped hypergranulosis.
explanation.ref: Rooks Textbook of Dermatology- 9th Edition Chapter 35 Page No 35.6-7

subject: Dermatology
question: Which of the following is the FALSE statement regarding Psoriatic Arthritis?
options: Classic Psoriatic Arthritis typically involves Distal Interphalangeal Joints
Arthritis Mutilans is worst type of Psoriatic Arthritis
Symmetric Oligoarthritis is the most common type of Psoriatic Arthritis.
Methotrexate is the drug of choice for Psoriatic Arthritis

explanation.short: Ans. C) Symmetric Oligoarthritis is the most common type of Psoriatic Arthritis
explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. Classic psoriatic arthritis typically involves Distal Interphalangeal joints
• Option B. Arthritis mutilans is worst type of Psoriatic arthritis.
• Option D. Methotrexate & apremilast are the first line drugs for the treatment of moderate to severe psoriasis associated with psoriatic arthritis.
• Educational Objective:
• Asymmetric oligoarthritis is the most common form of psoriatic arthritis, affecting fewer than five joints and usually not the same joints on both sides of the body, while symmetric involvement is more characteristic of rheumatoid arthritis.
• ABC of Rheumatology 2018, Wiley Blackwell British Medical Journal Pg No 85
• Rooks’ Textbook of Dermatology- 9th Edition Page No 35.31
explanation.ref: Rooks Textbook of Dermatology- 9th Edition Chapter 35 Page No 35.43

subject: Dermatology
question:
A 42-year-old man with a history of psoriasis vulgaris was referred to the derma OPD with 6 weeks of worsening psoriasis. He was started on captopril for hypertension 4 months prior. On Examination following findings were noted. What is the most likely diagnosis?

[Image: https://cerebellum-web-static.s3.amazonaws.com/media/public/images/2023/12/08/picture7_nOTOz4z.jpg]

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option B. Photosensitive Psoriasis
• It is a type of psoriasis that specifically worsens with exposure to sunlight , which is not indicated in the scenario.
• Option C. Guttate Psoriasis
• It typically presents as numerous small , drop-like lesions and often follows a streptococcal throat infection . It's not commonly associated with medication use.
• Option D. Flexural Psoriasis
• This typically affects body folds and is not usually related to medication use.
• Educational Objective:
• The development of Rupoid psoriasis can be associated with the use of medications such as captopril in a patient with a history of psoriasis vulgaris.
explanation.ref: Journal - https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/25279472/

subject: Dermatology
question:
A patient was treated with steroids for psoriasis. On stopping the treatment, she develops fever, malaise & lesions as shown in the image below. Pustular psoriasis is suspected with the given history. Now Drug of choice for this condition is to be initiated. Which of the following statements is FALSE regarding the drug?

[Image: https://cerebellum-web-static.s3.amazonaws.com/media/public/images/2023/04/07/picture17_BOc4RdS.jpg]
options: Female Patients should Avoid getting Pregnant for 6 Months After Therapy

explanation.short: Female Patients should Avoid getting Pregnant for 6 Months After Therapy
explanation.detail:
• Educational Objective:
• The pharmacological profile and safety c onsiderations of acitretin, particularly its teratogenic potential, necessitating prolonged contraception after cessation of therapy in female patients .
• Online Resources - https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/19998529/
• Rooks Textbook of Dermatology- 9 th Edition Page No 18.18, 26.22, 35.33
explanation.ref: Goodman Gillman’s the Pharmacological Basis of Therapeutics – 13 th Edition Page No 1276

subject: Dermatology
question: In Which of the following cases would you prescribe systemic corticosteroids?

explanation.short: Ans. A) 26-year-old Woman with Impetigo Herpetiformis
explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option B . Psoriatic Arthritis:
• While systemic corticosteroids might be used in severe psoriatic arthritis , they are generally not the first-line treatment due to potential long-term side effects and the availability of other treatments like DMARDs (Disease-Modifying Antirheumatic Drugs) or biologics.
• Option C . Guttate Psoriasis:
• Guttate psoriasis often resolves on its own and is usually treated with topical therapies or light therapy rather than systemic corticosteroids, unless other treatments have failed, and the condition is severe. DOC - Erythromycin (Macrolides)
• Option D . Erythrodermic Psoriasis
• Erythrodermic psoriasis is a severe form of psoriasis that can be life-threatening and may require systemic treatment ( Methotrexate); however, systemic corticosteroids are used with caution due to the risk of causing a rebound flare upon withdrawal.
• Educational Objective:
• Prescribe systemic corticosteroids for a 26-year-old woman with Impetigo herpetiformis , which is a pustular psoriasis of pregnancy requiring aggressive management due to its potential severity .
explanation.ref: Rooks Textbook of Dermatology- 9th Edition Page No 35.35, 35.37

subject: Dermatology
question: A researcher developed drug X for psoriasis that acts on IL-17. Drug X can be all EXCEPT?

explanation.detail:
• Educational Objective:
• While Brodalumab , Secukinumab , and Ixekizumab are IL-17 inhibitors used in the treatment of psoriasis, Infliximab targets TNF-alpha and is not an IL-17 inhibitor.
explanation.ref: Rooks Textbook of Dermatology- 9 th Edition Page No 35.46

subject: Dermatology
question:
Ten Days after discharge from the hospital following an Episode of Pharyngitis, a 13-Year-Old Boy developed Skin Lesions on his Trunk and the Proximal Part of his Arms. Which of the following is TRUE about this condition?

[Image: https://cerebellum-web-static.s3.amazonaws.com/media/public/images/2024/10/06/image_Zhdzvot.png]
options: It is associated with Fever & Hypocalcemia

explanation.short: Ans. B) Associated with HLA C: 06:02
explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. It follows a Chronic Course:
• Guttate psoriasis, especially in children, often presents as an acute eruption and may resolve spontaneously , making it not typically chronic.
• Option C. It responds to Acitretin:
• While acitretin can be used in the treatment of psoriasis , it is not the first-line treatment for guttate psoriasis, especially in children.
• Option D. It is associated with Fever & Hypocalcemia:
• Fever and hypocalcaemia are not typical features of guttate psoriasis. These symptoms are more indicative of pustular psoriasis or other systemic illnesses.
• Educational Objective:
• Guttate psoriasis , is a condition that often occurs following Streptococcal Infection and is associated with HLA C:06:02 . This form of psoriasis typically presents Acutely and may Resolve without progressing to a chronic course . DOC - Erythromycin (Macrolides)
• Andrew’s Diseases of Skin Clinical Dermatology – 12 Edition Page No 188-189
• Online resources - https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK482498/
explanation.ref: Rooks Textbook of Dermatology- 9th Edition Page No 35.15 & 35.16

subject: Dermatology
question: A 55-year-old man presents to the Clinic with a 2-Week History of Progressive Skin Redness and scaling covering more than 90% of his Body Surface Area. He reports associated Pruritus and Malaise. His Medical History is significant for Psoriasis, which has been well-controlled with Topical Therapies. On examination, his Vital Signs are within Normal Limits, but he Appears Uncomfortable due to the Skin Changes. Laboratory Tests reveal an Elevated Erythrocyte Sedimentation Rate (ESR) and Mild Eosinophilia. What is the most likely Diagnosis?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. Acute Generalized Exanthematous Pustulosis (AGEP):
• It is characterized by the rapid onset of numerous sterile pustules , fever , and leukocytosis . The absence of pustular lesions in this patient makes AGEP less likely.
• Option B. Drug-Induced Hypersensitivity Syndrome :
• It typically presents with fever , rash , and multiorgan involvement , often 2-6 weeks after exposure to a new medication. This patient’s symptoms are more consistent with erythroderma and there's no mention of new medication use or organ involvement.
• Option D. Pemphigus Vulgaris:
• It is an autoimmune blistering disorder that presents with flaccid blisters and erosions but not the widespread erythema and scaling seen in erythroderma.
• Educational Objective:
• Erythroderma in a patient with extensive skin redness and scaling, particularly in the context of a history of psoriasis.
• Rooks Textbook of Dermatology- 9th Edition Page No 35.35, 35.37
• IADVL Textbook of Dermatology 2 nd Edition Page No 441
explanation.ref: Bolognia Dermatology Textbook 4 th Edition Page No 175

subject: Dermatology
question: A 40-year-old woman presents with a 6-month history of joint pain and stiffness, primarily in her hands and feet. She mentions that the pain is worse in the morning and improves with activity. On examination, there is noticeable swelling of the distal interphalangeal joints. She also has silvery, scaly plaques on her elbows and knees. Her family history is positive for psoriasis. There is no history of gastrointestinal symptoms or eye inflammation. What is the most likely diagnosis?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. Rheumatoid Arthritis:
• It typically presents with symmetric polyarthritis affecting the proximal interphalangeal and metacarpophalangeal joints . While morning stiffness is a common feature, the presence of psoriatic skin lesions is not characteristic of rheumatoid arthritis.
• It usually presents with acute , severe pain and swelling , often starting in the big toe ( podagra ). Chronic gout can affect other joints, but it is less likely to present with the skin findings seen in psoriasis.
• Option D. Ankylosing Spondylitis:
• It is more commonly associated with lower back pain and stiffness that improves with exercise but worsens with rest. It also often presents with sacroiliitis and enthesitis , which are not described in the patient's presentation.
• Educational Objective:
• Diagnose psoriatic arthritis in a patient presenting with joint pain and stiffness in the distal interphalangeal joints , morning stiffness , and associated psoriatic skin lesions, especially with a family history of psoriasis.
• Rooks’ Textbook of Dermatology- 9th Edition Page No 7.7
explanation.ref: Bolognia Dermatology Textbook 4 th Edition Page No 148

subject: Dermatology
question:
A patient was treated with steroids for psoriasis. On stopping the treatment, he develops fever, malaise, and lesions as seen in the image below. What is the Most Probable Diagnosis?

[Image: https://cerebellum-web-static.s3.amazonaws.com/media/public/images/2023/12/08/picture10_OKRL4Xf.jpg]

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option B: Staphylococcal infection :
• While staphylococcal infections can cause skin lesions, there's no direct link provided between steroid withdrawal and the onset of such infections. In fact, steroid usage would initiate such staphylococcal infections, not withdrawal .
• Option C: Acute Generalized Exanthematous pustulosis (AGEP):
• AGEP is typically a reaction to medications not due to steroid withdrawal, and its presentation can be similar to pustular psoriasis.
• Option D: Sub Corneal Pustulosis Dermatosis is also known as Sneddon-Wilkinson disease .
• It is a rare condition, characterised by pustules that appear in crops over months or years . However, there is no direct link between steroid withdrawal and SCPD.
• Educational Objective:
• When a patient with a history of psoriasis presents with fever, malaise , and widespread pustules following the abrupt withdrawal of systemic corticosteroids, consider pustular psoriasis as the probable diagnosis . This condition can be a severe, life-threatening variant of psoriasis that requires prompt medical attention and management .
• Online Resources - https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/19998529/
• Rook’s Textbook of Dermatology - 9 th Edition Page No 18.18, 26.22, 35.28, 35.33
explanation.ref: Goodman Gillman’s he Pharmacological Basis of Therapeutics – 13 th Edition Page No 1276

subject: Dermatology
question: A 36-year-old male with a 10-year history of plaque psoriasis not adequately controlled with topical therapies is being considered for systemic treatment. Which of the following treatments is NOT an anti-TNF alpha inhibitor commonly used for psoriasis?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• It is an anti-TNF alpha inhibitor used in the treatment of moderate to severe plaque psoriasis, especially when it's associated with psoriatic arthritis.
• It is another anti-TNF alpha inhibitor indicated for the treatment of both psoriasis and psoriatic arthritis , reflecting its efficacy in treating skin lesions as well as joint inflammation.
• It is also an anti-TNF alpha inhibitor that has been used in the treatment of chronic moderate to severe plaque psoriasis.
• Educational Objective:
• Alefacept is not an anti-TNF alpha inhibitor and is no longer available for the treatment of psoriasis, unlike infliximab , adalimumab , and etanercept , which are anti-TNF alpha therapies used in this condition.
• Rook’s Textbook of Dermatology - 9 th Edition Page No 35.40
• Online Resources – https://www.japha.org/article/S1544-3191(15)31922-1/fulltext
explanation.ref: Bolognia Textbook of Dermatology 4 th Edition Page No 2257

subject: Dermatology
question: A 52-year-old male presented to the dermatology OPD with a skin lesion. On examination, there is a well-demarcated plaque on the right knee which was covered with silvery white scales. it has been growing slowly for the past 4 years. A diagnosis of psoriasis was made. Which of the following variants of the given condition will demonstrate the development of pinpoint bleeding on scrapping the scaly lesions?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. Pustular Psoriasis:
• It is a rare form of psoriasis characterized by white pustules surrounded by red skin . While it may have scaling, the hallmark is the pustules rather than the bleeding seen with plaque psoriasis .
• Option B. Guttate Psoriasis:
• It typically presents as numerous small, scaly, red or pink droplet-like lesions (papules) rather than thick plaques and does not typically demonstrate the Auspitz sign.
• Option C. Erythrodermic Psoriasis:
• It involves widespread redness and exfoliation of the skin over a large area , and while scaling is present, pinpoint bleeding is not a characteristic feature.
• Educational Objective:
• Identify the Auspitz sign , which is indicative of psoriasis vulgaris, characterized by pinpoint bleeding upon scraping of the silvery scales from a psoriatic plaque.
• Rooks Textbook of Dermatology- 9 th Edition Chapter 35 Page No 35.5
explanation.ref: Harrison’s Principles of Internal Medicine 19 th Edition Page No 347

subject: Dermatology
question: Following is the list of some Skin Diseases: Lichen Planus Xeroderma Pigmentosum Psoriasis Vitiligo Which of the following combinations of Diseases which are mentioned above can be treated with Ultraviolet (UV) Light?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option B: Xeroderma pigmentosum (2) cannot be treated with UV light therapy and is, in fact, a condition where UV exposure must be avoided.
• Option C: Lichen planus (1) is missing , which can be treated with UV light therapy.
• Option D: Includes Xeroderma pigmentosum (2), which is not treated with UV light therapy due to the condition's UV sensitivity.
• Educational Objective:
• UV light therapy is indicated for lichen planus, psoriasis, and vitiligo but is contraindicated in xeroderma pigmentosum due to the harmful effects of UV light in this DNA repair-deficient condition.
• Wolverton Comprehensive Dermatologic Drug Therapy Page No 265
explanation.ref: Rooks Textbook of Dermatology- 9 th Edition Chapter 35 Page No 35.63

subject: Dermatology
question:
Identify the finding associated with the condition shown in the image below which is Characterized by Development of Morphologically Similar Lesions along the Lines of Trauma:

[Image: https://cerebellum-web-static.s3.amazonaws.com/media/public/images/2023/12/08/picture16_txpryX0.jpg]

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A: Gottron's Papule
• It is seen associated with Dermatomyositis.
• Option B. Nikolsky’s Sign:
• Description : Positive when the superficial epidermis moves laterally with slight rubbing or pressure , leading to the separation of the epidermis from the underlying dermis as shown in the below image.
• Option D: Meyerson Phenomenon
• It is a Melanocytic Nevus that has developed an eczematous ring around it as seen in the image below.
• Educational Objective:
• Isomorphic Phenomenon (Koebner Phenomenon) is the appearance of skin lesions similar to a primary disease along areas of skin trauma , which is important for diagnosis and management in dermatology .
explanation.ref: Rooks Textbook of Dermatology- 9 th Edition Page No 123.2

subject: Dermatology
question:
The image shown below is a Pathognomonic Finding in Psoriasis. What is it Called?

[Image: https://cerebellum-web-static.s3.amazonaws.com/media/public/images/2023/12/08/picture20_wuDPkJL.jpg]

explanation.short: Ans. B) Subungual Oil Drop Sign
explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option D: Nail Pterygium
• It is the most specific nail change in Lichen planus . Fusion of proximal nail fold and nail bed with destruction of nail plate , leads to pterygium as shown in the below image.
• Educational Objective:
• Subungual Oil Drop Sign is a specific clinical finding in Nail Psoriasis, which presents as a Yellow to Orange Discoloration under the Nail Plate , distinguishing it from other nail changes such as Pitting, Thinning, or Pterygium .
• Harrison’s Principles of Internal Medicine 20 th Edition Page No 333
explanation.ref: Rooks Textbook of Dermatology- 9 th Edition Chapter 35 Page No 35.4-35.6, 35.9

subject: Dermatology
question:
Identify the Type of Psoriasis in the given picture?

[Image: https://cerebellum-web-static.s3.amazonaws.com/media/public/images/2024/10/06/image_lXqlnKR.png]

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option B. Erythrodermic Psoriasis:
• This is a severe form of psoriasis where the skin appears extensively red and inflamed. The skin sheds in sheets rather than scales. It lacks the distinct drop-shaped papules of guttate psoriasis.
• Option C. Pustular Psoriasis:
• Characterized by white blisters of non-infectious pus surrounded by red skin. The main feature is the presence of pustules , which are not described in your picture.
• Option D. Inverse Psoriasis:
• Found in the folds of the skin, such as under the armpits , in the groin, under the breasts, and other areas. Appears as very red, smooth, and shiny lesions . Unlike other forms, it lacks the classic scales seen in the " Rain Drop " pattern of guttate psoriasis .
• Educational Objective:
• Identify Guttate Psoriasis by its classic presentation of numerous small, drop-like papules , often appearing after a streptococcal infection, which is distinct from other psoriasis types that have different presentations and localizations .
• Andrew’s Diseases of Skin Clinical Dermatology – 12 Edition Page No 188-189
• Online Resources - https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK482498
explanation.ref: Rooks Textbook of Dermatology- 9 th Edition Page No 35.4, 35.15 & 35.16

subject: Dermatology
question: A patient with a known case of chronic plaque psoriasis with more than 50% skin involvement presents to you. Which of the following will you NOT recommend?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. Oral Methotrexate :
• Methotrexate is an anti-metabolite and immune modulator commonly used as a systemic treatment for moderate to severe plaque psoriasis . It is effective in reducing the proliferation of skin cells and has anti - inflammatory properties.
• Option B. NB UVB (Narrowband Ultraviolet B):
• NB UVB phototherapy is a well-established treatment for moderate to severe plaque psoriasis . It works by reducing skin cell proliferation and modulating the immune system.
• Option D. Oral cyclosporine:
• Cyclosporine is a calcineurin inhibitor that suppresses the immune system and is used for severe psoriasis . It is effective for quick control of the disease due to its potent immune suppressive effects.
• Educational Objective
• Oral steroids are not recommended for the long-term treatment of chronic plaque psoriasis due to the risk of disease exacerbation upon steroid withdrawal and the potential for serious side effects with long - term use .
explanation.ref: Rooks Textbook of Dermatology- 9 th Edition Page No 35.22, 35.35, 35.37

subject: Dermatology
question:
Match the following scale types with their lesions?

[Image: https://cerebellum-web-static.s3.amazonaws.com/media/public/images/2023/12/08/screenshot-2023-12-08-171414.jpg]

explanation.detail:

• Educational Objective:
• Some other diseases & special scales seen in them:
