subject: Dermatology
question: Irregular pitting of the nails with subungual hyperkeratosis is seen in?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. Lichen Planus:
• Lichen planus can affect the Nails and produce Nail Changes, but the common manifestations are Nail Thinning, Ridging , or even complete Loss of the Nail. Pterygium Formation , where there's Scarring of the Cuticle Area leading to the Nail's progressive Loss, can also be seen. While Lichen Planus can cause Nail Pitting, it's typically not associated with Subungual Hyperkeratosis , a feature more consistent with Psoriasis .
• Vitiligo is a disorder where there's a loss of pigment-producing cells ( melanocytes ) leading to white patches on the skin . It doesn't typically produce pitting of nails.
• Option D. Alopecia Areata :
• Geometric fine stippled pitting (Superficial, Regular, Fine) of the nails is the most likely expected finding as shown below.
• Educational Objective:
• Psoriasis is the condition most commonly associated with irregular nail pitting and subungual hyperkeratosis among the options provided.
• Harrison’s Principles of Internal Medicine 20 th Edition Page No 333
explanation.ref: Rooks Textbook of Dermatology- 9 th Edition Page No 4.9, 35.4-35.34, 37.12, 41.1, 41.21 89.28-33

subject: Dermatology
question:
A patient presented with lesions as shown in the image below and later developed arthritis also. What is the treatment of choice for a patient who presented with the following findings?

[Image: https://cerebellum-web-static.s3.amazonaws.com/media/public/images/2023/12/08/picture4_wkhskDI.jpg]

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option B. Cyclophosphamide –
• This is a cytotoxic medication most often used in the treatment of cancers and severe autoimmune diseases. It is not typically used as a first-line treatment for arthritis due to its significant side effect profile.
• Option C . Mycophenolate Mofetil –
• This medication is used as an immunosuppressant in organ transplantation and certain autoimmune conditions. While it may be used in the management of connective tissue diseases, it is not the first-line treatment for psoriatic arthritis or rheumatoid arthritis.
• Option D . Rituximab –
• This is a monoclonal antibody that targets CD20-positive B cells and is used in the treatment of rheumatoid arthritis after other treatments have failed. It is not typically the first choice of treatment for new-onset arthritis associated with skin lesions.
• Educational Objective:
• Methotrexate and apremilast are the first-line drugs for the treatment of moderate to severe psoriasis associated with psoriatic arthritis.
• Rook’s Textbook of Dermatology- 9 th Edition Page No 35.31, 35.43
• Harrisons Principle of Internal Medicine 20 th Edition Page No 2571-2572
• Katzungs Basic & Clinical Pharmacology 12 th Edition Page No 645
explanation.ref: ABC of Rheumatology 2018, Wiley Blackwell British Medical Journal Pg no 85

subject: Dermatology
question: Which of the following are having a established association?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A: Lichen Planus
• It is not typically associated with malignant melanoma. Lichen planus is a chronic inflammatory skin condition, and while it may have an increased risk of squamous cell carcinoma in chronic cases, it is not directly linked to malignant melanoma.
• It is a condition characterized by hyperpigmented patches , mainly on the face . There is no established association between melasma and malignant melanoma.
• It is an autoimmune condition that causes depigmentation of the skin. It has not been shown to have an established association with metabolic syndrome.
• Educational Objective:
• There is a recognized link between psoriasis and metabolic syndrome , reflecting the systemic nature of inflammation in psoriasis and its implications for comorbid conditions.
explanation.ref: Bolognia Dermatology Textbook 4 th Edition Page No 190

subject: Dermatology
question:
As a first-year derma resident, you see a pt with the following skin lesion. Which of the following disorders is LEAST likely to present in this form?

[Image: https://cerebellum-web-static.s3.amazonaws.com/media/public/images/2024/02/24/picture65.jpg]

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. Squamous Cell Carcinoma (SCC):
• It can present as a variety of skin lesions, including plaques, ulcers, or hyperkeratotic lesions, often with a scaly surface .
• Option B. Secondary Syphilis:
• This can present with a wide array of cutaneous manifestations, including maculopapular rashes and scaly plaques .
• Option C. Mycosis Fungoides:
• It is a form of cutaneous T-cell lymphoma , might present with patchy, plaque-like lesions that can be confused with eczema or psoriasis in their early stages . They typically have a scaly surface .
• Educational Objective:
• When assessing a patient with a scaly skin lesion, Basal Cell Carcinoma is least likely among the options provided, as it typically presents with pearly nodules and rolled edges, rather than scaling plaques.
• Rooks Textbook of Dermatology- 9th Edition Chapter 35 Page No 35.9
explanation.ref: Rooks Textbook of Dermatology- 9th Edition Chapter 35 Page No 35.9

subject: Dermatology
question:
A patient came with complaints of extremely itchy lesions over his hands. On examination following cutaneous lesions are present over the body. What is the most likely diagnosis?

[Image: https://cerebellum-web-static.s3.amazonaws.com/media/public/images/2024/02/24/picture67.jpg]

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• It typically presents with well-demarcated, erythematous plaques with silvery scales, most often on extensor surfaces like elbows and knees , and may involve the scalp and nails.
• Option C. Pityriasis Rubra Pilaris
• It usually shows orange-red plaques with a characteristic " islands of sparing ," and keratotic follicular papules , which are not as pruritic as lichen planus.
• Option D. Pityriasis Rosea
• It often begins with a single "herald patch," followed by a " Christmas tree " distribution of smaller lesions on the trunk, which are not typically as itchy as lichen planus.
• Educational Objective:
• When presented with pruritic, purple, polygonal papules , consider Lichen Planus as the most likely diagnosis, and differentiate it from other dermatoses like psoriasis, pityriasis rubra pilaris, and pityriasis rosea based on lesion morphology and distribution.
• Rooks Textbook of Dermatology- 9th Edition Volume 2 Chapter 37 Page No 37.1
explanation.ref: Rooks Textbook of Dermatology- 9th Edition Volume 2 Chapter 37 Page No 37.1

subject: Dermatology
question: In which of the following are Max Joseph Spaces characteristically seen?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• It is characterized by Munro micro abscesses and regular acanthosis , not typically associated with Max Joseph spaces.
• This involves intra-epidermal blisters and acantholysis, especially seen in Pemphigus vulgaris, not Max Joseph spaces.
• Option D. Pityriasis Rubra Pilaris
• It shows follicular plugging and psoriasiform hyperplasia . Max Joseph spaces are not a feature of this condition.
• Educational Objective:
• Max Joseph spaces a is a histological feature of Lichen Planus , which is important for the microscopic diagnosis of this condition.
explanation.ref: Rooks Textbook of Dermatology- 9th Edition Chapter 37 Page No 37.14

subject: Dermatology
question: Characteristic Nail change of Lichen Planus among the given options?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. Longitudinal ridging of nails
• This can be seen in a variety of conditions and is not specific to lichen planus . It is often a normal variant or associated with aging.
• Longitudinal ridging & nail plate thinning
• It is the absence of nails , can occur in severe cases of several conditions, but it is not characteristic of lichen planus specifically.
• Option C. The "Pup Tent" Sign
• It is associated with lichen planus , but not characteristic to LP like pterygium.
• Educational Objective:
• Pterygium is a distinctive nail change indicative of lichen planus, which can lead to nail dystrophy and requires differentiation from other nail abnormalities.
• Rooks Textbook of Dermatology- 9th Edition Chapter 37 Page No 37.12
explanation.ref: Rooks Textbook of Dermatology- 9th Edition Chapter 37 Page No 37.12

subject: Dermatology
question:
A 40-year-old female presents with subtle, fine, reticulate lacy white pattern in the buccal mucosa, flat topped purple papules on vulva, cicatricial alopecia of scalp & the following cutaneous finding limited to wrist. Most appropriate treatment strategy would be?

[Image: https://cerebellum-web-static.s3.amazonaws.com/media/public/images/2024/02/24/picture79.jpg]

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• They are used in some chronic viral infections and certain malignancies but are not the first-line treatment for lichen planus.
• Option C . PUVA ( Psoralen and Ultraviolet A )
• PUVA therapy may be used in severe cases of lichen planus that are not responsive to topical therapies, but it is not typically first-line due to its side effects and the intensive nature of the treatment.
• It is a systemic immunosuppressant that might be used in severe, widespread , or refractory lichen planus , but it is not usually the first step in treatment due to potential side effects and the need for monitoring.
• Educational Objective:
• For a patient presenting with lichen planus affecting mucosal and cutaneous surfaces, topical steroids represent the cornerstone of initial treatment for their anti-inflammatory and immunosuppressive effects .
explanation.ref: Rooks Textbook of Dermatology- 9th Edition Chapter 37-Page No 37.16

subject: Dermatology
question: A 45-year-old man presents to the Derma OPD with thickened palms & Soles. These lesions were insidious in onset. He was diagnosed with Pityriasis Rubra Pilaris. Which of the following features is most likely to be found in this patient?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. Nail pitting
• It is commonly seen in psoriasis , not typically in PRP. While nail changes can occur in PRP , they are not as characteristic as the islands of sparing.
• This is a finding more commonly associated with lichen planus and involves the progression of skin to encroach upon the nails, which is not a feature of PRP.
• Option D. Herald patch
• It is associated with Pityriasis Rosea , a different condition that presents with a large, solitary patch followed by a generalized body rash , not typically seen in PRP.
• Educational Objective:
• I slands of sparing is a distinctive clinical sign in patients with Pityriasis Rubra Pilaris, which can aid in differentiating it from other erythrodermic skin diseases.
• Rooks Textbook of Dermatology- 9th Edition Chapter 36 Page No 36.1 – 36.4
explanation.ref: Rooks Textbook of Dermatology- 9th Edition Chapter 36 Page No 36.1 – 36.4

subject: Dermatology
question: In which of the following conditions is the collarette scale characteristically seen?

explanation.detail:
• Educational Objective:
• C ollarette scale is a hallmark of Pityriasis Rosea , which helps in differentiating it from other scaling skin conditions.
• Andrew’s Diseases of the Skin: Clinical Dermatology – 11 th Edition Chapter 11 Page No 200

subject: Dermatology
question:
Identify the phenomenon shown in the image?

[Image: https://cerebellum-web-static.s3.amazonaws.com/media/public/images/2024/02/24/picture89.jpg]

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. Gottron papules :
• They are associated with dermatomyositis and are characterized by raised, violaceous papules and plaques over the dorsal aspect of the knuckles.
• Option C. Nikolsky sign:
• It is a clinical sign where the skin shears off easily when rubbed and is typically seen in conditions like pemphigus vulgaris . Nikolsky’s sign : On application of tangential pressure over skin on the border of an intact blister → upper layers of epidermis separate from the lower layers →→ skin peels off → extension of the blister .
• → Erosion formed → Nikolsky’s sign.
• Educational Objective:
• Koebner phenomenon ( Isomorphic phenomenon ) is the development of new skin lesions on previously unaffected skin due to trauma in patients with certain predisposing skin conditions.
• Harrison’s Principles of Internal Medicine 19 th Edition Page No 347
• Rooks Textbook of Dermatology- 9 th Edition Chapter 35 Page No 35.5
explanation.ref: Harrison’s Principles of Internal Medicine 19 th Edition Page No 347

subject: Dermatology
question:
A 29-year-old male presents with asymptomatic scaly lesions over body as shown in the image below. What is the most likely diagnosis?

[Image: https://cerebellum-web-static.s3.amazonaws.com/media/public/images/2024/02/24/picture92.jpg]

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. Pityriasis Versicolor
• It usually presents as hypopigmented, hyperpigmented , or erythematous patches with fine scale , predominantly on the upper trunk, neck, and arms , often with a variable response to sun exposure, making affected areas stand out more on tanned skin
• Option C . Pityriasis Alba
• It is commonly seen in children and presents as hypopigmented, slightly scaly patches , usually on the face , and is often associated with atopic dermatitis.
• Option D . Pityriasis Rubra Pilaris
• It typically presents with orange-red plaques with characteristic islands of sparing and palmoplantar keratoderma , which are not depicted in the provided image.
• Educational Objective:
• The diagnosis of Pityriasis Rosea is supported by the presence of a "Christmas tree" distribution of scaly lesions on the trunk.
• Bolognia Textbook of Dermatology 4 th Edition Page No 170
explanation.ref: Rooks Textbook of Dermatology- 9 th Edition Page No 25.90

subject: Dermatology
question: A 35-year-old patient presents with violaceous plane-topped papules associated with Pruritus over the wrist and antecubital fossae for 2 months. The lesions have a characteristic shiny surface and are arranged in a linear pattern. What is the first line of treatment for this patient?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A (Antifungals): Antifungal agents are not effective for lichen planus as it is not a fungal infection.
• Option B (Oral steroids): Oral steroids may be used for severe, widespread , or refractory lichen planus but are not usually the first line of treatment due to the potential for systemic side effects.
• Option D (Phototherapy): Phototherapy may be considered for widespread lichen planus or when topical treatment is not practical or effective, but it is not the first line of treatment.
• Educational objective:
• The first line of treatment for lichen planus, characterized by violaceous, flat-topped papules with pruritus, is high-potency topical steroids to reduce skin inflammation and pruritus.
explanation.ref: B ooks Textbook of Dermatology- 9th Edition Chapter 37-Page No 37.16

subject: Dermatology
question:
Match the papulosquamous disorders in Colum A with its characteristic feature in Colum B:

[Image: https://cerebellum-web-static.s3.amazonaws.com/media/public/images/2024/02/24/screenshot-2024-02-24-161008.jpg]

explanation.detail:
• Explanation
• Pityriasis rubra pilaris (1) with Palmoplantar keratoderma (b).
• This condition is characterized by thickening of the skin on the palms of the hands and soles of the feet , and it is one of the distinguishing features of Pityriasis rubra pilaris.
• Lichen planus (2) with Wickham's striae (c).
• Wickham's striae are fine white lines visible on the surface of the papules seen in lichen planus, typically found in the oral mucosa but can also be seen in cutaneous lesions.
• Pityriasis rosea (3) with Herald patch ( A)
• Pityriasis rosea often begins with a single, large, round or oval pink patch known as the herald patch .
• The option "d Row of Tombstones" is recognized feature associated with pemphigus vulgaris.
• Educational Objective:
• Correctly matching Pityriasis rubra pilaris with Palmoplantar keratoderma , Lichen planus with Wickham's striae , and Pityriasis rosea with the Herald patch is crucial in the diagnosis of these papulosquamous disorders.
• Rooks Textbook of Dermatology- 9 th Edition Page No 25.90. 36.2, 37.4, 154.2
explanation.ref: Rooks Textbook of Dermatology- 9 th Edition Page No 25.90. 36.2, 37.4, 154.2

subject: Dermatology
question: A 25-year-old male presents to the clinic with a 2-week history of a rash that started with a single, larger, scaly lesion on his trunk, followed by multiple smaller lesions. He reports mild itching. He denies any recent illness, medication changes, or high-risk behaviours. On examination, the smaller lesions are oval, with a collarette of scale and distributed along the lines of cleavage. Which of the following statements is INCORRECT regarding this patient's condition?
options: The condition can be associated with Hepatitis C Infection.
Narrowband UVB Therapy is sometimes used in the management of this condition.
The scales characteristically have a Free Inner Edge.

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option B . Narrowband UVB (NBUVB) therapy
• It may be used for symptomatic relief in extensive or persistent cases of pityriasis rosea , though it is not commonly needed as the condition is usually self-limiting.
• Option C . The description of the scales having a free inner edge
• This refers to the characteristic scaling pattern seen in pityriasis rosea , where the scale is attached at the periphery and free in the center ( Collarette scales).
• Option D . Pityriasis rosea is typically a self-limiting condition
• Pityriasis rosea is typically a self-limiting condition , resolving without treatment within 6-12 weeks to a few months.
• Educational Objective:
• P ityriasis rosea is NOT associated with Hepatitis C infection, whereas it is typically self-limiting, may feature scales with a free inner edge , and only occasionally requires treatment with narrowband UVB therapy .
• Rooks Textbook of Dermatology- 9 th Edition Page No 25.90
• Bolognia Textbook of Dermatology 4 th Edition Page No 170
explanation.ref: Rooks Textbook of Dermatology- 9 th Edition Page No 25.90

subject: Dermatology
question:
A 45-year-old male presents with a widespread erythematous rash over his back characterized by fine scaling and distinct areas of unaffected skin, or "islands of sparing." Considering Pityriasis Rubra Pilaris (PRP) as a potential diagnosis, which of the following clinical signs would be most supportive of this diagnosis?

[Image: https://cerebellum-web-static.s3.amazonaws.com/media/public/images/2024/02/24/picture80_ndZAndG.jpg]

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. Keratoderma blenorrhagica
• It is typically associated with reactive arthritis, not PRP, and presents with hyperkeratotic lesions on the palms and soles .
• Option B. The Koebner phenomenon
• This refers to the appearance of new skin lesions on the site of trauma and can be seen in various dermatologic conditions like psoriasis and lichen planus , but it is not specific to PRP.
• Option D. Nail Pitting
• It is commonly seen in psoriasis , and while nail changes can occur in PRP , they are not as diagnostic as perifollicular erythema.
• Educational Objective:
• When evaluating a patient with a scaly erythematous rash and islands of sparing, identification of perifollicular erythema can support the diagnosis of Pityriasis Rubra Pilaris, a condition that requires a distinct approach to management compared to other papulosquamous diseases.
• Rooks Textbook of Dermatology- 9 th Edition Page No 36.2
• Bolognia Textbook of Dermatology 4 th Edition Page No 166
explanation.ref: Rooks Textbook of Dermatology- 9 th Edition Page No 36.2

subject: Dermatology
question:
A 33-year-old female comes to the clinic complaining of severely itchy, purple-coloured lesions on her arms as shown in the image below. On physical examination, you note flat-topped papules with white lines on the surface. Given the clinical presentation, you suspect lichen planus. A skin biopsy is performed to confirm the diagnosis. Which characteristic feature is expected in the biopsy of this patient?

[Image: https://cerebellum-web-static.s3.amazonaws.com/media/public/images/2024/02/24/picture67_n2WRdhd.jpg]

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• It is typically seen in psoriasis , not lichen planus. Lichen planus is characterized by hypergranulosis , which is a thickening of the granular cell layer.
• Option C . Pautrier's microabscesses
• These are characteristic of mycosis fungoides , a type of cutaneous T-cell lymphoma , and are not associated with lichen planus.
• Option D. Munro's microabscesses
• These are seen in psoriasis and consist of collections of neutrophils in the stratum corneum , not typical of lichen planus.
• Educational Objective:
• When a patient presents with pruritic, purple, polygonal papules and white lines, expect to find basal cell degeneration on skin biopsy, supporting a diagnosis of lichen planus .
• Rooks Textbook of Dermatology- 9 th Edition Page No 37.4
• Bolognia Textbook of Dermatology 4 th Edition Page No 199
explanation.ref: Rooks Textbook of Dermatology- 9 th Edition Page No 37.4

subject: Dermatology
question: Reverse Koebner phenomenon is seen in which of the following conditions?

explanation.detail:
• Educational Objective:
• The reverse Koebner phenomenon, where skin lesions may clear following trauma , has been observed in some cases of psoriasis . This is contrary to the more commonly recognized Koebner phenomenon where new lesions appear at sites of injury .
• Harrison’s Principles of Internal Medicine 19 th Edition Page No 347

subject: Dermatology
question: A 10-year-old boy with a recent history of an upper respiratory tract infection now presents with multiple erythematous, annular lesions featuring a collarette of scale on the trunk. The lesions are neither pruritic nor painful. Based on the history and clinical presentation, which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. Lichen planus
• It is characterized by pruritic, purple, polygonal papules , and it commonly presents with Wickham's striae .
• It does not typically follow an upper respiratory tract infection and is not usually annular or scaling in presentation.
• Option B. Seborrheic Dermatitis
• This often presents with greasy scales and erythematous lesions, typically in areas rich in sebaceous glands such as the scalp, face, and upper trunk.
• It is more common in infants and adults and tends to be chronic.
• Option D. Tinea Versicolor
• It is a fungal infection that causes hypo- or hyperpigmented macules with fine scaling , primarily on the upper trunk, neck, and arms . The lesions can be mildly pruritic but are not typically annular or associated with recent infection.
• Educational Objective:
• Pityriasis rosea is a self-limited condition commonly preceded by an upper respiratory tract infection and presenting with characteristic annular lesions with a collarette of scale on the trunk , which can be distinguished from other dermatoses like lichen planus, seborrheic dermatitis, and tinea versicolor.
• Rooks Textbook of Dermatology- 9 th Edition Page No 37.6
• Bolognia Textbook of Dermatology 4 th Edition Page No 170
explanation.ref: Rooks Textbook of Dermatology- 9 th Edition Page No 37.6

subject: Dermatology
question: A 23-year-old male presents with a distinctive rash. Based on a comprehensive assessment, a diagnosis of Pityriasis Rubra Pilaris (PRP) is established. Which of the following clinical features is least likely to be associated with PRP and instead may suggest an alternative diagnosis such as psoriasis?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. Orange red skin plaques
• Orange-red plaques are a hallmark of PRP, characterized by their distinctive coloration which helps differentiate PRP from other dermatological conditions like psoriasis..
• Option B. The Islands of Sparing
• It is a feature that can be seen in PRP , contrasting with the more uniform involvement in Psoriasis.
• Option C . Keratoderma,
• Keratoderma or thickening of the palms and soles , is a hallmark of PRP and can help differentiate it from psoriasis, although palmoplantar involvement can occur in both conditions .
• Educational Objective:
• Understand that arthropathy is not typically associated with pityriasis rubra pilaris and may indicate psoriasis , especially when considering the potential diagnosis of psoriatic arthritis .
• Bolognia Textbook of Dermatology 4 th Edition Page No 166
explanation.ref: Rooks Textbook of Dermatology- 9 th Edition Page No 35.44

subject: Dermatology
question: Pityriasis Rosea is a self-limiting condition. How long does it usually last from the time of onset?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option B. Correct . Pityriasis rosea generally lasts about 6 weeks , although it can sometimes take longer, up to 8-12 weeks , before the lesions fully resolve.
• Educational Objective:
• Pityriasis rosea is a self-limiting condition that typically resolves within 6 weeks from onset, without the need for treatment.
• Bolognia Textbook of Dermatology 4 th Edition Page No 170
explanation.ref: Bolognia Textbook of Dermatology 4 th Edition Page No 170

subject: Dermatology
question: A 30-year-old female presents with lacy lesions in oral cavity and genitals. Her proximal nail fold has extended onto the nail bed. What is the most likely diagnosis?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A . Pityriasis Rubra Pilaris (PRP):
• It is a rare skin disorder that causes skin inflammation and scaling . PRP can sometimes resemble psoriasis, but it's characterized by salmon-colored patches with well-defined borders and areas of spared skin, known as islands of sparing as depicted in the image below.
• Option B . Psoriasis:
• Psoriasis is an autoimmune condition characterized by raised, red, scaly patches on the skin. It can also affect nails (leading to nail pitting, thickening, or separation from the nail bed) but does not produce the specific nail changes described in the question. While psoriasis can involve the genital area , it doesn't produce lacy oral lesions. Hence, this presentation is not typical for psoriasis.
• Option C . Pityriasis Rosea:
• Pityriasis rosea is a self-limited rash that starts with a single large pink or tan oval area called a " herald patch" followed by the appearance of smaller patches on the torso, often arranged along the lines of skin tension in a “Christmas tree” pattern as depicted in the image below.
• Educational Objective:
• Lichen planus with the characteristic findings of lacy white lesions on mucosal surfaces and specific nail changes.
explanation.ref: Rooks Textbook of Dermatology- 9 th Edition Chapter 37 Page No 37. 1-5

subject: Dermatology
question:
A young male, aged 24, has presented with asymptomatic scaly skin lesions on his back, symmetrically distributed. The provided images showcase the lesions. Can you identify the pattern of these skin lesions & the specific disease?

[Image: https://cerebellum-web-static.s3.amazonaws.com/media/public/images/2024/02/24/picture108.jpg]
options: Collarette Scale - Christmas Tree Pattern - Pityriasis Rosea

explanation.short: Ans. A) Collarette Scale - Christmas Tree Pattern - Pityriasis Rosea
explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option B. Wickham’s Striae - Lichen Planus:
• Wickham's striae are fine, white lines found on the surface of papules in lichen planus. Surface of lichen planus lesions seen with oil on top of lesion reveals these whitish linear streaks .
• Lichen planus typically presents with pruritic, purplish, polygonal papules and does not exhibit a Christmas tree pattern.
• Option C. Christmas Tree Pattern - Lichen Planus:
• This is incorrect because, although lichen planus is a common dermatologic condition, it does not present with a Christmas tree pattern. This pattern is specific to pityriasis rosea.
• Option D. Woronoff Ring – Seborrheic Dermatitis:
• The Woronoff ring is a hypopigmented ring surrounding individual psoriatic lesions . It is usually associated with treatment, most commonly UV radiation or topical steroids .
• Seborrheic dermatitis presents with greasy, scaly lesions predominantly on the scalp, face, and upper trunk .
• Educational Objective:
• When presented with symmetrically distributed , asymptomatic scaly skin lesions on the back in a young adult, consider pityriasis rosea , particularly if the lesions follow a Christmas tree pattern . This pattern, along with the presence of collarette scaling , is highly suggestive of pityriasis rosea.
explanation.ref: Rooks Textbook of Dermatology 9 th Edition Page No. 35.8, 37.3, 115.9

subject: Dermatology
question: Identify the INCORRECT statement among the following options?
options: Color of the Human Skin depends upon the Number of Melanocytes
Melasma in Pregnancy is called as Chloasma
The Ratio of the Epidermal Melanin Unit is 36 Keratinocytes: 1 Melanocyte
Bindi Leukoderma is caused by Para-Tertiary Butyl-Phenol (PTBP)

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option C: These melanosomes are distributed to the 36 keratinocytes that make up the " epidermal melanin " unit via the dendritic processes of melanocytes . (The ratio of the epidermal melanin unit is 36 keratinocytes: 1 melanocyte )
• Option B : Melasma in pregnant women is called as Chloasma or mask of pregnancy .
• Clinical Image depicting Melasma
• The third trimester of pregnancy is when chloasma is most prevalent and is most noticeable in brunettes . It results from elevated levels of estrogen and progesterone stimulating melanocyte activity. Histopathology shows no increase in number but hyper-functional melanocytes . Once the pregnancy is over , it resolves naturally . The most frequent cause of chronic facial melanosis is melasma . Hyperpigmented macules that are more pronounced after exposure to sunlight are its primary symptom . Upper lip , malar , forehead , and chin are the main areas of the body affected by this hyper melanosis . Women between the ages of 20 and 40 are more likely to have it. It is due to increased levels of estrogen & progesterone , stimulating the activity of melanocytes . Histopathology shows no increase in number but hyper functional melanocytes . It resolves on its own after pregnancy . It is frequently seen in women on OCPs.
• Woods lamp examination is useful to differentiate various types of Melasma , level of pigmentation & treatment response:
• Melasma is more Chronic & requires treatment with depigmenting agents like: Kligman triple combination - Topical Hydroquinone + Tretinoin + Steroid compound cream (First Line) Hydroquinone (reduces Tyrosinase Activity) Kojic Acid (Tyrosinase Inhibitor) Chemical peels
• Option D: Para-Tertiary Butyl Phenol is the culprit behind bindi Leukoderma (PTBP). It is present in adhesive sticker Bindi.
• Clinical Image depicting Bindi Leucoderma
• This condition is known as chemical or contact leukoderma , in which the chemical increases sensitivity and causes the melanocytes in the area of contact to undergo apoptosis. Additionally, there is proof that azo dyes , which share structural similarities with tyrosine and compete with it for the binding of the tyrosinase enzyme , can directly inhibit melanogenesis.
• Educational Objective:
• The correct identification of skin colour being determined by melanin production and NOT by the number of melanocytes is crucial for understanding the pathophysiology of pigmentary disorders .
• Bolognia Textbook of Dermatology 4 th Edition Page No 1119-1121
explanation.ref: Rook's Textbook of Dermatology 9th Edition Page No 2.17, 68.11, 75.19, 88.10

subject: Dermatology
question:
A 24-year-old college student presented to the OPD with depigmented patch on the back, in which some white hairs were also present. The lesion is stable for the past one year. Wood's lamp examination showed milky white fluorescence. Which of the following statements is INCORRECT among the options below regarding this condition?

[Image: https://cerebellum-web-static.s3.amazonaws.com/media/public/images/2023/04/07/picture3_f7HtTtq.jpg]
options: Loss of Melanocytes is the pathological basis for the given Skin condition
This condition has the Strongest association with Thyroid Disorders
﻿Positive Family History, Mucosal Involvement & Positive Koebner's Phenomenon are Bad Prognostic Factors for this condition
Cyclosporine is a Treatment Option for this condition

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A : The pathological basis of vitiligo is the loss of melanocytes.
• Studies using electron microscopy support the loss of melanocytes . An examination of the Wood's lamp reveals milky-white fluorescence.
• Vitiligo is a common form of localized depigmentation . Vitiligo is distinguished by its clearly defined depigmented macules . It is an autoimmune condition brought on by the body's destruction of the dopa-positive melanocytes that make up the basal layer of the epidermis. Vitiligo can appear at any age, but it typically first manifests itself in the third decade of life. Most likely, there is no difference in prevalence between the sexes or only a slight female preponderance .
• The gradual loss of melanocytes causes a localized depigmentation to take place. It is categorized as.
• Segmental vitiligo - which appears as unilateral depigmented macules in a segmented or band-shaped pattern. Non-segmental vitiligo - which appears as bilateral macules distributed in an acrofacial pattern or scattered symmetrically over the entire body.
• Koebner or isomorphic phenomenon:
• It is the development of new lesions along the line of trauma in a previously normal skin. The new lesions have iso (same), morphic (morphology ) as existing lesions.
• The image below shows vitiligo that exhibits the Koebner phenomenon:
• Option B: Strongest association of vitiligo is with Autoimmune thyroid disorders (hyperthyroidism and hypothyroidism).
• Other disorders associated with vitiligo are:
• ﻿Pernicious anemia ﻿Addison's disease ﻿Diabetes type I ﻿Myasthenia gravis ﻿Alopecia areata ﻿Uveitis ﻿Chronic mucocutaneous candidiasis ﻿Autoimmune polyendocrine syndromes (types I and II)
• Option C: Poor prognostic factors for vitiligo:
• ﻿Positive family history ﻿Mucosal vitiligo ﻿Lesions over bony prominences ﻿Isomorphic Koebner's phenomenon ﻿Non-segmental vitiligo
• Educational Objective:
• The association between vitiligo and various prognostic factors and treatment options is essential for the management of this condition. The correct identification of these factors can help guide treatment decisions.
explanation.ref: Rook's Textbook of Dermatology 9th Edition Page No’s 21.1-21.4, 70.4 and 70.5

subject: Dermatology
question: All of the following are associated with Hyperpigmentary Skin changes except?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A: certain drugs are associated with Hyperpigmentary skin changes . Minocycline is one among them, causing Blue black pigmentation in Acne scars as shown in the image below.
• Drugs causing Hyper melanosis:
• Option B: When exposed to UV rays , melanocytes produce more melanin , which results in freckles or ephelides (with a normal number of melanocytes).
• Image depicting freckles or ephelides :
• It has an autosomal dominant pattern of transmission. They are observed in a variety of inherited and acquired diseases, such as progeria, neurofibromatosis, and xeroderma pigmentosum.
• Fitzpatrick Classification type 1 and type 2 skin types both frequently have freckles.
• First-line treatment is the use of a high SPF sunscreen.
• Option D : Hyperthyroidism may cause generalized hyperpigmentation .
• Generalized Hyper melanosis or hyperpigmentation can be due to a variety of systemic diseases . A few common diseases are mentioned below:
• Systemic causes of hyperpigmentation
• Cushing disease, addisons, grave's disease Hemochromatosis Megaloblastic anemia Pernicious anemia Pregnancy Renal failure Systemic sclerosis
• Educational Objective:
• Nevus anemicus is associated with hypopigmentation , not hyperpigmentation, making it the correct answer. Minocycline can cause hyperpigmentation, freckles are a form of hyperpigmentation due to increased melanin, and Addison's disease leads to hyperpigmentation due to increased MSH production .
explanation.ref: Rook's Textbook of Dermatology - 9th Edition - Volume III Chapter 88 Page nos 88.14-16, 88.25 and 88.26

subject: Dermatology
question: All of the following are examples of Dermal melanocytosis Ceruloderma except?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A: Nevus of Ito: It affects the acromioclavicular region and upper chest. It primarily occurs in Chinese and Japanese people .
• Skin affected is innervated by posterior supraclavicular and lateral cutaneous brachial nerves .
• Option B: Naevus of Ota is more commonly seen in females.
• It is a severe case of dermal melanocytosis that is bluish and patchy . Scattered among collagen bundles , mostly in the superficial dermis , are numerous elongated dendritic melanocytes .
• The sclera and the skin around the eye are both impacted . It is distributed along the first and the second branches of the trigeminal nerve .
• Most lesions appear at birth or during the first year of life , and they get bigger and more numerous as time goes on. Asians are more likely to develop these lesions.
• Pigment targeting Q-switched laser systems is the treatment of choice for nevus of Ota & Ito.
• Option C: Mongolian spots
• These are congenital macular areas of blue-grey pigmentation of varying size and shape located on the lumbosacral area in normal infants . The lesion appears during pregnancy, grows during early childhood, and then disappears . Occasionally, it might continue into adulthood.
• Educational Objective:
• Becker's nevus is not an example of dermal melanocytosis, but an epidermal nevus characterized by hyperpigmentation and possible hypertrichosis , distinguishing it from Nevus of Ito, Nevus of Ota, and Mongolian spots, which are all examples of dermal melanocytosis.
• Bolognia Textbook of Dermatology 4 th Edition Page No 1189
explanation.ref: Rooks Textbook of Dermatology- 9 th Edition Page No 75.20

subject: Dermatology
question:
All of the following are true about the condition shown in the below image EXCEPT:

[Image: https://cerebellum-web-static.s3.amazonaws.com/media/public/images/2023/04/07/picture11_aTmy6FV.jpg]

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• This is a rare autosomal recessive disorder ( NOT Autosomal Dominant ) characterized by generalized depigmentation of the skin, hair , and eye . Anomalies related to the eyes are also noted. Melanocytes are normally present and distributed , but it is caused by a deficiency in melanin biosynthesis . Tyrosinase gene (TYR) mutations are the cause of it. There may be significant photophobia . Slit-lamp examination demonstrates iris translucency . Sun protection is required to prevent skin cancer and sunburns , with a focus on patients who reside in high UV risk areas . It is required to refer patients to an ophthalmologist early . Dark glasses are important to protect the eyes and prevent photophobia.
• Educational Objective:
• Albinism is typically inherited in an autosomal recessive manner, not autosomal dominant, and is characterized by a deficiency in the tyrosinase enzyme , leading to reduced or absent melanin production , normal melanocyte count , and associated photophobia.
• Bolognia Textbook of Dermatology 4 th edition Page No 1095
• Bolognia Textbook of Dermatology 4 th edition Page No
explanation.ref: Rooks Textbook of Dermatology- 9 th Edition Page No 70.09

subject: Dermatology
question:
Which of the following statements is true regarding Piebaldism?

[Image: https://cerebellum-web-static.s3.amazonaws.com/media/public/images/2023/04/07/picture12_gD1mfty.jpg]
options: Piebaldism along with Sensorineural Hearing Loss point towards ﻿﻿Peutz-Jeghers syndrome

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option C. Piebaldism is a Benign Autosomal Dominant (NOT AR) disease.
• There are c-KIT Gene Mutations. There are clearly defined, irregularly hypopigmented macules (NOT Hyperpigmented) that lack melanocytes . The depigmented macules are related to Leucotrichia . On normal skin or inside hypopigmented patches , small hyperpigmented spots can also be seen. On the middle of the forehead, a V-shaped Leucoderma is frequently present.
• Image below depicts White forelock in Piebaldism:
• The remedy is skin or epidermal cell grafting. The neonatal presence of white patches helps to distinguish Piebaldism from vitiligo . Piebaldism along with sensorineural hearing loss point towards ﻿﻿ Waardenburg syndrome
• Waardenburg syndrome : Autosomal dominant disorder having following characteristics:
• White forelock Hypopigmented patches Heterochromia iridis Sensorineural deafness
• Educational Objective:
• Piebaldism is characterized by an autosomal dominant inheritance pattern, well-defined hypopigmented macules , and a classic white forelock . It is not related to Peutz-Jeghers syndrome , which has different clinical features, including gastrointestinal polyps and mucocutaneous hyperpigmentation.
• Bolognia Textbook of Dermatology 4 th Edition Page No 1097
• Bolognia Textbook of Dermatology 4 th Edition Page No
explanation.ref: Rook's Textbook of Dermatology 9th Edition Page No 70.3

subject: Dermatology
question:
Identify the condition shown below?

[Image: https://cerebellum-web-static.s3.amazonaws.com/media/public/images/2023/04/07/picture15_N0Ahk1H.jpg]

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. Epidermal verrucous nevus:
• An epidermal verrucous nevus is a type of skin lesion that appears as a warty, skin-colored or pigmented plaque .
• It is usually present at birth or develops in early childhood and tends to have a verrucous (warty) surface , which is not apparent in the provided image.
• Melanoacanthoma is a rare , typically benign skin tumor that combines features of a melanocytic nevus and a seborrheic keratosis.
• It often has a mixed pigmentation and a slightly raised appearance , unlike the smooth , consistent pigmentation of the lesion shown.
• Option D: Malignant melanoma
• While malignant melanoma is a dangerous form of skin cancer that can arise de novo or from a pre-existing mole , it generally presents with irregular borders , varying colors , and can have a rapid change in size . The lesion in the image does not exhibit these malignant features.
• Educational Objective:
• Congenital Melanocytic Nevus (CMN) by its presentation is a large, uniform, darkly pigmented , and well-defined birthmark , distinguishing it from verrucous nevi , melanoacanthoma , and malignant melanoma .
explanation.ref: Rook's Textbook of Dermatology -9th Edition Chapter 75 Page No 75.14

subject: Dermatology
question:
A 31-year-old lady presents complaining of facial discolouration. She is 26 weeks pregnant. So far it has been an uncomplicated pregnancy. On examination she has a large, flat, symmetrical, brown-pigmented patch across her cheeks, forehead, nose, and upper lip as shown in the image below. What is the most likely diagnosis?

[Image: https://cerebellum-web-static.s3.amazonaws.com/media/public/images/2024/01/30/picture8_fjopLDo.jpg]

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. Systemic Lupus Erythematosus (SLE)
• It can present with a variety of skin manifestations , including a malar rash (butterfly rash) on the face. However, the malar rash in SLE is typically more erythematous (red) than brown and is often associated with other systemic symptoms. It is less likely in the absence of systemic symptoms and with a presentation of a large, flat , brown-pigmented patch .
• Option C. Polymorphic eruption of pregnancy
• It is also known as pruritic urticarial papules and plaques of pregnancy (PUPPP), is characterized by itchy urticarial papules and plaques that can occur during pregnancy . It is different from the described pigmented patch on the face.
• It is a chronic skin condition that primarily affects the face and is characterized by redness, visible blood vessels, and sometimes papules and pustules. It does not typically present as a large, flat, brown-pigmented patch.
• Educational Objective:
• In a pregnant woman with a large, flat, symmetrical, brown-pigmented patch on the face , the most likely diagnosis is melasma .
• Bolognia Textbook of Dermatology 4 th Edition Page No 1119-1121
explanation.ref: Rook's Textbook of Dermatology 9 th Edition Page No 88.10

subject: Dermatology
question:
A 33-year-old woman presents to the clinic with multiple well-demarcated, depigmented patches over her elbows and knees. She mentions that the lesions appeared after she had a minor abrasion on her elbow and knees. Her medical history is significant for vitiligo but no other chronic diseases. Which of the following phenomena is most likely responsible for the appearance of her skin lesions?

[Image: https://cerebellum-web-static.s3.amazonaws.com/media/public/images/2024/01/30/picture11_c7wV4fg.jpg]

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option B. False Koebner’s phenomenon
• This is related to the spread of infectious diseases such as viral warts and molluscum contagiosum along lines of trauma. Since the patient has Vitiligo and there is no mention of infectious disease, this option is less likely.
• Option C. The Reverse Koebner phenomenon
• This refers to the clearing and healing of psoriatic lesions following injury. This is not consistent with the patient's presentation, as she developed new vitiligo lesions after trauma, not clearance of existing lesions.
• Option D. The Remote reverse Koebner phenomenon
• This involves the spontaneous repigmentation of vitiligo patches distant from the sites of trauma, such as autologous skin graft sites. This is not applicable to the patient's current situation, as there is no mention of repigmentation or recent skin grafts.
• Educational Objective:
• True Koebner phenomenon in vitiligo , where depigmented patches may develop at sites of skin trauma , such as minor abrasions. This phenomenon is distinct from the classic Koebner phenomenon seen in psoriasis. Understanding these phenomena is essential for dermatologists in managing and counselling patients with vitiligo.
• Rook's Textbook of Dermatology 9 th Edition Page No 4.9
explanation.ref: Bolognia Dermatology Textbook 4 th Edition Page No 9

subject: Dermatology
question:
Parents of a 2-year-old child present to the dermatology clinic with a skin lesion on the arm. Upon examination, the lesion is identified as a melanocytic nevus with a noticeable eczematous halo around it. The parents report that the halo developed a few weeks ago, and the child has been scratching the lesion since the appearance of halo. Which of the following best describes the observed dermatological phenomenon?

[Image: https://cerebellum-web-static.s3.amazonaws.com/media/public/images/2024/01/30/picture12_hRlFaSi.jpg]

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option: A. The Halo nevus phenomenon
• This typically refers to a melanocytic nevus surrounded by a depigmented halo , often considered to be an immune response where the body's immune system attacks the pigment cells in the mole .
• It is not characterized by eczematous changes .
• Option: B. The Reverse Koebner phenomenon
• This refers to the clearing and healing of psoriatic lesions following injury. This is not consistent with the patient's presentation, as It is not characterized by eczematous changes.
• Option C: Sutton's phenomenon
• This is also known as halo nevus , is similar to option A and describes a melanocytic nevus with a surrounding area of depigmentation , not an eczematous ring.
• Educational Objectives:
• Meyerson phenomenon , which refers to the eczematous halo surrounding a pre-existing skin lesion , often a nevus , with associated pruritus and erythema.
• This phenomenon is distinct from the depigmented halo seen in a halo nevus .
• Bolognia Textbook of Dermatology 4 th Edition Page No 1108
• Bolognia Textbook of Dermatology 4 th Edition Page No
explanation.ref: Rook's Textbook of Dermatology 9 th Edition Page No 39.27

subject: Dermatology
question:
The most probable diagnosis for a young male presenting with Leucotrichia and lesions as depicted in the image is?

[Image: https://cerebellum-web-static.s3.amazonaws.com/media/public/images/2024/01/30/picture14_ubDKaex.jpg]

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. Focal vitiligo:
• This is characterized by one or more macules or patches in one area without any recognizable pattern as shown in the image below.
• It is a rare autosomal dominant disorder that is present at birth . The hallmark of piebaldism is a white forelock as shown in the image below.
• The distribution of lesions is usually symmetrical and remains stable throughout life , which differs from the presentation in the image.
• Option D. Nevus depigmentosus:
• This is a congenital, stable hypopigmented patch that does not progress or change with time. Unlike vitiligo, it does not show Leucotrichia , and the lesion does not spread .
• Educational Objective:
• Segmental vitiligo presents with one or more macules in a dermatomal or segmental pattern, often accompanied by Leucotrichia .
explanation.ref: Rooks Textbook of dermatology 9 th Edition page no 88.34

subject: Dermatology
question:
Match the following disorders of Hypopigmentation in column A with the Etiopathogenesis in Column B:

[Image: https://cerebellum-web-static.s3.amazonaws.com/media/public/images/2024/01/30/screenshot-2024-01-30-151047.jpg]

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Here's the explanation for each match:
• 1 Vitiligo : The Etiopathogenesis of Vitiligo is c. Melanocyte destruction by auto antibodies. In Vitiligo, there is an autoimmune destruction of melanocytes , which are the cells responsible for producing melanin , the pigment that gives color to the skin. This leads to depigmented patches on the skin. 2 Pityriasis Versicolor: The cause of this condition is a. Decrease in melanin synthesis. Pityriasis versicolor is caused by a fungal infection which leads to a decrease in melanin synthesis due to the production of azelaic acid by the fungus , resulting in hypopigmented patches on the skin. 3 Nevus Anemicus: The underlying mechanism is b. Vasoconstriction of blood vessels. Nevus anemicus is a congenital disorder where there is localized vasoconstriction or decreased blood flow, giving the appearance of a hypopigmented patch. It is not due to a lack of melanocytes or melanin. 4 Piebaldism: This condition is caused by d. Defect in migration of melanocytes from neural crest. Piebaldism is a genetic disorder characterized by a congenital absence of melanocytes in affected areas of the skin and hair, due to a defect in the migration of melanocytes from the neural crest during embryonic development
• Educational Objective:
• Vitiligo is caused by autoimmune destruction of melanocytes (c), Pityriasis versicolor is due to a decrease in melanin synthesis by fungal infection (a), Nevus anemicus involves vasoconstriction of blood vessels (b), and Piebaldism results from a defect in melanocyte migration from the neural crest (d).
• Bolognia Textbook of Dermatology 4 th Edition Page No 1087, 1097
explanation.ref: Rooks Textbook of Dermatology- 9 th Edition Page No 32.12, 32.70, 70.3, 88.35

subject: Dermatology
question:
A 34-year-old woman presents with hyperpigmented facial lesions following a tropical vacation. She experienced a high fever, severe joint pain, and a diffuse rash a week after being bitten by mosquitoes. The fever and rash resolved, but the facial pigmentation persisted. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

[Image: https://cerebellum-web-static.s3.amazonaws.com/media/public/images/2024/01/30/picture18_pwHhIjM.jpg]
options: Post-Inflammatory Hyperpigmentation due to an Allergic Reaction

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. Fixed Drug Eruption (FDE):
• FDE typically presents as a solitary or few well-demarcated, round or oval, red or purple patches or plaques .
• The lesions recur at the same site upon exposure to the offending drug . The history of mosquito bites and tropical travel , along with the absence of a new medication , makes this diagnosis less likely.
• Melasma is characterized by symmetrically distributed hyperpigmented patches , primarily on the face.
• It is more commonly associated with sun exposure and hormonal changes rather than an infectious etiology.
• Option D. Post-Inflammatory Hyperpigmentation due to an Allergic Reaction:
• While this could cause facial pigmentation , the patient's history of fever , joint pain , and diffuse rash following a mosquito bite in a tropical region is more suggestive of a systemic infectious process rather than a localized allergic reaction.
• Educational Objective:
• Chikungunya fever in patients presenting with a history of fever , joint pain , and rash following travel to tropical regions , along with distinctive facial hyperpigmentation known as the "Chik sign."
explanation.ref: Online resources:

subject: Dermatology
question:
A 35-year-old female presents to the clinic with a hypopigmented, round lesion on the middle of her forehead, as demonstrated in the accompanying image. There is no associated pruritus or scaling. She reports that the lesion appeared gradually over the past several months. She denies any use of topical creams or ointments prior to the onset of the lesion. On examination, the lesion is well-circumscribed with a diameter of approximately 1 cm. There is no evidence of erythema or induration. Which of the following is the most likely cause of this hypopigmentation?

[Image: https://cerebellum-web-static.s3.amazonaws.com/media/public/images/2024/01/30/picture21_ZXAxtHZ.jpg]

explanation.short: Ans. C) Para Tertiary Butyl Phenol
explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• This is a topical agent used to lighten hyperpigmented skin areas. Although it can cause hypopigmentation , it is typically used deliberately for cosmetic or therapeutic reasons and not typically associated with an unintended, isolated, round lesion as seen in the image.
• Option B: Mono Benzyl Ether of Hydroquinone (MBEH)
• This causes depigmentation and can be used therapeutically for extensive vitiligo . However, it is not commonly associated with the sudden appearance of a single hypopigmented lesion like the one depicted.
• Option D: Para Tertiary Butyl Catechol (PTBC)
• This is another phenol derivative that can cause depigmentation. However, it is less commonly encountered as a cause of chemical leukoderma compared to PTBP .
• Chemicals Causing Chemical Leucoderma and Their Sources
• Educational Objectives:
• The most likely cause of the hypopigmented lesion in the middle of the forehead , as shown in the image, is exposure to Para Tertiary Butyl Phenol (PTBP ), which is known to cause chemical leukoderma due to its cytotoxic effects on melanocytes.
• Rooks Textbook of Dermatology- 9 th Edition Page No 4.4
explanation.ref: Online sources:

subject: Dermatology
question:
A 29-year-old female presents to the dermatology clinic with several depigmented patches on her hands and face as shown in the image below. There were white hairs seen in the depigmented patches. These patches have progressively enlarged over the past year. Which of the following is the most likely cause for the depigmentation in this patient?

[Image: https://cerebellum-web-static.s3.amazonaws.com/media/public/images/2024/01/30/picture22_qFsNndA.jpg]

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option B: Defective Enzyme Tyrosinase
• This is a feature of albinism , a genetic condition where melanin synthesis is impaired due to a defect in the tyrosinase enzyme. In vitiligo, the enzyme is intact , but the melanocytes are absent.
• Option C: Defective Migration of Melanocytes
• This is seen in congenital conditions like piebaldism , where melanocytes do not migrate properly during development.
• This is not a feature of vitiligo, which is an acquired condition.
• Option D: External Chemical Destruction of Melanocytes
• This may cause a depigmentation pattern similar to vitiligo , known as chemical leukoderma , but it is not associated with the spontaneous development of vitiligo.
• Educational Objective:
• The hallmark of vitiligo is the absence of melanocytes in the skin, leading to depigmented patches , sometimes with white hair , distinguishing it from conditions related to enzyme defects , migration issues , or chemical exposure .
• Bolognia Textbook of Dermatology 4 th Edition Page No’s 1088
explanation.ref: Rook's Textbook of Dermatology 9 th Edition Page No’s 88.35

subject: Dermatology
question: A patient presents to the clinic with a unilateral blue-brown speckled patch on her malar face. Histological examination from a biopsy of the lesion reveals an increased number of elongated, dendritic melanocytes scattered throughout the dermis, with a normal collagen pattern. Based on the clinical and histopathological findings, which of the following is the correct diagnosis?

explanation.detail:
• Educational Objective:
• The Nevus of Ota is diagnosed by the clinical presentation of a unilateral blue-brown speckled patch on the malar face and histologically by an increased number of elongated , dendritic melanocytes in the dermis , without affecting the collagen pattern.
• Bolognia Textbook of Dermatology 4 th Edition Page No 1961
explanation.ref: Rooks’ Textbook of Dermatology- 9 th Edition Page No 73.2, 132.13

subject: Dermatology
question: A 29-year-old female presents with blotchy hyperpigmentation on her cheeks and nose that developed during her recent pregnancy. The hyperpigmentation is symmetric and primarily involves the sun-exposed areas of her face. She reports that the pigmentation tends to worsen with sun exposure. She has no other significant past medical history and is not on any medications. Considering the history and clinical presentation, what is the most likely diagnosis?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A: Senile Lentigenes
• These are also known as liver spots , are benign lesions that occur in older individuals due to chronic sun exposure . They are not related to pregnancy and typically appear as small, well-defined spots .
• These are small, brownish spots on the skin that can be exacerbated by sun exposure but are usually present from a young age and not specifically associated with pregnancy.
• Multiple freckles seen over face.
• Commonly known as moles , can vary in appearance but are typically well-demarcated growths on the skin that are not typically associated with pregnancy-related hormonal changes.
• Differences between Freckles and Lentigines
• Educational Objective:
• The diagnosis of chloasma is supported by the presentation of blotchy hyperpigmentation on the cheeks and nose that developed during pregnancy , worsens with sun exposure , and is not associated with aging, sun-induced freckles, or moles.
• Bolognia Textbook of Dermatology 4 th Edition Page No 1119-1121
explanation.ref: Rook's Textbook of Dermatology 9 th Edition Page No 88.10

subject: Dermatology
question: A 17-year-old male is referred to a genetics clinic after he presented with several episodes of abdominal pain and rectal bleeding. He has a history of frequent bowel movements containing blood since childhood. Physical examination reveals multiple, dark blue to brown macules on his lips and oral mucosa. His family history is significant for his father undergoing surgery for bowel obstruction in his thirties. Colonoscopy reveals multiple polyps throughout the colon. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A: Familial Adenomatous Polyposis
• This is characterized by hundreds to thousands of adenomatous colon polyps , but it does not include the mucocutaneous hyperpigmentation seen in Peutz-Jeghers syndrome.
• Option B: Cronkhite-Canada Syndrome
• This is a non-hereditary condition characterized by gastrointestinal polyps , diarrhea , weight loss, and abnormalities of the hair and nails but not the distinctive pigmentation of Peutz-Jeghers syndrome.
• Option C: Gardner Syndrome
• This is a variant of familial adenomatous polyposis that includes extracolonic features like osteomas and soft tissue tumors , but not the mucocutaneous hyperpigmentation that is diagnostic of Peutz-Jeghers syndrome.
• Differences between Freckles and Lentigines
• Educational Objective:
• The diagnosis of Peutz-Jeghers syndrome should be considered in patients with a personal and family history of gastrointestinal polyps , characteristic mucocutaneous hyperpigmentation, and an increased risk of gastrointestinal and extraintestinal malignancies.
• Bolognia Textbook of Dermatology 4 th Edition Page No 832, 1955
explanation.ref: Rooks Textbook of Dermatology- 9 th Edition Page No 88.15, 110.13

subject: Dermatology
question: A 20-year-old male presents to the dermatology clinic with a large, unilateral patch on his right shoulder that he has had since adolescence. He reports the area has become more pigmented and slightly hairy over the past few years. On examination, the patch is hyperpigmented and has a well-defined border, with an increase in terminal hairs within the same area. The lesion is non-tender, and there are no palpable masses. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• This presents as symmetrical, blotchy, brownish facial pigmentation and is common in women , particularly during pregnancy or when taking oral contraceptives . It does not typically involve hypertrichosis.
• Option B: Congenital Melanocytic Nevus
• This is present at birth or shortly thereafter and can range from small to garment-like in size.
• These lesions may have increased hair growth but are congenital , unlike the post-pubertal development of Becker's nevus .
• This is characterized by patches of depigmentation due to the loss of melanocytes and does not involve hypertrichosis or hyperpigmentation.
• Comparison between Becker’s Nevus and Nevus of Ito
• Educational Objective:
• The diagnosis of Becker's nevus is suggested by the appearance of a unilateral, hyperpigmented patch with associated hypertrichosis , usually developing around puberty and located on the shoulder or upper trunk.
• Bolognia Textbook of Dermatology 4 th Edition Page No 1189
explanation.ref: Rooks Textbook of Dermatology- 9 th Edition Page No 75.20

subject: Dermatology
question: A 6-year-old child presents to the clinic with a history of frequent and severe pyogenic infections, albinism, and easy bruising. The child has silvery hair and photophobia. Physical examination reveals hepatosplenomegaly and neurological examination shows mild ataxia. A peripheral blood smear shows giant granules in granulocytes and platelets. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A: Griscelli syndrome
• This also presents with partial albinism and immunodeficiency , but it is typically associated with mutations in the MYO5A, RAB27A, or MLPH genes and does not typically present with the giant granules found in leukocytes seen in Chediak-Higashi syndrome.
• Option B: Hermansky-Pudlak Syndrome
• This is another syndrome that presents with oculocutaneous albinism and a bleeding diathesis due to platelet dysfunction , but it does not feature the recurrent severe infections or neurological symptoms typical of Chediak-Higashi syndrome.
• Option C: Wiskott-Aldrich Syndrome
• This is characterized by immunodeficiency, eczema, and thrombocytopenia , but it does not cause albinism or the presence of giant granules in white cells.
• Characteristics of Various Syndromes
• Educational Objective:
• The diagnosis of Chediak-Higashi syndrome is based on the clinical triad of oculocutaneous albinism, recurrent pyogenic infections, and neurological symptoms, along with the characteristic finding of giant granules in leukocytes and platelets on peripheral blood smear.
• Bolognia Textbook of Dermatology 4 th Edition Page No 962
explanation.ref: Rooks Textbook of Dermatology- 9 th Edition Page No 70.8

subject: Dermatology
question:
A 35-year-old patient presents with symmetric depigmented patches on the dorsum of foot, as shown in the image. The patches have well-defined margins and there is no associated itching or scaling. The patient reports that these patches first appeared several months ago and have slowly increased in size. On further questioning, the patient mentions using rubber slippers after which he noted these patches. In which of the following diseases is the chemical agent responsible for this presentation used as a treatment option in extensive disease?

[Image: https://cerebellum-web-static.s3.amazonaws.com/media/public/images/2024/01/30/picture31_AdvsJDd.jpg]

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A: Piebaldism is a genetic condition characterized by a white forelock and stable depigmented patches present from birth , not acquired due to external agents like MBEH.
• Option B: Pityriasis versicolor is a fungal infection that typically presents with hypo- or hyperpigmented scaly patches, not depigmentation due to melanocyte destruction.
• Option C: Post-Inflammatory Hypopigmentation occurs after inflammatory skin conditions and is not induced by chemicals like MBEH, nor is it treated with such agents.
• Educational Objective:
• The clinical presentation of depigmented patches as shown in the image is indicative of chemical leukoderma , specifically rubber-induced leukoderma , which can be caused by chemicals such as MBEH that are also used in the depigmentation process of Extensive vitiligo treatment .
• Bolognia Textbook of Dermatology 4 th Edition Page No 1094
explanation.ref: Rooks Textbook of Dermatology- 9 th Edition Page No 88.12, 88.45

subject: Dermatology
question: Which of the following is not a component of the criteria used for the diagnosis of Bacterial vaginosis?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Amsel criteria is used for the diagnosis of Bacterial vaginosis.
• An altered microflora in vagina with a decrease in lactobacilli and an increase in anaerobic organisms like Gardnerella vaginalis, Mycoplasma hominis, Ureaplasma, and Prevotella species are the causes of bacterial vaginosis. The highly sensitive Nugent scoring system is regarded as the gold standard for bacterial vaginosis. It is calculated by looking at the results of Gram-stained smears , the quantity of lactobacilli, and the anaerobes morphology. Bacterial vaginosis is indicated by high scores of 7 to 10.
• Educational Objective:
• Recognize that an elevated vaginal pH greater than 4. 5, not less than 4.5, is a diagnostic criterion for Bacterial vaginosis , alongside a homogenous thin greyish discharge , a positive whiff test, and the presence of clue cells.
• Rook's Textbook of Dermatology 9th edition Chapter 112, Page no 112.23
explanation.ref: Journal https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC4802101 /

subject: Dermatology
question: A female comes with frothy, green vaginal discharge of 5 days duration. There was a strawberry cervix on examination. The drug of choice is?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option B. Tetracycline is not the treatment of choice for trichomoniasis; it is more commonly used for bacterial infections, including some sexually transmitted infections like chlamydia.
• Option C. Fluconazole is an antifungal medication used primarily to treat yeast infections, not protozoan infections like trichomoniasis.
• Option D. Ciprofloxacin is an antibiotic that is not effective against protozoan infections and is not indicated for the treatment of trichomoniasis.
• Educational Objective
• When a patient is present with frothy, green vaginal discharge and strawberry cervix, the most likely diagnosis is trichomoniasis. The first-line treatment for this condition is Metronidazole.
• Rook's Textbook of Dermatology 9th edition Chapter 33, Page no’s 33.35 and 33.36
explanation.ref: Rook's Textbook of Dermatology 9th edition Chapter 33, Page no’s 33.35 and 33.36

subject: Dermatology
question:
A 26-year-old man complains of burning pain when urinating. He reluctantly acknowledges that four days ago, he engaged in unprotected sexual activity. The following examination findings are seen. Which of the following organisms is most likely to be the cause?

[Image: https://cerebellum-web-static.s3.amazonaws.com/media/public/images/2024/02/17/picture5_0aUXwuz.jpg]

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. Chlamydia trachomatis:
• It is also a common cause of urethritis, but it typically causes a less pronounced or even asymptomatic presentation compared to gonorrhea.
• Option B. Mycoplasma genitalium:
• It is another cause of urethritis but is less common than gonorrhea and chlamydia and often causes milder symptoms.
• Option C. Trichomonas vaginalis:
• This causes trichomoniasis which typically presents with a frothy discharge and is more commonly symptomatic in females than males.
• Educational Objective
• When a male patient presents with purulent urethral discharge and reports recent unprotected sexual activity , the most likely diagnosis is gonococcal urethritis , caused by Neisseria gonorrhoeae . Identification is typically confirmed through culture of the discharge .
• Rook's Textbook of Dermatology 9th edition Chapter 30, Page no 30.3
• Harrison's Principles of Internal Medicine - 19th Edition Page no 1005
explanation.ref: Rook's Textbook of Dermatology 9th edition Chapter 30, Page no 30.3

subject: Dermatology
question:
A 36-year-old man with 60 kgs body weight presents with urethritis. Gram-staining of the purulent discharge is seen in the following image. What is the treatment of choice for this infection?

[Image: https://cerebellum-web-static.s3.amazonaws.com/media/public/images/2024/02/17/picture6_KmIst4v.jpg]
options: Ceftriaxone 500 mg IM + Azithromycin 2g PO stat
Ceftriaxone 500 mg IM in a single dose
Ceftriaxone 500 mg IM + Doxycycline 100 mg BD for 7 days
Azithromycin 2g PO stat + Doxycycline 100 mg BD for 14 days

explanation.short: Ans. C) Ceftriaxone 500mg IM + Doxycycline 100mg BD for 7 days
explanation.detail:
• In the absence of NAAT results to exclude chlamydial infection, all patients with gonococcal urethritis should receive dual therapy with:
• Ceftriaxone for N. gonorrhoeae , and Doxycycline for C. trachomatis , as per CDC 2021 recommendations.
• This ensures high efficacy , resistance prevention , and public health compliance .
• Sexually Transmitted Diseases and HIV/AIDS by Vinod K Sharma, 2 nd edition, chapter 23 Page no 390
explanation.ref: Rook's Textbook of Dermatology 9th edition Chapter 30, Page no 30.7

subject: Dermatology
question: Three weeks after an unprotected sexual encounter, a 46-year-old man experiences scanty mucoid discharge from his urethra. Which of the following diagnostic tests is advised?

explanation.short: Ans. B) Nucleic acid amplification test
explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. Culture and sensitivity
• Although culture is a definitive method for pathogen identification and antibiotic sensitivity testing, it is less sensitive than NAAT for detecting Chlamydia and gonorrhea in urethral swabs.
• Option C. Enzyme Linked Immunosorbent Assay (ELISA)
• ELISA can be used for serological testing of various infections, but it is not the first line for direct detection of pathogens in cases of urethritis.
• Option D. Microscopy showing inclusion bodies
• Microscopy can be used to identify inclusion bodies characteristic of Chlamydia in cell cultures, but it is less sensitive and more labor-intensive than NAAT.
• Educational Objective:
• For the diagnosis of urethritis likely caused by a sexually transmitted infection , NAAT is recommended due to its superior sensitivity and specificity compared to other diagnostic tests.
explanation.ref: Rook's Textbook of Dermatology - Volume II-9th Edition Chapter 30 Page no 30.13

subject: Dermatology
question: The syndromic management of urethral discharge includes treatment of?

explanation.short: Ans. C) Neisseria Gonorrhoea & Chlamydia Trachomatis
explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. Neisseria gonorrhoeae & herpes genitalis
• While Neisseria gonorrhoeae is a common cause of urethral discharge, herpes genitalis , caused by the herpes simplex virus, typically presents with painful ulcers rather than urethral discharge.
• Option B. Chlamydia trachomatis & herpes genitalis
• Chlamydia trachomatis is a common cause of urethral discharge, but as stated above, herpes genitalis usually does not present primarily with discharge.
• Option D. Syphilis & chancroid
• Syphilis, caused by Treponema pallidum , and chancroid, caused by Haemophilus ducreyi , typically presents with genital ulcers .
• Educational Objective:
• The syndromic management of urethral discharge should include treatment for both Neisseria gonorrhoeae and Chlamydia trachomatis, which are the most common bacterial pathogens causing this condition.
explanation.ref: Rook's Textbook of Dermatology - Volume II - 9th Edition Chapter 30 Page no 30.15

subject: Dermatology
question: Genital warts are most commonly caused by?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. Human Papillomavirus-11
• HPV-11 is also a cause of genital warts, along with HPV-6, and is included in the low-risk HPV types. But Genital warts are most commonly caused by HPV-6 > HPV-11
• Option B. Human Papilloma Virus-2
• HPV-2 is typically associated with common warts on the hands and feet , not with genital warts.
• Option C. Human Papillomavirus-10
• HPV-10 is not typically associated with genital warts. It is more commonly associated with other cutaneous warts.
• Educational Objective
• HPV types 6 and 11 are the predominant causes of genital warts and are classified as low-risk HPV types due to their low oncogenic potential . They are targeted by HPV vaccinations which aim to reduce the incidence of warts and other HPV-related conditions.
explanation.ref: Rook's Textbook of Dermatology - 9th Edition - Volume I Chapter 25 Page no 25.43 ​​​​​​​

subject: Dermatology
question:
A patient presents with following lesions on the genitals. Scrapings from the lesion would show.

[Image: https://cerebellum-web-static.s3.amazonaws.com/media/public/images/2024/02/17/picture11_lnmQLn1.jpg]

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option D. Henderson Patterson bodies :
• These are intracytoplasmic inclusions seen in cells infected with molluscum contagiosum , a skin infection caused by the molluscum contagiosum virus . This is not consistent with the lesion shown in the image.
• Educational Objective:
• In Herpes simplex virus infections, scrapings from lesions may show both Acantholytic / Tzanck cells and Multinucleate giant cells (MNGCs).
• Ananthanarayan & Paniker’s Textbook of Microbiology 10 th edition Page no 474-476
• Bolognia Textbook of Dermatology 4 th edition Page no 503
explanation.ref: Sexually Transmitted Diseases and HIV/AIDS by Vinod K Sharma, 2 nd edition, chapter 25 Page no 418

subject: Dermatology
question:
A 32-year-old patient presents with urethral discharge gram stain image below. which of the following could be the most likely diagnosis?

[Image: https://cerebellum-web-static.s3.amazonaws.com/media/public/images/2024/02/17/picture6_pc514OF.jpg]

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A: Chlamydia trachomatis
• This is also a common cause of urethritis , but it tends to produce a less pronounced , mucopurulent discharge.
• Option B: Gardnerella vaginalis
• This is associated with bacterial vaginosis and presents with a fishy odour and a Homogenous white adherent discharge It does not cause urethritis with purulent discharge.
• Option D: Trichomonas vaginalis
• This is a protozoan parasite causing trichomoniasis , which can result in urethritis, but it would not present with the gram-negative intracellular diplococci seen on gram stain.
• Educational Objective:
• When presented with a case of urethral discharge and gram-stain evidence of gram-negative intracellular diplococci , consider Neisseria gonorrhea as the most likely diagnosis. This organism causes gonococcal urethritis , which is characterized by a profuse and purulent discharge and can lead to serious complications if left untreated.
• Sexually Transmitted Diseases and HIV/AIDS by Vinod K Sharma, 2 nd edition, chapter 23 Page no 390
• Rooks textbook of dermatology- 9 th Edition page no 30.7
• Rooks textbook of dermatology- 9 th Edition page no
• Bolognia Textbook of Dermatology 4 th edition Page no 1462
explanation.ref: Sexually Transmitted Diseases and HIV/AIDS by Vinod K Sharma, 2 nd edition, chapter 23 Page no 390

subject: Dermatology
question: Wife of a truck driver with multiple sexual partners presents to the Derma OPD with dysuria & vaginal discharge. On examination, cervical erosion (+). Which colour STD kit should be given to this patient?

explanation.detail:
• Cervicitis often presents with vaginal discharge , but is distinguished by the presence of cervical erosion on speculum exam. According to NACO guidelines , such cases are treated with the Grey Kit (Kit 1) , which contains: Cefixime (for gonorrhea) and Azithromycin (for chlamydia)
• National Technical Guidelines on Sexually Transmitted Infections and Reproductive Tract Infections 2024

subject: Dermatology
question: A patient presents to you with multiple anogenital warts. The biopsy of these lesions showed squamous atypia. Which of the following HPV types are considered high risk?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. HPV 2 :
• This type of HPV is considered low-risk and is typically associated with common warts , not anogenital warts or cancers.
• Option C. HPV 6 :
• HPV 6 is also a low-risk type , commonly associated with the development of genital warts , but not with high-grade squamous atypia or cancer.
• Option D. HPV 11 :
• Like HPV 6, HPV 11 is known to cause genital warts , but it is not associated with high-grade squamous lesions or malignancy.
• Note - Gardasil-9 Nonavalent Vaccine - HPV-6,11,16,18,31,33,45,52 & 58. Protects against 6 cancers (Cervix, Vulva, Vagina, Anal Canal, Penis & Oral Cancers))+ Genital warts.
• Educational Objective:
• In the context of anogenital warts, HPV 18 is categorized as a high-risk type because of its association with anogenital carcinomas , especially cervical cancer. In contrast, HPV types 6 and 11 are low-risk types commonly associated with benign anogenital warts and do not typically lead to cancer. HPV 2 is typically associated with common skin warts and not anogenital lesions. It is crucial for healthcare professionals to distinguish between high-risk and low-risk HPV types to provide appropriate patient counselling and management.
• Sexually Transmitted Diseases and HIV/AIDS by Vinod K Sharma, 2 nd edition, Page no 440
• Rooks’ textbook of dermatology- 9 th Edition page no 25.44
• Bolognia Textbook of Dermatology 4 th edition Page no 1393

subject: Dermatology
question: A women with multiple sexual partners came with complaints of lower abdominal pain, intermenstrual bleed, and dysuria. The causative organism would be?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Candida species cause yeast infections , which typically present with pruritus, vaginal discharge that is often described as "cottage cheese-like ," and no significant lower abdominal pain, intermenstrual bleeding, or dysuria.
• Herpes Simplex Virus causes genital herpes , which is characterized by painful vesicular lesions , may cause dysuria if lesions are present in the urethra, but is not typically associated with lower abdominal pain or intermenstrual bleeding.
