• Tuberculosis in the genital tract can cause Pelvic Inflammatory Disease , but it is less common and usually not associated with the acute presentation of symptoms like dysuria or intermenstrual bleeding.
• Educational Objective:
• Neisseria gonorrhoeae is a common causative organism of Pelvic Inflammatory Disease , presenting with lower abdominal pain , intermenstrual bleeding , and dysuria, especially in a patient with multiple sexual partners.
• Rooks textbook of dermatology- 9 th Edition page no 30.3
• Bolognia Textbook of Dermatology 4 th edition Page no 1461
explanation.ref: Sexually Transmitted Diseases and HIV/AIDS by Vinod K Sharma, 2 nd edition, Page no 516

subject: Dermatology
question:
Match the Diseases in Colum A with STI Kit colour in Colum B:

[Image: https://cerebellum-web-static.s3.amazonaws.com/media/public/images/2024/02/17/picture17_kODE4Go.jpg]

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• 1. Urethritis - b Grey –
• 1. Urethritis - b Grey
• Urethritis, which is inflammation of the urethra, typically presents with dysuria and discharge . The grey color kit is often used for treatment protocols of urethritis, which includes antibiotics targeting common causative organisms like Neisseria gonorrhoeae and Chlamydia trachomatis .
• 2. Non-herpetic genital ulcers - c White / Blue –
• 2. Non-herpetic genital ulcers - c White / Blue
• Genital ulcers that are not caused by herpes simplex virus may be due to diseases like syphilis or chancroid . The treatment for these conditions often involves antibiotics like penicillin or azithromycin , which may be represented by a white/blue colored kit.
• 3. Vaginal discharge - a Green –
• 3. Vaginal discharge - a Green
• The green kit is commonly associated with the treatment of vaginal discharge syndromes, which can be caused by bacterial vaginosis, trichomoniasis, or candidiasis . Treatments usually include metronidazole , which are effective against these conditions.
• 4. Pelvic inflammatory disease - d Yellow –
• 4. Pelvic inflammatory disease - d Yellow
• The yellow kit is for managing pelvic inflammatory disease (PID), which requires broad-spectrum antibiotics to cover likely pathogens, including sexually transmitted ones.
• Educational Objective:
• Details of color coded STI/RTI kits -
• National Technical Guidelines on Sexually Transmitted Infections and Reproductive Tract Infections 2024

subject: Dermatology
question:
A college student came with painful grouped vesicles over genitalia. What will be next best investigation you want to perform and what could be the possible finding?

[Image: https://cerebellum-web-static.s3.amazonaws.com/media/public/images/2024/02/17/picture27_KVUI9jm.jpg]

explanation.short: Ans. D) Tzanck smear to detect Tzanck cells
explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. Gram stain to detect Gram-positive organisms
• The Gram stain is used to classify bacteria as either Gram-positive or Gram-negative. This test is not appropriate for the clinical scenario described, as the presentation is not suggestive of a bacterial infection.
• Option B. Skin biopsy to detect HP bodies
• HP (Henderson-Patterson) bodies are intracytoplasmic inclusions seen in molluscum contagiosum, which is not consistent with the presentation of painful genital vesicles. A skin biopsy is more invasive and not the first-line investigation in this scenario.
• Option C. SSS to detect Acid-fast bacilli
• An SSS (Slit Skin Smear) test for acid-fast bacilli is used in the diagnosis of leprosy , neither of which typically present as painful grouped vesicles in the genital area.
• Educational Objective:
• The clinical presentation of painful grouped vesicles over the genitalia is most consistent with a herpes simplex viru s infection, for which a Tzanck smear is a rapid diagnostic test that can demonstrate the presence of Tzanck cells , supporting the diagnosis.
• Sexually Transmitted Diseases and HIV/AIDS by Vinod K Sharma, 2 nd edition, Page no 418
• Rooks’ textbook of dermatology- 9 th Edition page no 25.21
• Bolognia Textbook of Dermatology 4 th edition Page no 1404

subject: Dermatology
question: A female married for 6 years and trying to conceive complaints of profuse non-foul smelling, non-itchy white discharge for 2 days. Her last menstrual period is 13-14 days back. What is the most likely diagnosis?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. Bacterial vaginosis
• Bacterial vaginosis (BV) is characterized by a foul-smelling discharge that is typically gray in color. It's also associated with a fishy odor , particularly after intercourse, which is not described in the presented case.
• Candidiasis, or a yeast infection, usually presents with a thick, white, cottage cheese-like discharge that is accompanied by severe itching . The lack of itching and the texture of the discharge make this diagnosis less likely.
• Trichomoniasis typically presents with a frothy, yellow-green discharge with a foul odor and may cause itching and irritation. The absence of these characteristics in the patient's description makes trichomoniasis an unlikely diagnosis.
• Educational Objective:
• The presence of a profuse, clear to white, non-foul smelling, and non-itchy discharge around the time of ovulation is most likely a physiological occurrence related to the normal menstrual cycle.
• Sexually Transmitted Diseases and HIV/AIDS by Vinod K Sharma, 2 nd edition, Page no 784

subject: Dermatology
question:
Match the Diseases in Colum A with STI Kit colour in Colum B:

[Image: https://cerebellum-web-static.s3.amazonaws.com/media/public/images/2024/02/17/picture30_MqaP1se.jpg]

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A: 1-b, 2-c, 3-a
• The correct match between the Viral organisms / infections in Colum A with its Histopathological characteristic features in Colum B
• A. 1-b, 2-c, 3-a
• 1. Bubo is associated with STI Kit color b Black .
• Inguinal Bubo under Genital Ulcer Disease Syndrome LGV Proctitis under anorectal discharge syndrome
• 2. Herpetic genital ulcers are linked with STI Kit color c Red .
• This reflects the association with herpes simplex virus infections, which often present as painful red ulcers.
• 3. Pelvic inflammatory disease (PID) has STI Kit color a Yellow .
• PID is a spectrum of infections affecting the upper genital tract and can be caused by multiple organisms , including those causing STI
• Educational Objective:
• Details of color-coded RTI/STI kits -
• National Technical Guidelines on Sexually Transmitted Infections and Reproductive Tract Infections 2024

subject: Dermatology
question: A 35-year-old male, who works as a truck driver, presents with a complaint of multiple painful ulcers on the glans penis, which have polycyclic margins. He also reports painful swelling in the groin area. Based on this clinical presentation, which of the following STI management kits should be administered?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• The Blue kit is used for the management of non-herpetic genital ulcers , not for Herpetic genital ulcers.
• The White kit is typically used for the management of non-herpetic genital ulcers , not for Herpetic genital ulcers.
• The Yellow kit is generally used for Pelvic Inflammatory disease in females and does not apply to the patient's presentation of genital ulcers and lymphadenopathy.
• Educational Objective:
• Painful genital ulcers with polycyclic margins and painful lymphadenopathy are consistent with genital herpes , which is treated with the Red kit according to syndromic management guidelines.
• National Technical Guidelines on Sexually Transmitted Infections and Reproductive Tract Infections 2024

subject: Dermatology
question: A female presented with malodorous white discharge. She didn't complain of any itching. Adding KOH produced fishy odour. Which of the following is TRUE about this condition?
options: On wet mount multiple WBCs are visible

explanation.short: Ans. C) pH of vagina is >4.5
explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. Cervical petechiae are visible.
• Cervical petechiae, also known as strawberry cervix , are indicative of trichomoniasis , not bacterial vaginosis.
• Option B. On wet mount multiple WBCs are visible
• While WBCs may be present in various vaginal infections, the presence of multiple WBCs on a wet mount is more characteristic of infections such as trichomoniasis or vulvovaginal candidiasis , not specifically bacterial vaginosis.
• Option D. This is an STD
• Bacterial vaginosis is not classified as an STD. It is associated with an imbalance of the vaginal flora and can be sexually associated but is not considered a traditional sexually transmitted infection.
• Educational Objective:
• The diagnostic criteria for bacterial vaginosis , include the absence of itching and a vaginal pH >4.5 .
• Sexually Transmitted Diseases and HIV/AIDS by Vinod K Sharma, 2 nd edition, Page no 401, 784
• Rooks’ textbook of dermatology- 9 th Edition page no 32.64
• Bolognia Textbook of Dermatology 4 th edition Page no 1256

subject: Dermatology
question:
A 25-year-old male presented with profuse purulent urethral discharge along with intense burning sensation as shown in the picture. He had moderate grade fever. Which of the following is TRUE regarding his condition?

[Image: https://cerebellum-web-static.s3.amazonaws.com/media/public/images/2024/02/17/picture41_Iikht4X.jpg]
options: Doxycycline is the treatment of choice
Gram negative Diplococci are seen on smear
His testes can also get involved

explanation.short: Ans. B) Gram negative Diplococci are seen on smear
explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. Doxycycline is the treatment of choice
• Doxycycline is commonly used for chlamydial infections , not gonococcal infections. The current recommended treatment for gonorrhoea is ceftriaxone .
• Option C. His testes can also get involved:
• Gonococcal infection can lead to epididymitis , but spares testes, and also the presence of Gram-negative diplococci on a smear is more directly related to the symptoms described.
• Option D. Incubation period is 10 days:
• The incubation period for gonorrhea is typically shorter, ranging from 2 to 5 days. While it can be longer, a 10-day incubation period is more suggestive for Non gonococcal urethritis.
• Educational Objective:
• Gonococcal urethritis in a male patient, characterized by profuse purulent urethral discharge and a burning sensation during urination , with diagnosis confirmed by the presence of Gram-negative diplococci on urethral smear.
• Sexually Transmitted Diseases and HIV/AIDS by Vinod K Sharma, 2 nd edition, Page no 75
• Rooks’ textbook of dermatology- 9 th Edition page no 30.3
• Bolognia Textbook of Dermatology 4 th edition Page no 1460

subject: Dermatology
question: A 25-year-old female presents with vaginal discharge and itching. Her male partner has no symptoms. Which of the following conditions necessitates treatment of the male partner?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A (Bacterial vaginosis):
• This is an overgrowth of normal vaginal flora and is not considered a sexually transmitted infection. Routine treatment of the male partner is not recommended.
• Option B (Herpes genitalis):
• This is a sexually transmitted infection caused by the herpes simplex virus. While sexual partners should be informed and preventive measures should be taken to avoid transmission, especially during outbreaks, treatment of the male partner is not required unless he shows symptoms.
• This is a yeast infection caused by Candida species. It is not classified as a sexually transmitted infection, and treatment of the male partner is not typically necessary unless he shows symptoms of infection (e.g., balanitis).
• Educational Objective
• Trichomoniasis is a sexually transmitted infection that requires the treatment of both the female patient and her male partner to prevent reinfection. Bacterial vaginosis and candidiasis are not considered sexually transmitted, and routine treatment of the male partner is not necessary. Herpes genitalis requires preventive measures rather than treatment of an asymptomatic partner.
• Rook's Textbook of Dermatology 9th edition Chapter 33, Page no’s 33.35 and 33.36
explanation.ref: Rook's Textbook of Dermatology 9th edition Chapter 33, Page no’s 33.35 and 33.36

subject: Dermatology
question: Treatment of choice for Anogenital warts in pregnancy:

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. Podophyllin application :
• This is not the treatment of choice in pregnancy due to its potential toxicity to the fetus . Podophyllin is contraindicated during pregnancy.
• Option C. Application of Imiquimod :
• Imiquimod is not the preferred treatment during pregnancy as its safety for the fetus has not been established.
• Option D. Application of 5 FU (5-fluorouracil) :
• 5-fluorouracil is not recommended during pregnancy due to potential risks to the developing fetus.
• Educational Objective
• Cryosurgery is the treatment of choice for anogenital warts in pregnancy because it is safe and effective , with minimal risk to the fetus.
• Sexually Transmitted Diseases and HIV/AIDS by Vinod K Sharma, 2 nd edition, Page no 438
• Rooks textbook of dermatology- 9 th Edition page no 112.29
• Bolognia Textbook of Dermatology 4 th edition Page no 1397

subject: Dermatology
question: A commercial sex worker presents with an indurated, painless ulcer over her vulva. On examination painless inguinal lymphadenopathy is also present. Which of the following statements is FALSE regarding this disease?
options: Extragenital syphilitic chancre can occur over fingers
Dory flop sign can be seen in uncircumcised males

explanation.short: Ans. B) Incubation period is 2-5 days.
explanation.detail:
• Educational Objective:
• The incubation period of syphilis is approximately 3 weeks to 3 months after exposure.
• Rook's Textbook of Dermatology 9th edition Chapter 29, Page no’s 29.7
explanation.ref: Rook's Textbook of Dermatology 9th edition Chapter 29, Page no’s 29.7

subject: Dermatology
question: A 35-year-old man presents with a hyperpigmented rash over his palms and soles. On further probing, he admits that his partner was recently diagnosed with syphilis and he himself had an ulcer on his penis a few months back. Which is NOT a characteristic feature of the secondary syphilis stage?
options: Coppery red coloured, non-pruritic lesions with Symmetrical distribution

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A: Rashes in secondary syphilis have three common features:
• Non-pruritic Coppery red color Symmetrically distributed Due to a lack of treponemes, cutaneous lesions from secondary syphilis are not contagious . The only exceptions are mucous patches and condyloma lata , both of which are very contagious.
• Option B: Vesiculobullous lesions are not seen in secondary syphilis. They are a feature of early congenital syphilis.
• The following are some signs of secondary syphilis:
• The earliest rash is the macular or roseolar rash . Leucoderma Syphiliticum , also known as the necklace of Venus, is a hypopigmentation that develops around the neck as it fades. The palms and soles are covered in a papule-like rash , and later, nummular lesions with scales that resemble psoriasis are visible. The scales are simple to remove because of the lesions' serous discharge .
• Option C. Syphilitic alopecia or moth-eaten alopecia as seen below is a form of non-scarring patchy hair loss.
• Option D: Olendroff probe sign or Buschke-Ollendorff sign refers to the deep dermal tenderness elicited by pressing the Papular lesions of secondary syphilis with a pinhead , when the patient winces with pain.
• Although ulceration is uncommon, papules on the oral mucosa can coalesce to form the so called "snail-track" lesions , as seen below.
• Condyloma lata refers to moist, well-demarcated papules and plaques which are seen on the genitalia. They are highly infectious.
• Condyloma acuminata / anogenital warts are caused by HPV (Human Papillomavirus) virus . They are different from Condyloma lata.
• Educational Objective:
• The typical rash of secondary syphilis is coppery red, non-pruritic, and symmetrically distributed, often involving the palms and soles.
explanation.ref: Rook's Textbook of Dermatology 9th edition Chapter 29, Page nos 29.10-13

subject: Dermatology
question:
A 49-year-old man presents with secondary syphilis-like features He has no history of chancre. On examination, he has epitrochlear lymphadenopathy. Which of the following is the likely cause of this presentation?

[Image: https://cerebellum-web-static.s3.amazonaws.com/media/public/images/2024/02/19/picture6.jpg]

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. Sexual contact :
• While sexual contact is the most common route of transmission for syphilis, the absence of a chance (primary syphilis lesion) might suggest a non-sexual mode of transmission in this case. Primary syphilis can go unnoticed, but in the case presented, another route of transmission is suggested.
• Option C. Endemic disease :
• While syphilis is endemic in certain populations, the term 'endemic disease' typically refers to diseases that are constantly present in a particular region and doesn't imply a transmission route.
• Option D. Skin to skin contact :
• Although Treponema pallidum can be transmitted through close physical contact, this route is less likely to cause syphilis without a chancre being present, and epitrochlear lymphadenopathy is not typically associated with such transmission.
• Educational Objective:
• Secondary syphilis without a history of chancre could suggest a transmission route other than sexual contact, such as a blood transfusion , especially if there is evidence of systemic involvement like epitrochlear lymphadenopathy.
explanation.ref: online source https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC3401849/

subject: Dermatology
question: All of the following can be seen associated with Early congenital syphilis EXCEPT?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Syphilitic rhinitis , commonly referred to as "snuffles," is frequently the first and most prevalent symptom of early congenital syphilis. A profuse, serous nasal discharge with a significant amount of Treponema pallidum is how it presents. Snuffles, Syphilitic pemphigus & Hepatosplenomegaly form the triad of Early Congenital syphilis. Due to the destruction of the nose's bone and cartilage in later stages, a saddle nose may develop. Congenital syphilis can spread intrapartum through lesions in the mother's genitalia or trans placentally from an infected mother. The primary and secondary stages of the disease are when the risk is greatest. It can result in fetal death or congenital syphilis in a live birth if untreated. The onset of congenital syphilis can be early or late . Early disease manifests within two years , while late disease does so after three years .
• Late congenital syphilis has Hutchinson's triad which includes the following:
• 2. Eighth nerve deafness
• Hutchinson teeth are abnormal permanent upper central incisors that are peg-shaped and notched.
• Seventh nerve palsy is NOT a feature of early congenital syphilis.
• It is associated with 8th nerve deafness, along with tinnitus and vertigo.
• Educational Objective:
• Early congenital syphilis may present with vesiculobullous lesions, hepatosplenomegaly, and rhinitis .
explanation.ref: Rook's Textbook of Dermatology 9th edition Chapter 29, Page nos 29.28-29

subject: Dermatology
question: Wimberger sign is seen in?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Scurvy is associated with vitamin C deficiency and can lead to various bone abnormalities , but Wimberger sign is not characteristic of scurvy. In scurvy, you might see Scorbutic rosary or Frankel's sign , which are different from the Wimberger sign.
• Scurvy: Wimberger ring sign refers to a circular calcification surrounding the osteoporotic epiphyseal center of ossification in scurvy, which may result from bleeding .
• Option C. Late congenital syphilis:
• Late congenital syphilis can present with several bone deformities, such as saber shins, but the Wimberger sign is specifically associated with early congenital syphilis. Musculoskeletal system signs & Symptoms in Late congenital syphilis: Cluttons joints refer to painless symmetrical swelling of the knees . Higoumenaki's sign is the unilateral enlargement of the sternal end of the clavicle .
• Educational Objective:
• The Wimberger sign is a radiological feature typically associated with early congenital syphilis , characterized by symmetrical destruction of the medial portion of the proximal tibial metaphyses .
• Rook's Textbook of Dermatology 9th edition
• Chapter 29, Page nos 29.30-32
explanation.ref: Rook's Textbook of Dermatology 9th edition

subject: Dermatology
question: Which of the following is FALSE about Chancre redux?
options: Recurring chancre at the site of previously healed chancre

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Chancre redux is seen in primary syphilis and is highly infectious.
• It must not be confused with Pseudochancre redux.
• Educational Objective:
• Chancre redux , also known as relapsing syphilis , is the recurrence of a syphilitic chancre at the site of the previously healed initial lesion and is indeed infectious.
• Rook's Textbook of Dermatology 9th edition
• Chapter 29, Page nos 29.20 & chapter 111 pg no 111.24-25
explanation.ref: Rook's Textbook of Dermatology 9th edition

subject: Dermatology
question: A woman with multiple sexual partners with painless Genital ulcer & associated Painless Lymphadenopathy. What is the diagnosis & what is the Investigation of choice for this stage of the disease?

explanation.short: Ans. B) Syphilis & dark field microscopy
explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• With the clinical scenario given, the diagnosis would be Primary syphilis . Syphilis is caused by Treponema pallidum .
• Primary syphilis commonly presents as a papule on external genitalia that rapidly ulcerates to form a primary chancre .
• Primary chancre: Painless , indurated , button-like ulcer with painless inguinal lymphadenopathy .
• Microscopically, Treponema pallidum is identified with a characteristic wave-like appearance on dark field microscopy .
• Treponema is a corkscrew organism . The coils are very close and regular with around 7 to 8 coils per diameter of a red blood corpuscle . It rotates along its long axis and appears to screw slowly forwards and backwards.
• Option C: Chancroid presents with multiple , soft painful ulcers and unilateral tender lymphadenopathy .
• Microscopy of chancroid lesions shows H. ducreyi in a sheet-like or school of fish or railroad pattern .
• Option D: LGV presents with a painless or painful Transient Genital ulcer later followed by Painful lymphadenopathy . NAAT is used for its diagnosis.
• Educational Objective:
• The diagnosis of primary syphilis is suggested by a painless genital ulcer and painless lymphadenopathy , with dark field microscopy being the investigation of choice to directly visualize Treponema pallidum spirochetes.
• Rook's Textbook of Dermatology 9th edition
• Chapter 29, Page nos 29.7
explanation.ref: Rook's Textbook of Dermatology 9th edition

subject: Dermatology
question: After 14 hours of receiving Injection Penicillin for her early latent syphilis, a 35-year-old woman suddenly develops a high-grade fever, myalgia, and bone pain. Which of the following statements is FALSE with respect to her condition?
options: Penicillin cannot be used in future
Fever usually comes down within 8 hours
D Jarisch-Herxheimer reaction occurs within 24 hours of penicillin therapy

explanation.short: Ans. B) Penicillin cannot be used in future
explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• A febrile reaction occurring within 24 hours of penicillin therapy for syphilis is suggestive of Jarisch-Herxheimer's reaction . Jarisch-Herxheimer reaction is self-limiting and is due to lipoprotein release from the dying treponemes , which trigger cytokine release. The patient presents with flu-like symptoms and exacerbation of skin lesions . Fever rarely lasts more than 8 hours . Symptoms may be controlled by antipyretics . It is not a contraindication for penicillin use in the future. Penicillin allergy is a contraindication for further use. JH reactions must be differentiated from penicillin allergy . Constitutional Symptoms such as Fever, Joint Pain, Hypotension along with Worsening of Skin Lesions. Treatment : Symptomatic
• Allergic reactions can be early or late as follows:
• Early type- anaphylaxis within 1 hour of the first dose. Late type- rash after several doses of penicillin.
• In Jarisch-Herxheimer's reaction, Corticosteroids are only indicated in pregnant women and patients with neurosyphilis in whom the reaction can be more serious and life-threatening sequelae.
• Educational Objective:
• The Jarisch-Herxheimer reaction is a temporary reaction to antibiotic therapy for syphilis, characterized by fever, myalgia, and bone pain , and is not a contraindication to future penicillin use.
• Rook's Textbook of Dermatology 9th edition
• Chapter 29, Page nos 29.26
• Fitzpatrick’s dermatology 9th edition page 3167
explanation.ref: Rook's Textbook of Dermatology 9th edition

subject: Dermatology
question: Which of the following disease is correctly correlated with its incubation period?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• The incubation period for syphilis can range from 10 to 90 days , with an average of 21 days. However, symptoms can sometimes take up to 90 days to appear, which is why this option is correct.
• Educational Objective:
• Syphilis can have a lengthy incubation period of up to 90 days before the first symptoms appear, which can include a painless ulcer known as a chancre.
• Rooks textbook of dermatology- 9 th Edition page no 29.3
• Bolognia Textbook of Dermatology 4 th edition Page no 1447
explanation.ref: Sexually Transmitted Diseases and HIV/AIDS by Vinod K Sharma, 2 nd edition, chapter 14 Page no 265

subject: Dermatology
question: Kassowitz law is related to which of the following?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A: Primary Syphilis –
• Option A: Primary Syphilis
• This stage involves the initial infection and genital ulceration but does not relate to Kassowitz's law, which is specific to congenital syphilis.
• Option B: Secondary Syphilis –
• Option B: Secondary Syphilis
• Secondary syphilis refers to the stage with systemic spread of the infection, characterized by rash and lymphadenopathy , not congenital transmission.
• Option C: Latent Syphilis –
• Option C: Latent Syphilis
• Latent syphilis is the stage where the disease is not active but remains in the body. This also does not pertain to congenital syphilis.
• Educational Objective:
• Kassowitz's law is associated with congenital syphilis , highlighting the impact of the timing of maternal infection on the clinical presentation of syphilis in the newborn.
• Rooks textbook of dermatology- 9 th Edition page no 29.27
• Bolognia Textbook of Dermatology 4 th edition Page no 1455
explanation.ref: Sexually Transmitted Diseases and HIV/AIDS by Vinod K Sharma, 2 nd edition, chapter 15 Page no 290

subject: Dermatology
question: Jarisch-Herxheimer reaction is due to? IFN Alpha IL – 6 TNF Alpha IL – 8

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• This is an acute febrile reaction that often includes headache, myalgia, fever, and other symptoms. It typically occurs after antibiotic treatment for certain bacterial infections, notably syphilis, Lyme disease , and other spirochetal infections. The reaction is believed to be caused by the release of cytokines from the body's immune system in response to endotoxins released from the death of harmful bacteria following antibiotic treatment.
• IL-6, TNF Alpha, and IL-8 are cytokines that are known to play a role in the i nflammatory response, including fever and inflammation, which are characteristic of the Jarisch-Herxheimer reaction. They are part of the body's immune response to infection and are released in larger quantities when large numbers of bacteria are killed off suddenly, as can happen with the initiation of antibiotic therapy.
• Educational Objective:
• The Jarisch-Herxheimer reaction is associated with the rapid destruction of bacteria following antibiotic treatment and is mediated by the release of pro-inflammatory cytokines such as IL-6, TNF Alpha , and IL-8. It is important to be aware of this reaction when treating bacterial infections that can release endotoxins , as the symptoms can mimic infection or sepsis and must be managed appropriately.
• Rooks textbook of dermatology- 9 th Edition page no 26.69
• Bolognia Textbook of Dermatology 4 th edition Page no 1458
explanation.ref: Sexually Transmitted Diseases and HIV/AIDS by Vinod K Sharma, 2 nd edition, chapter 17 Page no 322

subject: Dermatology
question: What is the cause of painless ulcer associated with painless inguinal lymph node?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• This is incorrect because Chancroid, caused by Haemophilus ducreyi , typically presents with painful genital ulcers and may be associated with painful inguinal lymphadenopathy , not painless
• Option B: Granuloma inguinale / Donovanosis
• This is caused by Klebsiella granulomatis , leads to painless genital ulcers , but it is often associated with beefy red granulation tissue and not classically linked with lymphadenopathy.
• Option C: Herpes genitalis
• This is incorrect because genital herpes , caused by the Herpes Simplex Virus , typically results in painful ulcers and inguinal lymph nodes are often tender.
• Educational Objective:
• For a patient presenting with a painless genital ulcer and painless inguinal lymph nodes , primary syphilis is the most likely diagnosis, while other STIs like chancroid and herpes genitalis usually present with painful ulcers and/or tender lymphadenopathy.
• Sexually Transmitted Diseases and HIV/AIDS by Vinod K Sharma, 2 nd edition, chapter 45 Page no 771
• Rooks’ textbook of dermatology- 9 th Edition page no 29.4
• Bolognia Textbook of Dermatology 4 th edition Page no 1447
explanation.ref: Sexually Transmitted Diseases and HIV/AIDS by Vinod K Sharma, 2 nd edition, chapter 45 Page no 771

subject: Dermatology
question:
A 45-year-old truck driver with a history of multiple sex partners presented to the dermatological department, as shown below. What is the likely diagnosis?

[Image: https://cerebellum-web-static.s3.amazonaws.com/media/public/images/2024/02/19/picture13_fFMHVDB.jpg]

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option B. Lichen Planus
• It presents pruritic, purple, polygonal, planar papules and plaques with fine scales. Wickham striae , reticular white lines on the surface of the lesions, are characteristic.
• It is an intensely pruritic skin condition caused by the mite Sarcoptes scabiei , leading to burrows , erythematous papules, and excoriation , typically involving the web spaces of the fingers , wrists , and waistline .
• This typically presents well-demarcated , erythematous plaques with silvery scales, often on extensor surfaces and the scalp. Nail changes and joint involvement can also occur.
• Educational Objective:
• Secondary syphilis is identified by characteristic systemic rash, which often involves the palms and soles, and its association with a history of sexual contact , which can include multiple partners .
• Rooks’ textbook of dermatology- 9 th Edition page no 29.10
• Bolognia Textbook of Dermatology 4 th edition Page no 1458
explanation.ref: Sexually Transmitted Diseases and HIV/AIDS by Vinod K Sharma, 2 nd edition, Page no 270

subject: Dermatology
question: A truck driver comes with complaints of painless, indurated genital ulcer after unprotected / unsafe intercourse. The motility of the causative agent can be detected by which type of microscope.

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option B. Electron microscopy
• While electron microscopy provides detailed ultrastructural images of organisms, it is not used in the routine clinical evaluation of genital ulcers due to its complexity and cost. Moreover, it cannot be used to observe live bacterial motility , which is key in the diagnosis of syphilis.
• Option C. Fluorescent microscope
• Fluorescent microscopy is used in conjunction with specific fluorescent markers or antibodies that can bind to a pathogen of interest. It is not typically used to observe bacterial motility or to diagnose syphilis.
• Option D. None of the above
• This option is incorrect because dark field microscopy is indeed a suitable method to observe the motility of spirochetes such as Treponema pallidum .
• Educational Objective:
• Dark field microscopy is the appropriate technique to visualize the motility of Treponema pallidum , the causative agent of syphilis, which presents as a painless, indurated genital ulcer.
• Rooks’ textbook of dermatology- 9 th Edition page no 29.27
• Bolognia Textbook of Dermatology 4 th edition Page no 1449
explanation.ref: Sexually Transmitted Diseases and HIV/AIDS by Vinod K Sharma, 2 nd edition, Page no 304

subject: Dermatology
question: Which of the following is typical of early stage of congenital syphilis?
options: Loss of density on medial side of upper end of tibia
Unilateral thickening of medial third of clavicle

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option B. Hutchinson's teeth
• Hutchinson's teeth refer to peg-shaped , notched permanent incisors that are a feature of late congenital syphilis, not the early stage.
• These dental anomalies typically become apparent when the permanent teeth erupt.
• Option C. Clutton's joints
• Clutton's joints, which refer to painless synovitis and effusions of the knees , are also a sign of late congenital syphilis and not typically seen in the early stage .
• Option D. Unilateral thickening of medial third of clavicle
• Unilateral thickening of the medial third of the clavicle, or Higouménakis’ sign, is another late manifestation of congenital syphilis. It is not a characteristic feature of the early stage of the disease.
• Educational Objective:
• Wimberger's sign is indicative of the early stage of congenital syphilis. Early diagnosis and appropriate management of congenital syphilis are crucial to prevent long-term complications.
• Rooks textbook of dermatology- 9 th Edition page no 29.29
• Bolognia Textbook of Dermatology 4 th edition Page no 1456
explanation.ref: Sexually Transmitted Diseases and HIV/AIDS by Vinod K Sharma, 2 nd edition, Page no 291

subject: Dermatology
question: A 29-years-old pregnant female's routine investigations showed VDRL positive with 1:16 titres. TPHA was also positive. The pregnant female was adequately treated with injection benzathine penicillin. What is the next step of management for the baby?
options: Give injection aqueous penicillin to baby
Perform serum VDRL of the baby
Perform CSF VDRL of the baby

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. Give injection aqueous penicillin to baby.
• Prophylactic treatment with penicillin is not recommended without evidence of congenital syphilis. The baby should be tested first before any treatment is given.
• Option C. Perform CSF VDRL of the baby.
• A CSF VDRL test is used to diagnose neurosyphilis. This would only be necessary if the baby's serum VDRL is positive or if there are clinical signs suggesting neurosyphilis.
• Option D. Wait and Watch.
• It is not appropriate to simply observe the baby without testing, given the risk of congenital syphilis. Early diagnosis and treatment , if necessary, are crucial.
• Educational Objective:
• The screening a newborn for congenital syphilis when the mother has a history of the infection, is done by performing a serum VDRL test.
• Rooks textbook of dermatology- 9 th Edition page no 29.34
• Bolognia Textbook of Dermatology 4 th edition Page no 1459
explanation.ref: Sexually Transmitted Diseases and HIV/AIDS by Vinod K Sharma, 2 nd edition, Page no 297

subject: Dermatology
question: A 4-year-old girl presents with a 6-month history of declining visual acuity and behavioural changes at school. Her mother states that the child had a home birth without any medical interventions and did not receive prenatal care. On examination, the child's growth parameters are below the 50th percentile for her age. She exhibits nummular keratitis, notched upper incisors and molars, and bilateral tibial bowing. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option B. Congenital toxoplasmosis
• It typically presents with chorioretinitis, hydrocephalus, and intracranial calcifications . Dental and bone deformities are not characteristic findings of congenital toxoplasmosis.
• Option C. Congenital rubella syndrome can lead to cataracts, heart defects, and deafness , known as the classic triad . The findings in this patient do not align with this triad.
• Option D. Congenital cytomegalovirus (CMV)
• This infection can cause a range of symptoms including hearing loss, microcephaly, and hepatosplenomegaly . While it can lead to retinitis , it is less likely to cause the dental and skeletal abnormalities seen in this patient.
• Educational Objective:
• The presence of nummular keratitis, Hutchinson's teeth, and sabre shins in a young child with behavioral changes and a history of no prenatal care strongly suggests a diagnosis of congenital syphilis.
• Rooks’ textbook of dermatology- 9 th Edition page no 29.29
• Bolognia Textbook of Dermatology 4 th edition Page no 1456
explanation.ref: Sexually Transmitted Diseases and HIV/AIDS by Vinod K Sharma, 2 nd edition, Page no 293

subject: Dermatology
question:
A 25-year-old patient presents to the clinic with painless lesions in the genital area. The patient reports having unprotected sexual intercourse several months ago. On examination, you observe multiple, broad, moist, flat-topped papules as shown below. No other symptoms are present. The patient's blood test is positive for Treponema pallidum. What is the stage of syphilis in this patient?

[Image: https://cerebellum-web-static.s3.amazonaws.com/media/public/images/2024/02/19/picture18_GHzFepm.jpg]

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option B. Latent - Early:
• This stage is asymptomatic and occurs after the secondary stage. There are no clinical signs during the latent phase, but serologic tests for syphilis are positive .
• Option C. Primary Syphilis:
• Primary syphilis is characterized by the presence of a chancre , a painless ulcer at the site of inoculation. The chancre typically appears 3 weeks after infection and resolves within 3-6 weeks .
• Option D. Latent - Late:
• This is the asymptomatic stage that can last for years. Late latent syphilis is less likely to be transmitted and may proceed to tertiary syphilis , which can affect multiple organ systems.
• Educational Objective:
• Condyloma lata is a hallmark of secondary syphilis , presenting as broad , moist , wart-like lesions , typically appearing weeks to months following the initial Treponema pallidum infection.
• Rooks’ textbook of dermatology- 9 th Edition page no 29.12
• Bolognia Textbook of Dermatology 4 th edition Page no 1451
explanation.ref: Sexually Transmitted Diseases and HIV/AIDS by Vinod K Sharma, 2 nd edition, Page no 270

subject: Dermatology
question: Manifestations of primary chancre in syphilis may include each of the following EXCEPT:
options: Sites usually affected are penis or vulva
Extragenital lesions occur over lips, finger
Chancre is a painless indurated ulcer, giving button-like feeling
Regional lymph nodes may become palpable as painful rubbery swelling

explanation.short: Ans. D) Regional lymph nodes may become palpable as painful rubbery swelling.
explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. Sites usually affected are penis or vulva:
• The primary chancre of syphilis typically occurs at the site of Treponema pallidum inoculation , commonly affecting the penis in men and vulva in women. This is the classic presentation of a primary syphilis lesion.
• Option B. Extragenital lesions occur over lips, finger:
• While less common, primary chancres can indeed occur at extragenital sites , such as the lips or fingers . This happens due to direct contact with the spirochete at these locations.
• Option C. Chancre is a painless indurated ulcer, giving button-like feeling:
• The primary chancre is typically a painless, indurated ulcer with a clean base and firm borders , often described as having a button-like consistency.
• Educational Objective:
• The primary chancre in syphilis is characterized by a painless , indurated ulcer at the site of infection, commonly on the genitals but possibly extragenitally, and is accompanied by painless , firm , rubbery regional lymphadenopathy.
• Rooks textbook of dermatology- 9 th Edition page no 29.8
• Bolognia Textbook of Dermatology 4 th edition Page no 1450
explanation.ref: Sexually Transmitted Diseases and HIV/AIDS by Vinod K Sharma, 2 nd edition, Page no 267

subject: Dermatology
question: Monitoring of response to treatment in syphilis is by?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. TPHA (Treponema Pallidum Hemagglutination Assay):
• TPHA is a treponemal test used for the detection of syphilis. It is used for confirmatory testing and remains positive for life even after successful treatment, so it is not useful for monitoring response to treatment.
• Option B. DGI Examination:
• DGI, or Dark Ground Illumination, is used to visualize live spirochetes in samples from lesions in syphilis. While it can demonstrate the presence of spirochetes, it is not typically used for monitoring the response to treatment.
• Option D. TPI (Treponema pallidum immobilization test):
• This test is historically significant but is now largely obsolete and not used in current practice for the diagnosis or monitoring of syphilis.
• Educational Objective:
• The VDRL test is used to monitor the response to treatment in syphilis, as titers are expected to decrease following successful therapy, indicating a decrease in the antigen load.
• Rooks’ textbook of dermatology- 9 th Edition page no 29.22
• Bolognia Textbook of Dermatology 4 th edition Page no 1456
explanation.ref: Sexually Transmitted Diseases and HIV/AIDS by Vinod K Sharma, 2 nd edition, Page no 269

subject: Dermatology
question:
A man is referred to the STI clinic with the following painful inguinal lymphadenopathy. He gives a history of a small transient painless ulcer on his penis 5 weeks ago, which resolved on its own. Which of the following statements regarding this condition is FALSE?

[Image: https://cerebellum-web-static.s3.amazonaws.com/media/public/images/2024/02/19/picture13.jpg]
options: Buboes can be seen in this disease
Caused by Chlamydia Trachomatis Serovars D-K
Groove sign of Greenblatt is pathognomonic in this disease

explanation.short: Ans. B) Caused by Chlamydia Trachomatis Serovars D-K
explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• This image shows the pathognomonic groove sign of Greenblatt (Option C) which is suggestive of lymphogranuloma venereum . It is caused by Chlamydia trachomatis serovars L1-L3 . In one-third of cases, a groove sign is visible. It describes the depression on either side of the inguinal ligament that is situated between the enlarged femoral and inguinal lymph nodes . Following a painless genital ulcer, buboes, or painful masses of lymph nodes , can be seen. Usually, lymphogranuloma venereum exhibits this. Incubation period is 3-30 days . There are three phases in it. (Option D)
• Starts with painless papules or transient ulcers that may go unnoticed at the inoculation site. The conventional ulcer is solitary , shallow , unindurated , and may or may not be painful.
• Appearance of tender femoral and/or inguinal lymphadenopathy develops 2–6 weeks later . Buboes or abscesses may develop from the nodes when they coalesce, and fistulae and sinus tracts may develop when they rupture. (Option A)
• Occurs several years later and is characterized by lymphatic obstruction and disfiguring conditions such as elephantiasis and Esthiomene.
• Educational Objective:
• Lymphogranuloma venereum (LGV) is caused by Chlamydia trachomatis serovars L1, L2, or L3 , not serovars D-K, and presents with painful inguinal lymphadenopathy and may have a transient genital ulcer. The groove sign is pathognomonic for LGV , and the incubation period ranges from 3 to 30 days.
explanation.ref: Rook's Textbook of Dermatology - Volume II-9th Edition Chapter 30 Page no 30.18

subject: Dermatology
question:
Esthiomene as shown in the image can occur in which of the following diseases?

[Image: https://cerebellum-web-static.s3.amazonaws.com/media/public/images/2024/02/19/picture12.jpg]

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Chancroid, caused by Haemophilus ducreyi , presents with painful genital ulcers and tender suppurative regional lymphadenopathy . It does not typically progress to Esthiomene.
• Option C. Granuloma inguinale (Donovanosis) :
• Caused by Klebsiella granulomatis , granuloma inguinale is characterized by beefy red ulcers without lymphadenopathy . It is not associated with Esthiomene.
• Syphilis, caused by Treponema pallidum , presents with different stages. Primary syphilis presents with a painless chancre , secondary syphilis can present with rash and generalized lymphadenopathy, and tertiary syphilis can cause gummas, aortitis, Tabes dorsalis , etc., but not Esthiomene.
• Educational Objective
• The presence of Esthiomene is indicative of the chronic inflammatory and scarring process associated with Lymphogranuloma venereum (LGV) , a sexually transmitted infection caused by certain serovars of Chlamydia trachomatis.
explanation.ref: Rook's Textbook of Dermatology - Volume II - 9th Edition Chapter 30 Page no 30.18

subject: Dermatology
question:
A 45-year-old man presents to Derma OPD with a painless and foul-smelling ulcer. On examination, there is no lymphadenopathy. The image below shows the microscopic analysis of a sample taken from a genital ulcer. Which of the following statements about this illness is FALSE?

[Image: https://cerebellum-web-static.s3.amazonaws.com/media/public/images/2024/02/19/picture11_vqDFOrs.jpg]
options: It has an incubation period of 8 to 80 days
It has the potential for malignant transformation

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• The given image shows Donovan bodies, which points to a diagnosis of Granuloma inguinale . The clinical picture of a painless, beefy red, foul-smelling ulcer with no lymphadenopathy is classical for Granuloma inguinale . Granuloma inguinale is a sexually transmitted infection caused by Calymmatobacterium granulomatis / Klebsiella granulomatis . (Option D) The incubation period varies from 8-80 days , the average being 50 days. (Option B) The primary ulcer, if left untreated, heals spontaneously with scarring , or persists and spreads . (Option A) Untreated genital ulcers are followed by genital disfigurement and have the potential for malignant change. (Option C) Donovan bodies refer to the Gram-negative rod-shaped or oval organisms that show bipolar staining or safety-pin appearance , present intracellularly in the cytoplasm of large mononuclear cells.
• Educational Objective
• In Granuloma inguinale , caused by Klebsiella granulomatis , and the primary ulcer, if left untreated, heals spontaneously with scarring , or persists and spreads .
explanation.ref: Rook's Textbook of Dermatology - Volume II-9th Edition Chapter 30 Page no 30.25

subject: Dermatology
question: Which of the following is FALSE about Donovanosis?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Pseudobubo refers to granulomatous lesions that may extend into subcutaneous tissue and mimic lymphadenopathy in Donovanosis. (Option A) Like the groove sign of Greenblatt seen in lymphogranuloma venereum, a pseudo-groove sign is produced when these lesions are present above and below the inguinal ligament. (Option B) The first-line treatment is azithromycin 1 g orally once weekly or 500 mg daily for 3 weeks.  (Option C)
• In this condition, prolonged antibiotic therapy is required. Antibiotics must be given for 3-6 weeks and until the ulcer has completely healed. The second-line drugs are as follows:
• Doxycycline 100 mg orally BD Erythromycin base 500 mg orally QID Trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole 160 mg/800 mg orally BD
• Donovanosis / Granuloma inguinale is a sexually transmitted infection caused by Calymmatobacterium granulomatis / Klebsiella granulomatis .
• Leishmania donovani is Causative organism for Visceral leishmaniasis. (Option D)
• Educational Objective
• The causative organism of Donovanosi s is Klebsiella granulomatis , and not Leishmania donovani , which is responsible for visceral leishmaniasis.
• Rook's Textbook of Dermatology - Volume II-9th Edition Chapter 30 Page no 30.24 30.25
explanation.ref: Rook's Textbook of Dermatology - Volume II-9th Edition Chapter 30 Page no 30.24 30.25

subject: Dermatology
question:
A 25-year-old woman has come down with painful lesions as shown below. She tells you that vesicles were present 5 days back over the same site and says that she is allergic to penicillin. On examination, she has bilateral tender lymphadenopathy. Which kit will you use to treat her?

[Image: https://cerebellum-web-static.s3.amazonaws.com/media/public/images/2024/02/19/picture10_ox0D7x9.jpg]

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• The clinical picture of multiple, grouped painful ulcers with tender lymphadenopathy in this patient points to a diagnosis of herpes genitalis infection by HSV-2. It is treated with a red kit . In herpetic genital ulcer disease, treatment of asymptomatic partners is not required. The red kit or kit 5 is meant for genital ulcer disease - herpetic. It contains acyclovir 400 mg tablets to be taken TDS for 7 days . Chancroid, which manifests as numerous, tender soft, painful ulcers, & bilateral lymphadenopathy is a close differential diagnosis. Chancroid, on the other hand, lacks vesicles and has deeper ulcers with ragged, undermined edges. Only when the patient presents with the primary chancre of syphilis is the patient's history of penicillin drug allerg y relevant. They would need to be treated with a blue kit in this situation rather than a white kit.
• Educational Objective
• The treatment of Genital Herpes involves antiviral therapy, which is typically provided in the red kit . It contains acyclovir 400 mg tablets to be taken TDS for 7 days , which is effective against the herpes simplex virus.
explanation.ref: National Health Programs of India http://naco.gov.in/sites/default/files/Syndromic%20Poster_REDUCED.pdf Harrison's Principles Of Internal Medicine - 19th Edition Page no 880

subject: Dermatology
question:
A young woman presents with painful deep ulcers on her genitals 4 days after unprotected sexual intercourse as shown below. She also has painful unilateral lymphadenopathy with suppurating buboes. What is the FALSE statement regarding this disease?

[Image: https://cerebellum-web-static.s3.amazonaws.com/media/public/images/2024/02/19/picture9_6i7um41.jpg]
options: Azithromycin 1g oral stat is the first line of treatment
Concomitant ulcer is not seen in this condition

explanation.short: Ans. D) Concomitant ulcer is not seen in this condition
explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• This patient has chancroid , which is evident by the painful, deep ulcers and tender lymphadenopathy . The etiologic agent for chancroid is Haemophilus ducreyi . (Option A) The incubation period is 3–10 days . The primary lesion starts as a painful papule that develops into painful, non-indurated ulcers with undermined edges after undergoing central necrosis. Kissing ulcers due to autoinoculation may also be present. (Option C) It is accompanied by painful lymphadenopathy , which develops into suppurating sinuses and buboes later on. Concomitant genital ulcer refers to genital ulcer that is present along with inguinal lymphadenopathy (Bubo) in the patient. Concomitant genital ulcer is seen associated with Chancroid but absent in LGV. (Option D)
• Differentiating features between Herpes genitalis & Chancroid:
• First line treatment for Chancroid is azithromycin 1g orally as a single dose or ceftriaxone 250 mg IM as a single dose. (Option B)
• The second-line options for chancroid are as follows:
• Ciprofloxacin 500 mg orally twice a day for 3 days (OR) Erythromycin base 500 mg orally four times a day for 7 days
• Educational Objective
• In Chancroid , caused by Haemophilus ducreyi , multiple painful ulcers can occur, including "kissing ulcers," and effective first-line treatment includes Azithromycin 1g orally as a single dose. Concomitant genital ulcer is seen associated with Chancroid.
explanation.ref: Rook's Textbook of Dermatology - Volume II - 9th Edition Chapter 30 Page no 30.21

subject: Dermatology
question:
A 40-year-old sexually active male presents to an STD clinic with painful genital sores as shown in the image below. He reports that the lesion appeared about two weeks ago and has been progressively worsening. On examination, you observe multiple painful bleeding ulcers with unilateral unilocular painful suppurative inguinal bubo. There are no other visible lesions, and the patient denies any systemic symptoms. He had multiple sexual partners in recent months. What is the most likely diagnosis?

[Image: https://img.medscapestatic.com/pi/meds/ckb/99/40499.jpg]

explanation.detail:
• Explanation
• OPTION A: Herpes simplex virus (HSV) infection :
• HSV typically presents as painful vesicles that can rupture to form ulcers. The key difference is that the inguinal lymphadenopathy seen in HSV (if present) is typically bilateral and not as dramatically painful or suppurative as that in chancroid.
• Differentiating features between Herpes genitalis & Chancroid:
• Moreover, the course of the lesion and the presence of multiple painful ulcers fit chancroid better than HSV.
• OPTION B: Syphilis (Primary Stage) :
• The primary lesion of syphilis is the chancre, which is a solitary, painless ulcer with a clean base and i ndurated borders. The patient's presentation of multiple painful ulcers with a painful inguinal bubo does not fit the primary syphilis picture.
• OPTION D: Donovanosis (Granuloma inguinale) :
• Caused by Klebsiella granulomatis , donovanosis typically presents as painless, beefy-red ulcers that bleed easily. They can progress to cause destruction of genital tissue. While there are similarities in the ulcer presentation between chancroid and donovanosis, the painful and suppurative inguinal bubo described in the case is more characteristic of chancroid.
• Educational Objective:
• In a sexually active individual with painful genital sores , multiple painful bleeding ulcers , and a unilateral unilocular painful suppurative inguinal bubo , the most likely diagnosis is Chancroid .
explanation.ref: Rook's Textbook of Dermatology - Volume II - 9th Edition Chapter 30 Page no 30.21

subject: Dermatology
question: Which of the following are true about chancroid? School of fish appearance Bleeds on touch Painful ulcer Grooves sign

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Chancroid: It is a sexually transmitted infection caused by the bacterium Haemophilus ducreyi . It is characterized by painful ulcers on the genitalia and swelling of the inguinal lymph nodes . Here are the characteristics based on the given options:
• School of fish appearance: This term is typically used to describe the Gram stain appearance of Haemophilus ducreyi , where the bacteria appear in a parallel arrangement resembling a "school of fish." Bleeds on touch : Chancroid ulcers can be very tender and may bleed when touched or during cleaning. Painful ulcer : A defining feature of chancroid is that the ulcer is painful, which is in contrast to the painless ulcer typically seen in syphilis. Groove sign: The groove sign is classically associated with lymphogranuloma venereum , not chancroid. It refers to the presence of a groove between two swollen lymph nodes, usually in the inguinal area.
• The correct answer is:
• Option B: 1, 2 & 3 only are correct
• This is because the first three features are indicative of chancroid. The fourth feature, the groove sign, is not associated with chancroid and is instead associated with another condition, lymphogranuloma venereum.
• Educational Objective:
• The diagnosis of chancroid is based on the presence of a painful genital ulcer and tender suppurative inguinal lymphadenopathy , with the causative agent Haemophilus ducreyi potentially giving a " school of fish " appearance on Gram stain .
• Rooks’ textbook of dermatology- 9 th Edition page no 30.20
• Bolognia Textbook of Dermatology 4 th edition Page no 1463
explanation.ref: Sexually Transmitted Diseases and HIV/AIDS by Vinod K Sharma, 2 nd edition, chapter 19 Page no 342

subject: Dermatology
question: Which among the following presents with painful genital ulcers? Hemophilus ducreyi Neisseria gonorrhea Chlamydia trachomatis Herpes simplex virus

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Hemophilus ducreyi is the causative agent of chancroid , a sexually transmitted infection characterized by painful genital ulcers. Neisseria gonorrhoeae causes gonorrhea , which typically presents with urethritis in men and may be asymptomatic or cause cervicitis in women. It does not typically cause painful genital ulcers. Chlamydia trachomatis causes chlamydia, which can result in cervicitis in women and urethritis in men , but like gonorrhea, it is not typically associated with painful genital ulcers. Herpes simplex virus causes genital herpes , which is characterized by painful genital ulcers or blisters .
• Educational Objective:
• When evaluating a patient for sexually transmitted infections, it is crucial to differentiate between organisms that cause painful genital ulcers such as Hemophilus ducreyi (chancroid) and Herpes simplex virus (genital herpes), and those that do not typically cause such ulcers like Neisseria gonorrhoeae and Chlamydia trachomatis. Accurate diagnosis is essential for effective treatment and prevention of transmission.
• Rooks’ textbook of dermatology- 9 th Edition page no 25.20
• Bolognia Textbook of Dermatology 4 th edition Page no 1400, 1463
explanation.ref: Sexually Transmitted Diseases and HIV/AIDS by Vinod K Sharma, 2 nd edition, Page no 771

subject: Dermatology
question:
Match the Genital ulcer disease in Column A with its clinical presentation in Column B.

[Image: https://cerebellum-web-static.s3.amazonaws.com/media/public/images/2024/04/18/image_YMA3Xte.png]

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• 1. Donovanosis (Granuloma inguinale) à Presentation: b. Lymphadenopathy absent
• Donovanosis is a bacterial infection caused by Klebsiella granulomatis . It presents beefy red ulcers that bleed easily on contact. The key feature is the absence of lymphadenopathy , distinguishing it from other causes of genital ulcers.
• 2. Hard Chancre (Primary syphilis) à Presentation: c. Painless genital ulcer
• The chancre of primary syphilis, caused by Treponema pallidum , is typically a single, firm, round, and painless ulcer . It is the hallmark of the initial stage of syphilis.
• 3. Lymphogranuloma venereum (LGV) à Presentation: a. Transient / Absent Genital Ulcer
• LGV is a sexually transmitted infection caused by specific serovars of Chlamydia trachomatis . The primary lesion may be a transient, painless ulcer or papule that often goes unnoticed, hence patient might not give history of a genital ulcer. The secondary stage is characterized by significant inguinal and/or femoral lymphadenopathy , which can be painful.
• Educational Objective:
• Donovanosis presents with painless genital ulcer but without any inguinal lymphadenopathy , Hard chancre from syphilis are painless ulcers , and LGV often presents with Transient and painless ulcer but with lymphadenopathy .
• Rooks’ textbook of dermatology- 9 th Edition page no 30.1-30.25
• Bolognia Textbook of Dermatology 4 th edition Page no 1463-1467
explanation.ref: Sexually Transmitted Diseases and HIV/AIDS by Vinod K Sharma, 2 nd edition, Page no 771

subject: Dermatology
question: A 25-year-old man presents to your clinic complaining of a painful genital ulcer that developed 5 days ago. He reports multiple unprotected sexual encounters in the past six months. On examination, the ulcer on the glans penis is non-indurated with undermined, sloughed edges. Based on the clinical presentation, which of the following is the most likely causative organism?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A: Treponema pallidum
• This is the causative agent of syphilis. Primary syphilis typically presents with a painless, indurated ulcer known as a chancre . The description of a painful, non-indurated ulcer does not fit the classic presentation of primary syphilis.
• Option C: Herpes Simplex Virus
• It typically causes painful genital ulcers , but these are often multiple, vesicular, or pustular rather than a single ulcer with undermined edges.
• Option D: Chlamydia trachomatis
• It can lead to lymphogranuloma venereum , which may present with genital ulcers. However, the ulcers in this condition are typically transient and may not be as pronounced as in the case described.
• Educational Objective:
• The clinical presentation of a painful, non-indurated genital ulcer with undermined edges in a sexually active individual is most indicative of chancroid , caused by Haemophilus ducreyi.
• Rooks’ textbook of dermatology- 9 th Edition page no 30.22
• Bolognia Textbook of Dermatology 4 th edition Page no 1465
explanation.ref: Sexually Transmitted Diseases and HIV/AIDS by Vinod K Sharma, 2 nd edition, Page no 340

subject: Dermatology
question: A 24-year-old woman presents with a 2-day history of vulvar pain and dysuria. She reports being sexually active with multiple new partners. On examination, there are grouped, shallow ulcers with surrounding erythema on the left labia minora, and the left inguinal lymph nodes are tender. What is the most likely Etiology of her symptoms?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A: Treponema pallidum
• Is the causative agent of syphilis. Primary syphilis typically presents with a painless, indurated ulcer known as a chancre, which does not match the patient's presentation of painful, grouped ulcers.
• Option B: Haemophilus ducreyi causes chancroid, characterized by painful, non-indurated ulcers with ragged edges and soft borders. While this could be consistent with the patient's symptoms, the presence of grouped ulcers is more characteristic of herpes simplex virus.
• Option D: Chlamydia trachomatis can cause a variety of genital infections, but it is not typically associated with the formation of ulcers.
• Educational Objective:
• Grouped , shallow , painful genital ulcers with surrounding erythema and tender regional lymphadenopathy are most indicative of a herpes simplex virus infection.
• Rooks’ textbook of dermatology- 9 th Edition page no 25.21
• Bolognia Textbook of Dermatology 4 th edition Page no 1404
explanation.ref: Sexually Transmitted Diseases and HIV/AIDS by Vinod K Sharma, 2 nd edition, Page no 418

subject: Dermatology
question:
A young male presents to the STD clinic with painful lesions over the genitals as shown in the image. What is the most likely microscopic finding of the lesion?

[Image: https://cerebellum-web-static.s3.amazonaws.com/media/public/images/2024/02/19/whatsapp-image-2024-02-19-at-14232-pm.jpeg]

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A: Halberstaeder Prowazek bodies :
• Halberstaedter Prowazek bodies are intracytoplasmic inclusions seen in the conjunctival epithelial cells in the case of chlamydial conjunctivitis , not associated with genital lesions.
• Option B: Henderson Peterson bodies:
• Henderson-Peterson bodies are intracytoplasmic inclusion bodies seen in molluscum contagiosum, which is not typically painful and does not present as the vesicular lesions seen in the image.
• Option D: Pund cells:
• Tissue smear in Donovanosis may show Greenblatt / Pund cells which are large macrophage / epithelioid cells containing cystic spaces, with nuclei pushed to one side and darkly staining inclusions called Donovan bodies .
• Educational Objective:
• The characteristic microscopic finding associated with painful genital vesicles as seen in the image is the presence of multinucleated giant cells , which is indicative of herpes genitalis caused by the herpes simplex virus.
• Rooks’ textbook of dermatology- 9 th Edition page no 25.21
• Bolognia Textbook of Dermatology 4 th edition Page no 1404
explanation.ref: Sexually Transmitted Diseases and HIV/AIDS by Vinod K Sharma, 2 nd edition, Page no 418

subject: Dermatology
question:
The girl seen in the image has had history of severe sunburns after just a few minutes in the sun, she also has history of photosensitivity & severe freckling in the sun exposed areas. The most likely diagnosis is?

[Image: https://cerebellum-web-static.s3.amazonaws.com/media/public/images/2024/02/23/01.jpg]

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. Blooms Syndrome :
• It is characterized by short stature , sun-sensitive skin lesions, and an increased risk of cancers , but the freckling noted in the clinical picture is more characteristic of XP.
• They are benign growths caused by human papillomavirus (HPV) and do not have a relationship with sun exposure or photosensitivity .
• Option D. SLE (Systemic Lupus Erythematosus) :
• SLE can cause photosensitivity and skin rashes , but the distribution and chronicity of freckling seen here are more specific to XP.
• Educational Objective
• The history of severe sunburns , photosensitivity , and severe freckling in sun-exposed areas are classic signs of Xeroderma Pigmentosum .
• Harpers Illustrated Biochemistry 30 th Edition Page Number 390
• Harpers Illustrated Biochemistry 31 st Edition Page Number 370
explanation.ref: Rook's Textbook of Dermatology - 9th Edition - Volume II Chapter 78 Page No 78.1

subject: Dermatology
question: Cutis Marmorata occurs due to?

explanation.short: Ans. C) Exposure to Cold Temperature
explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. Exposure to hot temperature
• Erythema Ab Igne over thigh
• Option B. An adverse reaction to drugs
• They can cause various skin manifestations , including rashes, urticaria, or specific reactions like Stevens-Johnson syndrome , but not cutis marmorata.
• It is not known to cause cutis marmorata. High humidity can lead to other skin issues like sweating and chafing , but it does not cause the vascular changes seen in cutis marmorata.
• Educational Objective:
• Cutis marmorata is a skin condition characterized by a marbled appearance of the skin, resulting from exposure to cold temperatures due to the vascular response of constriction and dilation .
explanation.ref: Rook's Textbook of Dermatology 9th Edition Chapter 125, Page No 125.7

subject: Dermatology
question:
A School-Going Child who had Applied Henna comes to Dermatology OPD with the following lesions. What is the Diagnosis?

[Image: https://cerebellum-web-static.s3.amazonaws.com/media/public/images/2024/02/23/04.jpg]

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A : Contact dermatitis –
• Chemicals in Henna may cause this condition. Papulovesiculation is present. Normally, henna is not applied to the face as in this instance.
• Option C: Polymorphic light eruption (PLE) :
• Lesions will appear on the hands, face, and neck as part of a polymorphic light eruption , but they will be small , itchy papules or Papulovesicles on an erythematous background . They will also be monomorphic (unlike as the name suggests).
• It presents as Depigmented / hypopigmented areas, but not as hyperpigmentation as shown in the image.
• Educational Objective:
• Dermatitis Artefacta is a condition where self-inflicted skin lesions are produced intentionally by the patient , often for psychological reasons . It is important to recognize and understand this condition for appropriate management .
explanation.ref: Online resource : https://ijdvl.com/dermatitis-artefacta-a-focal-suicide/

subject: Dermatology
question:
What is the probable diagnosis of this condition affecting Hair?

[Image: https://cerebellum-web-static.s3.amazonaws.com/media/public/images/2024/02/23/05.jpg]

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A: Trichorrhexis Nodosa
• It is associated with node formation. It may be seen in arginosuccinic aciduria . Characterized by classical appearance as if two paint brushes are rubbing against each other described as Thrust Paintbrush appearance.
• Option B: Trichorrhexis invaginata:
• Also known as Bamboo hair having Golf tee or Ball & Socket appearance, in which the distal part of the hair shaft is compressed against the dilated and cupped proximal shaft. Seen in Netherton’s syndrome.
• Option D: Monilethrix: Beaded hair seen in Menkes disease.
• Educational Objective
• " Tigertail " banding of hair under polarized light microscopy is indicative of trichothiodystrophy , a condition characterized by brittle hair with reduced sulfur content and potentially associated with multi-system involvement .
• Rooks Textbook of Dermatology 9 th Edition Pages No. 65.33, 68.21, 78.10 89.53-54
explanation.ref: Fitzpatrick’s Dermatology 9 th Edition Page No. 1537

subject: Dermatology
question: FALSE about Trichorrhexis Invaginata among the following options?
options: Abnormal Keratinisation in Distal Hair Shaft

explanation.short: Ans. D) Abnormal Keratinisation in Distal Hair Shaft
explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Trichorrhexis Invaginata (“Bamboo Hair”)
• It is associated with Netherton syndrome (Option B), an autosomal recessive disorder caused by SPINK5 gen e mutations. Netherton syndrome presents with a triad of ichthyosis linearis circumflexa, atopy, and hair abnormalities , including Trichorrhexis invaginata (TI) and trichorrhexis nodosa (TN). Trichorrhexis invaginata is indeed associated with bamboo hair (option A), which is a characteristic feature where the hair appears weak and constricted at regular intervals , resembling a bamboo stalk . Ball and socket hair deformity (Option C) is another feature seen in TI, where the distal end of the hair shaft appears as if it has a ball- and -socket joint .
• Hair abnormality arises in infancy , presents with short , sparse , and very fragile hair .
• Educational Objective:
• Trichorrhexis Invaginata (TI), often referred to as "Bamboo Hair ," is associated with Abnormal Keratinization in the proximal Hair Shaft and is a characteristic feature of Netherton Syndrome . Understanding this condition is important for recognizing Hair Abnormalities in patients with Netherton Syndrome .
explanation.ref: Rook's Textbook of Dermatology 9th Edition Chapter 89, Page No 89.53

subject: Dermatology
question:
A child with fever had multiple skin lesions on face and upper extremities as shown in the below image, on microscopic examination the skin lesions are seen to have neutrophilic and histiocytic infiltration in the dermis. What is the diagnosis?

[Image: https://cerebellum-web-static.s3.amazonaws.com/media/public/images/2024/02/23/10.jpg]

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option B. Behcet's syndrome
• It is characterized by recurrent oral and genital ulcers , uveitis , and skin lesions, but it does not typically present with neutrophilic and histiocytic infiltration in the dermis.
• Option C. Pyoderma gangrenosum
• It is characterized by rapidly progressing skin ulcers with a purple border and is often associated with underlying systemic diseases, which is different from the described case.
• Educational Objective:
• Sweet syndrome, also known as acute febrile neutrophilic dermatosis , presents with fever and painful skin lesions showing neutrophilic and histiocytic infiltration in the dermis .
explanation.ref: Rook's Textbook of Dermatology 9th Edition, Chapter 148 Page No 148.6

subject: Dermatology
question:
W hich of the following drugs can cause Aplasia Cutis?

[Image: https://cerebellum-web-static.s3.amazonaws.com/media/public/images/2024/02/23/11.jpg]

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Aplasia Cutis : Congenital absence of skin , with or without the absence of underlying structures such as bone . It most commonly affects the scalp , just lateral to the midline (70%) .
• Lesions are usually non-inflammatory , well-demarcated and have a variable appearance depending on the size (0.5–10 cm), extent , depth and degree of healing and scarring . The various causative pathophysiological mechanisms include genetic factors, intrauterine trauma , fetus papyraceus , intrauterine infections by varicellaor herpes viruses , drugs including Methimazole, Carbimazole and Misoprostol, feto-fetal transfusion , early rupture of amniotic membrane forming amniotic bands and abnormal elastic fiber biomechanical forces.
• Educational Objective:
• Aplasia cutis : Congenital absence of skin , with or without the absence of underlying structures such as bone . Methimazole, Carbimazole and Misoprostol are important drugs causing Aplasia Cutis.
• Rook's Textbook of Dermatology 9th Edition Chapter 75 Page No 75.19
• Bolognia Textbook of Dermatology 4 th Edition Chapter: 64 Page Number 1226
explanation.ref: Rook's Textbook of Dermatology 9th Edition Chapter 75 Page No 75.19

subject: Dermatology
question: Which of the following disease has a Blaschkoid Pattern of Cutaneous Lesions?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Incontinentia pigmenti has a Blaschkoid pattern of cutaneous lesions Herpes zoster has a Dermatomal / Zosteriform distribution. In Herpes labialis & genitalis , which are produced by Herpes simplex viruses , grouped vesicles can be seen near lips & genitalia respectively.
• Incontinentia pigmenti (IP) is a rare X‐linked dominant multisystemic ectodermal dysplasia that is usually lethal in males and that presents classically in females with skin lesions , teeth abnormalities, alopecia , nail dystrophy , and ocular and neurological findings. Skin changes have 4 stages as described below:
• 1. Bullous stage I Erythematous vesicular rash following Blaschko’s lines.
• 2.  Verrucous stage II , occurring mainly on the limbs
• 3. Stage III hyperpigmented streaks and whorls along Blaschko’s lines begin within months and fade in adolescence.
• 4. Stage IV patients have pale, hairless, atrophic linear streaks or patches Hypopigmentary changes mostly on the lower extremities.
• Educational Objective:
• Incontinentia pigmenti is characterized by a Blaschkoid pattern of cutaneous lesions, which is a distinctive feature of this rare genetic disorder .
• Bolognia Textbook of Dermatology 4 th Edition Chapter: 62 Page Number 1170
explanation.ref: Rook's Textbook of Dermatology 9th Edition Chapter 70 Page No 70.11

subject: Dermatology
question: A boy with Small Sized Platelets and a Platelet Count of 20,000 presents with Petechiae, Melena & Atopic Dermatitis. What is the next step in diagnosis?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A . Antiplatelet Antibodies:
• These are typically assessed when immune thrombocytopenia (ITP) is suspected. In ITP, the immune system mistakenly attacks and destroys platelets. However, the presence of eczema along with thrombocytopenia and gastrointestinal bleeding ( melena ) is more suggestive of WAS than ITP .
• Option B . Bone Marrow Examination:
• This test is important when there is suspicion of bone marrow failure or infiltration by malignant cells , which might be causing the low platelet count . Given the patient's symptoms, bone marrow failure is less likely, and other signs of marrow failure (such as anemia and leukopenia ) are typically also present.
• Option C . Electron Microscopy of Platelets:
• This is a more specialized diagnostic test and is not routinely used in the initial evaluation of thrombocytopenia. It might be considered in certain rare platelet disorders where the platelet structure might be altered , but it is not the first-line investigation in this clinical scenario.
• Educational Objective:
• In a male patient with thrombocytopenia , small-sized platelets , eczema, and bleeding manifestations , the most likely diagnosis is Wiskott-Aldrich Syndrome, and the most definitive next step in diagnosis is to check for mutations in the WASP gene.
• Rook's Textbook of Dermatology -9th Edition Page No 147.13
• Bolognia Textbook of Dermatology 4 th Edition Page No 981
explanation.ref: Rook's Textbook of Dermatology -9th Edition Page No 147.13

subject: Dermatology
question:
A farmer presented to the outpatient department with a non-tender, non-mobile, non-compressible firm swelling over his palm as shown in the image. A few months back he got injured with a thorn. What is the presentation given below?

[Image: https://cerebellum-web-static.s3.amazonaws.com/media/public/images/2024/02/23/17.jpg]

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Description : It is a benign tumor originating from odontogenic epithelium , primarily involved in tooth development . Why it is not the answer : Ameloblastoma typically manifests in the jaw , especially the mandible as shown in the image below.
• The presentation in the palm after a thorn injury is inconsistent with this diagnosis .
• Option B. Embryogenic Dermoid
• Description : These cysts arise from trapped pluripotent cells during fetal development . Why it is not the answer : Embryogenic dermoids are congenital and are frequently found at fusion lines such as the midline of the body. Their occurrence on the palm and their association with a traumatic event (like a thorn prick ) is not typical.
• Option C. Sequestration Dermoid
• Description : Formed due to sequestration (separation) of ectodermal tissue during embryonic development. Why it is not the answer : Like embryogenic dermoids, sequestration dermoids are commonly found in the midline, and their formation isn't associated with trauma or injury.
• Educational Objective:
• The most likely diagnosis for a farmer presenting with a non-tender, non-mobile, non-compressible firm swelling on the palm following a puncture wound from a thorn is an implantation dermoid cyst (D). These cysts are acquired through the traumatic implantation of epidermal elements into the dermis , which is consistent with the patient's history and clinical presentation.

subject: Dermatology
question:
A 4-year-old girl is brought to the clinic by her parents due to skin lesions that have been present since birth. The lesions initially appeared as blisters on her trunk and limbs as shown below, then evolved into wart-like rashes that followed a linear pattern. Currently, they have a swirled hyperpigmentation. The child's growth and development have been otherwise normal. Examination reveals that the teeth are small and conical in shape. The parents are concerned about the progression and the appearance of the skin lesions. Which of the following statement is INCORRECT about this disease?

[Image: https://cerebellum-web-static.s3.amazonaws.com/media/public/images/2024/02/23/19.jpg]
options: It is more common in Girls

explanation.short: Ans. D) Peg-Shaped Teeth are seen
explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. It is more common in girls :
• Incontinentia pigmenti (IP) is indeed more common in girls due to its X-linked dominant inheritance pattern, which is often lethal in utero for males , leading to a predominance of the condition in females .
• Option B. Lesions follow Blaschko's lines :
• In IP, the skin lesions typically follow Blaschko's lines, which are patterns of skin development that are invisible except when revealed by certain skin conditions .
• Option C. Mutation in IKBKG gene :
• IP is caused by mutations in the IKBKG gene (also known as NEMO ), which is involved in the regulation of immune responses and protection against cell death .
• Educational Objective:
