• Peg-shaped teeth are an associated feature of Congenital syphilis, NOT I ncontinentia pigmenti .
explanation.ref: Rooks Textbook of Dermatology- 9 th Edition Page No 70.11

subject: Dermatology
question: A 6-month-old boy presents with hypotonia, failure to thrive, and peculiar brittle, kinky hair. Laboratory tests show low serum copper and ceruloplasmin levels. Genetic analysis is most likely to reveal a mutation in which of the following?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A) The HFE gene
• This is associated with hereditary hemochromatosis, a condition characterized by iron overload, not copper deficiency.
• Option B) Mutations in the alpha-synuclein gene
• These are associated with certain types of Parkinson's disease , not Menkes disease.
• Option D) The ATP7B gene mutation
• This causes Wilson's disease, which also involves copper metabolism but leads to copper accumulation , not deficiency, and presents later in childhood or adolescence with hepatic and neurological symptoms.
• Educational Objective:
• Menke's disease is caused by mutations in the ATP7A gene , leading to copper transport defects, and is characterized by hypotonia, developmental delay, and distinctive brittle, kinky hair.
explanation.ref: Bolognia Textbook of Dermatology 4 th Edition Page No 937

subject: Dermatology
question:
Identify the condition shown in the image below?

[Image: https://cerebellum-web-static.s3.amazonaws.com/media/public/images/2024/02/23/21.jpg]

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A . Paget Disease:
• Usually manifests as red , scaly , and crusted patches on the vulvar skin as shown in the image below. It is often associated with an underlying malignancy . It does not present with white atrophic papules as shown in the question.
• Option C. Lichen Planus :
• Presents with purplish , flat - topped , itchy papules and plaques , often with a reticular pattern on mucous membranes , not typically white atrophic papules .
• Option D. Angioma of Vulva :
• This would present as a benign growth of small blood vessels and would be red and vascular in appearance as shown below.
• Educational Objective
• When encountering white atrophic papules and plaques on the vulva that may coalesce and distort the vulvar anatomy, lichen sclerosus should be considered as a primary diagnosis .
• William’s Gynecology 4 th Edition Page No 92-100, 688
• Shaws Textbook of Gynecology, 17 th Edition Page No 323, 473-475
explanation.ref: William’s Gynecology 4 th Edition Page No 92-100, 688

subject: Dermatology
question: A 50-year-old patient presents with a painful, necrotic ulcer on the lower leg. The ulcer has irregular, undermined borders and is associated with inflammatory pustules and violaceous hue at the periphery. There is no evidence of vascular compromise. What is the most likely diagnosis?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. Bechet's Disease
• It is characterized by recurrent oral and genital ulcers , uveitis , and skin lesions . While it can involve skin ulcers , the presentation is different from the rapidly progressing ulcers of pyoderma gangrenosum.
• Option B. Arterial Ulcers
• They are associated with arterial insufficiency and present with well-defined , punched-out ulcers , not the painful, rapidly progressing ulcers seen in this case.
• Option D. Venous Ulcers
• They typically present as painful, necrotic ulcers on the lower leg with irregular, undermined borders , but they are not associated with a purple border or neutrophilic infiltrate.
• Educational Objective:
• Pyoderma gangrenosum is characterized by painful, rapidly progressing skin ulcers with a purple border , often associated with underlying systemic diseases such as inflammatory bowel disease . Histologically, it shows a neutrophilic infiltrate .
• Bolognia Textbook of Dermatology 4 th Edition Page No 148.7
explanation.ref: Bolognia Textbook of Dermatology 4 th Edition Page No 148.7

subject: Dermatology
question: Cerebriform appearance of T-lymphocytes is classically seen in which of the following malignancies?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A . Pseudolymphoma:
• This term is used to describe a group of benign disorders that mimic lymphomas clinically and histologically but are not true lymphomas. T-lymphocytes in pseudolymphoma do not typically show cerebriform nuclei.
• Option C . Adult T Cell Leukaemia:
• This is a type of leukemia caused by the human T-cell lymphotropic virus ( HTLV-1 ). Although it involves T-lymphocytes , the cells do not have the classic cerebriform nuclei seen in Mycosis Fungoides.
• Option D . Chronic Myeloid Leukaemia:
• This is a type of blood cancer that typically affects the myeloid lineage of white blood cells and does not involve T-lymphocytes with cerebriform nuclei.
• Educational Objective:
• The Cerebriform appearance of T-lymphocytes is a characteristic feature of Mycosis fungoides , a form of cutaneous T-cell lymphoma .
• Rook's Textbook of Dermatology 9th Edition, Chapter 140, Page No 140.1 – 140.20
explanation.ref: Rook's Textbook of Dermatology 9th Edition, Chapter 140, Page No 140.1 – 140.20

subject: Dermatology
question: A 60-year-old male presented with a cancerous growth on his face. A diagnosis of Basal Cell Carcinoma is made based on Biopsy findings which showed characteristic peripheral palisading of tumour cells. Which of the following statements is INCORRECT regarding this condition?
options: Nodular Type is the commonest clinical presentation in this condition.
PUVA Therapy is a risk factor for this condition.
Early Diagnosis in Pre-Malignant Stage is crucial for the prognosis of this condition.

explanation.short: Ans. C) Early Diagnosis in Pre-Malignant Stage is crucial for the prognosis of this condition.
explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Clinical features : Presents as Pearly translucent nodule with surface telangiectasia , undergoes central necrosis resulting in rodent ulcer with a rolled-out margin . Nodular type is the commonest clinical type of basal cell carcinoma. Below image depicts the Nodular Basal Cell Carcinoma ( Option A )
• Metastasis in basal cell carcinoma is extremely rare . The typical basal cell carcinoma develops slowly and extends to the periphery. ( Option D )
• The following are the risk factors for basal cell carcinoma:
• ﻿﻿Solar Radiation ﻿﻿ Psoralen-UVA Therapy (PUVA) (Option B) ﻿﻿ Human Papilloma Virus infections ﻿﻿latrogenic Infections ﻿﻿Surgical Scar ﻿﻿UV-B Rays ﻿﻿lonising Radiation ﻿﻿Photosensitising Drugs ﻿﻿Previous history of Basal Cell Carcinoma
• Histopathology : The tumour cells resemble cells of stratum basale that have grown into the dermis & shows a characteristic peripheral palisading of tumour cells
• Educational Objective:
• Basal Cell Carcinoma (BCC) is a locally invasive skin cancer that rarely metastasizes . Early diagnosis and treatment are crucial to prevent local tissue damage and disfigurement caused by the tumor's growth.
• Rook's Textbook of Dermatology - 9th Edition - Volume IV Chapter 141, Page no 141.1 – 141.16
explanation.ref: Rook's Textbook of Dermatology - 9th Edition - Volume IV Chapter 141, Page no 141.1 – 141.16

subject: Dermatology
question: Identify the INCORRECT statement among the following options?
options: Keratoacanthoma is a Premalignant Lesion of Squamous Cell Carcinoma
Actinic Keratosis has Low Risk of progression to Squamous Cell Carcinoma
Bowen's Disease has Low Potential for Invasive Malignancy
Verrucous Carcinoma is a Well-Differentiated Squamous Cell Carcinoma.

explanation.short: Ans. A) Keratoacanthoma is a Premalignant Lesion of Squamous Cell Carcinoma
explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Actinic keratoses are hyperkeratotic lesions that develop on adult skin that has been exposed to UV rays over an extended period of time and have a low risk of developing into invasive squamous cell carcinoma.
• Bowen's disease is a slow growing papulosquamous lesion which has a low potential for invasive malignancy . Image below depicts Bowens disease lesion.
• It usually grows progressively but sometimes undergoes spontaneous partial resolution . It can occur anywhere , but it occurs more in the head and neck region .
• Verrucous carcinoma is a well-differentiated squamous cell carcinoma. It is slow-growing, and it rarely metastasizes .
• Educational Objective:
• Keratoacanthoma is often considered a low-grade variant of SCC rather than a premalignant lesion, actinic keratosis has a low but significant risk of progression to SCC, Bowen's disease is an early form of SCC with low but present risk of invasion, and verrucous carcinoma is a well-differentiated form of SCC with low metastatic potential but potential for local aggression.
explanation.ref: Rook's Textbook of Dermatology 9th Edition, Chapter 142, Page Nos 142.1-142.28

subject: Dermatology
question:
What long-term complication is most likely to occur in this patient having this lesion since birth:

[Image: https://cerebellum-web-static.s3.amazonaws.com/media/public/images/2024/02/22/05.jpg]

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• This is characterized by clouding of the lens within the eye, leading to a decrease in vision . It is not related to skin lesions such as a congenital melanocytic nevus.
• Sturge-Weber Syndrome (SWS) often leads to secondary glaucoma due to vascular malformations affecting eye drainage , with facial involvement increasing the risk . SWS presents clinically as Port wine stain.
• Features: Also known as nevus flammeus , it is a capillary malformation that presents as a flat , pink , red , or purplish mark . It remains unchanged throughout life and can be anywhere on the body but is often seen on the face as shown below in the image.
• Managing SWS -related glaucoma requires a multidisciplinary approach and regular eye exams for early detection and vision preservation .
• Option D. Basal cell carcinoma:
• This is the most common form of skin cancer, often associated with long-term sun exposure. It typically appears as a raised , pearly bump and does not usually develop from congenital melanocytic nevi.
• Educational Objective:
• Congenital melanocytic nevi , especially larger ones, carry an increased risk for the development of malignant melanoma . Monitoring and possibly biopsy of suspicious changes within these nevi are important for early detection of malignant transformation .
explanation.ref: Rooks Textbook of Dermatology 9 th Edition Page Number 75.9 to 75.14 73.2, 73.3, 143.2, 143.16

subject: Dermatology
question:
A 33-year-old truck driver who is a HIV positive, presents with the following lesion on his palate as shown in the below image, which has progressed in size from the time he first noticed them . What is your diagnosis?

[Image: https://cerebellum-web-static.s3.amazonaws.com/media/public/images/2024/02/22/08.jpg]

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. Squamous Cell Carcinoma –
• Squamous Cell Carcinoma (SCC) in the oral cavity often presents as a non-healing ulcer or mass with keratinizatio n. It is associated with risk factors like tobacco and alcohol use . This patient's HIV-positive status and the appearance of the lesions are less typical for SCC, which tends to be more localized and less vascular in appearance .
• Option C. Basal Cell Carcinoma –
• Basal cell carcinoma (BCC) is the most common skin cancer , characterized by pearly papules with telangiectasia and a propensity for local invasion. It is extremely rare in the oral cavity because it usually occurs in areas of the skin that are exposed to the sun . The lesion in the image is not consistent with the typical presentation of BCC.
• Option D. Melanoma –
• Melanoma in the oral cavity is rare and would typically present as a rapidly expanding, darkly pigmented lesion. The lesion in the image lacks the dark pigmentation that is commonly seen with melanoma . Additionally, melanoma tends to be more aggressive and less likely to present as multiple lesions in early stages.
• Educational Objective:
• In HIV-positive patients, a palatal lesion that is vascular in appearance and has progressed in size is most consistent with Kaposi sarcoma , particularly in the context of immune suppression and the absence of typical features of other listed conditions.
• Rook's Textbook of Dermatology - 9th Edition - Volume IV Chapter 139 Page No 139.1
explanation.ref: Rook's Textbook of Dermatology - 9th Edition - Volume IV Chapter 139 Page No 139.1

subject: Dermatology
question:
A 50-year-old male presented with sudden eruption of asymptomatic lesions as shown in the picture below. The lesions had a Stuck-on appearance suggestive of a diagnosis of Seborrheic keratosis. Which of the following malignancy should we suspect given that there was a sudden eruption of these lesions over body?

[Image: https://cerebellum-web-static.s3.amazonaws.com/media/public/images/2024/02/22/09.jpg]

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• While Leser-Trélat can be associated with various internal malignancies , gastric adenocarcinoma is the classic association . Intestinal cancers can sometimes present with this sign , but they are not the most commonly associated malignancy.
• Lung cancer can be associated with various paraneoplastic syndromes , but Leser-Trélat is more classically linked with gastric adenocarcinoma .
• There is no established association between cardiac malignancies and the sign of Leser-Trélat. Heart cancers are extremely rare and typically do not present with dermatologic paraneoplastic syndromes.
• Educational Objective:
• The sudden appearance of multiple seborrheic keratosis lesions, known as the sign of Leser-Trélat , is suggestive of an underlying malignancy , most classically gastric adenocarcinoma . This sign should prompt a thorough i nvestigation for Internal Malignancy.
explanation.ref: Rooks Textbook of Dermatology- 9 th Edition Page No 133.2

subject: Dermatology
question:
All of the following statements are true regarding the condition shown in the image EXCEPT?

[Image: https://cerebellum-web-static.s3.amazonaws.com/media/public/images/2024/02/22/10.jpg]
options: Can be associated with Insulin Resistant Diabetes Mellitus
Can be an indication of underlying Gastric Adenocarcinoma
Lesions are described as having Velvety Texture with Hyperpigmented and Coarse Skin
Oral Contraceptive Pills intake is the most common cause

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. Can be associated with Insulin resistant diabetes mellitus :
• The condition shown in the image, Acanthosis nigricans , is often associated with insulin resistance , and consequently, it can be a marker for diabetes mellitus , especially Type 2 diabetes .
• Option B. Can be an indication of underlying Gastric adenocarcinoma :
• While less common, Acanthosis nigricans can be a paraneoplastic syndrome associated with internal malignancies , such as gastric adenocarcinoma .
• Option C. Lesions are described as having velvety texture with hyperpigmented and coarse skin :
• The hallmark of Acanthosis nigricans is hyperpigmented, coarse, and velvety plaques , typically found in body folds such as the neck and axillae .
• Educational Objective:
• Acanthosis nigricans is a dermatologic condition that is primarily associated with insulin resistance and obesity . It can also be a sign of underlying malignancy but is not most commonly caused by oral contraceptive pills. The characteristic lesions have a velvety texture and are typically hyperpigmented .
explanation.ref: Rooks Textbook of Dermatology- 9 th Edition Page No 147

subject: Dermatology
question:
Based on the Image, a 60-year-old male patient with Diabetes presents with Progressive Skin Lesions. What could be the likely diagnosis?

[Image: https://cerebellum-web-static.s3.amazonaws.com/media/public/images/2024/02/22/11.jpg]

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. Squamous cell carcinoma (SCC)
• It typically presents as a firm, red nodule or a flat sore with a scaly crust as seen in the image below. While it's possible for diabetic patients to develop SCC, the description provided fits BCC more closely.
• It is characterized by brown to gray-brown patches as seen in the image below.
• It is usually seen on the face. It is more common in women , especially during pregnancy, and is not typically associated with progressive lesions.
• Option D. Discoid lupus erythematosus
• It often presents as a chronic rash with red, inflamed, and scaly patches that can cause scarring , usually on the face, ears, and scalp , as seen in the image below.
• It is an autoimmune condition and not directly linked to diabetes.
• Educational Objective:
• In an elderly patient , progressive pearly or waxy skin lesions, especially if located on sun-exposed areas, are likely indicative of Basal Cell Carcinoma .
• Robbins Basic Pathology 10 th Edition Page No 1148
• Bailey & Loves Short Practice of Surgery 27 th Edition Page No 599, 604-6011
explanation.ref: Rook’s Textbook of Dermatology -9th Edition Page No 4.15, 51.3, 88.10-12, 141.1-16

subject: Dermatology
question: Edges of a Basal Cell Carcinoma Lesion are described as?

explanation.detail:
• Educational Objective:
• Recognize that Basal Cell Carcinoma is characterized by pearly rolled edges, which are indicative of the slow-growing nature of the tumour, along with the presence of telangiectasia . Top of Form
explanation.ref: Rooks Textbook of Dermatology- 9 th Edition Page No 141.8

subject: Dermatology
question: A 50-year-old patient presents with a chronic, pruritic rash composed of erythematous patches and plaques. Histological examination of a skin biopsy shows clusters of atypical lymphocytes within intraepidermal abscesses. What is this histopathological finding known as?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A) Munro's microabscesses
• These are collections of neutrophils within the stratum corneum and are typically found in psoriasis, not mycosis fungoides.
• Option B) Spongiform Pustules of Kogoj
• These are also associated with psoriasis and represent a spongiform collection of neutrophils in the epidermis.
• Option D) Papillary Tip Microabscesses
• These are typically seen in dermatitis herpetiformis and consist of neutrophils at the tips of dermal papillae, usually accompanied by granular deposits of IgA.
• Educational Objective:
• Pautrier's microabscesses are a hallmark histopathological feature of mycosis fungoides, which are characterized by clusters of atypical lymphocytes within the epidermis.
• Rooks Textbook of Dermatology- 9 th Edition: page no 140.7
explanation.ref: Rooks Textbook of Dermatology- 9 th Edition: page no 140.7

subject: Dermatology
question:
A Female patient presented with a lesion as shown in the image below. What is the most likely diagnosis?

[Image: https://cerebellum-web-static.s3.amazonaws.com/media/public/images/2024/02/22/22.jpg]

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A . Squamous Cell Carcinoma
• It typically presents as a crusted or scaly area of skin with a red, inflamed base , which may bleed easily .
• It can also develop from a pre-existing actinic keratosis and is less likely to show the diverse pigmentation seen in the image.
• Option C. Basal Cell Carcinoma
• It often appears as a pearly or waxy bump , sometimes with visible blood vessels , mostly on sun-exposed areas of the body.
• It does not typically present with the same level of pigmentation variation as seen in the image.
• Option D. Seborrheic Keratosis
• This is a benign skin growth that usually presents as a brown, black, or light tan growth on the face, chest, shoulders, or back . The surface appears waxy , and it often looks like it is stuck onto the skin rather than being part of it. It does not typically have the irregular borders or color variations associated with melanoma.
• Educational Objective:
• Melanoma can be identified using the ABCDE criteria , which describe the asymmetry, border irregularity, colour variation, diameter, and evolving nature of pigmented lesions.
• Rooks’ Textbook of Dermatology- 9th Edition Page No 4.20-4.21, 144.1-144.5
• Robbins & Cotran Pathologic Basis of Disease, 9 th Edition Page No 1147-1150
• Schwartz’s Principles of Surgery 10 th Edition Page No 488
explanation.ref: Bolognia Dermatology Textbook 4 th Edition Page No 1991

subject: Dermatology
question: In the staging of malignant melanoma, which measurement is considered superior for determining the prognosis due to its higher accuracy in predicting outcomes?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A) Clark's level
• This categorizes the depth of melanoma into five levels based on anatomic landmarks.
• While it provides useful prognostic information, it is not as precise as Breslow thickness and can be subjective.
• Option B) Horizontal spread of melanoma
• This can indicate the potential for local tissue destruction but does not directly correlate with the risk of metastasis and survival as accurately as Breslow thickness.
• Option D) The presence of ulceration
• This is an important prognostic factor in melanoma but does not provide a direct measurement of tumor depth like Breslow thickness does.
• Educational Objective:
• Breslow thickness is a key prognostic indicator in the staging of malignant melanoma , as it provides a more accurate prediction of metastatic potential and patient survival than Clark's level of invasion.
• Bolognia Dermatology Textbook 4 th Edition Page No 1991
• Rooks’ Textbook of Dermatology- 9th Edition Page No 4.20-4.21, 144.1-144.5
• Robbins & Cotran Pathologic Basis of Disease, 9 th Edition Page No 1147-1150
• Schwartz’s Principles of Surgery 10 th Edition Page No 488
explanation.ref: Bolognia Dermatology Textbook 4 th Edition Page No 1991

subject: Dermatology
question: A 60-year-old male presents with erythroderma, generalized lymphadenopathy, and atypical circulating T-cells with cerebriform nuclei. A skin biopsy shows infiltration by neoplastic cells in the epidermis forming Pautriersmicro abscesses. Flow cytometry of peripheral blood reveals a CD4 to CD8 ratio greater than 10:1 with CD4+ T-cells exhibiting loss of CD7 expression. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. Glucagonoma Syndrome :
• Glucagonoma syndrome is associated with a glucagon-secreting pancreatic tumor and presents with necrolytic migratory erythema , weight loss , and diabetes , not with the features described in the question.
• Necrolytic migratory erythema depicted in the above image
• Option B. Mycosis Fungoides:
• Mycosis fungoides is the most common form of cutaneous T-cell lymphoma and can present with similar skin findings but is typically limited to the skin without the prominent circulating atypical T-cells found in Sézary syndrome .
• Option C. Basal Cell Carcinoma (BCC) :
• BCC is the most common type of skin cancer and presents with a localized lesion with a pearly rolled border and telangiectasia, not with erythroderma or circulating atypical T-cells.
• Educational Objective:
• Sézary syndrome is a leukemic variant of cutaneous T-cell lymphoma presenting with erythroderma, generalized lymphadenopathy, and the presence of atypical T-cells with cerebriform nuclei in the peripheral blood and skin.
explanation.ref: Rooks’ Textbook of Dermatology- 9 th Edition Page No 140.18

subject: Dermatology
question: Sezary Syndrome presents with all of the following EXCEPT?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation
• It is an aggressive form of cutaneous T-cell lymphoma characterized by erythroderma, lymphadenopathy , and the presence of malignant T-cells in the skin . Sézary syndrome typically presents with intense pruritus (Itching ) rather than Sensory Loss.
• Sezary Cells : Atypical circulating T-cells with cerebriform nuclei
• Sezary Syndrome → Triad: (SLE)
• Circulating Sezary cells (atypical mononuclear cells) >20% of Total Lymphocyte Count or 1000 X 109/L Generalized Lymphadenopathy (due to lymphoid infiltration) Erythroderma (4th stage of mycosis fungoides)
• Educational Objective:
• Sézary syndrome is characterized by the presence of Sezary cells , lymphadenopathy , and erythroderma , but it does not present with hypoanaesthetic skin patches , which are more characteristic of leprosy.
explanation.ref: Rooks Textbook of Dermatology- 9 th Edition Page No 140.18

subject: Dermatology
question: Which of the following is NOT a cutaneous marker of underlying internal malignancy?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. Necrolytic migratory erythema :
• Necrolytic migratory erythema is often associated with glucagonoma , which is a type of pancreatic islet cell tumor, making it a marker of underlying internal malignancy .
• Option B. Acanthosis nigricans :
• Acanthosis nigricans can be a cutaneous marker of internal malignancies , most commonly gastric adenocarcinoma .
• Erythroderma can be associated with various internal conditions, including lymphoma and leukemia .
• Educational Objective:
• Erythema chronicum migrans is not a cutaneous marker of internal malignancy but is associated with Lyme disease. In contrast, necrolytic migratory erythema , acanthosis nigricans , and erythroderma can all be cutaneous markers of various internal malignancies .
explanation.ref: Rooks Textbook of Dermatology- 9 th Edition Page No 147.1

subject: Dermatology
question: All of the following are risk factors for a pigmented lesion to progress to Malignant Melanoma EXCEPT?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. Asymmetry of shape of lesion :
• Asymmetry is a risk factor for melanoma , as benign moles are generally symmetrical .
• Option B. Irregular, scalloped border :
• Moles with irregular , scalloped , or poorly defined borders are more likely to be malignant melanomas than those with smooth , even borders .
• Option C. Variegation of colour :
• Multiple colors or an uneven distribution of color can be a warning sign of malignant melanoma .
• Educational Objective:
• While asymmetry , irregular borders , and variegated colors are risk factors for the progression of pigmented lesions to malignant melanoma , a diameter greater than 4 mm is not the classic criterion for risk; rather, it is a diameter greater than 6 mm that is concerning .
explanation.ref: Rook's Textbook of Dermatology - 9th Edition - Volume IV, Chapter 143 Page Nos 143.2, 143.16

subject: Dermatology
question: Identify the INCORRECT statement related to basal cell carcinoma
options: Moh's Microscopic Surgery gives best results
Relatively Early Nodal involvement is classically seen
Noduloulcerative variant is the most common type of BCC

explanation.short: Ans. C) Relatively Early Nodal involvement is classically seen
explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. They appear as "translucent" or "pearly" papules :
• Basal cell carcinomas (BCC) often appear as translucent or pearly papules , which may have telangiectatic vessels on the surface.
• Option B. Moh's microscopic surgery gives best results :
• Mohs micrographic surgery is considered the treatment of choice for BCC in critical areas because it offers the highest cure rate by allowing for the assessment of 100% of the peripheral margins .
• Option D. Noduloulcerative variant is the most common type of BCC :
• The noduloulcerative or nodular type is indeed the most common variant of basal cell carcinoma, often presenting as a pearly nodule with a central ulceration or depression , sometimes referred to as a " rodent ulcer."
• Educational Objective:
• Basal cell carcinoma typically presents as a translucent or pearly papule , is best treated with Mohs micrographic surgery , and the noduloulcerative type is the most common . Unlike other skin cancers, BCC rarely metastasizes and does not usually involve lymph nodes early in its course.
explanation.ref: Rooks Textbook of Dermatology- 9 th Edition Page No 141.8

subject: Dermatology
question: Malignant Transformation to Melanoma is commonly seen in?

explanation.short: Ans. C) Large Congenital Melanocytic Nevus
explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. Dermal nevus :
• Dermal nevi are typically benign and less likely to transform into melanoma compared to congenital melanocytic nevi .
• Option B. Compound nevus :
• Compound nevi have Nevus cells in both the epidermis and dermis . They have a slight risk of malignant transformation , but it is not as high as in large congenital melanocytic nevi.
• Option D. Beckers nevus :
• Becker's nevus is a benign cutaneous hamartoma that is usually not associated with malignant transformation.
• Educational Objective:
• The large congenital melanocytic nevus presents a higher risk for malignant transformation to melanoma compared to other types of nevi, necessitating regular monitoring for changes suggestive of malignancy.
• Rook's Textbook of Dermatology - 9th Edition - Volume IV, Chapter 143 Page Nos 143.2, 143.16
explanation.ref: Rook's Textbook of Dermatology - 9th Edition - Volume IV, Chapter 143 Page Nos 143.2, 143.16

subject: Dermatology
question: Identify the FALSE statement among the following options?
options: BCC is the most common Skin Malignancy
SCC occurs in Sun Exposed and also over Sun protected sites including Mucosae
SCC is Most Common in Transplant Recipients

explanation.short: Ans. D) SCC has Pearly Rolled Edges
explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. BCC is the most common skin malignancy :
• Basal Cell Carcinoma (BCC) is indeed the most common form of skin cancer .
• Option B. SCC occurs in sun exposed and also over sun protected sites including mucosae :
• Squamous Cell Carcinoma (SCC) can occur on sun-exposed skin , such as the head , neck , and arms , as well as in sun-protected areas and mucosal surfaces .
• Option C. SCC is Most common in Transplant recipients :
• SCC is more common in transplant recipients due to immunosuppression , which increases the risk of various skin cancers .
• Educational Objective:
• The edges of a lesion can provide critical diagnostic clues; in the case of Basal Cell Carcinoma, "pearly rolled edges " are characteristic, whereas Squamous Cell Carcinoma typically does not present with such a feature.
explanation.ref: Rooks’ Textbook of Dermatology- 9 th Edition Page No 141.8, 142.25

subject: Dermatology
question:
A female patient with a BMI of 35 kg/m2 presents to you with a skin lesion in the Axilla as shown below. Which of the following condition is she most likely to be suffering from?

[Image: https://cerebellum-web-static.s3.amazonaws.com/media/public/images/2024/02/23/screenshot-2024-02-23-103552.jpg]

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• It is associated with Dry Coarse Skin , Weight Gain & Alopecia.
• Option C: Addison’s disease
• It is associated with hyperpigmentation of skin (but without velvety texture) which is prone to sun exposure & friction .
• It affects Kidneys & Skeletal system.
• Educational Objective:
• Patients with metabolic syndrome are at increased risk of developing skin conditions like acanthosis nigricans, which can manifest as hyperpigmented, velvety skin changes, often observed in the axilla. Recognizing such skin changes can be important for identifying underlying metabolic issues .
• Williams Gynaecology 3rd Edition Page No 391
explanation.ref: Rook's Textbook of Dermatology 9th Edition Chapter 147, Page Nos 147.14-147.15

subject: Dermatology
question:
A 40-year-old woman with History of Bilateral Hilar Lymphadenopathy & Apple Jelly Nodules on Diascopy, presents with Skin Lesions as shown in the Pic. Identify this condition?

[Image: https://cerebellum-web-static.s3.amazonaws.com/media/public/images/2023/05/31/untitled-113.jpg]

explanation.detail:
• Educational Objective:
• Lupus Pernio is a distinct cutaneous manifestation of sarcoidosis characterized by Violaceous, Indurated Plaques, Nodules, or Papules , typically affecting the N ose, Cheeks, Lips, and Ears . Recognizing these skin lesions is important for the diagnosis of sarcoidosis .
explanation.ref: Rook's Textbook of Dermatology 9th Edition Chapter 98, Page Nos 98.6-98.9

subject: Dermatology
question:
A 60-Year-Old Male Patient presents with a sudden increase in the lesions given in picture. What should be suspected in this Pt?

[Image: https://cerebellum-web-static.s3.amazonaws.com/media/public/images/2023/05/31/untitled-114.jpg]

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. Malignant Melanoma:
• Malignant melanoma typically presents as a pigmented lesion with signs of asymmetry, irregular borders, color variation, and a diameter larger than 6 mm. The sudden increase in the lesions is not a characteristic feature of melanoma.
• Varicella ( chickenpox ) presents with vesicular skin lesions and is unlikely to cause a sudden increase in seborrheic keratosis -like lesions .
• Nevi (moles) are benign skin lesions and do not typically cause a sudden increase in lesions.
• Educational Objective:
• The Leser-Trélat sign is characterized by a sudden increase in seborrheic keratosis lesions, which may be associated with an underlying visceral malignancy . It is important to recognize this sign as it can be an early indicator of an occult malignancy.
explanation.ref: Rook's Textbook of Dermatology 9th Edition Chapter 133, 147 Page Nos 133.1 & 147.16

subject: Dermatology
question: All of the following are correct statements regarding skin manifestations associated with various systemic diseases EXCEPT?
options: Binkley's Spots is another name for Diabetic Dermopathy
Pinch Purpura can be seen in primary Systemic Amyloidosis.
Xanthelasma Palpebrarum are seen associated with Type I Hyperlipoproteinemia
Pretibial Myxoedema is seen in Hyperthyroidism.

explanation.short: Ans. C) Xanthelasma Palpebrarum are seen associated with Type I Hyperlipoproteinemia
explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A: Binkley's Spots / Diabetic Shin Spots / Diabetic Dermopathy.
• Clinical Presentation: A symptomatic, oval, dull- red papules which evolve slowly on the shins, forearms, thighs, and over bony prominences, producing a superficial scale and atrophic brown scars.
• Option B : Pinch purpura is diagnostic of Primary Systemic Amyloidosis.
• Due to Fragility from amyloid deposition around cutaneous vessels, purpuric macules and ecchymoses , particularly in the periorbital area , develop with minor trauma .
• Option D: Pretibial Myxoedema is seen in hyperthyroidism.
• Pretibial Myxoedema : Presentation: Non pitting edema Anterior aspect of legs due to depositio of Glycosaminoglycans ( GAGS ).
• Educational Objective:
• Skin manifestations associated with systemic diseases is important for clinicians as these signs can often provide valuable diagnostic clues to underlying medical conditions.
explanation.ref: Rook's Textbook of Dermatology 9th Edition Chapter 58, Chapter 62, Chapter 147, Chapter 149, Page No 149.14 149.20, Page Nos 58.10-58.11, Page No 147.19, Page No 62.3

subject: Dermatology
question: All of the following are correct matches of the diseases & the nutrient deficiencies associated with them EXCEPT?

explanation.short: Ans. D) Acrodermatitis Enteropathica - Copper Deficiency
explanation.detail:
• Explanation
• Option A : Phrynoderma:
• Phrynoderma (Toad Skin) is common in Children. Phrynoderma is associated with Multiple Nutritional Deficiencies including B Complex , Riboflavin , Vitamin C , Vitamin E, Essential Fatty Acids, and Malnutrition. Phrynoderma is also associated with Vit-A deficiency. Clinical Features : It manifests as groups of papules , each one around 3-4 mm in diameter , with a central keratotic plug . The follicular distribution of the papules gives the skin a rough texture . Although the buttocks and extensor surfaces of the limbs may also be affected , the elbows and knees are the most frequently affected body parts.
• Treatment: Diet correction and administration of multivitamin preparations.
• Option B: Scurvy Cutaneous Manifestations:
• Phrynoderma Corkscrew Hairs - fractured and coiled hairs due to impaired keratin cross-links by disulfide bonds Perifollicular hemorrhage and purpura Bleeding Gums
• ‘Flaky Paint' or 'Crazy Paving' Dermatosis Peripheral edema is a consistent feature due to the associated hypoproteinaemia . Hepatomegaly → Abdominal distention .
• Educational Objective:
• Acrodermatitis enteropathica is a genetic disorder characterized by impaired zinc absorption . Understanding the correct associations between diseases and nutrient deficiencies is essential for diagnosing and managing nutritional disorders in clinical practice .
explanation.ref: Rook's Textbook of Dermatology - Volume II-9th Edition Chapter 63 Chapter 87 Page Nos 63.21-63.26 Page Nos 87.13-87.14

subject: Dermatology
question: Which of the following mineral deficiency is associated with Poor Wound Healing, Dermatitis, Diarrhoea and Alopecia?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Iron deficiency is associated with anaemia, fatigue, and pallor but is not typically linked to dermatitis, Diarrhea, or alopecia.
• Calcium deficiency can lead to issues like muscle cramps, weak bones, and dental problems, but it is not primarily associated with poor wound healing, dermatitis, diarrhea, or alopecia.
• Magnesium deficiency can cause muscle cramps, irregular heartbeat, and weakness , but it is not the primary mineral associated with poor wound healing, dermatitis, diarrhea, or alopecia.
• Educational Objective:
• Zinc deficiency is associated with poor wound healing, dermatitis, diarrhea, and alopecia . It is essential for skin health, immune function , and overall well-being , making it a critical mineral for various physiological processes.
• Bolognia Textbook of Dermatology 4 th Edition Page No 806
• Bolognia Textbook of Dermatology 4 th Edition Page No
explanation.ref: Rooks’ Textbook of Dermatology- 9 th Edition Page No 81.17

subject: Dermatology
question:
Which of the following mineral deficiency can lead to the condition shown in the image below?

[Image: https://cerebellum-web-static.s3.amazonaws.com/media/public/images/2024/02/23/screenshot-2024-02-23-105408.jpg]

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option B. Iron Deficiency:
• While iron deficiency can cause skin changes , they are not typically as severe as those seen in the image. Iron deficiency may lead to pallor and in severe cases can cause glossitis or angular cheilitis , but not the widespread lesions seen in the images.
• Option C. Calcium Deficiency :
• Deficiency of calcium might lead to tetany or convulsions , dermatitis is not a characteristic feature.
• Option D. Vitamin A Deficiency:
• This typically leads to Xerosis (Dry Skin), Bitot's Spots, and Night Blindness , not the rash seen in the images.
• Educational Objective:
• Acrodermatitis Enteropathica is caused by zinc deficiency and is characterized by Periorificial and Acral Dermatitis, Alopecia, and Diarrhea . It is important to differentiate it from other deficiencies based on clinical presentation.
• Bolognia Textbook of Dermatology 4 th Edition Page No 806
• Bolognia Textbook of Dermatology 4 th Edition Page No
explanation.ref: Rooks Textbook of Dermatology- 9 th Edition Page No 81.17

subject: Dermatology
question:
A child with a history of night blindness presented to you with the following findings over the knees. What is the most likely diagnosis?

[Image: https://cerebellum-web-static.s3.amazonaws.com/media/public/images/2024/02/23/screenshot-2024-02-23-105807.jpg]

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option B. Keratosis Pilaris
• This typically presents as small, rough bumps found on the upper arms, thighs , or cheeks.
• They are not usually associated with systemic symptoms like night blindness.
• Option C. Darier's Disease
• This is a genetic disorder characterized by greasy, warty papules and plaques , primarily in seborrheic areas , not typically associated with night blindness.
• Option D. Follicular Eczema
• This manifests as itchy, inflamed skin around the hair follicles and is not associated with night blindness.
• Educational Objective:
• The clinical presentation of phrynoderma, which is associated with vitamin A deficiency and presents with follicular hyperkeratotic papules , in a patient with a history of night blindness .Top of Form
• Bolognia Textbook of Dermatology 4 th Edition Page No 797
explanation.ref: Rooks Textbook of Dermatology- 9 th Edition Page No 63.7

subject: Dermatology
question:
A 35-year-old male presents with painful, erythematous, and vesicular lesions on the soles of his feet. He reports experiencing left ankle and knee joint pain, along with discomfort during urination. Examination reveals no evidence of rheumatoid nodules or symmetrical joint involvement. Which of the following statements is TRUE regarding his likely diagnosis?

[Image: https://cerebellum-web-static.s3.amazonaws.com/media/public/images/2024/02/23/screenshot-2024-02-23-110158.jpg]
options: The Arthritis typically Mirrors Rheumatoid Arthritis.
The most implicated Causative Organism is E. Coli.
There is an association with HLA-Cw6.
The patient may also exhibit Conjunctivitis.

explanation.short: Ans. D) The patient may also exhibit Conjunctivitis.
explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A . The arthritis seen in Reiter's Disease , or Reactive Arthritis , is typically Asymmetrical and Oligoarticular , unlike the symmetrical Polyarticular involvement seen in Rheumatoid Arthritis.
• Option B. The most commonly implicated organisms are Chlamydia trachomatis for sexually acquired cases and Campylobacter, Salmonella, Shigella, and Yersinia for enteric cases, not E. coli .
• Option C. HLA-Cw6 is more strongly associated with psoriasis . Reiter's disease has a known association with HLA-B27 .
• Educational Objective:
• The signs and symptoms of Reiter's disease, which include the classic triad of Arthritis, Urethritis, and Conjunctivitis , and understand its association with infections and HLA-B27 , not HLA-Cw6.
• Bolognia Textbook of Dermatology 4 th Edition Page No 148
explanation.ref: Rooks Textbook of Dermatology- 9 th Edition Page No 154.2

subject: Dermatology
question:
A 50-year-old male who is a known case of diabetes, presents with lesion as shown in the picture below. What is the diagnosis in this patient?

[Image: https://cerebellum-web-static.s3.amazonaws.com/media/public/images/2024/02/23/screenshot-2024-02-23-110619.jpg]

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option B. Granuloma Annulare :
• Granuloma annulare is a different skin condition characterized by raised, reddish or skin-colored bumps arranged in a ring or arc shape . It is not typically associated with diabetes.
• Option C. Necrobiosis Lipoidica :
• Necrobiosis lipoidica is another skin condition that appears as reddish-brown patches with a shiny, atrophic surface . It is more commonly associated with diabetes but typically presents differently than the lesions in the picture.
• Option D. Pretibial Myxoedema :
• Pretibial myxoedema is a skin condition associated with hyperthyroidism , not diabetes.
• Educational Objective:
• Diabetic Dermopathy is a common skin finding in individuals with diabetes, characterized by multiple brown macules and patches , often with atrophy . Recognizing diabetic dermopathy can serve as a clinical marker for diabetes -related complications such as Retinopathy, Nephropathy, and Neuropathy .
explanation.ref: Rooks Textbook of Dermatology- 9 th Edition Page No 64.2

subject: Dermatology
question: A 25-year-old male presents with asymmetric arthritis, conjunctivitis, and urethritis two weeks following a diarrheal illness. He reports recent unprotected sexual encounters and has no significant past medical history. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A) Gonococcal arthritis
• This can cause arthritis and urethritis , but conjunctivitis is not typically a feature, and it usually presents more acutely with purulent arthritis.
• Option C) Rheumatoid arthritis
• It is a chronic autoimmune disorder that typically presents with symmetric arthritis affecting the small joints of the hands and feet .
• It does not typically follow an infection and is associated with other systemic symptoms and signs.
• It is a degenerative joint disease that is characterized by joint pain, stiffness, and loss of function , primarily affecting weight-bearing joints.
• It does not present with urethritis or conjunctivitis, nor it is related to an infectious process.
• Educational Objective:
• Reactive arthritis has a classic triad of asymmetric arthritis, conjunctivitis, and urethritis, typically following a genitourinary or gastrointestinal infection.
• Bolognia Textbook of Dermatology 4 th Edition Page No 148
explanation.ref: Rooks Textbook of Dermatology- 9 th Edition Page No 154.2

subject: Dermatology
question:
A 40-year-old woman with history of Bilateral Hilar Lymphadenopathy & Apple Jelly Nodules on Diascopy, presents with Skin Lesions as shown in the Picture. Which of the following investigations are recommended in this patient?

[Image: https://cerebellum-web-static.s3.amazonaws.com/media/public/images/2024/02/23/screenshot-2024-02-23-111055.jpg]

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Chest X-ray is an important investigation to evaluate the extent of Pulmonary involvement in sarcoidosis . Bilateral hilar lymphadenopathy , which is a hallmark of the disease is already mentioned in the MCQ, so next best option would be ACE levels.
• Option C. Giemsa Stain:
• Giemsa stain is not a routine diagnostic test for sarcoidosis. Giemsa stain is more commonly used for the detection of parasites and certain bacteria .
• Option D. Tuberculin Test:
• The tuberculin test may be considered in cases where tuberculosis is a differential diagnosis . Patients with sarcoidosis can have anergy, which is a lack of reaction to the tuberculin skin test . However, it is not specific to sarcoidosis.
• Educational Objective:
• In suspected cases of sarcoidosis, measuring ACE enzyme levels can be a valuable diagnostic tool due to their frequent elevation in this condition, aiding in the confirmation of the diagnosis.
explanation.ref: Rooks Textbook of Dermatology- 9 th Edition Page No 98.7

subject: Dermatology
question: A patient came with complaints of diarrhea, dermatitis and mental retardation. It was also found that he was a maize eater. What is the probable diagnosis?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A . Kwashiorkor:
• This is a form of severe protein malnutrition characterized by edema , an enlarged liver with fatty infiltrates , and dermatosis . The skin may appear flaky, with areas of redness and depigmentation , often described as a "flaky paint" dermatosis. This occurs because of the breakdown of the skin barrier and can lead to secondary bacterial infections.
• Option B . Night Blindness:
• Night blindness, or nyctalopia , is primarily caused by a deficiency of Vitamin A . This deficiency affects the eyes , leading to problems seeing in low light or darkness . The symptoms described in the question don't point towards a vitamin A deficiency.
• Option D . Beri Beri:
• Beri Beri is caused by a deficiency of thiamine (vitamin B1 ). There are two main forms: wet beriberi (affecting the cardiovascula r system) and dry beriberi (affecting the nervous system ). The symptoms can include weakness, pain, and mental confusion , but the classic triad seen in pellagra is not characteristic of beri beri.
• Educational Objective:
• Pellagra by recognizing its classic triad of Diarrhea, Dermatitis, and Dementia , especially in the context of a diet predominantly consisting of Maize , which lacks adequate Niacin and Tryptophan.
explanation.ref: Rook's Textbook of Dermatology 9th Edition Chapter 63 Page Number 63.4

subject: Dermatology
question:
Identify the condition given in the image:

[Image: https://cerebellum-web-static.s3.amazonaws.com/media/public/images/2024/02/23/screenshot-2024-02-23-111538.jpg]

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A . Pityriasis Rotunda
• Description: This is a rare skin condition characterized by large, round patches of dry, scaly skin as shown in the image below.
• Option B . Melasma
• Description: Melasma is characterized by brown to gray-brown patches , mainly on the Face (Cheeks, Bridge of the Nose, Forehead, Chin, and above the Upper Lip). It's often called the "Mask of Pregnancy."
• Description: An inflammatory muscle disease , dermatomyositis presents with skin rash and muscle weakness . The rash is often reddish or purple , seen on the eyelids, knuckles, elbows, knees , and sometimes the chest and back .
• Educational Objective:
• Acanthosis nigricans is marked by a Velvety Darkening of the skin in Body Folds. There's a notable link between Obesity, Acanthosis Nigricans , and Metabolic Syndrome , with the latter condition often being a visible indicator of Insulin Resistance . Recognizing Acanthosis Nigricans is vital as it can be a marker of underlying Systemic issues that may require comprehensive Medical Evaluation.
• Williams Gynecology 3rd Edition Page No 391
explanation.ref: Rook's Textbook of Dermatology 9th Edition Chapter 147, Page Nos 147.14-147.15

subject: Dermatology
question: A 60-year-old man, with a background of chronic obstructive pulmonary disease, long-term alcohol abuse, and homelessness, is hospitalized due to acute pneumonia. During a ward examination, you observe a brownish-red discoloration on his face, neck, forearms, and lower legs, accompanied by skin scaling and cracks. He reports difficulties with eating and drinking, ongoing vomiting, diarrhea, and appears somewhat disoriented. Which vitamin deficiency might be responsible for these symptoms?

explanation.detail:
• Niacin (Vitamin B3) deficiency
• Other Options Option A. B1 (thiamine): Deficiency leads to beriberi or Wernicke-Korsakoff syndrome , primarily affecting the cardiovascular and nervous systems , respectively. It does not typically cause the dermatological or extensive gastrointestinal symptoms seen in this patient.
• Option B. B2 (riboflavin ): Deficiency of riboflavin can cause angular cheilitis, glossitis, and seborrheic dermatitis , but these symptoms are distinct from the extensive skin discoloration and systemic symptoms described.
• Option D. B6 (pyridoxine) : Deficiency primarily presents with peripheral neuropathy, irritability, depression, and dermatitis , but unlike niacin deficiency, it does not typically result in the severe digestive and mental symptoms observed in this case.
• Educational Objective:
• niacin deficiency, or pellagra,
explanation.ref: Rook's Textbook of Dermatology 9th Edition Chapter 63 Page Number 63.4

subject: Dermatology
question:
A 43-year-old diabetic female presents with asymptomatic lesions as shown in the image below. On examination there was no scaling associated over the lesions. What is the diagnosis of this patient?

[Image: https://cerebellum-web-static.s3.amazonaws.com/media/public/images/2024/02/23/screenshot-2024-02-23-112251.jpg]

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A: Tuberculoid Leprosy –
• Tuberculoid leprosy can present with skin lesions, but it typically involves Sensory Loss , Nerve Involvement , and a Positive Skin Smear for Acid-Fast Bacilli . This patient's presentation does not match those characteristics.
• Option C: Lupus Vulgaris –
• Lupus vulgaris is a form of cutaneous tuberculosis and presents with reddish-brown plaques with apple jelly nodules on diascopy . This patient does not have the characteristic nodules.
• Option D: Tinea Corporis –
• Tinea corporis, a fungal infection , often presents with scaling and a more distinct border . The absence of scaling in this case makes it less likely.
• Educational Objective:
• The clinical features of granuloma annulare , which include asymptomatic lesions forming ring-like patterns without scaling , to arrive at the correct diagnosis .
explanation.ref: Rooks Textbook of Dermatology- 9 th Edition Page No 97.3

subject: Dermatology
question:
Match the following nutritional deficiencies in Column A with its clinical features in Column B:

[Image: https://cerebellum-web-static.s3.amazonaws.com/media/public/images/2024/02/23/screenshot-2024-02-23-112627.jpg]

explanation.detail:
• Explanation
• 1-c Vitamin A Deficiency - Phrynoderma , which is a condition characterized by Follicular Hyperkeratosis due to Deficiency of Vitamin A.
• 2-a Vitamin C Deficiency - Bleeding Gums , a hallmark feature of Scurvy , which is caused by a deficiency of Vitamin C. Vitamin C is essential for Collagen Synthesis , and its deficiency leads to weakened blood vessels , leading to bleeding gums among other symptoms.
• 3-b Vitamin B12 Deficiency - Beefy Red Tongue , also known as Glossitis , which can occur in the context of Vitamin B12 Deficiency , often part of the clinical syndrome of Megaloblastic Anaemia.
• 4-d Zinc Deficiency - Acrodermatitis Enteropathica , a condition specifically associated with Zinc Deficiency, characterized by Dermatitis, Alopecia, and Diarrhea .
• Educational Objective:
• The clinical features associated with Specific Nutritional Deficiencies :
• Vitamin A deficiency is associated with Phrynoderma , Vitamin C Deficiency with Bleeding Gums , Vitamin B12 Deficiency with Beefy Red Tongue , and Zinc deficiency with Acrodermatitis Enteropathica .
explanation.ref: Rooks Textbook of Dermatology- 9 th Edition Page No 63.7, 63.19, 63.21, 63.25

subject: Dermatology
question: A 6-Year-old child presented with palpable purpura over lower extremities and buttocks. There was associated Joint Pain and Abdominal Pain. What is the treatment option of this condition?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• The condition described in the case is Henoch-Schönlein Purpura (HSP) , which is characterized by Palpable Purpura over the Lower Extremities and buttocks , Joint Pain, and Abdominal Pain. Azathioprine (Option A) , Methotrexate (Option C) , and Cyclosporine (Option D) may be considered in refractory cases of HSP or when glucocorticoids are contraindicated , but they are not the first-line treatment options.
• Educational Objective:
• The primary treatment for Henoch-Schönlein Purpura (HSP ) is the use of glucocorticoids , especially in cases with severe symptoms such as abdominal pain or kidney involvement.
explanation.ref: Rooks’ Textbook of Dermatology- 9 th Edition Page No 102.15
